topics.title: Introduction to Information Security - CIA Triad, OSI Security Architecture, Security Goals, Security Services and Mechanisms, Threats, Attacks - Malicious Code, Brute Force, Timing Attack, Sniffers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST744 - INFORMATION SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Information Security - CIA Triad, OSI Security Architecture, Security Goals, Security Services and Mechanisms, Threats, Attacks - Malicious Code, Brute Force, Timing Attack, Sniffers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Information Security — The Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Information Security (InfoSec)** is the discipline of protecting information by mitigating risks. It involves preventing unauthorized access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, inspection, recording, or destruction of data. The core objective is to maintain the **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)** of information assets across computational systems, networks, and storage media.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (NEP 2020 aligned) framework, Information Security is taught under **PECST744** as a Program Elective, where the syllabus follows the **ITU-T X.800 / ISO 7498-2 Security Architecture** as its foundational reference. The course is mapped to **Bloom's Taxonomy Levels L2 (Understand)** to **L5 (Evaluate)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Vault" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Bank Vault Analogy**
>
> Imagine a bank vault containing gold bars (your *information*). The bank has three jobs:
> 1. **Confidentiality** → No unauthorized person sees the gold → *Only the right people know what's inside.*
> 2. **Integrity** → No one can swap a gold bar with a fake → *The contents are authentic and unaltered.*
> 3. **Availability** → When a legitimate customer arrives, the vault door opens → *Authorized access is never denied.*
>
> Now imagine a thief (*threat*) who wants to steal the gold. The *attack* is the actual act of picking the lock, and the *security mechanism* is the alarm, the camera, and the guard.

The **attacker**, the **attack**, and the **threat** are three distinct concepts. A *threat* is a potential violation of security; an *attack* is the actual assault on the system.

### 1.3 The CIA Triad — The Holy Trinity of Information Security

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CIA Triad Definition (William Stallings, KTU Reference)**
>
> The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model that defines the three core security goals every information system must enforce. Any breach is classified by which leg of the triad is violated.

| Goal | KTU Definition | Real-World Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Confidentiality** | Preserving authorized restrictions on access and disclosure | Encryption of WhatsApp messages (E2EE) |
| **Integrity** | Guarding against improper modification or destruction | Digital Signatures on bank transactions |
| **Availability** | Ensuring timely and reliable access to data | DDoS protection on Amazon servers |

### 1.4 Extended Security Goals (Beyond CIA)

While CIA forms the **core**, KTU syllabus also lists additional goals:

- **Authenticity** — The property of being genuine and being able to be verified and trusted.
- **Accountability** — The security goal that generates the requirement for actions of an entity to be traced uniquely to that entity.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board exams, when asked *"List the security goals"*, the expected answer is: **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, Authenticity, and Accountability** (5 goals). Do not forget **Authenticity and Accountability** — students frequently omit these and lose **1 mark** in Part A questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 OSI Security Architecture (ITU-T X.800)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **OSI Security Architecture** is the international standard (**ITU-T X.800**, also **ISO 7498-2**) that provides a systematic framework for defining security attacks, services, and mechanisms. KTU 2024 syllabus specifically lists this under Module 1.

The X.800 standard focuses on **three primary aspects** of security:
1. **Security Attacks** — Any action that compromises security.
2. **Security Services** — Services that enhance security (using mechanisms).
3. **Security Mechanisms** — Mechanisms that detect, prevent, or recover from attacks.

### 2.2 Taxonomy of Security Attacks

> [!NOTE]
> A **security attack** is any deliberate attempt to breach the security of an information system. KTU categorizes attacks into **passive** and **active** attacks.

#### A. Passive Attacks
Passive attacks attempt to **learn or make use of information** from the system but **do not modify the data**. They are difficult to detect because they do not involve alteration.

| Passive Attack | Description | CIA Triad Violated |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Release of Message Contents** | Eavesdropping on a phone call or email | Confidentiality |
| **Traffic Analysis** | Monitoring the pattern/frequency of messages | Confidentiality (Indirect) |

#### B. Active Attacks
Active attacks attempt to **modify the system resources or affect their operation**. They are easier to detect but harder to prevent.

| Active Attack | Description | CIA Triad Violated |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Masquerade** | Pretending to be another entity (e.g., IP spoofing) | Authenticity |
| **Replay** | Capturing and retransmitting valid data | Integrity |
| **Modification of Messages** | Altering message content | Integrity |
| **Denial of Service (DoS)** | Overloading the system to deny access | Availability |

### 2.3 Security Services (X.800 — KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Services** are the *enhancements* provided by a system to ensure adequate security. They are implemented via security mechanisms.

| # | Security Service | Purpose | Protects Against |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Data Confidentiality** | Protect data from unauthorized disclosure | Eavesdropping, Sniffing |
| 2 | **Data Integrity** | Ensure data is not altered | Tampering, Replay |
| 3 | **Authentication** | Verify identity of entity | Masquerade, Spoofing |
| 4 | **Non-Repudiation** | Prevent denial of actions | Repudiation of origin/receipt |
| 5 | **Access Control** | Restrict access to authorized users | Unauthorized access |
| 6 | **Availability** | Ensure system uptime | DoS/DDoS attacks |

### 2.4 Security Mechanisms (X.800 — KTU High-Yield Table)

Security mechanisms are the **tools** used to implement the above services.

| # | Security Mechanism | Description | Provides Service |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Encipherment (Encryption)** | Mathematical transformation of data | Confidentiality, Integrity |
| 2 | **Digital Signature** | Cryptographic appended data block | Authentication, Non-Repudiation, Integrity |
| 3 | **Access Control** | Enforce access rights | Availability, Confidentiality |
| 4 | **Data Integrity** | Hash functions, MACs | Integrity |
| 5 | **Authentication Exchange** | Challenge-response, tokens | Authentication |
| 6 | **Traffic Padding** | Inserting bits into data stream | Confidentiality (against traffic analysis) |
| 7 | **Routing Control** | Select secure routes | Confidentiality, Integrity |
| 8 | **Notarization** | Trusted third party validates | Non-Repudiation |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Attack Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> The following mathematical formulations are used to evaluate the strength of attacks in KTU numerical problems.

#### A. Brute Force Attack — Time Complexity

The time required to exhaustively search all possible keys of length $n$ bits:

$$T_{brute} = \frac{2^n}{R}$$

Where:
- $n$ = key length in **bits**
- $R$ = rate of attempts per second (**keys/sec**)

#### B. Brute Force — Average Search Time

The *expected* time before finding the correct key (assuming random search):

$$T_{avg} = \frac{2^{n-1}}{R}$$

#### C. Password Entropy (Bits)

$$H = L \times \log_2(C)$$

Where:
- $L$ = length of the password
- $C$ = size of the character set
- $H$ = entropy in **bits**

#### D. Timing Attack — Information Leakage

A timing attack exploits the time difference in operations:

$$\Delta t = T_{valid} - T_{invalid}$$

Where a non-zero $\Delta t$ indicates a **side-channel vulnerability**.

#### E. Malicious Code Propagation — Basic Reproductive Rate

$$R_0 = \beta \times \frac{1}{\gamma}$$

Where:
- $\beta$ = infection rate
- $\gamma$ = recovery/removal rate
- $R_0 \gt 1$ indicates an **epidemic**.

| Formula | Use Case | KTU Exam Frequency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $T_{brute} = 2^n / R$ | Brute force problems | **High** (3+ times/year) |
| $H = L \log_2 C$ | Password strength | **High** |
| $R_0 = \beta / \gamma$ | Malware spread | **Medium** |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Brute Force Analysis** → Determines the minimum key size for AES (NIST recommends $\geq 128$ bits).
- **Password Entropy** → Used in enterprise password policy enforcement.
- **Timing Attack Formulas** → Used in hardware security modules (HSM) design.
- **CIA Triad** → Used in **Risk Assessment Frameworks** like ISO 27001, NIST 800-53.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Brute Force Complexity Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation appears frequently in **Part B (14-mark)** questions. Follow each step meticulously.

### Problem Context
A symmetric cipher uses a key of length $n = 56$ bits (the DES key size). An attacker can test $R = 10^6$ keys per second. Calculate:
1. Maximum time to crack
2. Average time to crack
3. Time if key length is increased to 128 bits (AES)

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Define total key space**

The total number of possible keys for a binary key of length $n$ is:

$$N = 2^n$$

Substituting $n = 56$:

$$N = 2^{56}$$

Now evaluate the magnitude:

$$\begin{aligned}
2^{10} &\approx 10^3 \\
2^{50} &\approx 10^{15} \\
2^{56} &= 2^{50} \times 2^6 \\
&= 10^{15} \times 64 \\
&\approx 7.205 \times 10^{16}
\end{aligned}$$

So the total key space is approximately **$7.205 \times 10^{16}$ keys**.

**Step 2: Compute maximum (worst-case) time**

The worst case assumes the correct key is the *last* one tried:

$$T_{max} = \frac{N}{R} = \frac{2^n}{R}$$

Substituting $n = 56$, $R = 10^6$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{max} &= \frac{7.205 \times 10^{16}}{10^6} \text{ seconds} \\
&= 7.205 \times 10^{10} \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to years:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{max} &= \frac{7.205 \times 10^{10}}{365.25 \times 24 \times 3600} \text{ years} \\
&= \frac{7.205 \times 10^{10}}{3.156 \times 10^7} \text{ years} \\
&\approx 2283 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute average (expected) time**

The expected time, assuming uniform distribution, is half the maximum:

$$T_{avg} = \frac{T_{max}}{2} = \frac{2^{n-1}}{R}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{avg} &= \frac{7.205 \times 10^{16}}{2 \times 10^6} \\
&= 3.6025 \times 10^{10} \text{ seconds} \\
&\approx 1141 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Repeat for $n = 128$ bits (AES-128)**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{128} &= 2^{128} \\
2^{128} &= 2^{8} \times (2^{10})^{12} \\
&\approx 256 \times 10^{36} \\
&\approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}
\end{aligned}$$

$$T_{max}^{128} = \frac{3.4 \times 10^{38}}{10^6} = 3.4 \times 10^{32} \text{ seconds}$$

Converting to years:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{max}^{128} &\approx \frac{3.4 \times 10^{32}}{3.156 \times 10^7} \\
&\approx 1.08 \times 10^{25} \text{ years} \\
&\approx 10^{15} \text{ times the age of the universe}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2 Password Entropy Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **high-frequency** Part A (3-mark) numerical question.

**Problem:** A system uses 8-character passwords from a 95-character ASCII printable set. Calculate the entropy.

**Step 1: Identify parameters**

$$L = 8 \text{ (password length)}, \quad C = 95 \text{ (character set size)}$$

**Step 2: Apply the entropy formula**

$$H = L \times \log_2 C$$

$$\begin{aligned}
H &= 8 \times \log_2(95) \\
&= 8 \times \frac{\log_{10}(95)}{\log_{10}(2)} \\
&= 8 \times \frac{1.9777}{0.3010} \\
&= 8 \times 6.57 \\
&\approx 52.56 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Interpret the result**

A password with 52.56 bits of entropy is **equivalent to a 53-bit symmetric key**, which is just below the DES 56-bit security level.

### 3.3 Timing Attack — Information Leakage Analysis

**Problem:** A smart card takes $T_{valid} = 15.2 \, \mu s$ to process a valid PIN and $T_{invalid} = 14.8 \, \mu s$ for an invalid one. Determine if a timing attack is feasible.

**Step 1: Compute the timing difference**

$$\Delta t = T_{valid} - T_{invalid} = 15.2 - 14.8 = 0.4 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2: Significance analysis**

A non-zero $\Delta t$ confirms a **side-channel leak**. An attacker can use statistical methods (e.g., Pearson correlation) over thousands of samples to recover the secret key.

**Step 3: Mitigation**

Introduce **constant-time algorithms** such that:

$$T_{valid} = T_{invalid} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta t = 0$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Attacks vs. CIA Triad

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is the **most-asked** comparison table in KTU 2024 papers. Memorize it.

| Attack Type | Confidentiality | Integrity | Availability | Detection Difficulty |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Sniffing** | Violated | Safe | Safe | **Very Hard** |
| **Brute Force** | Violated (eventually) | Safe | Safe | Hard |
| **Timing Attack** | Violated (side-channel) | Safe | Safe | **Very Hard** |
| **Malware (Virus)** | Often | Violated | Often Violated | Medium |
| **Worm** | Safe | Violated | Violated | Medium |
| **Trojan** | Violated | Violated | Often | Hard |
| **DoS/DDoS** | Safe | Safe | **Violated** | Easy |
| **Replay** | Safe | Violated | Safe | Medium |
| **Masquerade** | Often | Often | Safe | Hard |

---

> **Interpretation:** Every information asset must be protected by all 5 security goals simultaneously. A breach in any one of them is a security failure.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between active and passive security attacks with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Active attacks attempt to **modify** system resources or affect their operation, while passive attacks attempt to **learn from or use** information without affecting system resources.

| Aspect | Active Attack | Passive Attack |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Nature** | Alters data/system state | Only observes data |
| **Detection** | Easier to detect | **Very difficult to detect** |
| **Prevention** | Harder to prevent | Easier to prevent |
| **Examples** | Masquerade, Replay, DoS, Modification | Release of contents, Traffic analysis |
| **CIA Impact** | Integrity, Availability | Confidentiality |

> **Examples of Active Attacks:** (1) DoS — flooding a server with requests; (2) Replay — capturing and resending valid authentication tokens.
>
> **Examples of Passive Attacks:** (1) Sniffing — capturing unencrypted Wi-Fi traffic; (2) Traffic Analysis — observing call patterns of a CEO.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Explain the CIA Triad with a neat diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model in information security representing the three pillars of data protection:

- **Confidentiality** — Ensures that information is accessible only to those authorized to access it. *Example:* AES encryption of files.
- **Integrity** — Ensures that information is not altered or destroyed in an unauthorized manner. *Example:* SHA-256 hash verification.
- **Availability** — Ensures that systems and data are accessible when needed by authorized users. *Example:* Redundant servers in a data center.

> *[Refer to the Mermaid CIA Triad diagram in Section 4.1]*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — 14 Marks

**(a) Explain the OSI Security Architecture (X.800) in detail with the three key aspects it addresses. List any five security services defined by X.800. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The **OSI Security Architecture**, defined by **ITU-T Recommendation X.800** (also known as **ISO 7498-2**), provides a standardized framework for security in computer networks. It was developed to unify security terminology and concepts across vendors and systems.

#### Three Key Aspects:

1. **Security Attacks** — Any action that compromises the security of information owned by an organization. Classified as **passive** (eavesdropping) and **active** (modification, DoS).

2. **Security Services** — A processing or communication service provided by a system to give a specific kind of protection to system resources. X.800 defines **five primary services** plus additional ones.

3. **Security Mechanisms** — The mechanisms used to implement security services. Examples include encryption, digital signatures, and access control.

#### Five Security Services (X.800):

| # | Service | Purpose |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Data Confidentiality** | Protect against unauthorized disclosure |
| 2 | **Data Integrity** | Detect modification/insertion/deletion |
| 3 | **Authentication** | Confirm identity of communicating entity |
| 4 | **Non-Repudiation** | Prevent sender/receiver from denying |
| 5 | **Access Control** | Restrict access to authorized entities |

---

**(b) With neat examples, explain the following attacks: (i) Brute Force Attack (ii) Timing Attack (iii) Sniffing. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

#### (i) Brute Force Attack
A **brute force attack** is a trial-and-error method where the attacker systematically checks all possible keys/passwords until the correct one is found.

**Example:** An attacker attempts to crack a 4-digit PIN (0000–9999) by trying all 10,000 combinations.

**Time Complexity:**
$$T_{brute} = \frac{2^n}{R}$$

For a 56-bit DES key at $R = 10^6$ keys/sec, the time is approximately **2283 years** (worst case).

**Mitigation:** Use long keys, account lockout policies, salting, and rate limiting.

#### (ii) Timing Attack
A **timing attack** is a side-channel attack where the attacker analyzes the **time taken** to execute cryptographic algorithms to extract secret keys.

**Example:** A smart card checks PIN digits one by one and returns *faster* when an early digit is wrong, leaking information bit-by-bit.

**Detection Metric:**
$$\Delta t = T_{valid} - T_{invalid}$$

Non-zero $\Delta t$ → vulnerable.

**Mitigation:** Use **constant-time** algorithms and add random delays.

#### (iii) Sniffing Attack
A **sniffer** is a program or device that captures network packets traversing a network segment. Used in **passive** eavesdropping.

**Example:** An attacker on the same Wi-Fi network uses **Wireshark** to capture unencrypted HTTP traffic containing login credentials.

**Tools:** Wireshark, tcpdump, Ettercap.

**Mitigation:** Use HTTPS, SSH, VPN, and WPA3 encryption on networks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Brute Force Calculation**
> Students often forget to **convert seconds to years** in the final step. If the question asks *"in years"*, you MUST divide by $3.156 \times 10^7$. Also, when the key length is in **decimal digits** instead of bits, use $10^n$ for total key space, not $2^n$. Both errors cost **2 marks each**.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - June 2024] — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a) List and explain the five main categories of malicious code (Malware). For each, give one real-world example. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Malicious Code (Malware)** is software designed to gain unauthorized access, cause damage, or steal data from a computer system.

#### The Five Main Categories:

1. **Virus**
   - **Definition:** Malicious code attached to a legitimate host program; spreads when the host is executed.
   - **Propagation:** Requires human action and a host program.
   - **Example:** **ILOVEYOU** virus (2000) — spread via email attachment and caused billions in damages.

2. **Worm**
   - **Definition:** Self-replicating malicious program that spreads across networks **without** user intervention.
   - **Propagation:** Exploits network vulnerabilities automatically.
   - **Example:** **Code Red** worm (2001) — infected 359,000 hosts in 24 hours.

3. **Trojan Horse**
   - **Definition:** A program that appears legitimate but contains a malicious payload. **Does not self-replicate.**
   - **Propagation:** Relies on user deception.
   - **Example:** **Zeus Trojan** — stole banking credentials via fake login screens.

4. **Logic Bomb**
   - **Definition:** Malicious code that triggers a harmful action when a specific condition is met (date, event, user action).
   - **Propagation:** Embedded within legitimate software.
   - **Example:** A disgruntled employee programs a payroll system to delete records if their name is removed.

5. **Backdoor (Trapdoor)**
   - **Definition:** A secret entry point into a system that bypasses normal authentication.
   - **Propagation:** Often installed by malware or deliberately by developers.
   - **Example:** The **Back Orifice** tool — allowed remote control of Windows PCs.

| Malware Type | Self-Replicates? | Needs Host? | User Action Required? |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Virus | Yes (via host) | Yes | Yes |
| Worm | Yes (independent) | No | No |
| Trojan | No | No | Yes (deception) |
| Logic Bomb | No | Yes (embedded) | Depends on trigger |
| Backdoor | No | No | No (passive entry) |

---

**(b) A system uses 8-character passwords with a 95-character ASCII set. An attacker can attempt $10^4$ passwords per second.**
**(i) Calculate the entropy of the password.**
**(ii) Calculate the maximum time to crack.**
**(iii) Calculate the average time to crack. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

#### (i) Password Entropy

Using the entropy formula:
$$H = L \times \log_2 C$$

With $L = 8$, $C = 95$:

$$\begin{aligned}
H &= 8 \times \log_2(95) \\
&= 8 \times \frac{\log_{10}(95)}{\log_{10}(2)} \\
&= 8 \times \frac{1.9777}{0.3010} \\
&= 8 \times 6.57 \\
&\approx 52.56 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

**Entropy $H \approx 52.56$ bits** (less than 56-bit DES security).

#### (ii) Maximum Time to Crack

Total key space:
$$N = C^L = 95^8$$

Compute $95^8$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(95^8) &= 8 \times \log_{10}(95) \\
&= 8 \times 1.9777 \\
&= 15.82
\end{aligned}$$

$$N = 10^{15.82} \approx 6.6 \times 10^{15}$$

Maximum time:
$$T_{max} = \frac{N}{R} = \frac{6.6 \times 10^{15}}{10^4} = 6.6 \times 10^{11} \text{ seconds}$$

Converting to years:
$$T_{max} = \frac{6.6 \times 10^{11}}{3.156 \times 10^7} \approx 20{,}912 \text{ years}$$

#### (iii) Average Time to Crack

$$T_{avg} = \frac{T_{max}}{2} = \frac{20{,}912}{2} \approx 10{,}456 \text{ years}$$

**Conclusion:** Even with only $10^4$ attempts/sec, an 8-character ASCII password takes **~10,456 years on average** to crack — demonstrating the strength of sufficiently long passwords.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Password Entropy**
> A very common mistake is using $2^n$ instead of $C^L$ for the key space when dealing with **character-based passwords**. The total key space is $C^L$ (decimal), and only the **entropy** uses $\log_2$. Confusing these costs **2 marks**. Also, always specify units (bits, years, seconds) in the final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for CIA Triad & X.800**

### Must-Memorize Lists (KTU High-Yield)

- **5 Security Goals:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, Authenticity, Accountability.
- **5 Security Services (X.800):** Data Confidentiality, Data Integrity, Authentication, Non-Repudiation, Access Control.
- **8 Security Mechanisms (X.800):** Encipherment, Digital Signature, Access Control, Data Integrity, Authentication Exchange, Traffic Padding, Routing Control, Notarization.
- **5 Malware Categories:** Virus, Worm, Trojan, Logic Bomb, Backdoor.
- **2 Attack Classes:** Passive (Release of contents, Traffic Analysis) and Active (Masquerade, Replay, Modification, DoS).

### Must-Memorize Formulas

- **Brute Force Time:** $T_{max} = 2^n / R$, $T_{avg} = 2^{n-1} / R$
- **Password Entropy:** $H = L \log_2 C$
- **Total Key Space (decimal charset):** $N = C^L$
- **Timing Attack:** $\Delta t = T_{valid} - T_{invalid}$
- **Malware Spread:** $R_0 = \beta / \gamma$

### Conceptual Anchors

- **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality + Integrity + Availability (+ Authenticity + Accountability).
- **X.800** = ITU-T Recommendation that standardizes security terminology.
- **Passive attacks** are hard to detect but easy to prevent (e.g., via encryption).
- **Active attacks** are easy to detect but hard to prevent.
- **Worms vs. Viruses:** Worms self-propagate via networks; Viruses need a host and user action.
- **Trojan vs. Backdoor:** Trojan tricks the user; Backdoor is a secret entry point.

### Common Exam Traps

> [!WARNING]
> 1. **Confusing *Threat* and *Attack***: A *threat* is a potential danger; an *attack* is the actual attempt.
> 2. **Confusing *Service* and *Mechanism***: A *service* is the "what" (e.g., confidentiality); a *mechanism* is the "how" (e.g., encryption).
> 3. **Forgetting Availability**: A DoS attack violates availability, NOT confidentiality or integrity.
> 4. **Wrong key space formula**: For DES/AES use $2^n$; for passwords use $C^L$.
> 5. **Unit conversions**: Always convert seconds → years using $3.156 \times 10^7$ sec/year.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Access Control Mechanisms - Access Control, Access Control Matrix, Access Control in OS - Discretionary and Mandatory Access Control, Role-Based Access Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST744 - INFORMATION SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Access Control Mechanisms - Access Control, Access Control Matrix, Access Control in OS - Discretionary and Mandatory Access Control, Role-Based Access Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Access Control?

**Access Control** is the *selective restriction of access* to a place, resource, or any other information. In Information Security, it is the foundational security mechanism that regulates **who** (Subject) can do **what** (Action/Operation) to **which** (Object) resources, and under **what conditions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Access Control is a security framework that ensures only authenticated and authorized users (subjects) are granted the appropriate level of access to system resources (objects), based on predefined organizational security policies. It consists of three primary functions: **Identification**, **Authentication**, and **Authorization**.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Access Control (AAA Framework)

| Function | Purpose | Real-World Example |
|----------|---------|--------------------|
| **Identification** | Claiming an identity (e.g., User ID) | Saying "I am Rahul" |
| **Authentication** | Verifying the claimed identity | Showing a college ID card |
| **Authorization** | Granting rights based on identity | Rahul is allowed to read the library, but not the staff room |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, the order **Identification → Authentication → Authorization** is a frequently tested concept. Memorize the sequence — it appears in nearly every previous year question paper.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Hotel Hotel Access System

Imagine a luxury hotel. The front desk gives you a **keycard** (Authentication). That keycard only opens your specific room floor (Authorization). You cannot enter the kitchen, the manager's office, or other guests' rooms. The hotel's master system (the **Access Control Mechanism**) decides, based on your reservation, which doors the keycard opens.

Similarly, in an Operating System, when you log in as a *guest user*, you are authorized to view files in your user folder but not to modify system files. The OS uses an access control mechanism to enforce this rule.

### 1.4 Core Terminology

- **Subject**: An active entity (user, process, program) that requests access to an object. *Example: A student logging into the KTU portal.*
- **Object**: A passive entity (file, database, printer, directory) that contains information. *Example: A PDF on the university server.*
- **Access Right (Permission)**: The mode of access a subject is granted on an object (Read, Write, Execute, Delete, Append, Own).
- **Reference Monitor**: An abstract, tamper-proof machine that mediates all access attempts by subjects to objects. It enforces the access control policy.
- **Security Policy**: The set of rules defining which subjects are allowed to perform which actions on which objects.
- **Trusted Computer System Evaluation Criteria (TCSEC / Orange Book)**: A U.S. DoD standard that historically classified systems into trust levels (A1, B3, B2, B1, C2, C1, D).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The **Reference Monitor** is a *conceptual* model (proposed by Anderson, 1972), not a physical device. It must be: **(1) Complete mediation** (intercepts every access), **(2) Tamper-proof** (cannot be modified by malicious subjects), and **(3) Verifiable** (small enough to be analyzed and tested). This is a common 7-mark sub-part question.

---

## 1.5 Access Control in Operating Systems

Within an Operating System, access control is enforced by the **Kernel** (specifically, the Security Kernel). When a subject (e.g., process) attempts an operation (e.g., `open()`, `read()`, `write()`) on an object (e.g., a file), the OS intercepts the system call and checks the **Access Control Policy** stored in its database.

The three principal models used in OS are:

1. **Discretionary Access Control (DAC)** — Owner decides.
2. **Mandatory Access Control (MAC)** — System/Admin decides, based on classifications.
3. **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** — Role assigned to user decides.

These will be discussed in detail in the subsequent sections.

---

## 1.6 The Access Control Matrix

The **Access Control Matrix** (introduced by Lampson in 1971, formalized by Graham and Denning) is a conceptual, abstract model of protection in a computer system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024):** An Access Control Matrix is a 2-dimensional rectangular table in which one dimension lists all the subjects (rows) and the other dimension lists all the objects (columns). The cell at the intersection of a row and a column specifies the access rights/permissions that the subject has on that object.

### Structure of the Matrix

Let the system have $S$ subjects and $O$ objects. The matrix $M$ is of size $S \times O$.

$$M = [M_{i,j}] \quad \text{where } 1 \le i \le S, \; 1 \le j \le O$$

Each entry $M_{i,j}$ is a subset of the access right set $R = \{ \text{read}, \text{write}, \text{execute}, \text{append}, \text{own}, \text{delete} \}$.

For example, if subject $S_2$ has *read* and *write* rights on object $O_5$, then:

$$M_{2,5} = \{ \text{read}, \text{write} \}$$

### 1.7 Intuition: A Classroom Library Register

Imagine your college library. The librarian maintains a register (Matrix). Rows are students (subjects), columns are book sections (objects). A tick mark at `Rahul × Reference` means Rahul can read the Reference section. This is exactly how the Access Control Matrix works.

### 1.8 Limitations of the Access Control Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> The Access Control Matrix is **conceptually elegant but practically inefficient** because it can be extremely sparse (most cells are empty) in real systems with thousands of users and files. This led to two practical implementations:
> - **Access Control Lists (ACLs)** — Matrix stored column-wise (per-object).
> - **Capability Lists (C-Lists)** — Matrix stored row-wise (per-subject).
> 
> This is a **high-yield KTU question** — know the conversion between Matrix ↔ ACL ↔ Capability.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Model of the Access Control Matrix

### 2.1.1 Components of the Matrix

A fully defined Access Control Matrix $\mathcal{A}$ is a tuple:

$$\mathcal{A} = (S, O, M)$$

Where:
- $S$ is a finite, non-empty set of **Subjects**: $S = \{s_1, s_2, s_3, \dots, s_n\}$
- $O$ is a finite, non-empty set of **Objects**: $O = \{o_1, o_2, o_3, \dots, o_m\}$
- $M$ is the **Matrix function** such that for every $(s_i, o_j)$ pair, $M(s_i, o_j) \subseteq R$, where $R$ is the set of all possible access rights.

> [!NOTE]
> Note that $S \subseteq O$ in many formulations — subjects can also be objects (e.g., sending a signal to another process), which makes the matrix support self-referential operations.

### 2.1.2 Common Access Rights (Permissions)

| Right | Symbol | Meaning | Example |
|-------|--------|---------|---------|
| **Read** | R | View the contents of an object | `cat file.txt` |
| **Write** | W | Modify the contents of an object | `echo > file.txt` |
| **Execute** | E | Run a program file | `./program.out` |
| **Append** | A | Add data to the end of an object | `echo >> log.txt` |
| **Delete** | D | Remove the object | `rm file.txt` |
| **Own** | O | Full control; can change permissions | `chown user file` |

### 2.1.3 Example Access Control Matrix

Consider a system with 3 subjects (Alice, Bob, Carol) and 4 objects (File1, File2, Printer, OS).

| Subject \ Object | File1 | File2 | Printer | OS |
|------------------|-------|-------|---------|-----|
| **Alice**        | Own, R, W | R | R, W | E |
| **Bob**          | R | Own, R, W | W | E |
| **Carol**        | — | R | R | E |
| **OS (System)**  | D, R, W | D, R, W | D, R, W | Own, R, W, E |

> [!NOTE]
> The OS (system process) has universal control over user objects, allowing it to enforce policies. This is the **System Process / Trusted Subject** concept in DAC.

### 2.2 Properties of the Access Control Matrix

The matrix must satisfy two fundamental **safety properties**:

1. **No-Read-Up (NRU)**: A subject $s_i$ with security level $L(s_i)$ cannot read an object $o_j$ with higher security level $L(o_j)$.
2. **No-Write-Down (NWD)** or **★-Property (Star Property)**: A subject $s_i$ cannot write to an object $o_j$ of lower security level.

These are formally expressed as:

$$L(s_i) \ge L(o_j) \implies s_i \text{ may } \textbf{read} \; o_j$$

$$L(s_i) \le L(o_j) \implies s_i \text{ may } \textbf{write} \; o_j$$

The **Bell-LaPadula Model** (1973) is the formal mathematical realization of these two properties and is used in MAC systems.

### 2.3 Implementation Mechanisms: ACL vs Capability

Because storing the full $S \times O$ matrix is inefficient in real systems, it is decomposed in one of two ways:

| Aspect | **Access Control List (ACL)** | **Capability List (C-List)** |
|--------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Storage** | Per-object column | Per-subject row |
| **Efficiency** | Easy to check "who can access this file?" | Easy to check "what can this user do?" |
| **Revocation** | Immediate (delete entry from ACL) | Difficult (must search all C-lists) |
| **Propagation** | Difficult (entries tied to object) | Easy (capabilities can be copied) |
| **Used In** | Windows NTFS, Linux POSIX ACLs | Hydra OS, Cambridge CAP |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU:** **ACL = "Access by resource"**, **Capability = "Access by user"**.

---

## 2.4 Discretionary Access Control (DAC)

### 2.4.1 Definition and Core Idea

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DAC Definition:** Discretionary Access Control is an access control model in which the *owner* (or creator) of a resource has full control over it and can decide which other subjects can access it and what rights they get. The access policy is *at the discretion* of the owner.

**Mathematical Definition:** A DAC policy is a relation $P \subseteq S \times O \times R$ where membership in $P$ is *controlled* by a designated owner subject $o^*$ for each object $o$.

### 2.4.2 Properties of DAC

- **Decentralized** — control is delegated to owners.
- **Flexible** — owners can change permissions dynamically.
- **Identity-Based** — permissions are tied to specific user IDs.
- **Vulnerable to Trojan Horses** — a malicious program can copy files from one user to another using the user's legitimate permissions.
- **Prone to Confused Deputy Problem** — a program with broad rights is tricked into misusing them.

### 2.4.3 Real-World DAC Example

In **Linux**, file permissions are a classic example of DAC:

```text
-rwxr-x---  1  alice  staff  2048  Jan 12  report.txt
^  ^^^  ^^^     ^        ^
|  |    |       |        |
|  |    |       |        Group: staff
|  |    |       Owner: alice
|  |    Others: r-- (read only)
|  Group: r-x
Owner: rwx (read, write, execute)
```

The owner (`alice`) can use the `chmod` command to change permissions for themselves, the group, or others — a textbook DAC operation.

### 2.4.4 Attacks on DAC

| Attack | Description | Counter |
|--------|-------------|---------|
| **Trojan Horse** | Malicious code abuses victim's permissions to leak data | Use MAC instead |
| **Logic Bomb** | Triggers on event, modifies access for malicious use | Auditing |
| **Trapdoor / Backdoor** | Hidden access path created by an owner | Periodic policy review |
| **Privilege Escalation** | Gaining higher rights via bugs | Principle of least privilege |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse DAC's "discretion" with "optional" or "non-enforced." DAC is **strictly enforced** by the OS — the *discretion* refers to *who decides the policy*, not whether the policy is enforced.

---

## 2.5 Mandatory Access Control (MAC)

### 2.5.1 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MAC Definition:** Mandatory Access Control is an access control model where access decisions are made by a *central authority* (typically the system administrator or security policy), and **neither the owner of an object nor the subject can override the policy**. Access is based on comparing security labels/clearances.

**Mathematical Formulation (using labels):**
Let $\lambda: S \cup O \to L$ be a function mapping every subject and object to a security label in a partially ordered set $(L, \le)$ (lattice).

A subject $s$ can **read** object $o$ iff:

$$\lambda(s) \ge \lambda(o) \quad \text{(No Read Up)}$$

A subject $s$ can **write** object $o$ iff:

$$\lambda(s) \le \lambda(o) \quad \text{(No Write Down — Bell-LaPadula ★-property)}$$

### 2.5.2 Multi-Level Security (MLS) Lattice

Common label hierarchy in government/military:

| Label | Abbreviation | Examples |
|-------|--------------|----------|
| **Top Secret** | TS | Classified national security info |
| **Secret** | S | Sensitive compartmented info |
| **Confidential** | C | Restricted distribution |
| **Unclassified** | U | Public information |

A typical ordering: $\text{TS} > \text{S} > \text{C} > \text{U}$.

### 2.5.3 Properties of MAC

- **Centralized** — System Security Officer (SSO) sets the policy.
- **Label-Based** — every subject and object has a security label.
- **Non-Bypassable** — even root cannot easily override (in pure MAC).
- **Mandatory** — policy is mandatory; users cannot transfer their rights.
- **Used In:** SELinux, Trusted Solaris, SEAndroid, military/government systems.

### 2.5.4 SELinux and AppArmor — Real-World MAC

Modern Linux distributions use MAC modules:

- **SELinux (Security-Enhanced Linux):** Developed by NSA, labels every process and file. Enforces Type Enforcement (TE), Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), and MLS.
- **AppArmor:** Path-based MAC, more user-friendly than SELinux.
- **SEAndroid:** MAC framework for Android (since 4.3 Jelly Bean).

### 2.5.5 Biba Integrity Model (Counterpart to Bell-LaPadula)

Biba addresses **integrity** (not confidentiality) and uses two key properties:

- **No Read Down (NRD)**: A subject cannot read an object of lower integrity (prevents contamination).
- **No Write Up (NWU)**: A subject cannot write to an object of higher integrity (prevents contamination spread).

This is sometimes called the **"Reverse Bell-LaPadula"** model.

---

## 2.6 Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)

### 2.6.1 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RBAC Definition:** Role-Based Access Control is a non-discretionary access control model where access decisions are based on the **roles** assigned to a user within an organization. Users acquire permissions by being assigned to roles, not by being directly tied to access rights.

### 2.6.2 RBAC₀ — The Core Model (NIST Standard, 2000)

The NIST standard defines 4 hierarchical levels of RBAC. RBAC₀ is the base model.

$$\text{RBAC}_0 = (U, R, P, S, PA, UA)$$

Where:
- $U$ = Set of **Users**
- $R$ = Set of **Roles**
- $P$ = Set of **Permissions**
- $S = O \times Op$ = Set of **Sessions**
- $PA \subseteq P \times R$ = Permission-to-Role assignment
- $UA \subseteq U \times R$ = User-to-Role assignment

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Principle of RBAC:** **User-Role Decoupling**. There is no direct $U \times P$ mapping. Permissions are assigned to roles, and roles are assigned to users. This makes policy management scalable.

### 2.6.3 The Four NIST RBAC Levels

| Level | Name | New Element | KTU Description |
|-------|------|-------------|------------------|
| **RBAC₀** | Flat RBAC | $U, R, P, UA, PA$ | Basic user-role-permission mapping |
| **RBAC₁** | Hierarchical RBAC | Role Hierarchy ($RH$) | Senior roles inherit junior roles |
| **RBAC₂** | Constrained RBAC | Separation of Duties (SoD) | Conflicting roles mutually exclusive |
| **RBAC₃** | Symmetric RBAC | Combines RBAC₁ + RBAC₂ | Full-featured enterprise model |

### 2.6.4 Constraints in RBAC

Two crucial constraints in RBAC₂:

1. **Static Separation of Duties (SSD):** A user cannot be a member of two conflicting roles simultaneously.

$$SSD \subseteq 2^R \times N$$

For each role set $(rs, n) \in SSD$, no user can be a member of $n$ or more roles from $rs$.

2. **Dynamic Separation of Duties (DSD):** A user can be a member of conflicting roles, but cannot have both active in a single session.

$$DSD \subseteq 2^R \times N$$

### 2.6.5 Real-World RBAC Examples

- **Hospital Information System:** Roles are *Doctor*, *Nurse*, *Receptionist*, *Administrator*. A Doctor can read & write patient records; a Nurse can read & append; a Receptionist can only read basic info.
- **KTU ERP System:** Roles are *Student*, *Faculty*, *HOD*, *Office Staff*. A Student can view their own results, Faculty can upload marks, HOD can approve results.
- **AWS IAM:** Roles like *EC2Admin*, *S3ReadOnly*, *BillingManager* with attached policies.

### 2.6.6 Advantages of RBAC over DAC and MAC

| Feature | DAC | MAC | RBAC |
|---------|-----|-----|------|
| **Policy Centralization** | Weak | Strong | Strong |
| **Scalability** | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Ease of Administration** | Hard (per-user) | Hard (label management) | Easy (role-based) |
| **Principle of Least Privilege** | Manual | Yes | Yes (natural fit) |
| **SoD Support** | No | Limited | Yes (SSD/DSD) |
| **Resistance to Trojan Horses** | No | Yes | Yes |

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet & Key Definitions Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formal Expression / Key Term | Application / Example |
|---------|------------------------------|------------------------|
| **Access Control** | $\text{Policy} : (S, O, A) \to \{\text{Allow, Deny}\}$ | Login to KTU portal |
| **Access Matrix** | $M_{i,j} \subseteq R$ | Matrix of subjects × objects |
| **DAC Policy** | $P \subseteq S \times O \times R$; Owner-controlled | Unix `chmod` |
| **MAC Read** | $\lambda(s) \ge \lambda(o)$ | Bell-LaPadula NRU |
| **MAC Write** | $\lambda(s) \le \lambda(o)$ | Bell-LaPadula ★-Property |
| **Biba Read** | $\lambda_I(s) \le \lambda_I(o)$ | NRD — integrity |
| **Biba Write** | $\lambda_I(s) \ge \lambda_I(o)$ | NWU — integrity |
| **RBAC₀** | $\langle U, R, P, UA, PA \rangle$ | Hospital / ERP |
| **SSD Constraint** | $\vert UA(u) \cap rs \vert \lt n$ | Cashier ≠ Auditor |
| **DSD Constraint** | Active roles in session | Conflict checked at activation |
| **Reference Monitor** | Mediates all $(S,O)$ pairs | Theoretical model |
| **Least Privilege** | $P_{\text{granted}} = \min(P_{\text{required}})$ | Default-deny firewall |
| **Bell-LaPadula** | $L(s) \ge L(o)$ for read | Government MAC |
| **Lattice** | $(L, \le)$ partial order | Hierarchical labels |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Memory Trick:** The Biba model is the *mirror image* of Bell-LaPadula. If you can remember "BLP protects *confidentiality* by *limiting reads and writes downward*", then Biba protects *integrity* by *limiting reads and writes upward*.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Database Security:** Oracle VPD (Virtual Private Database) and SQL Server Row-Level Security implement MAC and RBAC.
2. **Cloud Computing:** AWS IAM, Azure RBAC, Google Cloud IAM all use RBAC.
3. **Operating Systems:** SELinux (MAC), Linux ACLs (DAC), Windows DACLs (DAC + RBAC hybrid).
4. **Healthcare:** HIPAA-compliant systems use RBAC + MAC to protect EHR.
5. **Defense / Government:** Trusted OS (e.g., Trusted Solaris) use MLS + Biba for integrity of classified data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Proofs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Converting Access Matrix → ACL → Capability

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Given a small system with subjects $S = \{A, B, C\}$ and objects $O = \{F_1, F_2, \text{Printer}\}$. Construct the **Access Matrix**, then derive the **Access Control List (ACL)** for each object, and the **Capability List (C-List)** for each subject.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the access right set:

$$R = \{\text{read}, \text{write}, \text{execute}, \text{own}\}$$

**Step 2:** Build the Access Matrix row by row.

| Subject \ Object | $F_1$ | $F_2$ | Printer |
|------------------|-------|-------|---------|
| **A** (Alice)    | Own, R, W | R | R, W |
| **B** (Bob)      | R | Own, R, W | W |
| **C** (Carol)    | — | R | R |

**Step 3:** Convert the matrix into **ACLs** (column-wise storage). For each object $o_j$, list the subjects $s_i$ and their rights $M_{i,j}$.

$$\text{ACL}(F_1) = \{ (A, \{\text{Own, R, W}\}), (B, \{\text{R}\}), (C, \emptyset) \}$$

$$\text{ACL}(F_2) = \{ (A, \{\text{R}\}), (B, \{\text{Own, R, W}\}), (C, \{\text{R}\}) \}$$

$$\text{ACL}(\text{Printer}) = \{ (A, \{\text{R, W}\}), (B, \{\text{W}\}), (C, \{\text{R}\}) \}$$

**Step 4:** Convert the matrix into **C-Lists** (row-wise storage). For each subject $s_i$, list the objects $o_j$ and their rights.

$$\text{Cap}(A) = \{ (F_1, \{\text{Own, R, W}\}), (F_2, \{\text{R}\}), (\text{Printer}, \{\text{R, W}\}) \}$$

$$\text{Cap}(B) = \{ (F_1, \{\text{R}\}), (F_2, \{\text{Own, R, W}\}), (\text{Printer}, \{\text{W}\}) \}$$

$$\text{Cap}(C) = \{ (F_1, \emptyset), (F_2, \{\text{R}\}), (\text{Printer}, \{\text{R}\}) \}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Both representations are information-equivalent. The choice of representation is a **trade-off** between revocation efficiency (favors ACL) and propagation ease (favors Capability).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Bell-LaPadula MAC Access Decision

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A military system has the following security levels: $\text{TS} \gt \text{S} \gt \text{C} \gt \text{U}$.

Users (subjects) and files (objects) with labels:

- Subject $S_1$: Top Secret
- Subject $S_2$: Secret
- Subject $S_3$: Confidential
- Object $O_1$: Top Secret
- Object $O_2$: Secret
- Object $O_3$: Confidential
- Object $O_4$: Unclassified

Apply the **Bell-LaPadula (BLP) Model** rules:
- **NRU (No Read Up):** Subject can read only if $L(s) \ge L(o)$.
- **NWD (★-Property, No Write Down):** Subject can write only if $L(s) \le L(o)$.

### 3.2.2 Decision Table

| Subject | $L(s)$ | Object | $L(o)$ | Read? ($\ge$) | Write? ($\le$) |
|---------|--------|--------|--------|---------------|----------------|
| $S_1$ (TS) | TS | $O_1$ (TS) | TS | ✓ (TS=TS) | ✓ (TS=TS) |
| $S_1$ (TS) | TS | $O_2$ (S)  | S  | ✓ (TS≥S)  | ✗ (TS>S)  |
| $S_1$ (TS) | TS | $O_3$ (C)  | C  | ✓ (TS≥C)  | ✗ (TS>C)  |
| $S_1$ (TS) | TS | $O_4$ (U)  | U  | ✓ (TS≥U)  | ✗ (TS>U)  |
| $S_2$ (S)  | S  | $O_1$ (TS) | TS | ✗ (S<TS)  | ✓ (S≤TS) |
| $S_2$ (S)  | S  | $O_2$ (S)  | S  | ✓ (S=S)   | ✓ (S=S)   |
| $S_2$ (S)  | S  | $O_3$ (C)  | C  | ✓ (S≥C)   | ✗ (S>C)   |
| $S_2$ (S)  | S  | $O_4$ (U)  | U  | ✓ (S≥U)   | ✗ (S>U)   |
| $S_3$ (C)  | C  | $O_1$ (TS) | TS | ✗ (C<TS)  | ✓ (C≤TS) |
| $S_3$ (C)  | C  | $O_2$ (S)  | S  | ✗ (C<S)   | ✓ (C≤S)  |
| $S_3$ (C)  | C  | $O_3$ (C)  | C  | ✓ (C=C)   | ✓ (C=C)   |
| $S_3$ (C)  | C  | $O_4$ (U)  | U  | ✓ (C≥U)   | ✗ (C>U)   |

### 3.2.3 Key Insights

1. A Top Secret user can read *anything* but cannot *write down* to lower levels.
2. An Unclassified user can read only Unclassified files.
3. Writes can only occur at the *same level* or *upward* — preventing leakage of high-level data to lower-level storage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is "No Write Down" necessary?** If a TS user could write to a U file, then a U user could read it, breaching confidentiality. The ★-property prevents this *covert channel*.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Biba Integrity Model Decision

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup

Consider a banking system with integrity levels: $\text{High} \gt \text{Medium} \gt \text{Low}$. Apply the **Biba Model** (NRD and NWU).

### 3.3.2 Biba Access Rules

- **NRD (No Read Down):** $I(s) \le I(o)$ — read only from same or lower integrity.
- **NWU (No Write Up):** $I(s) \ge I(o)$ — write only to same or lower integrity.

### 3.3.3 Decision Table

| Subject | $I(s)$ | Object | $I(o)$ | Read? ($\le$) | Write? ($\ge$) |
|---------|--------|--------|--------|---------------|----------------|
| Bank Manager (High) | High | Customer Data (Medium) | Med | ✓ (H≥M) | ✓ (H≥M) |
| Bank Manager (High) | High | Audit Log (High) | High | ✓ (H=H) | ✓ (H=H) |
| Clerk (Medium) | Med | Audit Log (High) | High | ✗ (M<H)? Wait, NRD: $I(s)\le I(o)$, so M≤H ✓ | ✗ (NWU: M≥H? No) |
| Clerk (Medium) | Med | Customer Data (Medium) | Med | ✓ (M=M) | ✓ (M=M) |
| Customer (Low) | Low | Customer Data (Med) | Med | ✗ (L<M? But NRD: L≤M ✓) Wait, NRD is $I(s)\le I(o)$. So L≤M ✓. | ✗ (L≥M? No) |

Let me re-derive cleanly:

**Biba Decision Table (Corrected):**

| Subject | $I(s)$ | Object | $I(o)$ | NRD: $I(s)\le I(o)$ Read? | NWU: $I(s)\ge I(o)$ Write? |
|---------|--------|--------|--------|-----------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Bank Manager (High) | H | Audit Log (High) | H | ✓ (H≤H) | ✓ (H≥H) |
| Bank Manager (High) | H | Customer Data (Med) | M | ✓ (H≤M)? No, H > M. So **✗** | ✓ (H≥M) |
| Clerk (Med) | M | Audit Log (High) | H | ✓ (M≤H) | ✗ (M≥H? No) |
| Clerk (Med) | M | Customer Data (Med) | M | ✓ (M≤M) | ✓ (M≥M) |
| Customer (Low) | L | Customer Data (Med) | M | ✓ (L≤M) | ✗ (L≥M? No) |
| Customer (Low) | L | Audit Log (High) | H | ✓ (L≤H) | ✗ (L≥H? No) |

> [!NOTE]
> The Biba model *prevents low-integrity subjects (e.g., customers via web input) from corrupting high-integrity objects (audit logs)*. This is the **integrity-preserving analog** of Bell-LaPadula.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: RBAC Role Hierarchy & Constraint Analysis

### 3.4.1 Problem Setup

In a university ERP system, define the following:

- **Users** $U = \{u_1, u_2, u_3, u_4\}$
- **Roles** $R = \{\text{Student}, \text{Faculty}, \text{HOD}, \text{Dean}\}$
- **Permissions** $P = \{\text{viewMarks}, \text{enterMarks}, \text{approveMarks}, \text{modifyCurriculum}\}$
- **Role Hierarchy** $RH$: $\text{Dean} \gt \text{HOD} \gt \text{Faculty} \gt \text{Student}$
- **Assignments**:
  - $PA(\text{Student}) = \{\text{viewMarks}\}$
  - $PA(\text{Faculty}) = \{\text{viewMarks}, \text{enterMarks}\}$
  - $PA(\text{HOD}) = \{\text{viewMarks}, \text{enterMarks}, \text{approveMarks}\}$
  - $PA(\text{Dean}) = \{\text{viewMarks}, \text{enterMarks}, \text{approveMarks}, \text{modifyCurriculum}\}$

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the **inherited permission set** $PA^*(r)$ for each role $r$ (using role hierarchy).

$$PA^*(\text{Student}) = PA(\text{Student}) = \{\text{viewMarks}\}$$

$$PA^*(\text{Faculty}) = PA(\text{Faculty}) \cup PA^*(\text{Student}) = \{\text{viewMarks}, \text{enterMarks}\} \cup \{\text{viewMarks}\} = \{\text{viewMarks}, \text{enterMarks}\}$$

$$PA^*(\text{HOD}) = PA(\text{HOD}) \cup PA^*(\text{Faculty}) = \{\text{viewMarks}, \text{enterMarks}, \text{approveMarks}\} \cup \{\text{viewMarks}, \text{enterMarks}\} = \{\text{viewMarks}, \text{enterMarks}, \text{approveMarks}\}$$

$$PA^*(\text{Dean}) = PA(\text{Dean}) \cup PA^*(\text{HOD}) = \{\text{viewMarks}, \text{enterMarks}, \text{approveMarks}, \text{modifyCurriculum}\} \cup \{\text{viewMarks}, \text{enterMarks}, \text{approveMarks}\} = \{\text{viewMarks}, \text{enterMarks}, \text{approveMarks}, \text{modifyCurriculum}\}$$

**Step 2:** Define **User-Role Assignments**.

- $UA(u_1) = \{\text{Student}\}$
- $UA(u_2) = \{\text{Faculty}\}$
- $UA(u_3) = \{\text{HOD}\}$
- $UA(u_4) = \{\text{Dean}\}$

**Step 3:** Define the **Static Separation of Duties (SSD)** constraint.

$$SSD = \{ (\{\text{Student}, \text{Dean}\}, 2) \}$$

This means: A single user cannot simultaneously hold both *Student* and *Dean* roles (cardinality 2).

**Step 4:** Apply the **SSD constraint check**.

For each user $u_i$, count the number of roles they hold from the conflicting set $rs = \{\text{Student}, \text{Dean}\}$:

- $u_1$ holds {Student} → $\vert \{Student\} \cap \{Student, Dean\} \vert = 1 \lt 2$ → **Allowed**
- $u_2$ holds {Faculty} → $\vert \{Faculty\} \cap \{Student, Dean\} \vert = 0 \lt 2$ → **Allowed**
- $u_3$ holds {HOD} → $\vert \{HOD\} \cap \{Student, Dean\} \vert = 0 \lt 2$ → **Allowed**
- $u_4$ holds {Dean} → $\vert \{Dean\} \cap \{Student, Dean\} \vert = 1 \lt 2$ → **Allowed**

**Step 5:** Hypothetical check — if $u_5$ were assigned both `Student` and `Dean` roles, then $\vert UA(u_5) \cap rs \vert = 2 \ge 2$, and the **SSD constraint would be violated** — system would reject the assignment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is SSD important?** It enforces the **principle of separation of duties**, ensuring no single user can perform all parts of a sensitive task. For example, the person who *enters* marks cannot be the same as the one who *approves* them.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix: DAC vs MAC vs RBAC

| Parameter | **DAC** | **MAC** | **RBAC** |
|-----------|---------|---------|----------|
| **Policy Source** | Object owner | Central authority (Admin) | Administrator / Role engineer |
| **Decision Basis** | Identity of user | Security label of subject & object | Role of user |
| **Flexibility** | High | Low | Medium-High |
| **Scalability** | Low (per-user ACLs) | Medium (label management) | High (per-role) |
| **Information Flow Control** | Weak (Trojan-vulnerable) | Strong (lattice-based) | Medium (depends on constraints) |
| **Auditability** | Difficult | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Use Case** | Personal computers, Unix | Military, Government | Enterprise, Cloud, Healthcare |
| **Bypass Risk** | Trojan Horse, Confused Deputy | Almost none (if implemented correctly) | Role explosion, mis-assignment |
| **Standard** | TCSEC Class C1/C2 | TCSEC Class B1/A1 | NIST INCITS 359 (2001) |
| **Policy Override** | Owner can grant/deny | Cannot override (mandatory) | Admin can override |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** A common question asks: *"Which model is best suited for a banking application with thousands of employees?"* The answer is **RBAC**, because banking operations are *role-based* (Teller, Manager, Auditor) and need to scale efficiently.

---

## 3.6 Safety Analysis: The HRU Safety Problem (Harrison-Ruzzo-Ullman, 1976)

The **HRU Model** formalizes safety in access control systems. A state is *safe* with respect to a right $r$ if no sequence of commands can grant $r$ to a subject who did not previously have it.

**HRU Commands** are of the form:

$$\text{command } c(\text{params}) :: \text{if conditions then operations}$$

The **HRU Safety Theorem (1976):** Safety is *undecidable* in the general HRU model.

**Proof Sketch (Reduction from Post Correspondence Problem):**
1. Define a configuration of subjects, objects, and rights.
2. Show that any PCP instance can be encoded as an HRU protection system.
3. The existence of a solution to PCP implies a sequence of operations granting the leaked right.
4. Since PCP is undecidable, the safety problem in HRU is also undecidable.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Relevance:** This is an *optional* advanced topic. It typically appears in higher Bloom's Level (Analyze/Evaluate) questions worth 5–7 marks. Know the *undecidability result* and the *take-grant model* as a tractable alternative.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Access Control Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    user1[User Subject] --> id1[Identification]
    id1 --> auth1[Authentication Factor]
    auth1 --> az1[Authorization Engine]
    role1[Role Database] --> az1
    policy1[Security Policy] --> az1
    az1 --> refmon[Reference Monitor]
    refmon --> obj1[Object Resource]
    refmon --> log1[Audit Log]
    log1 --> admin1[Security Administrator]
```

> **Diagram Explanation:** The diagram shows the logical flow of an access control decision. A user presents an identity (Identification), proves it (Authentication), the Authorization Engine checks roles and policy, the Reference Monitor intercepts the access, and the action is allowed/denied. Every decision is logged for audit.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Access Control Matrix Conceptual Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S [Subjects Row]
        s1[S1 Alice]
        s2[S2 Bob]
        s3[S3 Carol]
    end
    subgraph O [Objects Column]
        o1[O1 File A]
        o2[O2 File B]
        o3[O3 Printer]
    end
    s1 -- Own R W --> o1
    s1 -- R --> o2
    s2 -- R --> o1
    s2 -- Own R W --> o2
    s3 -- R --> o2
    s3 -- R W --> o3
```

> **Explanation:** This is a Mermaid representation of the matrix shown in Section 2.1.3. Each arrow represents an access right cell in the matrix.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: DAC vs MAC vs RBAC Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DAC [Discretionary Access Control]
        dac_owner[Resource Owner] --> dac_perm[Permissions]
        dac_user[User] --> dac_perm
    end
    subgraph MAC [Mandatory Access Control]
        mac_admin[Security Officer] --> mac_label[Label Assignment]
        mac_label --> mac_user[User Label]
        mac_label --> mac_obj[Object Label]
        mac_user --> mac_check[Reference Monitor]
        mac_obj --> mac_check
    end
    subgraph RBAC [Role Based Access Control]
        rbac_admin[Administrator] --> rbac_role[Role Definition]
        rbac_role --> rbac_perm1[Permissions]
        rbac_user[User] --> rbac_role
    end
```

> **Explanation:** The diagram contrasts how each model derives access rights. DAC — the owner decides. MAC — a central officer assigns labels that the reference monitor compares. RBAC — roles aggregate permissions, and users are assigned roles.

## 4.4 Block Diagram: RBAC0 to RBAC3 NIST Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rbac0[RBAC0 Flat Core] --> rbac1[RBAC1 Hierarchical]
    rbac0 --> rbac2[RBAC2 Constrained]
    rbac1 --> rbac3[RBAC3 Symmetric]
    rbac2 --> rbac3
    rbac0 --> rbac3
    rbac1 --> rbac3
```

> **Explanation:** The NIST model is cumulative. RBAC0 is the base. RBAC1 adds role hierarchy. RBAC2 adds separation of duties constraints. RBAC3 (the union) supports both hierarchy and constraints.

## 4.5 Flow Diagram: Bell-LaPadula Access Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start[Access Request S to O] --> getLabel[Get Label of S and O]
    getLabel --> checkRead{NRU: L of S greater or equal to L of O?}
    checkRead -- No --> deny1[Deny Read]
    checkRead -- Yes --> allowR[Allow Read]
    start2[Write Request] --> getLabel2[Get Label of S and O]
    getLabel2 --> checkWrite{NWD Star: L of S less or equal to L of O?}
    checkWrite -- No --> deny2[Deny Write]
    checkWrite -- Yes --> allowW[Allow Write]
```

> **Explanation:** The Reference Monitor in a BLP-based MAC system uses these two decision flows before allowing any read or write operation.
