# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Lifecycle of a Computer Virus

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Host Program on Disk"] --> B["User Executes Host"]
    B --> C["Virus Code Runs First"]
    C --> D{"Trigger Condition Met?"}
    D -- "No" --> E["Return Control to Host"]
    E --> F["Normal Program Execution"]
    F --> G["Virus Hunts for New Hosts"]
    G --> A
    D -- "Yes" --> H["Execute Payload"]
    H --> I["Damage: Delete Files / Corrupt Data / Exfiltrate"]
    I --> G
```

## 4.2 Worm Propagation Across a Network (Hit-List Style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Infected Host A0"] --> B["Pre-loaded Hit List of N Targets"]
    B --> C["A0 Probes Target T1"]
    C --> D{"T1 Vulnerable?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["Compromise T1, Upload Worm"]
    E --> F["T1 Now Infected; Halve List"]
    F --> G["T1 Probes Next Target T2"]
    G --> H["... continues recursively"]
    D -- "No" --> I["Skip; try next on list"]
    I --> G
```

## 4.3 Man-in-the-Middle Attack Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Alice as Alice (Client)
    participant Mallory as Mallory (Attacker)
    participant Bob as Bob (Server)
    Alice->>Mallory: "GET /login (intended for Bob)"
    Note over Mallory: Intercepts and Reads
    Mallory->>Bob: Forwards Request (possibly modified)
    Bob-->>Mallory: "200 OK + Login Page"
    Note over Mallory: Records credentials
    Mallory-->>Alice: Forwards Response
    Note over Alice, Bob: Neither party is aware of Mallory
```

## 4.4 Trapdoor / Backdoor Insertion Points in a System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1["Application Layer<br/>(User-space binaries)"] --> L2["OS Kernel<br/>(System calls, drivers)"]
    L2 --> L3["Hypervisor / Firmware<br/>(Ring -1)"]
    L3 --> L4["Hardware<br/>(Management Engine, baseband)"]
    L4 --> L5["Compiler<br/>(Thompson attack: backdoor in compiler output)"]
```

## 4.5 Salami Attack — Payroll Rounding Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Employee Salary Input<br/>e.g., Rs. 45,237.876"] --> B["Modified Payroll Program"]
    B --> C["Round Down to Rs. 45,237.87"]
    B --> D["Residual Rs. 0.006 → Shadow Account"]
    C --> E["Employee Bank Account"]
    D --> F["Attacker's Shadow Account"]
    F --> G["After 1,000,000 Salaries:<br/>Cumulative Theft ≈ Rs. 6,000"]
```

## 4.6 Covert Channel — Storage Channel via TCP ISN

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sender as Covert Sender
    participant Net as Network
    participant Receiver as Covert Receiver
    Sender->>Net: TCP SYN with ISN = 0x4A5B6C7D (encoded payload)
    Net->>Receiver: SYN forwarded
    Note over Receiver: Extracts 32 bits from ISN field
    Receiver->>Receiver: Decodes covert message
```

## 4.7 Topological Worm — Information Harvesting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Worm Infects Host H1"] --> B["Read H1's Address Book / Buddy List"]
    B --> C["Build Target List: T1, T2, ... Tn"]
    C --> D["Send Worm-Laden Mail to Each Ti"]
    D --> E{"Ti Opens Attachment?"}
    E -- "Yes" --> F["Ti Infected; Recurse"]
    F --> A
    E -- "No" --> G["Skip; Try Next"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L7["Application Layer (L7)"]
        V1["Macro Virus<br/>Salami Attack"]

    end

    end
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a Virus and a Worm. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Virus | Worm |
|--------|-------|------|
| Definition | Malicious code that **attaches to a host program** and propagates only when the host is executed. | Self-contained malicious program that **replicates over a network** autonomously. |
| User Action | **Required** to trigger (open file, run program). | **Not required**; exploits network vulnerabilities. |
| Host Dependency | **Needs a host file.** | **No host file needed.** |
| Speed of Spread | Slow; depends on file exchange. | Very fast; can saturate the Internet in minutes. |
| Example | **CIH (Chernobyl) virus**, **Melissa macro virus** | **Code Red**, **Slammer**, **Conficker** |

> **Marking Note (1 mark each):** Definition, Host vs. Network distinction, Example.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a Salami Attack? Explain with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Salami Attack** is a financial fraud in which an attacker steals **very small amounts of money** (typically fractions of a cent) from a large number of accounts or transactions, such that the individual losses are too small to be noticed, but the aggregate theft is substantial. (1½ marks)

**Example:** A programmer modifying a bank's payroll program so that every employee's salary is **rounded down to the nearest cent** and the fractional remainder (e.g., 0.003 paise per salary) is diverted to the programmer's account. Over millions of pay cycles, the cumulative stolen amount becomes significant, while no individual employee notices. (1½ marks)

> **Marking Note:** Mention (i) micro-theft, (ii) high-volume, (iii) sub-detection threshold, (iv) one concrete example.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **classification of malware** with a neat diagram, distinguishing at least **six types** (Virus, Worm, Trojan, Trapdoor, Salami, MITM). Describe **how a virus attaches to a host and triggers its payload**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **phases of a Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack**. Discuss **three specific MITM techniques** (e.g., ARP spoofing, DNS spoofing, SSL stripping) and the **defenses** against each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Definition of malware** (1 mark): Malicious software intentionally designed to damage, disrupt, or gain unauthorized access.
2. **Classification Table/Diagram** (3 marks):

| Type | Key Trait |
|------|-----------|
| Virus | Attaches to host, needs user |
| Worm | Self-replicates over network |
| Trojan | Disguised as legitimate software |
| Trapdoor | Secret undocumented entry point |
| Salami | Fractional financial theft |
| MITM | Intercepts communication |
| Covert Channel | Hidden information path |

3. **Virus attachment + trigger mechanism** (3 marks):
   - **Attachment:** Virus code is inserted at the entry point, beginning, end, or in a free area of the host executable.
   - **Trigger:** A *logic bomb* activates the payload when a condition is satisfied (e.g., date, time, file count, user action).
   - **Payload:** Once triggered, the virus performs its malicious action (data deletion, encryption, exfiltration).
   - Lifecycle: **Dormant → Propagation → Triggering → Execution**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **MITM Phases Diagram** (3 marks): Show Interception → Decryption → Forwarding.
2. **Three Techniques** (3 marks):
   - **ARP Spoofing:** Attacker sends forged ARP replies to associate their MAC with the victim's IP. **Defense:** Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI), static ARP entries.
   - **DNS Spoofing:** Attacker poisons the resolver cache to redirect domain lookups. **Defense:** DNSSEC, validating resolvers.
   - **SSL Stripping:** Attacker downgrades HTTPS to HTTP. **Defense:** HSTS, certificate pinning, TLS 1.3.
3. **Conclusion** (1 mark): MITM attacks require defense-in-depth across L2, L3, L7.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Pitfall (Part a):** Many students describe a virus's *behavior* but **forget the lifecycle phases**. The KTU key explicitly awards 2 marks for correctly naming **Dormant → Propagation → Triggering → Execution**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Pitfall (Part b):** Listing MITM techniques **without defenses** is a frequent error. The 2024 scheme requires *both* technique and countermeasure for full marks.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the **propagation model of a computer worm** with reference to the **Kermack-McKendrick epidemiological model**. Derive the **threshold condition** for an outbreak. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **covert channel**? Distinguish between **covert storage** and **covert timing channels** with examples. Explain **how a covert channel can be detected and prevented**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Introduction to epidemiological model** (1 mark): Treat the network as a population; hosts are *Susceptible (S)*, *Infected (I)*, or *Recovered (R)*.
2. **Define variables** (1 mark): $S(t), I(t), R(t), N, \beta, \delta$.
3. **Differential equations** (2 marks):

$$\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta S I, \quad \frac{dI}{dt} = \beta S I - \delta I, \quad \frac{dR}{dt} = \delta I$$

4. **Threshold derivation** (2 marks): At outbreak start, $S \approx N$, so $dI/dt \approx (\beta N - \delta) I$, giving exponential growth if $\beta N - \delta > 0$. Hence:

$$R_0 = \frac{\beta N}{\delta} > 1 \quad \text{for an outbreak}$$

5. **Conclusion** (1 mark): Defenders reduce $R_0$ by lowering $\beta$, lowering $N$, or raising $\delta$.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Covert channel definition** (1 mark): A hidden communication path between two processes that violates the system's security policy.
2. **Storage vs. Timing table** (3 marks):

| Aspect | Storage Channel | Timing Channel |
|--------|-----------------|----------------|
| Mechanism | Embeds data in a storage location | Modulates event timing |
| Example | TCP ISN, IP-ID, unused header fields | Inter-packet delay (0/1 bit) |
| Detection | Field-value anomaly detection | Statistical timing analysis |
| Capacity | 32 bits/SYN (ISN), 16 bits/pkt (IP-ID) | 1 bit/packet typical |

3. **Detection methods** (2 marks):
   - **Anomaly-based IDS** (statistical, ML)
   - **Information flow analysis** (non-interference models in trusted OS)
   - **Padding/normalization** of protocol fields
4. **Prevention** (1 mark): Use high-assurance kernels (SELinux, seL4), reference monitors, and regular covert-channel audits (per TCSEC Orange Book A1 class).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Pitfall (Part a):** Writing the equations *without* deriving the **threshold condition $R_0 > 1$** leads to loss of 2 marks. The KTU key mandates explicit derivation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Pitfall (Part b):** Confusing **covert channel** with **steganography**. Covert channels exploit *system mechanisms*; steganography hides data in *content*. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this conflation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Summary

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students lose marks on Module 2 malware questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Virus vs. Worm** — Worm does *not* need a host file or user action.
> 2. **Calling a Trojan a "type of virus"** — Trojan is *non-replicating* and *deception-based*.
> 3. **Omitting defenses in MITM/Trapdoor questions** — 2024 scheme expects *technique + countermeasure*.
> 4. **Writing worm equations without deriving $R_0 > 1$** — The threshold is the key takeaway.
> 5. **Treating covert channels and steganography as identical** — They are distinct concepts.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision before KTU ESE — go through this list:**

- ✅ **Virus** = needs *host file + user action*; has 4 lifecycle phases (Dormant, Propagation, Triggering, Execution).
- ✅ **Worm** = needs *network + vulnerable service*; no user action required; uses scanning strategies (random, routable, hit-list, topological, permutation).
- ✅ **Trojan** = *non-replicating*, deception-based; disguised as legitimate software.
- ✅ **Topological worm** = uses *victim's local topology* (address book, P2P); $R_0 = \beta \cdot \bar{k}$.
- ✅ **Trapdoor** = secret entry point, often developer-introduced; modern synonym = *backdoor*.
- ✅ **Salami attack** = micro-theft in high-volume financial systems; $L_{\text{total}} = n \cdot p \cdot \Delta$.
- ✅ **MITM** = Interception → Decryption → Forwarding; defenses include HSTS, cert pinning, DNSSEC, mutual TLS.
- ✅ **Covert storage channel** = hides data in protocol fields (TCP ISN, IP-ID, unused bits).
- ✅ **Covert timing channel** = hides data in inter-event timing.
- ✅ **Epidemic model** = $\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta S I - \delta I$; **outbreak iff** $R_0 = \beta N / \delta > 1$.
- ✅ **Defender's triangle**: reduce $\beta$ (slow scanning), reduce $N$ (segmentation), increase $\delta$ (patching).
- ✅ Always give **technique + countermeasure** in MITM/trapdoor questions.
- ✅ Always **derive the threshold** in worm-modelling questions.
- ✅ Remember the **Orange Book A1** requirement of covert-channel analysis for highest-evaluated systems.
- ✅ Covert channel ≠ Steganography; trapdoor ≠ Rootkit; Trojan ≠ Virus.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Malwares & Advanced Attacks**
*Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | 2024 Scheme | PECST744 — Information Security*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST744 - INFORMATION SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Security of Information Storage - Processing, and Transmission. Information Security Management - The ISO Standards Relating to Information Security - Other Information Security Management Frameworks - Security Policies - Security Controls - The Risk Management Process - Regulations and Legal Frameworks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Security of Information Storage, Processing, and Transmission

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
**Information Security** is the practice of protecting information by mitigating risks. It involves preventing unauthorized access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, or destruction of data, whether in **storage (data at rest)**, **processing (data in use)**, or **transmission (data in motion)**. The discipline is built upon the foundational **CIA Triad**: **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability**, often extended to include **Authenticity, Non-repudiation, and Accountability**.

> [!NOTE]
> **CIA Triad — The Core Mandate of KTU Module 3**
> - **Confidentiality**: Ensures that information is accessible only to those authorized to view it.
> - **Integrity**: Guarantees that information is accurate and has not been tampered with.
> - **Availability**: Ensures that information and systems are accessible when needed by authorized users.

### The Three States of Data (KTU High-Yield Concept)

| State | Description | Example Threats | Typical Controls |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data at Rest** | Information stored on physical media (HDDs, SSDs, USBs, databases, cloud storage). | Theft, physical damage, unauthorized file access. | **Encryption (AES-256)**, access control lists (ACLs), disk-level security. |
| **Data in Processing (Data in Use)** | Information actively being read, written, or manipulated in memory or CPU registers. | Memory dumps, cold-boot attacks, side-channel attacks, malicious insiders. | **TPM, Secure Enclaves, Memory encryption, Sandboxing**. |
| **Data in Motion (Data in Transit)** | Information traveling across a network between systems. | Eavesdropping, Man-in-the-Middle (MitM), packet sniffing. | **TLS/SSL, IPsec, VPN, SSH**. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** Questions in this module often test the *distinction* between these three states. A common 3-mark question is: *"Differentiate between data at rest, data in use, and data in transit with suitable examples."*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of information security like securing a **highly confidential courier package** in a high-security logistics company:
- **Storage (At Rest)** = The package inside a bank vault. The vault must have thick steel doors, biometric locks, and cameras.
- **Processing (In Use)** = An authorized bank teller opening the vault to count the cash. The teller is supervised, and only specific notes can be touched.
- **Transmission (In Motion)** = The armored truck carrying the cash from one bank branch to another. The truck is GPS-tracked, has armed guards, and uses an encrypted radio channel.

If any one of these three links fails — vault, teller, or truck — the money is at risk. Similarly, an organization must secure data in all three states simultaneously.

---

## 1.2 Information Security Management (ISM)

### Formal Definition
**Information Security Management (ISM)** refers to the systematic, risk-based approach an organization adopts to manage and protect its information assets. It encompasses the policies, procedures, standards, guidelines, and controls that collectively ensure the **CIA Triad** is upheld across the enterprise.

### Why ISM is Required
- **Proactive Defense**: Reactive security (patching after an attack) is costly. ISM shifts posture to *preventive* and *detective* controls.
- **Regulatory Compliance**: Laws like GDPR, HIPAA, and PCI-DSS mandate structured ISM.
- **Business Continuity**: Protects reputation, customer trust, and revenue.
- **Standardization**: Provides a common language and framework across departments and geographies.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — ISM as the Immune System:** Just as the human immune system does not rely on a single white blood cell but on a coordinated network (skin, antibodies, T-cells, memory), ISM is not a single firewall but a coordinated ecosystem of policies, technologies, people, and processes.

---

## 1.3 ISO Standards Relating to Information Security

### The ISO/IEC 27000 Family — The Global Benchmark
The **ISO/IEC 27000 series** is the most widely recognized international standard for Information Security Management Systems (ISMS). It is published jointly by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** and the **International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)**.

| Standard | Full Title | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO/IEC 27000** | Vocabulary and definitions | Foundational glossary. |
| **ISO/IEC 27001** | ISMS Requirements | The *certifiable* standard. Specifies mandatory requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and improving an ISMS. |
| **ISO/IEC 27002** | Code of Practice for Information Security Controls | Provides guidelines and best-practice *controls* (organizational, people, physical, technological). |
| **ISO/IEC 27003** | ISMS Implementation Guidance | "How-to" guide. |
| **ISO/IEC 27004** | ISMS Monitoring, Measurement, Analysis | Defines KPIs for security. |
| **ISO/IEC 27005** | Information Security Risk Management | Risk methodology aligned with 27001. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** *ISO 27001 is the standard against which organizations are audited and certified.* ISO 27002 is the *supporting* code of practice that contains the control catalog. Students often confuse the two — remember: **27001 = Requirements (what to do), 27002 = Controls (how to do it).**

---

## 1.4 Other Information Security Management Frameworks

### Formal Definition
A **Framework** is a structured set of guidelines, best practices, and processes that organizations use to manage complex security challenges. While ISO 27001 is the global standard, several other frameworks address specific niches.

| Framework | Origin | Focus | KTU Key Takeaway |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NIST CSF (Cybersecurity Framework)** | USA (NIST) | Voluntary, risk-based, flexible for critical infrastructure. | Five functions: **Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover**. |
| **COBIT (Control Objectives for Information and Related Technologies)** | ISACA | IT governance and management (aligns IT with business goals). | Strong on *audit* and *governance*. |
| **ITIL (Information Technology Infrastructure Library)** | UK (AXELOS) | IT Service Management (ITSM). | Focuses on *service delivery*, not pure security. |
| **PCI-DSS** | PCI Security Standards Council | Protection of **cardholder data** (credit/debit). | Mandatory for any entity handling card payments. |
| **HIPAA** | USA (Health) | Protection of **PHI** (Protected Health Information). | Healthcare-specific. |
| **SOC 2** | AICPA | Trust service criteria for service providers. | Audited reports: Security, Availability, Confidentiality. |

> [!NOTE]
> **NIST CSF Five Core Functions (Memorize This!)**
> 1. **Identify** — Asset management, risk assessment.
> 2. **Protect** — Access control, training, encryption.
> 3. **Detect** — Monitoring, anomaly detection.
> 4. **Respond** — Incident response, communication.
> 5. **Recover** — Restoration, lessons learned.

---

## 1.5 Security Policies

### Formal Definition
A **Security Policy** is a formal, high-level document that defines the *rules, principles, and practices* an organization uses to manage, protect, and distribute its information assets. It is the *cornerstone* of any ISMS.

### Hierarchy of Security Documents

| Level | Document | Audience | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Policies** (Strategic) | High-level, management-approved. | Entire organization. | "All employees must use multi-factor authentication." |
| **2. Standards** (Tactical) | Mandatory technical rules. | IT/Security teams. | "Passwords must be 12+ characters, alphanumeric + symbol." |
| **3. Procedures** (Operational) | Step-by-step instructions. | System administrators. | "To reset a password, navigate to Admin Console → Users → Reset." |
| **4. Guidelines** (Advisory) | Recommended best practices. | All employees. | "Use a password manager such as Bitwarden or 1Password." |

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — Security Policy as Traffic Law:** The *Policy* is the law ("Do not exceed 80 km/h"). The *Standard* is the technical spec ("Use calibrated speedometers"). The *Procedure* is the manual for traffic police ("Step 1: Radar gun... Step 2: Signal..."). The *Guideline* is the advisory ("Maintain safe distance").

### Types of Security Policies (KTU Frequently Tested)

- **Acceptable Use Policy (AUP)**: Defines permitted use of organizational IT assets.
- **Access Control Policy**: Who can access what, and how.
- **Data Classification Policy**: Sensitivity levels (Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted).
- **Incident Response Policy**: Steps to take during a breach.
- **Bring Your Own Device (BYOD) Policy**: Rules for personal devices.
- **Remote Work / Work-from-Home Policy**: VPN, secure Wi-Fi requirements.
- **Email and Communication Policy**: Anti-phishing rules.
- **Backup and Recovery Policy**: RTO (Recovery Time Objective) and RPO (Recovery Point Objective) targets.

---

## 1.6 Security Controls

### Formal Definition
**Security Controls** are the *safeguards* or *countermeasures* prescribed to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets and the systems that process them. They are the *practical implementation* of policies.

### Three Primary Categories (per ISO 27002 and NIST SP 800-53)

1. **Administrative / Managerial Controls** — Policies, procedures, training, background checks.
2. **Physical Controls** — Locks, fences, CCTV, biometric access, mantraps.
3. **Technical / Logical Controls** — Firewalls, encryption, IDS/IPS, antivirus, access control lists.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Control Functional Types:**
> - **Preventive** — Stop an incident *before* it occurs (e.g., firewall).
> - **Detective** — Identify that an incident *has* occurred (e.g., SIEM, CCTV).
> - **Corrective** — Restore systems *after* an incident (e.g., backups, patching).
> - **Deterrent** — Discourage attackers (e.g., warning banners).
> - **Compensating** — Substitute when primary control is infeasible.

---

## 1.7 The Risk Management Process

### Formal Definition
**Risk Management** is the systematic process of identifying, assessing, prioritizing, and treating information security risks to acceptable levels, in alignment with the organization's risk appetite and business objectives.

### The Six-Stage Process (Aligned with ISO 27005 and NIST SP 800-30)

1. **Context Establishment** — Define scope, assets, risk appetite, and evaluation criteria.
2. **Risk Identification** — Identify threats, vulnerabilities, and potential consequences.
3. **Risk Analysis** — Determine likelihood and impact.
4. **Risk Evaluation** — Compare against risk criteria; prioritize.
5. **Risk Treatment** — Mitigate, transfer, accept, or avoid.
6. **Risk Monitoring & Review** — Continuous oversight.

### The Four Risk Treatment Strategies (Memorize!)

- **Mitigation (Reduce)**: Apply controls to reduce likelihood or impact.
- **Transfer (Share)**: Insurance, outsourcing, third-party contracts.
- **Acceptance (Retain)**: Accept the risk if cost of mitigation exceeds potential loss.
- **Avoidance (Eliminate)**: Discontinue the activity that generates the risk.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — Risk as Rain:** You cannot stop the rain (threat), but you can (1) carry an umbrella (**mitigate**), (2) buy rain insurance (**transfer**), (3) decide rain doesn't bother you (**accept**), or (4) move to a desert (**avoid**).

---

## 1.8 Regulations and Legal Frameworks

### Formal Definition
**Regulations and Legal Frameworks** are *mandatory, government-imposed rules* that dictate how organizations must handle, protect, and disclose data. Non-compliance results in legal penalties, fines, and reputational damage.

### Key Global Regulations (KTU High-Yield)

| Regulation | Jurisdiction | Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **GDPR** (General Data Protection Regulation) | European Union | Personal data of EU citizens. Fines up to **4% of global annual turnover or €20 million**, whichever is higher. |
| **IT Act, 2000 (India)** | India | Legal recognition of e-records, e-signatures, cybercrime penalties. Amended in 2008 to include data privacy. |
| **HIPAA** | USA | Protected Health Information (PHI). |
| **SOX (Sarbanes-Oxley)** | USA | Financial reporting integrity. Section 404 mandates IT controls. |
| **PCI-DSS** | Global (Industry) | Cardholder data. |
| **CCPA** (California Consumer Privacy Act) | USA (California) | Consumer data rights. |
| **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023** | India | India's first comprehensive data protection law. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse *Standards* (voluntary, e.g., ISO 27001) with *Regulations* (mandatory, e.g., GDPR). ISO 27001 certification is optional but advisable; GDPR compliance is legally required for any entity processing EU residents' data.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of **Regulations as Traffic Laws** (mandatory, enforced by police, fines for breaking) and **Standards as Best-Practice Driving Manuals** (voluntary, used for training, no penalty for not following). ISO 27001 is a manual; GDPR is the law.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CIA Triad — Extended Analysis

The **CIA Triad** is the conceptual foundation upon which *all* information security controls, policies, and frameworks are built. Every security control in any standard (ISO 27002, NIST 800-53) can be mapped back to one or more of these principles.

### Confidentiality
- **What it guarantees:** Information is not disclosed to unauthorized individuals, entities, or processes.
- **Key Techniques:**
  - **Encryption** (symmetric: AES; asymmetric: RSA, ECC).
  - **Access Control Lists (ACLs)** and **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)**.
  - **Data classification** and **labeling**.
  - **Steganography** (hiding existence of data).
- **Threats:** Eavesdropping, shoulder surfing, social engineering, dumpster diving.

### Integrity
- **What it guarantees:** Information is accurate, complete, and has not been modified without authorization.
- **Key Techniques:**
  - **Hashing** (SHA-256, SHA-3, MD5 — deprecated).
  - **Digital signatures** (RSA, ECDSA).
  - **Message Authentication Codes (MAC, HMAC)**.
  - **Version control** and **audit trails**.
- **Threats:** Man-in-the-middle, malware modification, SQL injection.

### Availability
- **What it guarantees:** Information and services are accessible when required by authorized users.
- **Key Techniques:**
  - **Redundancy** (RAID, failover clusters, hot/cold sites).
  - **Backups** (full, incremental, differential).
  - **DDoS mitigation** (CDN, rate limiting, scrubbing centers).
  - **Disaster Recovery (DR)** and **Business Continuity Plans (BCP)**.
- **Threats:** DDoS attacks, ransomware, hardware failure, natural disasters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Extended Security Properties (Often asked as 3-mark questions):**
> - **Authenticity** — Verifying the identity of the sender/entity.
> - **Non-Repudiation** — Sender cannot deny having sent the message (achieved via digital signatures).
> - **Accountability** — Actions can be traced back to an individual (achieved via audit logs).

---

## 2.2 Data Protection Across the Three States — Engineering View

### 2.2.1 Data at Rest
Data at rest is *stationary* — stored on a medium. The most effective control is **encryption**, which transforms plaintext into ciphertext using a key.

- **Full Disk Encryption (FDE)**: BitLocker (Windows), FileVault (macOS), LUKS (Linux).
- **Database Encryption**: Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) in SQL Server/Oracle.
- **File-Level Encryption**: PGP/GPG for individual files.
- **Key Management**: Hardware Security Modules (HSMs), Key Management Interoperability Protocol (KMIP).

### 2.2.2 Data in Use
Data in use is in volatile memory (RAM, CPU caches, registers). This is the *hardest* state to protect because it must be unencrypted for the CPU to process it.

- **Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs)**: Intel SGX, ARM TrustZone, Apple Secure Enclave.
- **Homomorphic Encryption**: Allows computation on encrypted data (still emerging).
- **Memory Protection**: ASLR, DEP, Control Flow Guard.
- **Secure Boot**: Ensures only signed OS components execute at startup.

### 2.2.3 Data in Motion
Data in motion is transmitted over a network.

- **TLS 1.3**: Universal standard for web traffic (HTTPS).
- **IPsec**: Network-layer VPN protocol.
- **SSH (Secure Shell)**: Secure remote administration.
- **MACsec**: Layer 2 link encryption.
- **DNSSEC**: Authenticates DNS responses.

---

## 2.3 ISO 27001 — The Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) Cycle

ISO 27001 mandates the **PDCA cycle** for continuous improvement of the ISMS.

| Phase | Application in ISMS | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|
| **Plan** | Establish ISMS scope, risk assessment, risk treatment plan, Statement of Applicability (SoA). | "Establish the ISMS." |
| **Do** | Implement and operate the controls, provide training, manage operations. | "Implement controls." |
| **Check** | Monitor, measure, audit, review against objectives. | "Monitor and review." |
| **Act** | Take corrective actions, continually improve. | "Maintain and improve." |

### Mandatory Clauses of ISO 27001 (Clauses 4–10)

| Clause | Title | Focus |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | Context of the Organization | Internal/external issues, scope. |
| 5 | Leadership | Top management commitment. |
| 6 | Planning | Risk assessment, risk treatment, SoA. |
| 7 | Support | Resources, competence, awareness, communication. |
| 8 | Operation | Operational planning, risk treatment implementation. |
| 9 | Performance Evaluation | Monitoring, measurement, internal audit, management review. |
| 10 | Improvement | Non-conformity, corrective action, continual improvement. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Annex A Controls:** ISO 27001:2022 reorganized Annex A into **4 themes** with **93 controls** (down from 114 in 2013):
> - **A.5 Organizational** (37 controls)
> - **A.6 People** (8 controls)
> - **A.7 Physical** (14 controls)
> - **A.8 Technological** (34 controls)

---

## 2.4 Risk Management — Quantitative & Qualitative Analysis

### 2.4.1 Quantitative Risk Analysis
Numerical values are assigned to assets, threats, and impacts. The core formulas are:

$$ \text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value (AV)} \times \text{Exposure Factor (EF)} $$

$$ \text{ARO} = \text{Annualized Rate of Occurrence} $$

$$ \text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} $$

$$ \text{ROSI} = \frac{\text{ALE (before)} - \text{ALE (after)} - \text{Annual Control Cost (ACS)}}{\text{ACS}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Definitions:**
> - **SLE (Single Loss Expectancy)**: Monetary loss expected from a *single* occurrence of a risk.
> - **EF (Exposure Factor)**: Fraction of asset value lost (e.g., 0.5 = 50% loss).
> - **ARO**: Expected number of occurrences per year.
> - **ALE (Annual Loss Expectancy)**: Expected annual monetary loss.
> - **ROSI (Return on Security Investment)**: A positive ROSI justifies the control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\\vert` style for absolute values to avoid breaking markdown tables.** For example, write $\vert \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} \vert$ when needed.

### 2.4.2 Qualitative Risk Analysis
- Uses descriptive scales: **Low, Medium, High** or **Negligible, Minor, Moderate, Major, Catastrophic**.
- Often uses a **risk matrix** (Likelihood × Impact).
- Faster, cheaper, more suitable for intangible assets (reputation, brand).

### 2.4.3 Risk Matrix (5×5)

| | **Impact: Low (1)** | **Impact: Med (2)** | **Impact: High (3)** | **Impact: V.High (4)** | **Impact: Severe (5)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Likelihood: V.High (5)** | Med | High | High | Critical | Critical |
| **Likelihood: High (4)** | Med | Med | High | High | Critical |
| **Likelihood: Med (3)** | Low | Med | Med | High | High |
| **Likelihood: Low (2)** | Low | Low | Med | Med | High |
| **Likelihood: V.Low (1)** | Low | Low | Low | Med | Med |

> Risk Score = Likelihood × Impact. Action thresholds: Low (1–4) = Accept; Medium (5–9) = Monitor; High (10–15) = Treat; Critical (16–25) = Immediate action.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Comprehensive)

| # | Formula / Concept | Notation | Unit / Range | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF}$ | AV = Asset Value, EF = Exposure Factor (0–1) | Currency | Calculate single-incident loss. |
| 2 | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | ARO = events/year | Currency/year | Annual expected loss. |
| 3 | $\text{ROSI} = \frac{(\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}}) - \text{ACS}}{\text{ACS}}$ | ACS = Annual Control Cost | Ratio (unitless) | Justify control spending. |
| 4 | $\text{Risk Score} = \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Impact}$ | Both 1–5 | 1–25 | Risk prioritization matrix. |
| 5 | $\text{RTO}$ (Recovery Time Objective) | Max downtime tolerated | Hours | DR/BCP planning. |
| 6 | $\text{RPO}$ (Recovery Point Objective) | Max data loss tolerated | Hours | Backup frequency. |
| 7 | $\text{MTTF}$ (Mean Time To Failure) | Hardware reliability | Hours | Availability planning. |
| 8 | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | MTTR = Mean Time To Repair | Hours | Maintenance metrics. |
| 9 | $\text{Availability} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | 0–100% | SLA design. |
| 10 | GDPR Maximum Fine | $4\%$ of global turnover or €20M | Currency | Legal compliance. |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> - **ALE/ROSI** are used by CISO offices to build *budget proposals* for security tools (firewalls, SIEM).
> - **RTO/RPO** drive *disaster recovery site* selection (hot, warm, cold).
> - **MTBF/Availability** feed into *cloud SLA* contracts with AWS/Azure/GCP.

---

## 2.6 NIST Cybersecurity Framework — Deep Dive

The **NIST CSF** organizes all cybersecurity activities into **5 functions** and **23 categories** across **4 implementation tiers** (Partial, Risk-Informed, Repeatable, Adaptive).

| Function | Categories (Selected) | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Identify** | Asset Management, Business Environment, Risk Assessment, Governance. | Maintain an inventory of all servers in a CMDB. |
| **Protect** | Access Control, Awareness & Training, Data Security, Maintenance, Protective Tech. | Enforce MFA on all admin accounts. |
| **Detect** | Anomalies & Events, Security Continuous Monitoring, Detection Processes. | Deploy a SIEM with correlation rules. |
| **Respond** | Response Planning, Communications, Analysis, Mitigation, Improvements. | Activate the CSIRT when ransomware is detected. |
| **Recover** | Recovery Planning, Improvements, Communications. | Restore from backups, conduct post-mortem. |

---

## 2.7 COBIT 2019 — Governance & Management Objectives

COBIT 2019 organizes 40 governance/management objectives into **5 domains**:

| Domain | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **EDM** (Evaluate, Direct, Monitor) | Governance (Board level). |
| **APO** (Align, Plan, Organize) | Strategy and tactics. |
| **BAI** (Build, Acquire, Implement) | Delivery of solutions. |
| **DSS** (Deliver, Service, Support) | Operational delivery. |
| **MEA** (Monitor, Evaluate, Assess) | Performance management. |

COBIT is *heavy* on governance and is the preferred framework for *auditors*.

---

## 2.8 Security Policies — Structural Components

A well-written security policy document typically contains:

1. **Purpose** — Why the policy exists.
2. **Scope** — To whom/what it applies.
3. **Policy Statement** — The actual rules.
4. **Roles & Responsibilities** — Who enforces it.
5. **Compliance & Enforcement** — Penalties for non-compliance.
6. **Review Cycle** — Frequency of revision (annually, bi-annually).
7. **Approval** — Signature of CISO / CEO / Board.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often write "policy" for everything. Remember: *a Policy states the rule; a Procedure tells you how to execute it.* For example:
> - **Policy**: "All laptops must be encrypted."
> - **Procedure**: "Open BitLocker → select full-disk encryption → save recovery key to AD → restart."

---

## 2.9 Control Selection — The COSO Internal Control Framework (Related)

While COSO is *financial* in origin, the principle of selecting controls is universal:

1. **Control Environment** — Tone at the top.
2. **Risk Assessment** — Identify and analyze.
3. **Control Activities** — Approvals, authorizations, verifications.
4. **Information & Communication** — Flow of security info.
5. **Monitoring Activities** — Continuous oversight.

---

## 2.10 Legal Frameworks — Comparative Analysis

| Aspect | ISO 27001 | GDPR | IT Act (India) | HIPAA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Standard (voluntary) | Regulation (mandatory) | Law (mandatory) | Law (mandatory) |
| **Geographic Scope** | Global | EU residents | India | USA |
| **Penalty** | Loss of certification | Up to **4%** of global turnover or **€20M** | Imprisonment up to 3 years + fines | Up to **$1.5M/year** per violation |
| **Data Focus** | All information | Personal data | All electronic records | PHI |
| **Certifiable** | Yes | No (auditable) | No | No |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Risk Calculation (Worked Example)

> This is a classic KTU 14-mark problem: *"Calculate the ALE, justify control purchase using ROSI, and recommend a risk treatment strategy."*

### Problem Statement
An organization has an **e-commerce database server** worth **₹10,00,000 (10 Lakhs)**. A risk analyst has identified the following:
- **Threat:** SQL Injection attack.
- **Exposure Factor (EF):** 0.60 (60% of the asset is damaged per incident).
- **Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO):** 2 incidents per year.
- **Proposed Control:** Web Application Firewall (WAF) costing **₹2,00,000 per year** as a subscription.
- **After implementing WAF:** ARO drops to **0.5** incidents/year, EF drops to **0.30**.

**Determine:** (a) SLE before WAF, (b) ALE before WAF, (c) ALE after WAF, (d) ROSI of the WAF, (e) Recommendation.

### Step 1: Calculate SLE (Single Loss Expectancy) — Before WAF

The SLE represents the loss from a *single* incident.

$$ \text{SLE}_{\text{before}} = \text{Asset Value (AV)} \times \text{Exposure Factor (EF)} $$

$$ \text{SLE}_{\text{before}} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 $$

$$ \text{SLE}_{\text{before}} = 6{,}00{,}000 $$

**Result:** $\text{SLE}_{\text{before}} = \text{₹}6{,}00{,}000$ per incident.

*Explanation:* If a single SQL injection attack succeeds, the organization expects to lose ₹6 Lakhs (60% of the ₹10 Lakh server's value due to data corruption, downtime, and recovery costs).

### Step 2: Calculate ALE (Annual Loss Expectancy) — Before WAF

The ALE represents the *expected annual loss* across all expected incidents.

$$ \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = \text{SLE}_{\text{before}} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{before}} $$

$$ \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 2 $$

$$ \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = 12{,}00{,}000 $$

**Result:** $\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = \text{₹}12{,}00{,}000$ per year.

*Explanation:* With 2 attacks expected per year, the annual loss is ₹12 Lakhs.

### Step 3: Calculate SLE — After WAF

$$ \text{SLE}_{\text{after}} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF}_{\text{after}} $$

$$ \text{SLE}_{\text{after}} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30 $$

$$ \text{SLE}_{\text{after}} = 3{,}00{,}000 $$

**Result:** $\text{SLE}_{\text{after}} = \text{₹}3{,}00{,}000$ per incident.

*Explanation:* The WAF mitigates 50% of the impact (EF dropped from 0.60 to 0.30).

### Step 4: Calculate ALE — After WAF

$$ \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = \text{SLE}_{\text{after}} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{after}} $$

$$ \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 $$

$$ \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = 1{,}50{,}000 $$

**Result:** $\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = \text{₹}1{,}50{,}000$ per year.

*Explanation:* The WAF reduces the ARO from 2 to 0.5 (75% reduction) and EF by 50%, leading to a much smaller annual loss.

### Step 5: Calculate ROSI (Return on Security Investment)

$$ \text{ROSI} = \frac{(\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}}) - \text{ACS}}{\text{ACS}} $$

Substituting values:

$$ \text{ROSI} = \frac{(12{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000) - 2{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} $$

$$ \text{ROSI} = \frac{10{,}50{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} $$

$$ \text{ROSI} = \frac{8{,}50{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} $$

$$ \text{ROSI} = 4.25 $$

**Result:** $\text{ROSI} = 4.25$ (or 425%).

*Explanation:* The organization saves **₹8.5 Lakhs annually** *after* paying the WAF cost. Every ₹1 spent on the WAF returns **₹4.25 in net benefit**. This is a strongly positive ROSI — the control is justified.

### Step 6: Recommendation

Since ROSI > 1 (in fact, 4.25), the WAF control is **highly recommended**. The recommended risk treatment strategy is **Risk Mitigation** (specifically, applying a preventive technical control — the WAF — to reduce both likelihood and impact).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always state the final recommendation in *one of the four* treatment strategies: Mitigate, Transfer, Accept, or Avoid. Do not invent a fifth.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Framework Comparison

| Criterion | ISO 27001 | NIST CSF | COBIT 2019 | PCI-DSS | GDPR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Issuing Body** | ISO/IEC | NIST (USA) | ISACA | PCI SSC | EU |
| **Type** | Standard | Framework | Framework | Standard | Regulation |
| **Mandatory?** | No (voluntary) | No | No | Yes (for card handlers) | Yes |
| **Certifiable?** | Yes | No | No | Yes (QSA audit) | No (DPO) |
| **Primary Focus** | ISMS | Cybersecurity risk | IT governance | Card data | Personal data |
| **Best For** | All sectors | Critical infra | Auditors/governance | Retail/payments | Any EU-touching org |
| **Geographic Reach** | Global | Global (US origin) | Global | Global | EU + extraterritorial |
| **Maturity Model** | PDCA | 4 Tiers | CMMI-like | 4 Levels | N/A |
| **Update Cycle** | ~5–7 years | Continuous | Periodic | ~3 years | As needed |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Application of PDCA to an ISMS

> Another common 14-mark KTU question: *"Explain how the Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle is applied to an ISMS."*

### Phase 1: PLAN
**Objective:** Establish the ISMS.

1. Define the **scope** of the ISMS (e.g., the IT department of XYZ Pvt. Ltd.).
2. Identify **interested parties** (customers, regulators, employees).
3. Conduct a **risk assessment** (assets, threats, vulnerabilities, impacts).
4. Develop a **risk treatment plan** (which controls to apply, who is responsible).
5. Produce the **Statement of Applicability (SoA)** — a document listing all 93 Annex A controls and indicating which are applicable.
6. Get **top management approval**.

### Phase 2: DO
**Objective:** Implement and operate the ISMS.

1. Deploy technical controls (firewall, IDS, encryption).
2. Deploy administrative controls (policies, training, background checks).
3. Deploy physical controls (locks, CCTV).
4. Conduct **awareness training** for all employees.
5. Operate the **incident response** process.
6. Maintain **documentation** of all activities.

### Phase 3: CHECK
**Objective:** Monitor, measure, and review.

1. Conduct **internal audits** annually.
2. Measure **KPIs** (mean time to detect, mean time to respond, % of patched systems).
3. Conduct **management review** meetings.
4. Identify non-conformities and weak controls.

### Phase 4: ACT
**Objective:** Improve the ISMS.

1. Take **corrective actions** for non-conformities.
2. Update the **risk treatment plan** as new threats emerge.
3. Implement **preventive actions** for anticipated issues.
4. Re-enter **Plan** phase with updated risk register.

> The cycle then **repeats continuously**, ensuring the ISMS evolves with the threat landscape.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step — Building a Risk Register

> A 7-mark sub-question often asks: *"Prepare a sample risk register for an organization."*

A **Risk Register** is a living document listing all identified risks. Each row is a single risk. The columns are:

| Risk ID | Asset | Threat | Vulnerability | Likelihood | Impact | Risk Score | Treatment | Owner | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| R-001 | Customer DB | SQL Injection | Unpatched web app | High (4) | High (4) | 16 (Critical) | Mitigate (WAF) | DBA | Open |
| R-002 | Employee laptops | Theft | No encryption | Med (3) | Med (3) | 9 (High) | Mitigate (BitLocker) | IT Ops | Closed |
| R-003 | Cloud contract | Vendor failure | Single provider | Low (2) | V.High (4) | 8 (High) | Transfer (multi-cloud) | CTO | Open |
| R-004 | Reputation | Negative PR | No social media plan | Low (2) | High (3) | 6 (Medium) | Accept | Marketing | Open |

> [!NOTE]
> **Risk Register Best Practices:**
> - Assign a unique ID to each risk.
> - Assign an **owner** (accountable person).
> - Re-evaluate at least **annually** or when major changes occur.
> - Link risks to specific **Annex A controls** in the SoA.

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step — Selecting a Framework for an Organization

> Another frequently asked 14-mark question: *"Which framework should a startup in India adopt and why?"*

### Step 1: Identify Constraints
- **Industry**: Healthcare? Finance? E-commerce? Education?
- **Size**: 10 employees? 1000 employees?
- **Geography**: India only? Global customers?
- **Mandatory regulations**: GDPR? HIPAA? IT Act? DPDP Act 2023?
- **Budget**: ₹5L/year or ₹5Cr/year?
- **Customer demands**: SOC 2 reports? ISO 27001 certificate?

### Step 2: Map Requirements
- E-commerce + Indian customers + global payments → **PCI-DSS (mandatory) + IT Act + DPDP Act**.
- Healthcare + Indian patients → **DPDP Act + DISHA (now part of DPDP) + ISO 27001 (voluntary)**.
- SaaS provider serving US/EU enterprises → **SOC 2 + ISO 27001 + GDPR**.

### Step 3: Select a Framework as a *Scaffold*
- A **startup** → start with **NIST CSF** (lightweight, free).
- A **mid-size** organization → implement **ISO 27001** (certifiable, customer trust).
- A **regulated bank** → add **COBIT 2019** for governance.

### Step 4: Layer the Frameworks
Frameworks are *not mutually exclusive*. Mature organizations use **layered compliance**:
- **Layer 1 (Foundation):** ISO 27001 ISMS.
- **Layer 2 (Operations):** NIST CSF for tactical security controls.
- **Layer 3 (Governance):** COBIT for board-level reporting.
- **Layer 4 (Compliance):** GDPR, IT Act, PCI-DSS overlays.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "I-N-C-P"**: **I**SO 27001, **N**IST CSF, **C**OBIT, **P**CI-DSS — think of these as the four pillars most often asked in KTU.
