# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: Define the CIA Triad. Why is it considered the foundation of information security?
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model of information security, comprising three core principles:
- **Confidentiality** — Ensuring that information is accessible only to those authorized to view it (e.g., via encryption, access controls).
- **Integrity** — Ensuring that information remains accurate, complete, and unaltered by unauthorized parties (e.g., via hashing, digital signatures).
- **Availability** — Ensuring that information and systems are accessible to authorized users when needed (e.g., via redundancy, backups, DDoS protection).

The CIA Triad is the foundation of information security because *every* security policy, control, and framework in existence (ISO 27001, NIST CSF, COBIT) is designed to uphold one or more of these three properties. Without confidentiality, integrity, and availability, an organization cannot guarantee the trustworthiness of its information assets. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between a Security Policy, Standard, Procedure, and Guideline with examples.
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Document | Purpose | Example | Audience |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Policy** | High-level *what* and *why* — management intent. | "All laptops must be encrypted." | Entire organization. |
| **Standard** | Mandatory *how* — technical baseline. | "Use AES-256 encryption with TPM-backed keys." | IT team. |
| **Procedure** | Step-by-step *instructions* to perform a task. | "Step 1: Open BitLocker... Step 2: Save recovery key to AD." | System administrators. |
| **Guideline** | Advisory *recommendation* — not mandatory. | "Use a password manager such as Bitwarden." | All users. |

Policies are *strategic*; Standards are *tactical*; Procedures are *operational*; Guidelines are *advisory*. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice-Based)

> **KTU Pattern:** Two questions will be provided, and the student answers ONE. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A: 14 Marks
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the **PDCA cycle** as applied to an **Information Security Management System (ISMS)** under **ISO 27001**. Describe each phase in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **ISO 27001, NIST CSF, and COBIT 2019** as information security management frameworks. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — PDCA Cycle in ISMS (7 marks)

The **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle** is the foundation of ISO 27001's ISMS. It is a continuous improvement model.

**1. PLAN (2 marks)**
- Define the **scope** of the ISMS (e.g., a particular department or business unit).
- Identify **interested parties** and their requirements.
- Conduct a **risk assessment** — identify assets, threats, vulnerabilities, and impacts.
- Develop a **risk treatment plan** — select controls from Annex A.
- Produce the **Statement of Applicability (SoA)** — a document justifying which of the 93 controls are applicable and why.
- Obtain **top management commitment**.

**2. DO (2 marks)**
- **Implement the selected controls** — technical (firewall, encryption), administrative (policies, training), and physical (CCTV, locks).
- Conduct **awareness programs** for all employees.
- Operate the **incident management** process.
- Maintain **documented information** (procedures, records).

**3. CHECK (1.5 marks)**
- Conduct **internal audits** at planned intervals.
- **Monitor and measure** KPIs (e.g., MTTD, MTTR, % of systems patched).
- Hold **management review meetings** to evaluate ISMS performance.
- Identify **non-conformities** and weak areas.

**4. ACT (1.5 marks)**
- Take **corrective actions** to address non-conformities.
- Implement **preventive actions** for anticipated issues.
- **Continually improve** the ISMS based on findings.
- The cycle **re-enters PLAN** with an updated risk register.

> The PDCA cycle ensures the ISMS is a **living, evolving system** rather than a one-time project. ISO 27001 certification requires the ISMS to operate on this cycle for at least one full iteration before audit.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — ISO 27001 vs NIST CSF vs COBIT 2019 (7 marks)

| Dimension | **ISO 27001** | **NIST CSF** | **COBIT 2019** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Issuer** | ISO/IEC (international) | NIST (USA, but globally adopted) | ISACA (international) |
| **Type** | Certifiable standard | Voluntary framework | Governance framework |
| **Focus** | Information Security Management System | Operational cybersecurity risk | IT governance and management |
| **Structure** | PDCA cycle + Annex A controls | 5 Functions, 23 Categories, 108 Subcategories | 5 Domains, 40 Governance/Management Objectives |
| **Risk Approach** | Risk-based with mandatory risk assessment | Risk-based, flexible tiers | Aligns IT risk with enterprise risk |
| **Mandatory?** | No (voluntary certification) | No | No |
| **Best For** | Any organization seeking certification | Critical infrastructure, US-based firms | Auditors, boards, governance teams |
| **Cost** | Moderate (certification audit fees) | Free (publicly available) | Moderate (ISACA membership/guides) |
| **Maturity Model** | Implicit through PDCA iteration | 4 Tiers (Partial → Adaptive) | CMMI-like capability levels |

**Synthesis:** (1 mark)
- **ISO 27001** is best for organizations seeking a *certifiable, internationally recognized* ISMS.
- **NIST CSF** is best for *operational* cybersecurity teams needing a flexible, risk-based *tactical* playbook.
- **COBIT 2019** is best for *boards and auditors* needing a *governance* lens over IT and security.

These frameworks are **complementary, not competing**. A mature enterprise may implement ISO 27001 for the ISMS, use NIST CSF for operational controls, and apply COBIT for governance reporting to the board.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the **Risk Management Process** as per ISO 27005. Describe all six stages with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company's online payment gateway has the following parameters:
- Asset Value (AV) = ₹20,00,000
- Exposure Factor (EF) = 0.50
- Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO) = 3
- Proposed control cost (ACS) = ₹3,00,000/year
- After control: EF = 0.20, ARO = 1

**Calculate: (i)** SLE before, **(ii)** ALE before, **(iii)** ALE after, **(iv)** ROSI. **Recommend** whether the control should be purchased. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Risk Management Process (ISO 27005) (7 marks)

**Stage 1: Context Establishment (1 mark)**
- Define the **scope** of risk management (e.g., the e-commerce platform).
- Identify **assets** within scope (servers, databases, customer data, payment gateway).
- Define **risk appetite** — the level of risk the organization is willing to accept.
- Establish **risk evaluation criteria** (likelihood and impact scales, e.g., 1–5).

*Example:* A bank defines its risk appetite as: "No single risk shall have an annual expected loss exceeding ₹10 Lakhs."

**Stage 2: Risk Identification (1.5 marks)**
- Identify **threats** (e.g., SQL injection, DDoS, insider theft, ransomware).
- Identify **vulnerabilities** (e.g., unpatched software, weak passwords, missing MFA).
- Identify **consequences** (e.g., data loss, downtime, regulatory fines, reputational damage).
- Compile findings in a **risk register**.

*Example:* "The customer database is vulnerable to SQL injection due to unpatched input validation, leading to potential data breach."

**Stage 3: Risk Analysis (1.5 marks)**
- **Qualitative**: Use a risk matrix (Likelihood × Impact) on a 1–5 scale.
- **Quantitative**: Use ALE, SLE, ARO calculations.
- Determine the **inherent risk** (before controls).

*Example:* "SQL injection risk: Likelihood = 4 (High), Impact = 5 (Severe), Inherent Risk Score = 20 (Critical)."

**Stage 4: Risk Evaluation (1 mark)**
- Compare each risk score against the **risk acceptance criteria**.
- **Prioritize** risks for treatment.
- Categorize: Acceptable (Low), Monitor (Medium), Treat (High), Immediate Action (Critical).

*Example:* A score of 20 falls in the "Critical" zone — must be treated immediately.

**Stage 5: Risk Treatment (1.5 marks)**
- Select one of **four strategies** for each risk:
  - **Mitigation** — Apply controls (e.g., WAF, encryption, MFA).
  - **Transfer** — Cyber insurance, outsourcing to a SOC provider.
  - **Acceptance** — Formally document acceptance by management.
  - **Avoidance** — Discontinue the activity (e.g., stop accepting credit card payments).
- Document in the **risk treatment plan**.

*Example:* "Apply WAF and input validation to mitigate SQL injection risk."

**Stage 6: Risk Monitoring & Review (0.5 marks)**
- **Continuous monitoring** of risk indicators (KRIs).
- Periodic **review** (annually or after major changes).
- Update the **risk register** as the threat landscape evolves.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Quantitative Risk Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- AV = ₹20,00,000
- EF (before) = 0.50
- ARO (before) = 3
- ACS = ₹3,00,000/year
- EF (after) = 0.20
- ARO (after) = 1

**(i) SLE before control (1.5 marks):**

$$ \text{SLE}_{\text{before}} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF}_{\text{before}} $$

$$ \text{SLE}_{\text{before}} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.50 = 10{,}00{,}000 $$

**Result:** $\text{SLE}_{\text{before}} = \text{₹}10{,}00{,}000$

**(ii) ALE before control (1.5 marks):**

$$ \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = \text{SLE}_{\text{before}} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{before}} $$

$$ \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 3 = 30{,}00{,}000 $$

**Result:** $\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = \text{₹}30{,}00{,}000$ per year.

**(iii) ALE after control (1.5 marks):**

$$ \text{SLE}_{\text{after}} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.20 = 4{,}00{,}000 $$

$$ \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = 4{,}00{,}000 \times 1 = 4{,}00{,}000 $$

**Result:** $\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = \text{₹}4{,}00{,}000$ per year.

**(iv) ROSI Calculation (2 marks):**

$$ \text{ROSI} = \frac{(\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}}) - \text{ACS}}{\text{ACS}} $$

$$ \text{ROSI} = \frac{(30{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000) - 3{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} $$

$$ \text{ROSI} = \frac{26{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = \frac{23{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} $$

$$ \text{ROSI} = 7.67 $$

**Result:** $\text{ROSI} = 7.67$ (or 767% return).

**Recommendation (0.5 mark):**
Since ROSI is **7.67**, which is significantly greater than 1, the control is **highly justified** for purchase. Every ₹1 spent returns ₹7.67 in net benefit. The risk treatment strategy is **Mitigation** — applying the control to reduce both likelihood (ARO) and impact (EF).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract ACS** in the ROSI numerator — many students calculate `(ALE_before - ALE_after) / ACS` which is wrong; you must *subtract the control cost first*.
> 2. **Confusing SLE and ALE** — SLE is per-incident; ALE is per-year. The multiplication with ARO converts.
> 3. **Missing units** — Always write ₹ or currency in your final answer.
> 4. **Wrong final recommendation** — ROSI > 1 means **buy**; ROSI < 1 means **don't buy**; ROSI = 0 means **break-even**.
> 5. **Ignoring ISO 27001 vs ISO 27002 distinction** — 27001 is *requirements* (certifiable); 27002 is *controls* (code of practice). Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> 6. **Writing "policy" instead of distinguishing Policy / Standard / Procedure / Guideline** — Always use the correct hierarchy term.
> 7. **Listing only 2 risk treatment strategies** — You MUST list all four: Mitigate, Transfer, Accept, Avoid.
> 8. **Not mentioning Annex A controls in ISO 27001:2022** — The current revision has **93 controls in 4 themes** (A.5, A.6, A.7, A.8). Older answers referencing 114 controls lose marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### 🔐 Core Concepts
- ☐ **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability (the bedrock of all security).
- ☐ **Three States of Data** = At Rest, In Use, In Motion.
- ☐ **Extended Properties** = Authenticity, Non-repudiation, Accountability.

### 📜 ISO 27000 Family
- ☐ **ISO 27001** = Certifiable ISMS requirements (PDCA-based).
- ☐ **ISO 27002** = Code of practice with 93 controls (A.5–A.8).
- ☐ **ISO 27005** = Risk management methodology.
- ☐ **Statement of Applicability (SoA)** is a mandatory document.
- ☐ ISO 27001:2022 has **93 Annex A controls** (down from 114 in 2013).

### 🏛️ Other Frameworks
- ☐ **NIST CSF** = 5 Functions: Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover.
- ☐ **COBIT 2019** = 5 Domains: EDM, APO, BAI, DSS, MEA.
- ☐ **PCI-DSS** = Mandatory for card payments; **HIPAA** = Mandatory for US healthcare.

### 📋 Security Policies & Documents
- ☐ Hierarchy: **Policy → Standard → Procedure → Guideline**.
- ☐ A policy states the *rule*; a procedure states the *steps*.
- ☐ Common policies: AUP, Data Classification, Incident Response, BYOD, Backup.

### 🛡️ Security Controls
- ☐ **Three categories**: Administrative, Physical, Technical.
- ☐ **Five functional types**: Preventive, Detective, Corrective, Deterrent, Compensating.
- ☐ Always pair a *preventive* control with a *detective* control.

### 📊 Risk Management
- ☐ **Six stages** (ISO 27005): Context, Identification, Analysis, Evaluation, Treatment, Monitoring.
- ☐ **Four treatment strategies**: Mitigate, Transfer, Accept, Avoid.
- ☐ **Formulas**: $\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF}$, $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$, $\text{ROSI} = \frac{(\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}}) - \text{ACS}}{\text{ACS}}$.
- ☐ **Risk Score** = Likelihood × Impact (1–25 scale).
- ☐ **ROSI > 1** = Buy the control; **ROSI < 1** = Don't buy; **ROSI = 0** = Break-even.

### ⚖️ Regulations
- ☐ **Regulations** are mandatory; **Standards** are voluntary.
- ☐ **GDPR** fines = 4% global turnover or €20M (whichever higher).
- ☐ **DPDP Act 2023** = India's comprehensive data protection law.
- ☐ **IT Act 2000** (amended 2008) = India's cyber law.
- ☐ **HIPAA** fines up to $1.5M/year per violation; **SOX** for financial integrity.

### 🎯 KTU 2024 Exam Day Tips
- ☐ Always define acronyms on first use (e.g., "ISMS — Information Security Management System").
- ☐ Use **tables and bullet points** in 14-mark answers to improve readability and fetch full marks.
- ☐ For calculation questions, **show all steps** — partial marking requires visible work.
- ☐ Draw **diagrams** (PDCA cycle, CIA triad) wherever possible — visual answers score higher.
- ☐ End 14-mark answers with a **concluding sentence** that synthesizes your answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Authentication - User Authentication, Token Based, Biometric Authentication, Remote User Authentication, Multifactor Authentication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST744 - INFORMATION SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Authentication - User Authentication, Token Based, Biometric Authentication, Remote User Authentication, Multifactor Authentication # Authentication in Information Security — Module 3 ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Authentication** is the security mechanism by which a system verifies the claimed identity of a principal (user, device, or process) before granting access to protected resources. In the AAA (Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting) framework prescribed by KTU, authentication forms the *first* and most critical gatekeeper. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Authentication vs. Authorization (A common KTU exam trap)** > - **Authentication** = *Who are you?* (Identity verification) > - **Authorization** = *What can you do?* (Permission/privilege grant) > - **Accounting** = *What did you do?* (Audit trail) In formal cryptographic notation, authentication is modeled as a protocol between a **Claimant (C)** and a **Verifier (V)**, where V must be convinced with overwhelming probability that C is the legitimate entity. This is expressed in the **Goldwasser–Micali–Rackoff (GMR) zero-knowledge model**: $$ \text{Verify}: V \leftarrow \text{Protocol}(C, \text{Secret}_C) \to \{0,1\} $$ where the verifier outputs 1 (accept) or 0 (reject). --- ### 1.2 The Three (and a Half) Factors of Authentication Authentication rests on **three canonical factors**, often expanded to a fourth: | Factor Class | Description | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Knowledge Factor (Type 1)** | Something the user *knows* | Password, PIN, security question | | **Possession Factor (Type 2)** | Something the user *has* | Smart card, OTP token, mobile phone | | **Biometric Factor (Type 3)** | Something the user *is* | Fingerprint, iris, voice, face | | **Location Factor (Type 0)** | Somewhere the user *is* | IP address, GPS, geofence | > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 syllabus (PECST744, Module 3) explicitly clusters these into four sub-modules: **User Authentication**, **Token-Based**, **Biometric**, and **Remote User Authentication** (which uses *Multifactor* as a final extension). All four factors are individually examinable. --- ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Security Metaphor Think of authentication like an **airport boarding gate**: 1. **Identity Claim** — You walk up to the gate and present your boarding pass ("I am Mr. X, seat 12A"). 2. **First Check (Knowledge)** — The agent asks for your **passport number** (something you know). 3. **Second Check (Possession)** — You must also hand over the **physical passport and boarding pass** (something you have). 4. **Third Check (Biometric)** — The e-gate scans your **face or fingerprint** (something you are). 5. **Authorization** — Only after all three checks does the gate open, giving you access to the *aircraft* (the protected resource). > [!TIP] > If any one check fails, you are denied. This is exactly how **Multifactor Authentication (MFA)** works — failure of *any* factor blocks access, exponentially raising the cost of an attack for an adversary. --- ### 1.4 The Authentication Strength Spectrum Authentication is *not binary*; it exists on a **strength continuum**: $$ \text{Weakest} \xrightarrow{\text{Password alone} \to \text{Password+SMS} \to \text{Password+TOTP} \to \text{Smart Card+Biometric}} \text{Strongest} $$ The KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education framework maps this onto **Bloom's Level 2 (Understand)** when distinguishing mechanisms, and **Level 4 (Analyze)** when comparing them — meaning a typical KTU essay question will ask you to *compare*, not merely *list*. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 User Authentication — The Foundation ### 2.1.1 Password-Based Authentication The simplest, yet most vulnerable, mechanism. Security depends on: - **Password Entropy** $H$ (in bits) - **Guesswork** — entropy loss per dictionary match - **Storage** — never plaintext, only salted hashes (bcrypt, Argon2, scrypt) **Operational Logic Steps:** 1. User submits identity $ID$ and password $PW$. 2. System retrieves stored hash $h = H(PW \oplus \text{salt})$. 3. System compares $h$ with recomputed $h' = H(PW_{input} \oplus \text{salt})$. 4. Constant-time comparison prevents timing side-channel attacks. > [!NOTE] > **Pass-the-Hash (PtH)** and **Credential Stuffing** are the two highest-frequency KTU exam attack vectors on password systems. Always mention *salting* and *peppering* in your model answers. ### 2.1.2 Challenge–Response Authentication The verifier sends a **nonce** $N$ (a number used once), and the claimant proves knowledge of a secret $K$ without revealing it. **Variants:** - **Hash-based:** $C \to V : H(K \vert\vert N)$ - **Symmetric-key:** $C \to V : E_K(N)$ - **Public-key (Digital Signature):** $C \to V : \text{Sign}_{SK_C}(N)$ This defeats **replay attacks** because each $N$ is fresh. --- ## 2.2 Token-Based Authentication A **token** is a *physical or digital artifact* that the user presents as proof of identity or authorization. ### 2.2.1 Classification of Tokens | Token Type | Construction | Example | Security Level | |---|---|---|---| | **Memory Card** | Magnetic stripe, stored data | Old credit cards | **Low** | | **Smart Card** | Embedded microprocessor + crypto chip | EMV debit card | **High** | | **USB Token** | Hardware Security Module on USB | YubiKey, RSA SecurID | **Very High** | | **Software Token** | Mobile app generating OTPs | Google Authenticator, Authy | **High** | | **JWT (Web Token)** | JSON Web Token — signed header/payload | OAuth 2.0 Bearer Token | **Medium–High** | ### 2.2.2 One-Time Password (OTP) Algorithms — Exam Favourites **(a) HMAC-Based OTP — HOTP (RFC 4226):** $$ \text{HOTP}(K, C) = \text{Truncate}\Big(\text{HMAC-SHA1}(K, C)\Big) \pmod{10^d} $$ where $K$ = shared secret (160-bit), $C$ = 8-byte counter, $d$ = digit length (typically 6). **(b) Time-Based OTP — TOTP (RFC 6238):** $$ \text{TOTP}(K, T) = \text{HOTP}(K, \lfloor (T - T_0) / X \rfloor) $$ where $T$ = current Unix timestamp, $T_0$ = epoch start (0), $X$ = time step (30s or 60s). > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists *TOTP* and *HOTP* as examinable — you must derive both formulas in any 14-mark question on token-based authentication. ### 2.2.3 JWT (JSON Web Token) Anatomy A JWT is three **Base64URL-encoded** parts separated by dots: $$ \text{JWT} = \underbrace{\text{Base64}(\text{Header})}_{\text{Algorithm \& Type}} . \underbrace{\text{Base64}(\text{Payload})}_{\text{Claims}} . \underbrace{\text{Base64}(\text{Signature})}_{\text{HMAC or RSA}} $$ - **Header** — `{"alg":"HS256","typ":"JWT"}` - **Payload** — Claims: `iss`, `sub`, `aud`, `exp`, `iat` - **Signature** — $\text{HMAC-SHA256}(\text{Header}.\text{Payload}, K)$ --- ## 2.3 Biometric Authentication ### 2.3.1 Biometric Modalities | Modality | Type | Universality | Uniqueness | Permanence | Collectability | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Fingerprint** | Physiological | Medium | High | High | Medium | | **Face** | Physiological | High | Medium | Medium | High | | **Iris** | Physiological | High | Very High | Very High | Medium | | **Retina** | Physiological | High | Very High | High | Low | | **Voice** | Behavioral | Medium | Low | Low | High | | **Gait** | Behavioral | Medium | Low | Low | Medium | | **Signature** | Behavioral | Medium | Low | Low | High | ### 2.3.2 Verification vs. Identification (KTU Favourite Distinction) - **Verification (1:1):** "Is this person who they claim to be?" — Compares probe $P$ against one stored template $T_i$. - **Identification (1:N):** "Who is this person?" — Searches probe $P$ against the entire database of $N$ templates. > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 paper almost always tests this distinction. Identification scales **linearly** $O(N)$ in database size; verification is **constant-time** $O(1)$. ### 2.3.3 Biometric Error Rate Formulas (The Core Math) Let **$M$** = total legitimate attempts, **$I$** = total impostor attempts, **$FA$** = false accepts, **$FR$** = false rejects, **$TA$** = true accepts, **$TR$** = true rejects. **False Accept Rate (FAR) — Type II error (false positive):** $$ \text{FAR} = \frac{FA}{I} = \frac{\text{Impostor accepts}}{\text{Total impostor attempts}} $$ **False Reject Rate (FRR) — Type I error (false negative):** $$ \text{FRR} = \frac{FR}{M} = \frac{\text{Legitimate rejects}}{\text{Total legitimate attempts}} $$ **Genuine Acceptance Rate (GAR):** $$ \text{GAR} = 1 - \text{FRR} $$ **Crossover Error Rate (CER) / Equal Error Rate (EER):** $$ \text{CER} = \text{FAR} = \text{FRR} \quad \text{(at the operating threshold } \theta^* \text{)} $$ **Failure to Enrol Rate (FTE):** $$ \text{FTE} = \frac{\text{Users who cannot enrol}}{\text{Total enrolment attempts}} $$ **Failure to Capture Rate (FTC):** $$ \text{FTC} = \frac{\text{Attempts with un-capturable samples}}{\text{Total capture attempts}} $$ ### 2.3.4 The FAR–FRR Trade-off Curve As the matching threshold $\theta$ increases: - FAR **decreases** (stricter matching → fewer impostors accepted) - FRR **increases** (stricter matching → more legitimate users rejected) $$ \text{FRR}(\theta) \uparrow \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{FAR}(\theta) \downarrow $$ The **CER** point is the *operating sweet-spot* for a balanced biometric system. > [!TIP] > A **low CER** ≈ strong biometric. For exam purposes, cite: fingerprint CER ≈ 0.001%, iris CER ≈ 0.0001%, face CER ≈ 1–2%. --- ## 2.4 Remote User Authentication The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists **four remote authentication protocols**: ### 2.4.1 Protocol Taxonomy | Protocol | Mechanism | Attacks Defended | Weakness | |---|---|---|---| | **Password over SSL/TLS** | Encrypted channel | Eavesdropping | Phishing, server breach | | **Hashed Challenge–Response** | Nonce + hash | Replay | Online brute force | | **Token + PIN (Two-Factor)** | Possession + Knowledge | Credential theft | Token loss | | **Kerberos (Ticket-based)** | TGT + Service Tickets | Replay, MITM | KDC single point of failure | | **Mutual TLS (mTLS)** | PKI certs both ways | MITM | PKI complexity | ### 2.4.2 Kerberos — The Gold Standard Developed at MIT (Project Athena, 1988). Uses **symmetric-key cryptography** with a central **Key Distribution Center (KDC)**. **Three Actors:** - **Client (C)** — User wanting service - **AS (Authentication Server)** — Issues Ticket-Granting Ticket (TGT) - **TGS (Ticket-Granting Server)** — Issues Service Ticket (ST) - **V (Verifier/Service Server)** — The actual resource **Three Key Derivations:** $$ K_C = \text{User's password-derived key} $$ $$ K_{C,TGS} = \text{AS-TGS session key} $$ $$ K_{C,V} = \text{Client-Service session key} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Kerberos 5 uses **AES-256** for encryption and enforces **time-bound tickets** (default lifetime 8 hours) to defeat replay attacks. The **realm** concept allows cross-domain trust. --- ## 2.5 Multifactor Authentication (MFA) ### 2.5.1 The MFA Equation MFA is *not* two passwords. It is the **logical AND** of factors from **distinct** factor classes: $$ \text{Access} = \text{Type1} \;\land\; \text{Type2} \;\land\; \text{Type3} $$ i.e., a password (knowledge) **AND** a smart card (possession) **AND** a fingerprint (biometric) — never two passwords, never two tokens. ### 2.5.2 KTU-Standard MFA Examples | Scenario | Factor 1 | Factor 2 | Factor 3 | |---|---|---|---| | **ATM Withdrawal** | PIN (Knowledge) | Card (Possession) | — | | **Banking App Login** | Password | SMS OTP | — | | **High-Security Vault** | Password | Smart Card | Fingerprint | | **Windows Hello + Token** | PIN | TPM Key | Biometric | ### 2.5.3 Quantitative Security Gain If each factor has independent failure probability $p_i$, total compromise probability is: $$ P_{\text{break}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i $$ > [!EXAMPLE] > Suppose: password guess probability $p_1 = 10^{-6}$, token theft probability $p_2 = 10^{-3}$, biometric spoof probability $p_3 = 10^{-4}$. > Then $P_{\text{break}} = 10^{-6} \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-4} = 10^{-13}$ — a 13-bit security gain over a single factor. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: TOTP Time-Based OTP **Given:** Secret key $K$ shared out-of-band (e.g., QR code enrollment), time step $X = 30$ s, digit count $d = 6$, epoch $T_0 = 0$. **Step 1 — Compute the time counter.** The counter $C$ is the integer number of time steps that have elapsed since the Unix epoch $T_0$: $$ C = \left\lfloor \frac{T_{\text{current}} - T_0}{X} \right\rfloor $$ *Logical conversion:* $T_{\text{current}}$ is the live Unix timestamp; subtracting $T_0$ yields elapsed seconds; integer-dividing by $X$ yields the discrete counter value. **Step 2 — Compute the HMAC.** Apply HMAC-SHA-1 (per RFC 4226) using $K$ as the key and an 8-byte big-endian encoding of $C$ as the message: $$ \text{HMAC} = \text{HMAC-SHA1}(K, \; C_{8\text{-byte}}) $$ *Logical conversion:* SHA-1 outputs 160 bits (20 bytes); HMAC adds an outer key pad and inner key pad. The 8-byte encoding of $C$ is required because RFC 4226 mandates a fixed 8-byte counter. **Step 3 — Dynamic Truncation (DT).** Let $\text{HMAC}[i]$ be the $i$-th byte ($0 \leq i \leq 19$). The **offset** is the low-order 4 bits of $\text{HMAC}[19]$: $$ \text{offset} = \text{HMAC}[19] \;\&\; 0\text{x}0F $$ Extract a 31-bit unsigned integer from 4 consecutive bytes starting at offset: $$ \text{S} = \big(\text{HMAC}[\text{offset}] \;\&\; 0\text{x}7F\big) \;\ll\; 24$$ $$ \quad \lor\; \big(\text{HMAC}[\text{offset}+1] \;\&\; 0\text{x}FF\big) \;\ll\; 16$$ $$ \quad \lor\; \big(\text{HMAC}[\text{offset}+2] \;\&\; 0\text{x}FF\big) \;\ll\; 8$$ $$ \quad \lor\; \big(\text{HMAC}[\text{offset}+3] \;\&\; 0\text{x}FF}\big) $$ *Logical conversion:* The top bit of the first byte is masked off (`0x7F`) to avoid signedness issues in modular reduction. Subsequent bytes are combined into a 32-bit integer. **Step 4 — Modulo to 6-digit decimal.** $$ \text{TOTP} = S \bmod 10^d = S \bmod 10^6 $$ *Logical conversion:* This yields a uniform integer in $[0, \; 999999]$, which is formatted as a 6-digit decimal OTP. **Step 5 — Final OTP assembly.** $$ \text{TOTP}(K, T) = \text{HOTP}\!\left(K, \; \left\lfloor \frac{T - T_0}{X} \right\rfloor\right) \bmod 10^6 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Security Note for KTU Valuation:** Examiners will *deduct marks* if you write the modulus before dynamic truncation. The RFC-mandated order is **HMAC → Truncate → Modulo**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Biometric Crossover Error Rate (CER) **Given:** A biometric system with FAR curve $\text{FAR}(\theta)$ and FRR curve $\text{FRR}(\theta)$ as functions of matching threshold $\theta \in [0,1]$. **Step 1 — Define the curves.** For a threshold $\theta$: - A sample with genuine score $s_g \geq \theta$ is accepted; $s_g < \theta$ is falsely rejected. - A sample with impostor score $s_i \geq \theta$ is falsely accepted; $s_i < \theta$ is correctly rejected. $$ \text{FRR}(\theta) = \int_{0}^{\theta} f_g(s) \, ds $$ $$ \text{FAR}(\theta) = \int_{\theta}^{1} f_i(s) \, ds $$ where $f_g$ and $f_i$ are the probability density functions of genuine and impostor scores. **Step 2 — Set FAR equal to FRR for CER.** By definition of CER, the threshold $\theta^*$ is the point where the two curves intersect: $$ \text{FAR}(\theta^*) = \text{FRR}(\theta^*) $$ *Logical conversion:* Below $\theta^*$, FRR $>$ FAR (system is too strict); above $\theta^*$, FAR $>$ FRR (system is too loose); the unique crossover gives the balanced operating point. **Step 3 — Solve for $\theta^*$.** Differentiating the equality and applying Leibniz's rule: $$ \frac{d}{d\theta}\Big[\text{FAR}(\theta) - \text{FRR}(\theta)\Big] = -f_i(\theta) - f_g(\theta) = 0 $$ Since $f_i, f_g \geq 0$, no closed-form analytic solution generally exists; in practice, CER is found by **graphical intersection** of the two empirical curves. **Step 4 — Express CER as a single scalar.** $$ \boxed{\text{CER} = \text{FAR}(\theta^*) = \text{FRR}(\theta^*) = \text{Equal Error Rate}} $$ **Numerical Example for KTU 14-mark question:** A fingerprint system is tested on 10,000 genuine users and 100,000 impostors. At threshold $\theta^* = 0.72$: - 25 genuine users rejected → $\text{FRR} = 25/10000 = 0.25\%$ - 30 impostors accepted → $\text{FAR} = 30/100000 = 0.03\%$ **Step 5 — Compute the security-usability trade-off.** The system is *security-leaning*. To balance, lower $\theta^*$ until FRR = FAR (e.g., 0.1% each), at the cost of slightly higher impostor acceptance. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Password Entropy & Search Space **Given:** A password of length $L$ drawn from an alphabet of size $A$ (e.g., 95 printable ASCII characters). **Step 1 — Total keyspace.** The total number of possible passwords is: $$ N = A^L $$ *Logical conversion:* Each of the $L$ positions can independently take $A$ values, yielding $A \times A \times \ldots \times A$ ($L$ times). **Step 2 — Convert to entropy in bits.** $$ H = \log_2 N = L \cdot \log_2 A $$ **Step 3 — Apply to KTU-typical password.** Let $L = 12$ characters, $A = 95$ (full printable ASCII): $$ H = 12 \cdot \log_2 95 $$ $$ \log_2 95 \approx 6.5699 $$ $$ H = 12 \times 6.5699 \approx 78.84 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 4 — Expected time to brute-force at rate $R$ guesses/sec:** $$ t_{\text{crack}} = \frac{N}{2R} = \frac{A^L}{2R} $$ *Logical conversion:* On average, an attacker will guess the correct password halfway through the search space (the factor 1/2). **Step 5 — Numerical case:** A GPU cluster guesses $R = 10^{10}$ / sec. $$ t_{\text{crack}} = \frac{95^{12}}{2 \times 10^{10}} \approx \frac{5.4 \times 10^{23}}{2 \times 10^{10}} \approx 2.7 \times 10^{13} \text{ seconds} $$ $$ t_{\text{crack}} \approx 8.6 \times 10^5 \text{ years} $$ > [!TIP] > This is the *single most-rewarded* numerical question on the KTU PECST744 Module 3 paper. Memorize the entropy formula and the unit conversion (years vs. seconds). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Authentication Mechanisms | Mechanism | KTU Bloom Level | Key Strength | Key Weakness | Primary Attack | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Password** | L2-Understand | Simple, cheap | Phishing, reuse | Dictionary attack | | **Challenge-Response (Hash)** | L3-Apply | Replay-resistant | Needs crypto engine | MITM on channel | | **Smart Card** | L3-Apply | Cloning hard | Reader required | Side-channel on card | | **TOTP** | L4-Analyze | Time-bounded | Phone-dependent | SIM swap | | **Fingerprint** | L4-Analyze | Always with user | Latency, dirt | Latent print lift | | **Iris** | L4-Analyze | Very stable | Cost, distance | Coercion | | **Kerberos** | L5-Evaluate | Mutual, ticket-based | KDC bottleneck | KDC compromise | | **MFA (2-factor)** | L5-Evaluate | Exponential security gain | User friction | Social engineering | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 General Authentication Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[User Initiates Login] --> B[Submit Identity Claim
User ID and Credentials] B --> C[Authentication Module
Receives Claim] C --> D{Factor Type?} D -->|Type 1 Knowledge| E1[Password or PIN check] D -->|Type 2 Possession| E2[Token or Smart Card verify] D -->|Type 3 Biometric| E3[Biometric sample capture] D -->|Multifactor| E4[Combine multiple factor checks] E1 --> F[Match against stored template] E2 --> F E3 --> F E4 --> F F --> G{Match Score above threshold} G -->|Yes| H[Identity Verified - Grant Session Token] G -->|No| I[Reject - Log Failure Attempt] H --> J[Authorization Layer checks permissions] I --> K[Increment failure counter
Apply lockout policy] J --> L[Access Granted to Resource] K --> M[Audit Log Entry Created] L --> N[Session Activity Monitored] M --> O[End] N --> O ``` ## 4.2 TOTP Token-Based Authentication Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Client participant A as Authenticator App participant S as Auth Server Note over U,S: Enrollment Phase U->>S: Request enrollment S->>U: Send secret K as QR code U->>A: Scan QR code, store K locally S->>S: Store K, user ID, X, T0 Note over U,S: Verification Phase U->>S: Submit username and password S->>S: Verify password hash S->>S: Generate nonce challenge S->>U: Send challenge U->>A: Request TOTP code A->>A: Compute TOTP from K and current time A->>U: Display 6-digit code U->>S: Submit TOTP code S->>S: Compute expected TOTP using stored K S->>S: Allow plus or minus 1 time step window S->>U: Grant or deny access ``` ## 4.3 Biometric Authentication Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Biometric Sensor
Capture Device] --> B[Quality Check Module
Reject poor samples] B --> C[Feature Extraction
Minutiae or Texture Map] C --> D[Template Database
Stored Reference] D --> E[Matcher Engine
Compute Similarity Score] C --> E E --> F[Decision Module
Apply Threshold theta] F --> G{Score versus threshold} G -->|Above theta| H[Accept - Genuine User] G -->|Below theta| I[Reject - Impostor] H --> J[Log FAR or FRR metrics] I --> J J --> K[Adaptive Threshold Tuning
Update theta for CER] ``` ## 4.4 Kerberos Remote Authentication (5-Step Protocol) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant C as Client participant AS as Authentication Server participant TGS as Ticket Granting Server participant V as Service Server Note over C,V: Step 1 - Initial Authentication C->>AS: Request TGT for user ID AS->>C: Encrypted response with K_C_TGS and TGT Note over C,V: Step 2 - Decrypt and extract C->>C: Decrypt using K_C derived from password Note over C,V: Step 3 - Service Ticket Request C->>TGS: Present TGT and Authenticator for service V TGS->>TGS: Validate TGT and check timestamp Note over C,V: Step 4 - Receive Service Ticket TGS->>C: Encrypted response with K_C_V and Service Ticket Note over C,V: Step 5 - Access Service C->>V: Present Service Ticket and new Authenticator V->>V: Decrypt and verify timestamp V->>C: Grant access to service ``` ## 4.5 Multifactor Authentication Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[User Login Initiated] --> F1[Factor 1: Knowledge
Password Verification] F1 --> Q1{Password Valid?} Q1 -->|No| R1[Reject - Log Failure] Q1 -->|Yes| F2[Factor 2: Possession
Token or Device Check] F2 --> Q2{Token Valid?} Q2 -->|No| R2[Reject - Log Failure] Q2 -->|Yes| F3[Factor 3: Biometric
Sample Capture] F3 --> Q3{Score above CER threshold?} Q3 -->|No| R3[Reject - Log Failure] Q3 -->|Yes| OK[All Factors Pass - Grant Access] R1 --> Log[Increment failure counter] R2 --> Log R3 --> Log OK --> Session[Establish secure session] Log --> Lockout{Threshold reached?} Lockout -->|Yes| Block[Lock account or trigger MFA escalation] Lockout -->|No| End[End authentication attempt] Session --> End Block --> End ``` ## 4.6 Token Type Classification Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD Root[Authentication Tokens] --> Static[Static Tokens] Root --> Dynamic[Dynamic Tokens] Static --> Memory[Memory Cards
Magnetic stripe] Static --> Contact[Contact Smart Cards
EMV chip] Dynamic --> Async[Asynchronous
Challenge Response] Dynamic --> Sync[Synchronous
Time or Event based] Async --> Sig[Digital Signature based] Sync --> Time[TOTP - Time based] Sync --> Event[HOTP - Event Counter] Memory --> Use1[Low Security - Single factor] Contact --> Use2[High Security - Crypto processor] Time --> Use3[RFC 6238 - 30s window] Event --> Use4[RFC 4226 - Counter increment] ``` > [!NOTE] > The above diagrams are **functional architecture flows** rather than physical renderings — per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol for cybersecurity topics where interaction sequences (not physical layouts) carry the pedagogical weight. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **Differentiate between authentication and authorization. List the four factors used for user authentication.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Authentication** is the process of verifying the identity of a user, device, or system. It answers the question *"Who are you?"* — (1 mark) - **Authorization** is the process of determining what an authenticated entity is allowed to do. It answers *"What can you do?"* — (1 mark) - The **four factors** of authentication are: 1. **Knowledge factor** — Something the user knows (password, PIN) 2. **Possession factor** — Something the user has (smart card, token) 3. **Biometric factor** — Something the user is (fingerprint, iris) 4. **Location factor** — Somewhere the user is (IP geolocation) — (1 mark) --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **What is a JSON Web Token (JWT)? List its three parts.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **JSON Web Token (JWT)** is a compact, URL-safe, digitally signed token used for stateless authentication in web and API environments. (1 mark) The three parts of a JWT are: 1. **Header** — Contains the algorithm (`alg`) and token type (`typ`), e.g., `{"alg":"HS256","typ":"JWT"}` (1 mark) 2. **Payload** — Contains the *claims* about the user, e.g., `iss`, `sub`, `aud`, `exp`, `iat` (0.5 mark) 3. **Signature** — Computed as `HMACSHA256(Base64(Header) + "." + Base64(Payload), Secret_Key)`, which guarantees integrity and authenticity (0.5 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Module 3 Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Don't confuse authentication with encryption.** Authentication is *identity verification*; encryption is *confidentiality*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks when students mix these. > 2. **Always state the modulus step LAST** in OTP derivations (HMAC → Truncate → Modulo). Reversed order = −2 marks. > 3. **Distinguish verification (1:1) from identification (1:N)** clearly. A common 7-mark sub-part tests exactly this. > 4. **MFA is NOT two passwords.** Two passwords are *single-factor*. Marks are deducted for incorrectly calling two passwords "multifactor". > 5. **Always include the attack vector** in your answers (replay, MITM, phishing, brute-force) — KTU examiners award 1 mark for attack mention in 7-mark sub-parts. > 6. **Diagrams fetch 1–2 bonus marks** in Part B. Always include a Mermaid/hand-drawn flow for protocol questions. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **Q.A. (a)** Explain the **TOTP algorithm** (RFC 6238) in detail with all its steps. Compare TOTP with HOTP. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **TOTP Overview (1 mark):** TOTP (Time-Based One-Time Password) is an OTP algorithm defined in RFC 6238. It generates a single-use password valid for a fixed time window (typically 30 or 60 seconds) using a shared secret and the current time. **Step-by-Step Algorithm (4 marks):** **Step 1 — Time Counter Calculation:** $$ C = \left\lfloor \frac{T - T_0}{X} \right\rfloor $$ where $T$ = current Unix timestamp, $T_0$ = epoch start, $X$ = time step (30s default). **Step 2 — HMAC Computation:** $$ \text{HMAC} = \text{HMAC-SHA1}(K, C) $$ where $K$ is the pre-shared 160-bit secret, $C$ is encoded as an 8-byte big-endian integer. **Step 3 — Dynamic Truncation:** Take offset = `HMAC[19] & 0x0F`, then extract a 31-bit integer from 4 bytes starting at offset. **Step 4 — Modulo Reduction:** $$ \text{TOTP} = S \bmod 10^6 $$ **Step 5 — Display:** Format as 6-digit decimal. **TOTP vs HOTP Comparison (2 marks):** | Parameter | HOTP (RFC 4226) | TOTP (RFC 6238) | |---|---|---| | **Triggering Event** | Event counter $C$ | Time value $T$ | | **Resynchronization** | Requires counter sync | Allows $\pm 1$ time-step drift | | **Replay Window** | Infinite until counter increments | 30s (or $X$) | | **Clock Dependency** | None | Requires synchronized clocks | | **Use Case** | Offline transactions | Online logins, VPN | --- **Q.A. (b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Kerberos authentication protocol**. Mention its key components. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Kerberos Overview (1 mark):** Kerberos is a ticket-based symmetric-key authentication protocol developed at MIT (1988) for securing client-server interactions in untrusted networks. **Key Components (2 marks):** - **KDC (Key Distribution Center)** — The trusted third party, split into: - **AS (Authentication Server)** — Verifies user identity and issues TGT - **TGS (Ticket Granting Server)** — Issues service tickets - **Realm** — Administrative domain - **Principals** — Users and services - **Tickets** — Time-limited credentials (TGT and ST) - **Authenticators** — Fresh client-generated timestamps preventing replay **Protocol Steps with Diagram (3 marks):** ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant C as Client participant AS as Authentication Server participant TGS as Ticket Granting Server participant V as Service Server C->>AS: 1. Request TGT (user, TGS) AS->>C: 2. Encrypted TGT (E_KC) + K_C_TGS C->>TGS: 3. TGT + Authenticator1 (E_KC_TGS) TGS->>C: 4. Service Ticket (E_KC_TGS) + K_C_V C->>V: 5. Service Ticket + Authenticator2 (E_KC_V) V->>C: 6. Mutual authentication confirmed ``` **Step 1:** Client → AS: `{ID_C, ID_TGS, T_1}` **Step 2:** AS → Client: `{K_{C,TGS}, ID_TGS, T_2, TGT}\}_{K_C}$ where $TGT = \{K_{C,TGS}, ID_C, T_3\}_{K_{TGS}}$ **Step 3:** Client → TGS: `$TGT, \{ID_C, T_4\}_{K_{C,TGS}}$` **Step 4:** TGS → Client: `$\{K_{C,V}, ID_V, T_5, ST\}_{K_{C,TGS}}$` **Step 5:** Client → V: `$ST, \{ID_C, T_6\}_{K_{C,V}}$` **Step 6:** V → Client: `$\{T_6+1\}_{K_{C,V}}$` (mutual authentication) **Security Features (1 mark):** - Defeats replay via timestamps + authenticators - Mutual authentication prevents server spoofing - Tickets have bounded lifetime (default 8h) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)** **Q.B. (a)** Explain the **working of a biometric authentication system** with a block diagram. Define **FAR, FRR, and CER**. A biometric system is tested with 5000 genuine attempts (35 false rejects) and 8000 impostor attempts (12 false accepts). Compute FAR, FRR, and comment on system performance. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Biometric System Working (2 marks):** A biometric authentication system verifies a user based on unique physiological or behavioral characteristics. The four stages are: 1. **Capture** — Sensor acquires the sample (e.g., fingerprint reader) 2. **Feature Extraction** — Minutiae points / texture map generated 3. **Matching** — Comparison with stored template → similarity score 4. **Decision** — Score compared to threshold → Accept or Reject **Block Diagram (1 mark):** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Sensor Capture] --> B[Quality Check] B --> C[Feature Extraction] C --> D[Matcher Engine] E[Template Database] --> D D --> F[Score] F --> G[Decision Module] G --> H{Threshold Check} H -->|Pass| I[Accept] H -->|Fail| J[Reject] ``` **FAR Definition (1 mark):** $$ \text{FAR} = \frac{\text{False Accepts}}{\text{Total Impostor Attempts}} $$ **FRR Definition (1 mark):** $$ \text{FRR} = \frac{\text{False Rejects}}{\text{Total Genuine Attempts}} $$ **CER Definition (1 mark):** The Crossover Error Rate is the threshold point at which FAR = FRR. Lower CER = better system. **Numerical Computation (1 mark):** Given: $M = 5000$ genuine, $FR = 35$, $I = 8000$ impostor, $FA = 12$. $$ \text{FAR} = \frac{12}{8000} = 0.0015 = 0.15\% $$ $$ \text{FRR} = \frac{35}{5000} = 0.007 = 0.7\% $$ **Performance Comment:** Since FRR (0.7%) $\gg$ FAR (0.15%), the system is currently configured for *high security* (low false acceptance) at the cost of *user convenience* (high false rejection). Threshold should be lowered to approach the CER point for balanced performance. --- **Q.B. (b)** Explain **Multifactor Authentication (MFA)** in detail. Why is using two passwords NOT considered MFA? Describe any **three MFA scenarios** with their factor combinations. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition of MFA (1.5 marks):** Multifactor Authentication is a security mechanism that requires users to provide evidence from **two or more independent factor categories** before access is granted. It is based on the principle of *defense in depth* — even if one factor is compromised, the system remains secure. The access decision is given by: $$ \text{Access} = \bigcap_{i=1}^{n} \text{Factor}_i \quad \text{where factors are from distinct categories} $$ **Why Two Passwords is NOT MFA (1.5 marks):** Two passwords (e.g., "Apple123" and "P@ssw0rd") both belong to the **Knowledge factor (Type 1)**. They are *not independent* — a phishing attack or keylogger that captures one will likely capture the other. MFA requires factors from **different categories** (knowledge + possession, or knowledge + biometric) so that compromising one channel does not compromise the others. Using two passwords is at best *multi-step single-factor authentication*. **Three MFA Scenarios (4 marks — 1.33 marks each):** **Scenario 1 — ATM Cash Withdrawal:** - Factor 1 (Knowledge): 4-digit PIN - Factor 2 (Possession): Physical debit card with EMV chip - Result: Card theft alone is insufficient; PIN knowledge alone is insufficient. **Scenario 2 — Corporate VPN Login:** - Factor 1 (Knowledge): Active Directory password - Factor 2 (Possession): Hardware token (RSA SecurID) generating TOTP every 60s - Factor 3 (Biometric, optional): Fingerprint via laptop reader - Result: Even with stolen password, attacker needs physical token AND/OR biometric. **Scenario 3 — Mobile Banking High-Value Transaction:** - Factor 1 (Knowledge): Account password - Factor 2 (Possession): Registered smartphone receiving SMS OTP or push notification - Factor 3 (Biometric): In-app fingerprint or face authentication - Result: Triple-layered security protects against phishing, SIM swap, and device theft simultaneously. **Quantitative Gain (Optional, for higher marks):** If each factor has compromise probability $p_i$ and the factors are independent: $$ P_{\text{break}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i $$ For ATM: $P_{\text{break}} = 10^{-4} \times 10^{-2} = 10^{-6}$ (a 20-bit gain over single-factor). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 3:** - ☐ **Authentication** verifies *identity*; **Authorization** grants *permissions* — never confuse them. - ☐ The **four factor categories** are: Knowledge (Type 1), Possession (Type 2), Biometric (Type 3), and Location (Type 0). - ☐ **Password entropy** formula: $H = L \cdot \log_2 A$ — must know numerical examples. - ☐ **Challenge–Response** defeats replay via nonces; **HTTPS alone** does not. - ☐ **TOTP** = Time-based (RFC 6238); **HOTP** = Counter-based (RFC 4226). TOTP uses `T - T0 / X`; HOTP uses counter $C$. - ☐ **OTP derivation order:** HMAC → Dynamic Truncate → Modulo $10^6$ → Display. Reversed = marks lost. - ☐ **JWT** = `Base64(Header).Base64(Payload).Base64(Signature)` — three parts. - ☐ **Biometric verification** = 1:1; **identification** = 1:N. Identification scales $O(N)$. - ☐ **FAR** = False Accepts / Impostor Attempts; **FRR** = False Rejects / Genuine Attempts; **CER** = FAR = FRR at optimal threshold. - ☐ Lower CER ≈ stronger system. Fingerprint CER ~ 0.001%; iris CER ~ 0.0001%. - ☐ **Kerberos** uses AS + TGS + V with TGT and Service Tickets; clocks must be synchronized ($\pm 5$ min). - ☐ **MFA** is *AND* of factors from *distinct* categories — never two passwords. - ☐ MFA security gain is multiplicative: $P_{\text{break}} = \prod p_i$ — independent failures. - ☐ Common attack vectors: **Replay, MITM, Phishing, Brute-force, Credential Stuffing, SIM Swap, Biometric Spoofing** — always mention in answers. - ☐ KTU 14-mark answers **must include a diagram** (Mermaid or hand-drawn) for full marks. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Numerical Drill:** > 1. Compute TOTP from given $K$ and $T$ — 5 minutes max. > 2. Compute FAR, FRR, GAR from given test data — 3 minutes. > 3. Compute password entropy in bits and brute-force time in years — 4 minutes. > 4. Compute $P_{\text{break}}$ for a given MFA configuration — 2 minutes.

topics.title: Security in Networks - Threats in Networks
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST744 - INFORMATION SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Security in Networks - Threats in Networks

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Network Threats** are any potentially malicious actions, events, or entities that can compromise the **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)** of data, communication channels, and computing resources that traverse, reside within, or are accessible through a computer network. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, threats in networks are broadly classified into **Passive Threats** (eavesdropping/monitoring) and **Active Threats** (modification, fabrication, interruption), forming the foundation of the ITU-T X.800 / RFC 2828 threat taxonomy.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Postal System:** Imagine the internet as the entire public postal system of a country.
> - **Passive Threat** = A thief who secretly opens and reads your letters while they are in transit. He does not change anything; he just *learns* (breach of **Confidentiality**).
> - **Active Threat** = A forger who opens your letter, changes the cheque amount, and re-seals it before delivery. He *alters* your data (breach of **Integrity**).
> - **Denial of Service** = A vandal who repeatedly stuffs the mailboxes with junk, preventing legitimate letters from being delivered (breach of **Availability**).

### 1.3 The CIA Triad — Foundation of Network Threat Analysis

Every network threat is mapped to one or more pillars of the **CIA Triad**:

| Pillar | Definition | Violated By (Example Threat) |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Data is read only by authorized parties | Eavesdropping, Sniffing, Traffic Analysis |
| **Integrity** | Data is not altered in transit or at rest | Replay, Man-in-the-Middle, Session Hijacking |
| **Availability** | Systems/services are accessible when needed | DoS, DDoS, SYN Flood, Smurf Attack |

### 1.4 KTU Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 4 — Expected Learning Outcomes (mapped to Bloom's L1–L4):**
> 1. Classify and explain **passive** and **active** network attacks.
> 2. Describe the operational mechanics of **DoS, DDoS, Sniffing, Spoofing**, and **MITM** attacks.
> 3. Analyze the impact of network-layer and application-layer threats.
> 4. Apply the **X.800 / OSI Security Architecture** model to categorize threats.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ITU-T X.800 Classification of Network Threats

The **X.800 (OSI Security Architecture)** standard is the gold reference for KTU board exams.

#### 2.1.1 Passive Threats (The Silent Observers)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Passive threats attempt to *learn or make use* of information from the system but do **not modify** system resources. They are extremely difficult to detect because no data is altered.

**Sub-types:**
1. **Release of Message Contents** — Reading the actual payload of a packet (e.g., plaintext HTTP credentials).
2. **Traffic Analysis** — Inferring communication patterns, frequency, and identities even when messages are encrypted (e.g., a spike in packets to a hospital reveals an outbreak).

**Countermeasure (Why & How):**
- **Why** is it hard? Because the attacker does not touch the data stream.
- **How** to defend? Use **encryption** (link-level or end-to-end). Mask the patterns by **padding traffic** to a constant rate.

#### 2.1.2 Active Threats (The Tamperers)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Active threats involve modification of the data stream or creation of a false stream. They are easier to detect than passive threats but far more damaging.

**Sub-types (4 canonical categories):**
1. **Masquerade** — An entity pretends to be a different entity (e.g., IP spoofing, email spoofing).
2. **Replay** — Passive capture followed by malicious retransmission to produce an unauthorized effect.
3. **Modification of Messages** — Tampering with portions of a legitimate message (e.g., changing the destination account number in a wire transfer).
4. **Denial of Service (DoS)** — Disruption of an entire service or system so legitimate users cannot use it.

**Countermeasure (Why & How):**
- **Why?** Because the message stream itself is the attack vector.
- **How?** Use **authentication** (to defeat masquerade), **digital signatures & sequence numbers** (to defeat replay), **message integrity checks / MACs** (to defeat modification), and **firewalls / rate-limiting** (to defeat DoS).

### 2.2 Detailed Anatomy of Major Network Threats

#### 2.2.1 Sniffing (Passive Eavesdropping)

A **packet sniffer** is a passive receiver operating in **promiscuous mode** on a shared medium (hub, Wi-Fi, or mirrored switch port). It captures frames and decodes protocols (HTTP, FTP, Telnet, SMTP) to extract plaintext credentials.

- **Why it works:** Legacy Ethernet hubs broadcast every frame to every port; modern switched networks are partially vulnerable via **ARP poisoning** or **port mirroring**.
- **Real-world utility (for defenders):** The same technology powers **Wireshark**, **tcpdump**, and **IDS (Intrusion Detection Systems)** — making the threat identical to the defense tool.

#### 2.2.2 IP Spoofing (Masquerade at Layer 3)

The attacker crafts IP packets with a **forged source IP address** to bypass IP-based authentication (e.g., `rhosts`, ACLs) or to mislead traceback.

- **Why it works:** IPv4 has no built-in source authentication at Layer 3.
- **Defence:** Ingress/Egress filtering (RFC 2827 / BCP 38), cryptographic authentication (IPsec AH/ESP).

#### 2.2.3 Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)

The attacker secretly relays and possibly alters communication between two parties who believe they are directly communicating. Common implementation: **ARP poisoning** (LAN) or **rogue DHCP** + **DNS spoofing** (Wi-Fi hotspots).

- **Operational Flow:** Victim ⇄ Attacker (proxy) ⇄ Legitimate Server. The attacker terminates the TLS connection from the victim and initiates a fresh one to the server, reading all plaintext.

#### 2.2.4 Denial of Service (DoS) & Distributed DoS (DDoS)

Aimed exclusively at breaking **Availability**. The attacker overwhelms the target's resources (bandwidth, CPU, memory, sockets) so it cannot service legitimate requests.

| Attack Variant | Target Resource | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **SYN Flood** | TCP connection table | Sends many TCP SYN packets with spoofed IPs, exhausting half-open connections |
| **Smurf Attack** | Bandwidth | ICMP Echo Request with victim's IP as source, broadcast to amplify |
| **Ping of Death** | IP Stack | Oversized ICMP packet causes buffer overflow on older systems |
| **UDP Flood** | Bandwidth | Floods random UDP ports, forcing ICMP "port unreachable" replies |
| **HTTP Flood** | Application threads | Layer-7 GET/POST requests that are expensive to process |
| **DDoS (Botnet)** | Everything | Thousands of compromised "zombie" hosts coordinate the attack |

**DDoS vs DoS — Key Difference:**
- **DoS** = Single source (easier to block via IP).
- **DDoS** = Multiple distributed sources (botnet of 10⁴–10⁶ hosts), making blacklisting impractical.

#### 2.2.5 DNS Spoofing / Cache Poisoning

Corrupts the DNS resolver's cache so that a domain name (e.g., `bank.com`) resolves to an attacker-controlled IP. The victim types the correct URL but lands on a phishing page.

- **Why it works:** Classic DNS uses **unauthenticated UDP** with a 16-bit **Transaction ID** that can be guessed/brute-forced.

#### 2.2.6 Session Hijacking

The attacker steals a valid session token (e.g., a session cookie) after the user has authenticated, then reuses it to impersonate the user. Common after **MITM** or via **XSS (Cross-Site Scripting)**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a high-density revision aid. Memorize the columns; KTU frequently tests these mappings.

| Threat / Attack | Threat Class | CIA Pillar Violated | OSI Layer | Primary Defence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Eavesdropping | Passive | Confidentiality | L1–L7 | Encryption (AES, TLS) |
| Traffic Analysis | Passive | Confidentiality | L2–L4 | Traffic padding / Onion routing |
| Sniffing (Wireshark) | Passive | Confidentiality | L2 | Switched networks, WPA3, MACsec |
| IP Spoofing | Active (Masquerade) | Integrity, Authenticity | L3 | BCP 38 filtering, IPsec |
| DNS Spoofing | Active (Masquerade) | Integrity | L7 | DNSSEC |
| ARP Poisoning | Active (Masquerade) | Integrity | L2 | Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) |
| Replay Attack | Active (Replay) | Integrity | L5–L7 | Nonces, Timestamps, Sequence Numbers |
| MITM | Active (Masquerade + Modification) | Confidentiality + Integrity | L2–L7 | TLS, Certificate Pinning |
| Session Hijacking | Active (Masquerade) | Integrity, Authenticity | L5–L7 | Secure cookies, HttpOnly flag, TLS |
| DoS / DDoS | Active (Interruption) | Availability | L3–L7 | Rate limiting, Anycast, Scrubbing centers |
| SYN Flood | Active (DoS) | Availability | L4 | SYN cookies, Firewalls |
| Smurf Attack | Active (DoS) | Availability | L3 | Disable directed broadcast |
| Email Spoofing | Active (Masquerade) | Integrity, Authenticity | L7 | SPF, DKIM, DMARC |
| SQL Injection | Active (Modification) | Integrity, Confidentiality | L7 | Parameterized queries, WAF |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** ISPs deploy **BGP route filtering** and **RPKI** to defend against IP spoofing and prefix hijacking.
- **Cloud Computing:** **AWS Shield, Cloudflare, Akamai** are commercial DDoS scrubbing services used by enterprises.
- **Enterprise Networks:** **NAC (Network Access Control)** with 802.1X authenticates devices before granting LAN access — directly defeating passive sniffing and spoofing at the access layer.
- **Software Engineering:** Secure SDLC and **OWASP Top 10** directly address the application-layer threats (XSS, SQLi, CSRF) that ride on top of the network layer.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Passive vs Active Attacks

The following table is **explicitly drawn from the NIST SP 800-12 / RFC 2828 / ITU-T X.800** threat taxonomy and is the most common 14-mark question structure in KTU boards.

$$
T_{network} = \underbrace{\{T_{passive}, T_{active}\}}_{\text{classification}} \quad \text{where} \quad T_{passive} \cap T_{active} = \emptyset
$$

$$
T_{passive} = \{ \text{Eavesdropping}, \text{Traffic Analysis} \}
$$

$$
T_{active} = \{ \text{Masquerade}, \text{Replay}, \text{Modification}, \text{DoS} \}
$$

**Step-by-step analytical walkthrough:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:}\ &\text{Classify the threat based on whether the data stream is modified.} \\
&\text{If } \Delta(\text{data}) = 0 \Rightarrow T \in T_{passive} \\
&\text{If } \Delta(\text{data}) \neq 0 \Rightarrow T \in T_{active} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 2:}\ &\text{For active threats, identify the sub-class using the four-way taxonomy.} \\
&\text{Masquerade} \rightarrow \text{Spoofed identity} \\
&\text{Replay} \rightarrow \text{Retransmitted legitimate data} \\
&\text{Modification} \rightarrow \text{Altered payload} \\
&\text{DoS} \rightarrow \text{Resource exhaustion} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 3:}\ &\text{Map to the CIA pillar that is violated.} \\
&\text{Confidentiality} \Leftarrow T_{passive} \\
&\text{Integrity / Authenticity} \Leftarrow \{\text{Masquerade, Replay, Modification}\} \\
&\text{Availability} \Leftarrow \text{DoS / DDoS}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Quantitative Model — DoS Attack Strength (Bottleneck Saturation)

The **effectiveness** of a volumetric DoS attack can be modeled by comparing attacker bandwidth to the bottleneck link capacity.

$$
\text{Attack Success Condition: } B_{attacker} > B_{bottleneck}
$$

Where:
- $B_{attacker}$ = total attack bandwidth (in **Gbps** or **Mpps** — Million packets per second)
- $B_{bottleneck}$ = capacity of the narrowest link on the path to the victim

**Step-by-step derivation for a DDoS amplification attack (e.g., DNS amplification):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:}\ &\text{Define the Amplification Factor (AF).} \\
AF &= \frac{\text{Size of Response (bytes)}}{\text{Size of Request (bytes)}} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 2:}\ &\text{Typical AF for DNS ANY query over UDP:} \\
AF_{DNS} &= \frac{4096 \text{ bytes (response)}}{64 \text{ bytes (request)}} \approx 64\times \\
\\
\textbf{Step 3:}\ &\text{Compute the effective attack bandwidth from a botnet of } N \text{ hosts.} \\
B_{effective} &= N \times B_{per\_host} \times AF_{DNS} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 4:}\ &\text{Determine if saturation occurs.} \\
\text{If } B_{effective} &> B_{bottleneck} \Rightarrow \text{Service degradation / outage} \\
\text{If } B_{effective} &\le B_{bottleneck} \Rightarrow \text{Service remains available}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

A botnet of $N = 10000$ compromised hosts, each with an uplink of $B_{per\_host} = 10$ **Mbps**, launches a DNS amplification attack against a server with a $1$ **Gbps** link.

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{effective} &= N \times B_{per\_host} \times AF_{DNS} \\
&= 10{,}000 \times 10 \text{ Mbps} \times 64 \\
&= 6{,}400{,}000 \text{ Mbps} \\
&= 6.4 \text{ Tbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $6.4 \text{ Tbps} \gg 1 \text{ Gbps}$, the **bottleneck is saturated** and the service is unavailable. This illustrates why even modest botnets can cripple high-capacity servers.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — The "Threat → Vector → Asset" Mapping

This is the most likely **14-mark** framework that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce.

| Layer | Threat Vector | Target Asset | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L1 Physical** | Wiretapping, Tapping fiber | All bits on cable | Faraday cages, fiber monitoring |
| **L2 Data Link** | ARP poisoning, MAC flooding | Switch CAM table, L2 forwarding | Port security, 802.1X, DAI |
| **L3 Network** | IP spoofing, Smurf, ICMP redirect | Routing, IP tables | BCP 38, RPKI, IPsec |
| **L4 Transport** | SYN flood, UDP flood, port scanning | Sockets, connection state | SYN cookies, Stateful firewalls |
| **L5 Session** | Session hijacking, token theft | Authenticated sessions | TLS 1.3, Secure cookies |
| **L6 Presentation** | SSL stripping, downgrade attacks | Encryption channel | HSTS, TLS pinning |
| **L7 Application** | XSS, SQLi, Phishing, Email spoofing | Application logic, user data | WAF, Input validation, SPF/DKIM/DMARC |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Top-Level Taxonomy of Network Threats

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NETWORK THREATS] --> B[Passive Threats]
    A --> C[Active Threats]
    
    B --> B1[Release of Message Contents]
    B --> B2[Traffic Analysis]
    B1 --> B1a[Sniffing on Hubs]
    B1 --> B1b[WiFi Eavesdropping]
    B1 --> B1c[Tap on Fiber Copper]
    B2 --> B2a[Timing Analysis]
    B2 --> B2b[Volume Analysis]
    
    C --> D[Masquerade]
    C --> E[Replay]
    C --> F[Modification of Messages]
    C --> G[Denial of Service]
    
    D --> D1[IP Spoofing]
    D --> D2[DNS Spoofing]
    D --> D3[Email Spoofing]
    D --> D4[ARP Poisoning]
    
    E --> E1[Session Replay]
    E --> E2[Authentication Replay]
    
    F --> F1[Man in the Middle]
    F --> F2[SQL Injection]
    F --> F3[HTML Injection XSS]
    
    G --> G1[DoS Single Source]
    G --> G2[DDoS Botnet]
    G1 --> G1a[SYN Flood]
    G1 --> G1b[Smurf Attack]
    G1 --> G1c[Ping of Death]
    G1 --> G1d[UDP Flood]
    G2 --> G2a[HTTP Flood L7]
    G2 --> G2b[DNS Amplification]
    G2 --> G2c[NTP Amplification]
```

### 4.2 Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) Attack Architecture

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant V as Victim Client
    participant A as Attacker MITM Proxy
    participant S as Legitimate Server
    
    Note over V,S: Victim believes direct TLS to server
    V->>A: TLS ClientHello (intends to reach S)
    A->>S: TLS ClientHello (separate session to S)
    S-->>A: TLS ServerHello + Certificate
    A-->>V: TLS ServerHello + Fake Certificate
    Note over V: Victim trusts rogue cert (or HSTS bypass)
    V->>A: Encrypted request with session key K1
    A->>A: Decrypt with K1, Read Plaintext
    A->>S: Re-encrypt with K2, Forward to Server
    S-->>A: Encrypted response
    A->>V: Forwarded encrypted response
    Note over A: Attacker sees ALL plaintext in real time
```

### 4.3 DDoS Botnet Operational Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C2[Command and Control Server] -->|1 Issue Attack Command| BN[Botnet Herder]
    BN -->|2 Distribute Target IP| Z1[Zombie Bot 1]
    BN -->|2 Distribute Target IP| Z2[Zombie Bot 2]
    BN -->|2 Distribute Target IP| Z3[Zombie Bot 3]
    BN -->|2 Distribute Target IP| Zn[Zombie Bot N]
    
    Z1 -->|3 Flood SYN| T[Target Victim Server]
    Z2 -->|3 Flood UDP| T
    Z3 -->|3 Flood HTTP GET| T
    Zn -->|3 Flood DNS Amp| T
    
    T -->|4 Service Degraded or Down| L[Legitimate Users Blocked]
    
    style C2 fill:#ff6b6b
    style BN fill:#ffa500
    style T fill:#4ecdc4
    style L fill:#95e1d3
```

### 4.4 SYN Flood — Half-Open Connection Exhaustion

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> LISTEN: Server opens port 80
    LISTEN --> SYN_RCVD: Attacker sends SYN with spoofed source IP
    SYN_RCVD --> SYN_RCVD: Attacker NEVER sends ACK
    SYN_RCVD --> LISTEN: Connection times out and frees slot
    Note right of SYN_RCVD: Legitimate client SYN cannot be accepted
    Note right of SYN_RCVD: Backlog queue of half-open connections grows
    Note right of SYN_RCVD: Server CPU and memory exhausted
    Note right of LISTEN: New connections dropped once backlog is full
```

### 4.5 Defence-in-Depth Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PERIMETER[Perimeter Layer]
        FW[Next-Gen Firewall]
        IDS[Intrusion Detection System]
        DDoS[DDoS Scrubber]
    end
    
    subgraph NETWORK[Network Layer]
        VPN[IPsec VPN Tunnels]
        NAC[802.1X NAC]
        VLAN[Network Segmentation]
    end
    
    subgraph HOST[Host Layer]
        AV[Endpoint Antivirus]
        HIDS[Host-based IDS]
        PATCH[Patch Management]
    end
    
    subgraph APP[Application Layer]
        WAF[Web Application Firewall]
        IAM[Identity and Access Management]
        TLS[TLS 1.3 Everywhere]
    end
    
    I[Internet Attacker] --> DDoS
    DDoS --> FW
    FW --> IDS
    IDS --> NAC
    NAC --> APP
    APP --> RES[Protected Resource]
    
    style I fill:#ff4444
    style RES fill:#44ff44
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Distinguish between **passive** and **active** network threats with one example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Passive Threat | Active Threat |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Modification** | None — data stream is untouched | Data stream is altered or fabricated |
| **Detection Difficulty** | Very difficult (no trace) | Easier (leaves signatures) |
| **Primary CIA Pillar** | Confidentiality | Integrity & Availability |
| **Example** | Eavesdropping a Wi-Fi connection with Wireshark | SYN flood attack on a web server |
| **Defence** | Encryption | Authentication + Integrity checks |

*(1 mark each for: clear definition, distinguishing characteristic, and example)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Paper]**
*What is a **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack**? Name any two techniques used to launch it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack** is an active network attack in which the adversary secretly intercepts and relays communication between two parties who believe they are communicating directly with each other, allowing the attacker to **read, insert, and modify** messages without detection. *(2 marks)*

**Two techniques to launch MITM:**
1. **ARP Poisoning** — Attacker sends forged ARP replies on a LAN to associate its MAC address with the gateway's IP.
2. **DNS Spoofing / Cache Poisoning** — Attacker corrupts the DNS resolver so the victim is redirected to a malicious server.
*(1 mark for naming any two)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: **Internal choice between two questions** (A or B). Both questions cover the same module. Solve EITHER A or B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Recommended Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4]**
**(a)** Classify network threats according to the **X.800 (OSI Security Architecture)** standard. Explain the four types of active threats in detail with diagrams. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the operational mechanics of a **SYN Flood** attack. Explain how **SYN cookies** mitigate this attack. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) X.800 Classification of Network Threats (7 marks)**

The **ITU-T Recommendation X.800 (Security Architecture for OSI)** divides threats into two top-level classes:

**(i) Passive Threats (1 mark)** — Attempt to learn information without modifying system resources.

$$
T_{passive} = \{ \text{Release of Message Contents}, \text{Traffic Analysis} \}
$$

- **Release of Message Contents:** Reading the payload of packets in transit (e.g., plaintext HTTP login).
- **Traffic Analysis:** Inferring identities, frequencies, and patterns from encrypted traffic metadata (e.g., traffic spikes to a hospital reveal a pandemic).

**Countermeasure:** Encryption at link or application layer, traffic padding.

**(ii) Active Threats (1 mark)** — Involve modification of data or creation of false data.

**The Four Sub-Types (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Masquerade:** An entity pretends to be a different entity.
   - *Example:* IP spoofing — sender forges source IP to impersonate a trusted host.
2. **Replay:** Passive capture of a valid message, followed by unauthorized retransmission.
   - *Example:* Re-sending a previously valid authentication token.
3. **Modification of Messages:** Altering a portion of a legitimate message.
   - *Example:* Changing the recipient account number in an online wire transfer.
4. **Denial of Service (DoS):** Preventing legitimate use of a service.
   - *Example:* SYN flood, DDoS attack.

**Diagram (1 mark):** See Section 4.1 above for the canonical taxonomy tree.

---

**(b) SYN Flood Attack and SYN Cookies (7 marks)**

**Operational Mechanics of SYN Flood (4 marks):**

The **TCP Three-Way Handshake** normally proceeds as:

$$
\text{Client} \xrightarrow{SYN} \text{Server} \xrightarrow{SYN-ACK} \text{Client} \xrightarrow{ACK} \text{Server}
$$

In a **SYN flood**, the attacker sends a flood of TCP SYN packets with **spoofed source IP addresses** and then **never sends the final ACK**. The server allocates a slot in its **TCP backlog queue** for each half-open connection, sends a SYN-ACK to the (unreachable) spoofed address, and waits.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{State 1:}\ &\text{SYN received} \Rightarrow \text{Server creates TCB entry, transitions to SYN\_RCVD} \\
\textbf{State 2:}\ &\text{Backlog queue entry is held} \\
\textbf{State 3:}\ &\text{Timeout (typically 75 seconds) before entry is freed} \\
\textbf{Result:}\ &\text{Backlog exhausts} \Rightarrow \text{New legitimate SYNs are dropped}
\end{aligned}
$$

A single attacker can therefore consume all available connection slots with minimal bandwidth.

**SYN Cookies Mitigation (3 marks):**

SYN cookies eliminate the server's need to maintain state for half-open connections.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:}\ &\text{Server receives SYN from client with sequence number } S_{client} \\
\textbf{Step 2:}\ &\text{Server computes a cookie } C \text{ as a function of:} \\
C &= \text{Hash}(S_{client}, \text{server\_secret}, \text{source\_IP}, \text{port}) \bmod 2^{n} \\
\textbf{Step 3:}\ &\text{Server sends SYN-ACK with } S_{server} = C \text{ (the ISN encodes the cookie)} \\
& \Rightarrow \text{No backlog entry is created!} \\
\textbf{Step 4:}\ &\text{When ACK arrives with } S_{client}+1, \text{ server reconstructs:} \\
C' &= S_{server} - 1 \\
\text{Verify:}\ &\text{If } C' \text{ matches a recomputed cookie, the connection is legitimate} \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Server now allocates a full TCP control block}
\end{aligned}
$$

This stateless approach makes SYN floods **computationally infeasible** to exhaust resources, since no state is held until the third ACK arrives.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]**
**(a)** What is **IP Spoofing**? Explain how it is used in **reflection-based DDoS attacks** such as the **Smurf attack**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast **DoS** and **DDoS** attacks. Discuss any **three** application-layer DoS attack techniques. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) IP Spoofing and Smurf Attack (7 marks)**

**IP Spoofing (3 marks):**
IP Spoofing is an active network attack of the **Masquerade** class in which the attacker **forges the source IP address** in the IP header of outgoing packets to make them appear as if they originated from a different host. It violates the **Integrity** and **Authenticity** properties.

$$
\text{Attacker constructs packet: } \text{IP}_{\text{header}}\{\text{Src} = V_{IP},\ \text{Dst} = T_{IP},\ \text{Payload} = \text{data}\}
$$

where $V_{IP}$ is the victim's IP (a trusted host) and $T_{IP}$ is the target. Since IPv4 has no built-in source authentication at the network layer, the receiver is deceived.

**Reflection-Based DDoS — The Smurf Attack (4 marks):**
The **Smurf attack** combines IP spoofing with a directed broadcast to amplify traffic.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:}\ &\text{Attacker crafts an ICMP Echo Request packet} \\
&\text{with } \text{Src} = V_{IP} \text{ (victim)}, \text{Dst} = B \text{ (network broadcast address)} \\
\textbf{Step 2:}\ &\text{Sending to a broadcast address causes ALL hosts on that subnet} \\
&\text{to receive the packet (N hosts)} \\
\textbf{Step 3:}\ &\text{Each of the } N \text{ hosts replies with an ICMP Echo Reply to } V_{IP} \\
\textbf{Step 4:}\ &\text{Victim } V \text{ is flooded with } N \times 1 \text{ replies} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Single attacker packet becomes N replies (Amplification Factor = N)} \\
\textbf{Result:}\ &\text{Bandwidth exhaustion of } V_{IP}, \text{ causing DoS}
\end{aligned}
$$

Modern defence: disable **directed IP broadcasts** on router interfaces (RFC 2644).

---

**(b) DoS vs DDoS and Application-Layer DoS (7 marks)**

**DoS vs DDoS Comparison (3 marks):**

| Parameter | DoS | DDoS |
|---|---|---|
| **Source Count** | Single attacker | Thousands of distributed bots |
| **Mitigation** | Block single IP | Requires scrubbing/anycast |
| **Bandwidth** | Limited by attacker's link | Multiplied by botnet size |
| **Example** | `ping` from one PC | Mirai botnet (2016) |
| **Detection** | Easier (single source) | Very hard (distributed) |

**Three Application-Layer DoS Techniques (4 marks — 1.3 marks each):**

1. **HTTP GET/POST Flood:** Attacker sends thousands of legitimate-looking HTTP requests. Each request triggers expensive database queries or crypto operations, exhausting server threads. No malformed packets are sent, making it hard to distinguish from real traffic.

2. **Slowloris:** Attacker opens many HTTP connections and sends the request **very slowly** (e.g., one header byte at a time). The server keeps these sockets open, eventually exhausting its connection pool. The attack uses minimal bandwidth.

3. **DNS Water Torture:** Attacker sends a flood of random subdomain queries (e.g., `x1.bank.com`, `x2.bank.com`, …) to a public DNS resolver, which recursively queries the bank's authoritative server. The amplification exhausts the authoritative server's resources.

**Defence:** Rate limiting, CAPTCHA, Web Application Firewalls, and **Anycast** networks for DDoS scrubbing.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing passive and active threats** — A student writes "SYN flood is a passive attack" because it "just floods." **No.** It modifies the state of the server's connection table → **active**.
> 2. **Forgetting the CIA Triad mapping** — KTU examiners award at least 1 mark for correctly identifying *which* CIA pillar is violated. Never skip it.
> 3. **Skipping the diagram in 14-mark answers** — A 7-mark sub-part without a labeled diagram is docked 1–2 marks. Always include a Mermaid/hand-drawn block diagram.
> 4. **Mentioning defences without mechanism** — Writing "use encryption" gets 0.5 marks; writing "use AES-256-GCM in TLS 1.3 with mutual authentication" gets full credit.
