> 5. **Mixing up DoS and DDoS** — DoS is one source; DDoS is many distributed sources. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 6. **Forgetting X.800 framework** — When asked to "classify threats," always anchor your answer to the **ITU-T X.800** taxonomy, as it is the syllabus's named framework.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Threats in Networks**
> - [ ] **Two top-level classes:** Passive (eavesdropping, traffic analysis) vs Active (masquerade, replay, modification, DoS).
> - [ ] **CIA Triad:** Every threat maps to Confidentiality, Integrity, or Availability.
> - [ ] **X.800 Framework:** The canonical ITU-T reference for threat classification — must be cited by name.
> - [ ] **Passive attacks** are hard to detect, easy to prevent (encryption).
> - [ ] **Active attacks** are easy to detect, hard to prevent.
> - [ ] **Sniffing** = passive; **Spoofing** = active (masquerade).
> - [ ] **MITM** attacks both Confidentiality AND Integrity — it is the most damaging active attack.
> - [ ] **SYN Flood** exhausts the TCP backlog; defended by **SYN cookies** (stateless).
> - [ ] **Smurf Attack** = ICMP to directed broadcast with spoofed source IP; AF = number of hosts on subnet.
> - [ ] **DoS = 1 source; DDoS = botnet** (e.g., Mirai 2016, 1.2 Tbps).
> - [ ] **Amplification Factor (AF)** = Response size / Request size. DNS AF ≈ 50–80×; NTP AF ≈ 500×.
> - [ ] **DNS Spoofing** defeated by **DNSSEC**; **IP Spoofing** defeated by **BCP 38 ingress filtering** and **RPKI**.
> - [ ] **Defence-in-Depth** requires layers: Perimeter (FW/IDS) → Network (VPN/NAC) → Host (AV/HIPS) → Application (WAF/IAM/TLS).
> - [ ] **Always include a labeled diagram** in 14-mark answers — a Mermaid block diagram earns at least 1 mark.
> - [ ] **Key defence mechanisms to memorize:** Encryption, Authentication, MAC/HMAC, Digital Signatures, Nonces, Sequence Numbers, SYN cookies, BCP 38, DNSSEC, WAF, Rate limiting.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Security Controls - Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST744 - INFORMATION SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Network Security Controls - Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Network Security Controls – Architecture** refers to the systematic, layered arrangement of hardware, software, policies, and procedural safeguards designed to protect the **confidentiality, integrity, and availability (CIA Triad)** of data while it is in transit across or resides within computer networks. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the architecture denotes the *blueprint* that specifies *where*, *how*, and *why* specific security mechanisms (firewalls, IDS/IPS, VPNs, segmentation, DMZ, etc.) are placed in a network topology to enforce a defense-in-depth strategy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology (Syllabus-Exact):**
> - **Control**: A safeguard or countermeasure prescribed to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information and information systems.
> - **Architecture**: A structured, defensible framework that defines the placement, interaction, and lifecycle of security controls within a network ecosystem.
> - **Defense-in-Depth**: A layered security strategy in which multiple, independent controls are deployed such that failure of one control does not result in total system compromise.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine your house has **three concentric rings of protection**:

1. **The outer fence** (perimeter firewall) – keeps most strangers out.
2. **The locked front door with a camera** (DMZ + IDS) – inspects what gets past the fence.
3. **The safe inside your bedroom** (internal segmentation + encryption) – protects valuables even if an intruder gets inside.

Network Security Architecture does the same thing for a corporate network. Instead of relying on a single wall, it builds **multiple, overlapping, independently meaningful security layers**, each catching threats the others might miss. A single failed control never collapses the entire security posture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Architecture Matters in KTU Examinations:**
> Examiners frequently test whether the student can *classify* controls, *justify* their placement, and *map* them to a real network topology. A common 14-mark question asks to draw and justify a security architecture for a given enterprise scenario (e.g., a 3-tier e-commerce company, a university campus, or a cloud-hosted application).

## 1.3 Core Principles of Network Security Architecture (KTU High-Yield List)

| # | Principle | One-Line Meaning |
|---|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Least Privilege** | A subject gets *only* the minimum access necessary to do its job. |
| 2 | **Defense-in-Depth** | No single control is the sole guardian; multiple layers coexist. |
| 3 | **Fail-Safe / Fail-Secure** | On failure, the system defaults to a *deny* state, not *allow*. |
| 4 | **Separation of Duties** | Critical tasks require more than one person to complete. |
| 5 | **Complete Mediation** | Every access request is checked against access rules — no shortcuts. |
| 6 | **Open Design** | Security should not depend on secret mechanisms, but on well-audited public ones (e.g., Kerckhoffs' principle). |
| 7 | **Economy of Mechanism** | Keep security designs small, simple, and verifiable. |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** Memorize the seven principles verbatim. In KTU 7-mark sub-questions, a list-only answer without a one-line explanation often fetches only 4 out of 7 marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Network Security Controls

Network security controls are classified along **three orthogonal axes** in the KTU framework:

### Axis A — By Function (What the control *does*)
1. **Preventive Controls** – Block attacks before they occur (firewalls, ACLs, encryption).
2. **Detective Controls** – Identify attacks as they occur (IDS, SIEM, log monitoring).
3. **Corrective Controls** – Restore systems after an incident (backups, patches, incident response).
4. **Deterrent Controls** – Discourage attackers (warning banners, legal notices, audit trails).
5. **Recovery Controls** – Bring operations back to normal (DR sites, redundancy).

### Axis B — By Layer (Where the control *lives*)
- **Physical Layer**: Cable locks, locked server rooms, CCTV.
- **Data Link / Network Layer**: Switch port security, ARP inspection, firewalls, IDS/IPS.
- **Transport / Application Layer**: TLS, SFTP, WAF, API gateways, DLP.

### Axis C — By Form (What the control *is*)
- **Technical / Logical Controls** (hardware/software) — firewalls, antivirus, encryption.
- **Administrative Controls** (policies/procedures) — security policies, training, audits.
- **Physical Controls** (tangible) — guards, fences, biometric door locks.

## 2.2 Architectural Components of a Modern Secure Network

| Component | Functional Role | Typical Placement |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Perimeter Firewall** | First-line packet filtering | Network edge (between ISP and internal LAN) |
| **DMZ (Demilitarized Zone)** | Buffer for public-facing servers | Between two firewalls (front + back) |
| **Back-end Firewall** | Protects internal servers from DMZ | Between DMZ and internal LAN |
| **IDS (Intrusion Detection System)** | Passive monitoring, alerts on anomalies | In-line tap or SPAN port |
| **IPS (Intrusion Prevention System)** | Active blocking of malicious traffic | In-line with traffic flow |
| **VPN Concentrator** | Encrypts remote-user tunnels | Edge of perimeter, before internal firewall |
| **Network Access Control (NAC)** | Authenticates devices before admission | Switch / wireless controller layer |
| **SIEM** | Aggregates logs, correlates events | Internal management VLAN |
| **Honeypot / Honeynet** | Decoys to study attacker behavior | Isolated VLAN, no production data |

## 2.3 Three Canonical Architectural Models

### Model 1 — Single-Firewall (Screened Subnet Light)
A single firewall with three interfaces: **Public (WAN) | DMZ | Internal (LAN)**. Sufficient for very small businesses with limited budget.

### Model 2 — Dual-Firewall DMZ (Industry Standard)
Two firewalls in series:
- **Front Firewall (Screening Router / Edge Firewall)** — filters unsolicited inbound traffic.
- **Back Firewall (Internal Firewall)** — protects LAN from DMZ.
- **DMZ** sits between them and hosts public servers (web, mail, DNS).

### Model 3 — Layered / Zero-Trust Architecture
Adds **micro-segmentation**, **identity-aware proxies**, and **policy enforcement points** inside the LAN itself. Every internal communication is authenticated and authorized — never trusted by default.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Metric Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Although Network Security Architecture is conceptual, KTU examiners occasionally ask students to compute basic **availability / reliability metrics** for redundant control designs.

| Formula | Symbol Legend | Units / Use |
|---------|---------------|-------------|
| $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | $A$ = Availability, $MTBF$ = Mean Time Between Failures, $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair | Ratio (0–1) or % |
| $R_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | $R_i$ = reliability of each component | Used when controls fail *independently* in sequence |
| $R_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)$ | Reliability of *redundant* parallel controls | Used in fail-over firewall pairs |
| $P_{compromise} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - C_i)$ | $C_i$ = coverage/effectiveness of control *i* | Probability all layers fail (1 − coverage each) |
| $E_{layer} = 1 - \prod (1 - E_i)$ | $E_i$ = individual layer effectiveness | Combined effectiveness of layered controls |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LATEX RULE:** Never write $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table — always use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to avoid breaking table syntax.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a corporate setting, a well-designed network security architecture:
- **Reduces attack surface** through segmentation (lateral movement containment).
- **Improves regulatory compliance** (ISO 27001, PCI-DSS, NIST SP 800-53 mandate layered controls).
- **Enables measurable risk management** using the probability formulas above.
- **Supports business continuity** by ensuring redundant controls (parallel firewalls, multi-site DR).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Combined Effectiveness of Layered Controls

**Problem Statement:** A network has three independent security controls. Control A blocks 90% of attacks, Control B blocks 80%, and Control C blocks 75%. Assuming the controls are independent and an attacker must defeat *all* to succeed, what is the *combined* effectiveness $E_{combined}$ of the architecture?

**Step 1 — Define the failure probability of each control.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{A,fail} &= 1 - 0.90 = 0.10 \\
P_{B,fail} &= 1 - 0.80 = 0.20 \\
P_{C,fail} &= 1 - 0.75 = 0.25
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* If a control blocks a fraction of attacks, the fraction it *lets through* is $1 - \text{(blocking fraction)}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the probability that the *attacker defeats all three* (independent events).**

$$
P_{all,fail} = P_{A,fail} \times P_{B,fail} \times P_{C,fail}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{all,fail} &= 0.10 \times 0.20 \times 0.25 \\
&= 0.005 \\
&= 0.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* For independent events, the joint probability of *all* occurring is the product of individual probabilities.

**Step 3 — Convert to combined effectiveness.**

$$
E_{combined} = 1 - P_{all,fail} = 1 - 0.005 = 0.995
$$

*Result:* The layered architecture blocks **99.5%** of attacks, even though no single control exceeds 90%. This is the **multiplicative power of defense-in-depth**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reliability of a Redundant Parallel Firewall Pair

**Problem Statement:** Two identical firewalls are deployed in active-passive failover. Each has reliability $R = 0.95$ (i.e., it functions correctly 95% of the time). What is the reliability of the firewall pair?

**Step 1 — Identify the failure probability of one firewall.**

$$
P_{single,fail} = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05
$$

**Step 2 — The pair fails only if *both* firewalls fail simultaneously.**

$$
P_{pair,fail} = P_{single,fail} \times P_{single,fail} = 0.05 \times 0.05 = 0.0025
$$

**Step 3 — Reliability of the parallel pair.**

$$
R_{parallel} = 1 - P_{pair,fail} = 1 - 0.0025 = 0.9975
$$

*Engineering Insight:* Going from 95% to 99.75% reliability by adding **one identical backup** is a 20× reduction in risk — this is exactly why banks, data centers, and ISPs always deploy redundant security infrastructure.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Controls to Threats

| Threat (STRIDE category) | Primary Control | Secondary Control | Architectural Placement |
|--------------------------|----------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| **S**poofing (identity) | Mutual TLS, Kerberos | NAC, 802.1X | Application + Network Access Layer |
| **T**ampering (data) | Digital signatures, hashing | WAF, file integrity monitoring | Application + Host Layer |
| **R**epudiation | Digital signatures, audit logs | SIEM, NTP-synchronized logs | Application + Management Plane |
| **I**nformation Disclosure | Encryption (TLS, IPsec, AES) | DLP, network segmentation | Transport + Network Layer |
| **D**enial of Service | Rate-limiting, scrubbing center | Anycast, redundant paths | Edge / ISP Layer |
| **E**levation of Privilege | RBAC, least privilege | Patch management, MFA | Host + Identity Layer |

> [!NOTE]
> **STRIDE** is the threat-modeling framework developed at Microsoft. KTU examiners sometimes expect students to name it and link one threat to a control. Memorize the mapping for at least three categories.

## 3.4 Design Decision Flow — Choosing the Right Architecture

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Identify assets and their value.} &\rightarrow \text{Classify data (Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted).} \\
\text{Step 2: Identify threat actors.} &\rightarrow \text{Script kiddies, insiders, APTs, nation-states.} \\
\text{Step 3: Map attack surfaces.} &\rightarrow \text{Internet-facing apps, APIs, remote users, supply chain.} \\
\text{Step 4: Choose control set.} &\rightarrow \text{Preventive + Detective + Corrective, in layers.} \\
\text{Step 5: Compute combined effectiveness.} &\rightarrow E_{combined} = 1 - \prod (1 - E_i) \\
\text{Step 6: Compute cost-benefit.} &\rightarrow \text{ROI} = \frac{\text{Expected Loss Avoided}}{\text{Control Cost}}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Dual-Firewall DMZ

> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (prefixed with letters). No reserved keywords are used as node names. All labels are uppercase alphanumeric only — no markdown formatting inside quotes.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ISP[INTERNET SERVICE PROVIDER] --> EDGEFW
    EDGEFW[EDGE FIREWALL - SCREENING ROUTER] --> DMZZONE
    DMZZONE[DMZ ZONE - PUBLIC SERVERS] --> BACKFW
    DMZZONE --- WEBSRV[WEB SERVER]
    DMZZONE --- MAILSRV[MAIL SERVER]
    DMZZONE --- DNSSRV[DNS SERVER]
    BACKFW[BACK FIREWALL - INTERNAL GATEWAY] --> INTERNALNET
    INTERNALNET[INTERNAL LAN - CORPORATE NETWORK] --- DB[PRODUCTION DATABASE]
    INTERNALNET --- AD[ACTIVE DIRECTORY]
    INTERNALNET --- SIEM[SIEM SERVER]
    INTERNALNET --- USERS[END USERS]
    INTERNALNET --- IDPS[INLINE IPS SENSOR]
    VPNU[REMOTE VPN USERS] --> EDGEFW
    NACSVR[NAC APPLIANCE] --> INTERNALNET
    MON[VLAN 10 MANAGEMENT] --> SIEM
    HONEY[HONEYPOT VLAN 99] --- HONEYSVR[DECOY SERVER]
    INTERNALNET -.isolated.-> HONEY
```

**Reading the Diagram (KTU Examiner-Ready Interpretation):**

- The **Edge Firewall** acts as the *first filter* — it drops unsolicited traffic from the Internet using stateful packet inspection.
- The **DMZ** is intentionally *isolated*; a compromise of the web server cannot directly reach the internal LAN because the **Back Firewall** enforces a second, stricter rule set.
- The **IPS** sits in-line within the internal network to catch lateral movement (e.g., a compromised laptop trying to reach the database).
- The **SIEM** aggregates logs from *all* the devices (firewalls, IPS, AD, hosts) and correlates events for the SOC team.
- The **Honeypot** lives in its own isolated VLAN (VLAN 99) with no production data — any traffic there is by definition suspicious.

## 4.2 Defense-in-Depth Onion Model (Layered View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[LAYER 1 - PHYSICAL SECURITY] --> L2
    L2[LAYER 2 - PERIMETER NETWORK - FIREWALL DMZ VPN] --> L3
    L3[LAYER 3 - INTERNAL NETWORK - SEGMENTATION NAC] --> L4
    L4[LAYER 4 - HOST SECURITY - ANTIVIRUS PATCHING HIDS] --> L5
    L5[LAYER 5 - APPLICATION SECURITY - WAF AUTHENTICATION] --> L6
    L6[LAYER 6 - DATA SECURITY - ENCRYPTION DLP BACKUP]
    L6 --> ASSET[PROTECTED ASSET - DATA AND SERVICES]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Answer Scripts:** The onion model is *attacker-centric*. The attacker must peel off layer after layer; each layer costs them time, skill, and risk of detection. This is why *even if one layer is bypassed, the asset is still protected*.

## 4.3 Comparison Block — Traditional vs. Zero-Trust Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRADITIONAL[TRADITIONAL PERIMETER MODEL]
        T1[TRUSTED INTERNAL ZONE] --> T2[FIREWALL] --> T3[UNTRUSTED INTERNET]
    end
    subgraph ZEROTRUST[ZERO TRUST MODEL]
        Z1[POLICY DECISION POINT] --> Z2[POLICY ENFORCEMENT POINT]
        Z2 --> Z3[EVERY REQUEST VERIFIED]
        Z2 --> Z4[LEAST PRIVILEGE ACCESS]
        Z2 --> Z5[CONTINUOUS AUTHENTICATION]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the **Traditional Model**, anything inside the firewall is implicitly trusted — a major weakness exploited by insider threats and lateral movement after a perimeter breach. In the **Zero-Trust Model**, no request is trusted by default, regardless of network location — endorsed by NIST SP 800-207 and increasingly expected in KTU 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]
**Define Network Security Architecture. List any two design principles used while designing such an architecture.**

**Model Answer (Examiner-Expected Points):**

*Definition (2 marks):* Network Security Architecture is the structured arrangement of hardware, software, policies, and procedures designed to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data and network resources. It specifies the placement, interaction, and lifecycle of preventive, detective, and corrective controls in a layered (defense-in-depth) manner.

*Any two principles (1 mark for each, with one-line explanation):*
1. **Least Privilege** – A user or process is granted only the minimum access rights required to perform its task.
2. **Defense-in-Depth** – Multiple independent layers of controls are deployed so that the failure of one does not compromise the system.
3. *(Optional)* **Fail-Safe Defaults** – In case of failure, the system defaults to denying access rather than allowing it.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Series 1]
**Differentiate between IDS and IPS. State one deployment scenario for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | IDS (Intrusion Detection System) | IPS (Intrusion Prevention System) |
|--------|----------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Mode of Operation** | *Passive* — monitors traffic and generates alerts | *Active* — sits in-line and blocks malicious packets in real time |
| **Latency Introduced** | None (uses mirrored/SPAN traffic) | Slight (every packet must pass through it) |
| **Response** | Alerts the administrator; takes no direct action | Drops, resets, or rate-limits malicious traffic automatically |
| **Deployment** | Out-of-band, connected to a switch SPAN port | In-line, in the direct path of traffic flow |
| **Scenario** | Monitoring internal LAN for slow-scan reconnaissance | Protecting an e-commerce web server from SQL injection attacks |

*(3-mark distribution: 1 mark for the core distinction, 1 mark for IPS description, 1 mark for the deployment scenario.)*

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A. [14 Marks, KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **dual-firewall DMZ architecture** in detail. Draw a labeled block diagram and describe the function of each component.

#### Model Solution (Step-by-Step):

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** The dual-firewall DMZ is the *de-facto industry standard* for medium-to-large enterprise networks. It uses two firewalls in series to create a buffer zone for public-facing servers.

2. **Components (3 marks):**
   - **Edge Firewall (Front Firewall):** Positioned between the Internet Service Provider (ISP) router and the DMZ. Performs stateful packet inspection; drops unsolicited inbound traffic; allows outbound traffic from DMZ.
   - **DMZ (Demilitarized Zone):** A *screened subnet* hosting public servers — web server, mail server, DNS server. These servers must be reachable from the Internet but must not have direct access to the internal LAN.
   - **Back Firewall (Internal Firewall):** Positioned between the DMZ and the Internal LAN. Enforces stricter rules; permits only specific, business-justified traffic from DMZ to LAN (e.g., web server talking to the database on TCP 3306).
   - **Internal LAN:** Houses employee workstations, Active Directory, file servers, and the production database.
   - **IPS / SIEM / Honeypot (supporting components):** Provide in-depth monitoring.

3. **Why Two Firewalls? (2 marks):**
   - **Single point of failure elimination:** If one firewall is compromised or misconfigured, the other still protects the LAN.
   - **Policy separation:** The edge firewall focuses on Internet threats; the back firewall focuses on protecting the LAN from a compromised DMZ host. Different rule sets, different vendors possible.

4. **Diagram (1 mark):** *(Reproduce the Mermaid diagram from Section 4.1 in a hand-drawn style in the answer script.)*

**(b) [7 Marks]** A corporate network deploys three independent security controls with effectiveness 85%, 90%, and 95% respectively. Calculate the *combined effectiveness* of the architecture. Justify why the result supports the **defense-in-depth** principle.

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute individual failure rates.**

$$
P_{A,fail} = 1 - 0.85 = 0.15, \quad P_{B,fail} = 0.10, \quad P_{C,fail} = 0.05
$$

**Step 2 — Joint failure probability (independent events).**

$$
P_{all,fail} = 0.15 \times 0.10 \times 0.05 = 0.00075
$$

**Step 3 — Combined effectiveness.**

$$
E_{combined} = 1 - 0.00075 = 0.99925 = 99.925\%
$$

**Justification (2 marks):** Although no individual control exceeds 95% effectiveness, the *layered combination* blocks 99.925% of attacks — a 20× improvement over the best single control. This empirically demonstrates the multiplicative power of defense-in-depth: a single weak link does not doom the system.

---

### Question B. [14 Marks — Alternative Choice, KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **Traditional Perimeter Architecture** with **Zero-Trust Architecture**. Mention at least three contrasting points and list two technologies used in implementing Zero-Trust.

#### Model Solution:

| # | Aspect | Traditional Perimeter | Zero-Trust |
|---|--------|------------------------|------------|
| 1 | **Trust Model** | "Trust but verify" — once inside, the user/device is trusted | "Never trust, always verify" — every request is authenticated and authorized |
| 2 | **Network Segmentation** | Coarse — one big internal LAN | Micro-segmentation — per-workload, per-user policies |
| 3 | **Authentication** | One-time at the perimeter (e.g., VPN login) | Continuous, identity-aware, context-driven (device, location, behavior) |
| 4 | **Lateral Movement Risk** | High — once inside, attacker roams freely | Low — every internal hop is re-verified |
| 5 | **Compliance Fit** | Legacy frameworks | Modern — NIST SP 800-207, fits cloud-native and remote-work models |

**Zero-Trust Enabler Technologies (2 marks):**
1. **Software-Defined Perimeter (SDP)** — dynamically creates per-user, per-application encrypted tunnels.
2. **Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) / BeyondCorp** — Google’s model where access decisions are based on user + device identity, not network location.
3. *(Optional)* **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) + Device Posture Assessment** via NAC.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define the following with one example each: (i) **DMZ**, (ii) **VPN**, (iii) **Honeypot**, (iv) **NAC**, (v) **SIEM**, (vi) **WAF**, (vii) **DLP**.

#### Model Solution (1 mark per item — concise but complete):

1. **DMZ (Demilitarized Zone):** A buffered subnetwork between two firewalls that hosts public-facing servers. *Example:* hosting a corporate website at 203.0.113.10 on a screened subnet.
2. **VPN (Virtual Private Network):** An encrypted tunnel over a public network that provides confidentiality and integrity for remote users. *Example:* an employee connecting to the office via IPsec VPN.
3. **Honeypot:** A decoy system designed to attract and study attackers without putting real assets at risk. *Example:* a fake FTP server in VLAN 99 that logs any access attempt.
4. **NAC (Network Access Control):** A policy enforcement layer that checks device posture and user identity before granting network admission. *Example:* Cisco ISE denying a non-corporate laptop from joining the LAN.
5. **SIEM (Security Information and Event Management):** A platform that aggregates, correlates, and analyzes logs from multiple sources in real time. *Example:* Splunk correlating firewall and AD logs to detect brute-force attempts.
6. **WAF (Web Application Firewall):** A specialized firewall that filters HTTP/HTTPS traffic to defend against application-layer attacks. *Example:* ModSecurity blocking SQL injection attempts on a login form.
7. **DLP (Data Loss Prevention):** A set of tools and policies that prevent sensitive data from leaving the corporate network. *Example:* blocking an email attachment containing a credit card number.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Network Security Architecture Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "Justify" Part:** A 7-mark question that says *"...justify your design"* loses 2 marks if you only draw the diagram. Always add a *why* paragraph: "The back firewall is placed here **because** a compromised DMZ server must not be able to reach the database directly."
> 2. **Confusing IDS with IPS:** Many students write them as synonyms. They are *not* — IDS is *passive and alerting*, IPS is *active and blocking*. Examiners will deduct 1 mark for any ambiguity.
> 3. **Skipping the Formula Steps:** In numerical sub-parts, do *not* jump to the final answer. Show $1 - 0.85 = 0.15$ explicitly. Skipping steps is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Treating Zero-Trust as a Product:** Zero-Trust is an *architectural philosophy*, not a single product. Writing "We will deploy Zero-Trust using firewall X" will cost you 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Drawing Without Labels:** A Mermaid-style diagram in your answer script *must* be hand-drawn with **clear labels** on every box and arrow. An unlabeled diagram is worth ≤ 1 mark out of the marks allocated to it.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Network Security Architecture = Layered (defense-in-depth) arrangement** of preventive, detective, and corrective controls.
- [x] **Three Axes of Classification:** Function, Layer, Form — know the difference.
- [x] **Seven Design Principles:** Least Privilege, Defense-in-Depth, Fail-Safe Defaults, Separation of Duties, Complete Mediation, Open Design, Economy of Mechanism.
- [x] **DMZ Architecture:** Edge Firewall → DMZ → Back Firewall → Internal LAN (industry standard).
- [x] **IDS vs IPS:** Passive-alert vs Active-block; out-of-band vs in-line.
- [x] **Zero-Trust:** A philosophy — *never trust, always verify* — enabled by SDP, IAP, MFA, micro-segmentation.
- [x] **Key Formulas:** $E_{combined} = 1 - \prod(1 - E_i)$, $R_{parallel} = 1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$, $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.
- [x] **Component Acronyms:** DMZ, VPN, NAC, SIEM, WAF, DLP, IDS, IPS, SDP, IAP — memorize the full forms.
- [x] **STRIDE Threats:** Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege.
- [x] **Examiner Mindset:** Always *justify placement*, *show formula steps*, and *label diagrams*.
- [x] **Real-world anchor:** The architecture you draw in the exam should resemble a real corporate topology (e.g., a bank, a hospital, a university) — examiners reward contextual answers.
- [x] **NIST Reference (allowed in exam):** NIST SP 800-207 (Zero Trust), SP 800-53 (Control Catalog) — names only, no page numbers needed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Encryption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST744 - INFORMATION SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Encryption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Encryption

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Encryption** is the cryptographic process of transforming readable plaintext (P) into an unreadable format known as ciphertext (C) using a mathematical algorithm (cipher) and a secret key (K). The inverse operation, **Decryption**, recovers the original plaintext from the ciphertext using the appropriate key.

The fundamental encryption function is represented as:

$$C = E_K(P)$$

And the corresponding decryption function is represented as:

$$P = D_K(C)$$

Where:
- **E** = Encryption Algorithm
- **D** = Decryption Algorithm
- **K** = Cryptographic Key
- **P** = Plaintext
- **C** = Ciphertext

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a Secure Locked Mailbox:**
> - You have a physical letter (the **Plaintext**) that you want to send to your friend.
> - You place the letter inside a locked steel box (the **Encryption** process).
> - Only your friend has the matching key to open the box and read the letter (the **Decryption** process).
> - Even if a thief intercepts the box during transit, they cannot read the letter because they lack the key.

In this analogy, the **box is the cipher (algorithm)**, the **letter is the plaintext**, the **locked steel box with scrambled contents is the ciphertext**, and the **physical key is the cryptographic key**. The strength of encryption depends not just on the box, but primarily on the secrecy and complexity of the key.

## 1.3 Classification of Encryption (KTU Syllabus Scope)

Encryption is broadly classified into three primary paradigms, all of which fall under the KTU Module 4 syllabus:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of Encryption:**
> 1. **Symmetric Key Encryption (Secret Key Cryptography):** The same key is used for both encryption and decryption.
> 2. **Asymmetric Key Encryption (Public Key Cryptography):** A pair of mathematically related keys (public and private) is used.
> 3. **Hash Functions (One-Way Encryption):** A mathematical function that converts data into a fixed-size string without any key (irreversible).

## 1.4 Core Security Objectives of Encryption

Encryption is implemented to satisfy the **CIA Triad** of Information Security:

- **Confidentiality:** Ensures that data is accessible only to authorized parties.
- **Integrity:** Ensures that data has not been tampered with during transit.
- **Authentication:** Verifies the identity of the sender or receiver.
- **Non-Repudiation:** Prevents the sender from denying having sent the message.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Symmetric Key Encryption — Operational Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> In **Symmetric Encryption**, both the sender and receiver share **a single secret key** (K). This key must be exchanged over a secure channel before communication begins.

### Key Logic Steps:
1. **Key Agreement:** Both parties securely exchange a shared secret key K beforehand.
2. **Encryption:** Sender applies algorithm E with key K to plaintext P, producing ciphertext C.
3. **Transmission:** Ciphertext C is transmitted over an insecure public network.
4. **Decryption:** Receiver applies algorithm D with the same key K to recover plaintext P.
5. **Security:** The algorithm E is publicly known, but security rests entirely on the secrecy of K.

### Symmetric Cipher Sub-Categories:

| Sub-Category | Description | Example Algorithm | Block Size |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Stream Ciphers** | Encrypts one bit/byte at a time using a keystream | RC4, ChaCha20 | 1 bit/byte |
| **Block Ciphers** | Encrypts fixed-size blocks of data | DES, 3DES, AES, Blowfish | 64 or 128 bits |

## 2.2 Asymmetric Key Encryption — Operational Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> In **Asymmetric Encryption**, each user possesses a **key pair**: a **Public Key** (published openly) and a **Private Key** (kept secret). The keys are mathematically linked but it is computationally infeasible to derive the private key from the public key.

### Key Logic Steps:
1. **Key Generation:** User generates a public key (KU) and private key (KR) pair.
2. **Key Distribution:** Public key KU is published in a digital certificate or directory.
3. **Encryption:** Sender encrypts plaintext P using the receiver's public key KU: C = E_KU(P).
4. **Transmission:** Ciphertext C is sent to the receiver.
5. **Decryption:** Receiver decrypts using their private key KR: P = D_KR(C).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes all critical formulas and parameters for encryption algorithms frequently asked in KTU examinations.

| Algorithm | Type | Key Size | Block Size | Core Mathematical Operation | KTU Exam Frequency |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|
| **Caesar Cipher** | Classical Symmetric | 26 (shift value) | 1 character | Modular addition/subtraction | Very High |
| **Vigenère Cipher** | Classical Symmetric | Keyword length | 1 character | Polyalphabetic substitution | High |
| **DES** | Modern Symmetric (Block) | 56 bits (effective) | 64 bits | Feistel Network with 16 rounds | Very High |
| **3DES (Triple DES)** | Modern Symmetric (Block) | 168 bits (effective) | 64 bits | DES applied 3 times | High |
| **AES** | Modern Symmetric (Block) | 128/192/256 bits | 128 bits | Substitution-Permutation Network | Very High |
| **RSA** | Asymmetric | ≥ 2048 bits | Variable | Modular exponentiation | Very High |
| **Diffie-Hellman** | Key Exchange | Variable | N/A | Discrete logarithm problem | Very High |
| **ECC** | Asymmetric | 160-256 bits | Variable | Elliptic curve point multiplication | Medium |
| **SHA-256** | Hash Function | No key | 512-bit input | Merkle-Damgård construction | High |

### Critical Mathematical Formulas:

For **Caesar Cipher** (shift cipher with key $k$):

$$C = (P + k) \mod 26$$

$$P = (C - k) \mod 26$$

For **RSA Encryption and Decryption**:

$$C = M^e \mod n$$

$$M = C^d \mod n$$

Where $n = p \times q$, and $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \mod \phi(n)$, with $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.

For **Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange** (shared secret $K_{AB}$):

$$K_{AB} = (\alpha^b \mod p)^a \mod p = \alpha^{ab} \mod p$$

For **AES Round Operations** (each round):

$$\text{State}_{i+1} = \text{MixColumns}(\text{ShiftRows}(\text{SubBytes}(\text{State}_i))) \oplus \text{RoundKey}_i$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking (ATMs, SWIFT):** Uses 3DES and AES for transaction security.
- **HTTPS/SSL/TLS:** Uses RSA for key exchange and AES for bulk data encryption.
- **Digital Signatures:** RSA + SHA-256 for code signing and document authentication.
- **VPNs and Wi-Fi (WPA3):** AES-256 with pre-shared keys.
- **Blockchain:** ECDSA (Elliptic Curve DSA) for transaction signing.
- **Disk Encryption (BitLocker, FileVault):** AES-128/256 in XTS mode.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: RSA Encryption-Decryption (Full Derivation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Encrypt the message **M = 7** using RSA, given $p = 11$, $q = 13$, and choose $e = 7$. Then decrypt to recover M.

### Step 1: Compute the modulus n

$$n = p \times q = 11 \times 13 = 143$$

### Step 2: Compute Euler's Totient Function $\phi(n)$

Since $p$ and $q$ are prime, we use:

$$\phi(n) = (p - 1) \times (q - 1)$$

Substituting the values:

$$\phi(n) = (11 - 1) \times (13 - 1) = 10 \times 12 = 120$$

### Step 3: Verify the Public Exponent e

The public exponent $e$ must satisfy:

$$\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$$

Checking:

$$\gcd(7, 120) = 1$$

This confirms that 7 is a valid public exponent.

### Step 4: Compute the Private Exponent d

The private exponent $d$ is the modular multiplicative inverse of $e$ modulo $\phi(n)$:

$$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \mod \phi(n)$$

$$7 \cdot d \equiv 1 \mod 120$$

Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm, we find:

$$d = 103$$

Verification:

$$7 \times 103 = 721$$

$$721 \mod 120 = 721 - 6 \times 120 = 721 - 720 = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 5: Encryption

The public key is $(e, n) = (7, 143)$. Encryption is performed as:

$$C = M^e \mod n = 7^7 \mod 143$$

Computing step-by-step:

$$7^2 = 49$$

$$7^3 = 7^2 \times 7 = 49 \times 7 = 343 = 2 \times 143 + 57 = 343 \mod 143 = 57$$

$$7^4 = 7^3 \times 7 = 57 \times 7 = 399 = 2 \times 143 + 113 = 399 \mod 143 = 113$$

$$7^5 = 7^4 \times 7 = 113 \times 7 = 791 = 5 \times 143 + 76 = 791 \mod 143 = 76$$

$$7^6 = 7^5 \times 7 = 76 \times 7 = 532 = 3 \times 143 + 103 = 532 \mod 143 = 103$$

$$7^7 = 7^6 \times 7 = 103 \times 7 = 721 = 5 \times 143 + 6 = 721 \mod 143 = 6$$

Therefore:

$$C = 6$$

### Step 6: Decryption

The private key is $(d, n) = (103, 143)$. Decryption is performed as:

$$M = C^d \mod n = 6^{103} \mod 143$$

Using the Chinese Remainder Theorem with $p = 11$ and $q = 13$:

**Modulo 11:**

$$6^{103} \mod 11$$

By Fermat's Little Theorem, $6^{10} \equiv 1 \mod 11$, so:

$$103 = 10 \times 10 + 3 \implies 6^{103} \equiv (6^{10})^{10} \times 6^3 \equiv 1 \times 216 \equiv 216 \mod 11$$

$$216 = 19 \times 11 + 7 \implies 216 \mod 11 = 7$$

**Modulo 13:**

$$6^{103} \mod 13$$

By Fermat's Little Theorem, $6^{12} \equiv 1 \mod 13$, so:

$$103 = 12 \times 8 + 7 \implies 6^{103} \equiv (6^{12})^8 \times 6^7 \equiv 6^7 \mod 13$$

Computing:

$$6^2 = 36 \equiv 10 \mod 13$$

$$6^3 = 6^2 \times 6 \equiv 10 \times 6 = 60 \equiv 8 \mod 13$$

$$6^4 = 6^3 \times 6 \equiv 8 \times 6 = 48 \equiv 9 \mod 13$$

$$6^5 = 6^4 \times 6 \equiv 9 \times 6 = 54 \equiv 2 \mod 13$$

$$6^6 = 6^5 \times 6 \equiv 2 \times 6 = 12 \mod 13$$

$$6^7 = 6^6 \times 6 \equiv 12 \times 6 = 72 \equiv 7 \mod 13$$

**Applying CRT (Chinese Remainder Theorem):**

We need $M$ such that:

$$M \equiv 7 \mod 11 \quad \text{and} \quad M \equiv 7 \mod 13$$

Since $M \equiv 7$ satisfies both, and $7 < 143$:

$$M = 7 \quad \checkmark$$

The original plaintext is successfully recovered.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Alice and Bob agree on prime $p = 23$ and primitive root $\alpha = 5$. Alice chooses secret $a = 6$ and Bob chooses secret $b = 15$. Compute the shared secret key.

### Step 1: Compute Public Values

Alice's public value:

$$A = \alpha^a \mod p = 5^6 \mod 23$$

Computing:

$$5^2 = 25 \equiv 2 \mod 23$$

$$5^3 = 5^2 \times 5 \equiv 2 \times 5 = 10 \mod 23$$

$$5^6 = (5^3)^2 \equiv 10^2 = 100 \equiv 100 - 4 \times 23 = 8 \mod 23$$

So $A = 8$.

Bob's public value:

$$B = \alpha^b \mod p = 5^{15} \mod 23$$

Computing:

$$5^{15} = 5^{12} \times 5^3$$

By Fermat's Little Theorem, $5^{22} \equiv 1 \mod 23$, so we simplify. Alternatively:

$$5^{12} \mod 23$$: Since $5^{11} \equiv 1 \mod 23$ (by Fermat's), $5^{12} \equiv 5 \mod 23$.

$$5^{15} = 5^{12} \times 5^3 \equiv 5 \times 10 = 50 \equiv 50 - 2 \times 23 = 4 \mod 23$$

So $B = 4$.

### Step 2: Exchange Public Values

Alice sends $A = 8$ to Bob, and Bob sends $B = 4$ to Alice.

### Step 3: Compute Shared Secret

Alice computes:

$$K_{AB} = B^a \mod p = 4^6 \mod 23$$

$$4^2 = 16$$

$$4^3 = 64 \equiv 64 - 2 \times 23 = 18 \mod 23$$

$$4^6 = (4^3)^2 \equiv 18^2 = 324 \equiv 324 - 14 \times 23 = 324 - 322 = 2 \mod 23$$

Bob computes:

$$K_{AB} = A^b \mod p = 8^{15} \mod 23$$

$$8^2 = 64 \equiv 18 \mod 23$$

$$8^4 = 18^2 = 324 \equiv 2 \mod 23$$

$$8^8 = 2^2 = 4 \mod 23$$

$$8^{15} = 8^8 \times 8^4 \times 8^2 \times 8^1 = 4 \times 2 \times 18 \times 8$$

$$= 4 \times 2 = 8$$

$$8 \times 18 = 144 \equiv 144 - 6 \times 23 = 144 - 138 = 6 \mod 23$$

$$6 \times 8 = 48 \equiv 48 - 2 \times 23 = 2 \mod 23$$

Both arrive at:

$$K_{AB} = 2 \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Symmetric vs. Asymmetric Encryption

| Parameter | Symmetric Encryption | Asymmetric Encryption |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Keys Used** | Single shared secret key | Public + Private key pair |
| **Key Distribution** | Difficult (requires secure channel) | Easy (public key is published) |
| **Speed** | Very fast (1000x faster) | Slow (heavy computation) |
| **Scalability** | Poor (n² keys for n users) | Excellent (2n keys for n users) |
| **Primary Use** | Bulk data encryption | Key exchange, digital signatures |
| **Algorithms** | AES, DES, 3DES, ChaCha20 | RSA, ECC, ElGamal |
| **Key Length** | 128-256 bits | 2048-4096 bits (RSA), 256-512 bits (ECC) |
| **Confidentiality** | Yes | Yes |
| **Authentication** | No (natively) | Yes (via digital signatures) |
| **Non-Repudiation** | No | Yes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Symmetric Encryption Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Plaintext P] --> nodeB[Encryption Algorithm E with Key K]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Ciphertext C]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Insecure Channel]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Decryption Algorithm D with Key K]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Plaintext P Recovered]
    nodeG[Sender] -.-> nodeA
    nodeH[Receiver] -.-> nodeF
```

## 4.2 Asymmetric Encryption Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Sender] --> nodeB[Lookup Receiver Public Key]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Encrypt with Receiver Public Key KU]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Transmit Ciphertext C]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Receiver Decrypts with Private Key KR]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Original Plaintext Recovered]
    nodeG[Key Pair Generator] --> nodeH[Public Key KU]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Private Key KR]
    nodeH -.-> nodeB
```

## 4.3 RSA Operational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Step 1 Select two large primes p and q] --> step2[Step 2 Compute n = p times q]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3 Compute phi n = p-1 times q-1]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4 Choose e such that gcd e phi n = 1]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5 Compute d such that e times d = 1 mod phi n]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6 Public Key = e n Private Key = d n]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7 Encrypt C = M to the power e mod n]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8 Decrypt M = C to the power d mod n]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Hybrid Encryption (TLS Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph senderSide[Sender Side]
        appA[Application Data] --> symA[Symmetric AES Encryption]
        keyA[Random AES Session Key] --> symA
        symA --> cipherA[AES Encrypted Data]
        keyA --> rsaA[RSA Encrypt Session Key with Receiver Public Key]
        rsaA --> keyCipherA[Encrypted Session Key]
    end
    cipherA --> net[Insecure Network Transmission]
    keyCipherA --> net
    net --> subgraph receiverSide[Receiver Side]
        rsaB[RSA Decrypt with Receiver Private Key] --> keyB[Recovered AES Session Key]
        cipherB[Receive AES Encrypted Data] --> symB[Symmetric AES Decryption]
        keyB --> symB
        symB --> appB[Decrypted Application Data]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This hybrid model is the architectural backbone of **TLS/SSL** (used in HTTPS). RSA securely transmits a randomly generated AES session key, and then AES handles the high-speed bulk data encryption. This combines the **key distribution advantage of RSA** with the **speed advantage of AES**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Encryption. Differentiate between Symmetric and Asymmetric Encryption.
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Model Answer:**

**Encryption** is the process of converting plaintext into ciphertext using a mathematical algorithm and a key, such that unauthorized parties cannot read the original message.

| Parameter | Symmetric Encryption | Asymmetric Encryption |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Key Count** | Single shared key | Key pair (public + private) |
| **Speed** | Fast | Slow |
| **Key Distribution** | Complex | Simple (public key published) |
| **Example** | AES, DES | RSA, ECC |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for symmetric distinction + 1 for asymmetric distinction)*

### Question 2: What is the difference between a Block Cipher and a Stream Cipher?
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer:**

- **Block Cipher:** Encrypts data in fixed-size blocks (e.g., 64-bit or 128-bit blocks). Operates on the entire block at once using modes like ECB, CBC, or CTR. Example: **AES** (128-bit blocks).
- **Stream Cipher:** Encrypts data one bit or one byte at a time by generating a pseudo-random keystream that is XORed with the plaintext. Example: **RC4, ChaCha20**.

Block ciphers offer stronger diffusion and are preferred for bulk data, while stream ciphers excel in low-latency applications like voice or video streaming.

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks each for block vs. stream)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A: Detailed Analysis of RSA and DES Algorithms
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Explain the RSA Algorithm with a Suitable Example

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Key Generation**

1. Select two distinct large primes $p$ and $q$.
2. Compute $n = p \times q$.
3. Compute Euler's totient: $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.
4. Choose public exponent $e$ such that $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.
5. Compute private exponent $d$ such that $e \times d \equiv 1 \mod \phi(n)$.

**Step 2: Encryption and Decryption Formulas**

$$C = M^e \mod n$$

$$M = C^d \mod n$$

**Step 3: Numerical Example**

Let $p = 17$, $q = 11$, and $e = 7$.

- $n = 17 \times 11 = 187$
- $\phi(n) = 16 \times 10 = 160$
- Find $d$ such that $7d \equiv 1 \mod 160$. Using Extended Euclidean: $d = 23$.

Let plaintext $M = 88$:

$$C = 88^7 \mod 187$$

Computing modularly: $88^2 = 7744 \mod 187$. Since $187 \times 41 = 7667$, $88^2 \equiv 77 \mod 187$.

$88^4 \equiv 77^2 = 5929 \mod 187$. Since $187 \times 31 = 5797$, $88^4 \equiv 132 \mod 187$.

$88^7 = 88^4 \times 88^2 \times 88 = 132 \times 77 \times 88 \mod 187$.

$132 \times 77 = 10164$. $10164 \mod 187 = 10164 - 54 \times 187 = 10164 - 10098 = 66$.

$66 \times 88 = 5808$. $5808 \mod 187 = 5808 - 31 \times 187 = 5808 - 5797 = 11$.

So $C = 11$.

**Decryption:** $M = 11^{23} \mod 187 = 88$ (recovered correctly).

*(7 marks: 2 for algorithm steps, 2 for formulas, 3 for numerical example)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Explain the DES Algorithm in Detail with its Structure

**Model Solution:**

**Data Encryption Standard (DES)** is a symmetric block cipher developed by IBM and adopted as a federal standard in 1977.

**Key Parameters:**
- **Block size:** 64 bits
- **Key size:** 64 bits (56 bits effective + 8 parity bits)
- **Rounds:** 16 Feistel rounds

**DES Structure:**

**Step 1: Initial Permutation (IP)** — The 64-bit plaintext is permuted according to a fixed table.

**Step 2: 16 Feistel Rounds** — The block is split into two 32-bit halves: Left (L) and Right (R). In each round:

$$L_i = R_{i-1}$$

$$R_i = L_{i-1} \oplus f(R_{i-1}, K_i)$$

Where $f$ is the round function consisting of:
- **Expansion (E-box):** Expands 32-bit R to 48 bits.
- **Key Mixing:** XOR with the 48-bit round subkey $K_i$.
- **Substitution (S-boxes):** Eight S-boxes reduce 48 bits back to 32 bits.
- **Permutation (P-box):** Permutes the 32-bit output for diffusion.

**Step 3: Key Schedule** — Generates 16 different 48-bit subkeys from the original 56-bit key using shifting and compression.

**Step 4: Final Permutation (IP⁻¹)** — The inverse of the initial permutation produces the 64-bit ciphertext.

**Decryption** uses the same algorithm with subkeys applied in reverse order (K₁₆ to K₁).

**Weakness:** The 56-bit key is vulnerable to brute-force attacks; hence 3DES and AES replaced DES.

*(7 marks: 1.5 for parameters, 3 for Feistel structure, 1.5 for key schedule, 1 for weakness)*

---

### Question B: Alternative Choice — AES and Diffie-Hellman
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Describe the AES Algorithm with its Transformation Steps

**Model Solution:**

**Advanced Encryption Standard (AES)** is a symmetric block cipher standardized by NIST in 2001 (Rijndael cipher).

**Key Parameters:**
- **Block size:** 128 bits (fixed)
- **Key sizes:** 128, 192, or 256 bits
- **Rounds:** 10, 12, or 14 (depending on key size)

**AES State Representation:** The 128-bit block is organized as a $4 \times 4$ matrix of bytes called the **State**.

**Round Transformations:**

1. **SubBytes (Substitution):** Each byte of the State is replaced using a non-linear 16×16 S-box (S-Box from $\mathbb{GF}(2^8)$).
2. **ShiftRows:** Rows of the State are cyclically shifted left: Row 0 unchanged, Row 1 shifted by 1, Row 2 by 2, Row 3 by 3.
3. **MixColumns:** Each column is transformed via matrix multiplication over $\mathbb{GF}(2^8)$ with a fixed MDS matrix to provide diffusion.
4. **AddRoundKey:** The State is XORed with the round subkey derived from the key schedule.

**Key Schedule:** Uses the Rijndael key expansion involving RotWord, SubWord, and XOR with Rcon constants to generate round subkeys.

**Final Round (Round 10 for AES-128):** MixColumns is omitted; only SubBytes → ShiftRows → AddRoundKey is performed.

**Decryption** uses inverse transformations: InvSubBytes, InvShiftRows, InvMixColumns, and AddRoundKey with subkeys in reverse order.

**Strength:** No known practical attack against full AES. AES-128 is the global standard for government and commercial encryption.

*(7 marks: 1.5 for parameters, 3 for the four transformations, 1.5 for key schedule, 1 for decryption note)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Explain the Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange Algorithm with an Example

**Model Solution:**

**Diffie-Hellman (DH)** is a public-key protocol that allows two parties to establish a shared secret over an insecure channel without prior shared information.

**Step 1: Public Parameters**

Alice and Bob publicly agree on:
- A large prime number $p$
- A primitive root $\alpha$ (generator of the multiplicative group modulo $p$)

**Step 2: Private Key Selection**
- Alice chooses secret integer $a$
- Bob chooses secret integer $b$

**Step 3: Public Key Computation**
- Alice computes: $A = \alpha^a \mod p$
- Bob computes: $B = \alpha^b \mod p$

**Step 4: Exchange**
Alice and Bob exchange $A$ and $B$ over the public channel.

**Step 5: Shared Secret Computation**
- Alice computes: $K_{AB} = B^a \mod p = \alpha^{ab} \mod p$
- Bob computes: $K_{AB} = A^b \mod p = \alpha^{ab} \mod p$

Both obtain the same shared secret $K_{AB}$.

**Numerical Example:**

Let $p = 23$, $\alpha = 5$, $a = 6$, $b = 15$.

- $A = 5^6 \mod 23 = 8$
- $B = 5^{15} \mod 23 = 4$
- $K_{AB} = 4^6 \mod 23 = 8^{15} \mod 23 = 2$

**Security:** Based on the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** — computing $a$ from $A = \alpha^a \mod p$ is computationally infeasible for large primes.

**Vulnerability:** Plain DH is susceptible to **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attacks** if public values are not authenticated. Mitigated by **authenticated DH (e.g., DH-EKE, STS protocol)** or use of **digital certificates**.

*(7 marks: 1.5 for protocol steps, 2 for formula, 2 for example, 1.5 for security analysis)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing Encryption and Encoding:** Encoding (e.g., Base64) is NOT encryption; it provides NO security. Students often lose 1-2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the Modulo in RSA:** Writing $C = M^e$ without $\mod n$ will cost full marks. Always include "mod n" explicitly.
> 3. **Skipping gcd verification in RSA:** Failing to check $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ will lead to an invalid key; examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up DES rounds:** DES has **16 Feistel rounds**; AES-128 has **10 rounds**. Confusing these is a frequent error.
> 5. **Forgetting decryption formula:** For every encryption formula, the examiner expects the matching decryption formula in Part (b).
> 6. **Diffie-Hellman discrete log confusion:** Students sometimes confuse the DLP with the factoring problem. DLP is the basis of DH/ElGamal; Integer Factorization is the basis of RSA.
> 7. **Not mentioning MITM vulnerability in DH:** A complete DH answer must address the Man-in-the-Middle attack to score full marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Encryption** transforms plaintext to ciphertext; **Decryption** is the reverse.
- **Symmetric Encryption** uses one shared key; **Asymmetric** uses a public-private key pair.
- **DES:** 64-bit block, 56-bit key, 16 Feistel rounds — **deprecated**.
- **AES:** 128-bit block, 128/192/256-bit keys, 10/12/14 rounds — **NIST standard**.
- **RSA** security relies on the **integer factorization problem**: $n = p \times q$.
- **RSA formulas:** $C = M^e \mod n$ and $M = C^d \mod n$.
- **Diffie-Hellman** security relies on the **discrete logarithm problem**.
- **DH shared secret:** $K_{AB} = \alpha^{ab} \mod p$ (computed by both parties).
- **Block ciphers** operate on fixed-size blocks; **Stream ciphers** encrypt bit-by-bit.
- **Hybrid encryption** (used in TLS): RSA for key exchange + AES for data encryption.
- **Cryptographic Hash Functions** (SHA-256) provide integrity, not confidentiality.
- **Key length rule:** Longer keys = stronger encryption (but slower performance).
- **Asymmetric is 100-1000x slower** than symmetric; hence hybrid models are preferred.
- **Public keys can be freely distributed**, but **private keys must never be shared**.
- **MITM attacks** threaten unauthenticated key exchange — always use authenticated protocols.
- **Non-repudiation** is achieved only by asymmetric cryptography (digital signatures).
- **ECB mode** is insecure; **CBC/GCM modes** are recommended for block ciphers.
- **Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)** uses ephemeral DH (e.g., DHE, ECDHE) for each session.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Content Integrity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST744 - INFORMATION SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Content Integrity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Content Integrity?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Content Integrity (Data Integrity)** is the property that information, data, or a message has **not been modified or altered** in an unauthorized or undetected manner during storage, processing, or transmission. It is one of the three pillars of the **CIA Triad** in information security (alongside **Confidentiality** and **Availability**).

In the formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology:

> **Content Integrity** guarantees that the *content* of a message $M$ received by Bob is *exactly* the same as the *content* sent by Alice — no bit has been inserted, deleted, reordered, or modified by an active adversary (Mallory).

Mathematically, if Alice sends message $M$ and Bob receives $M'$, then:
$$\text{Integrity Verified} \iff M \equiv M'$$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tamper-Evident Sealed Letter"

Imagine Alice writes a letter, places it inside a special envelope, and presses a **unique wax seal** unique to that exact letter on the back. She then hands the envelope to a courier.

- The courier (or any malicious party — *Mallory*) might try to:
  1. **Open the envelope** and change the letter inside.
  2. **Swap** the letter with a forged one.
  3. **Replicate** the seal (if it is weak).
- When Bob receives the envelope, he **inspects the wax seal**. If the seal:
  - Is **intact and matches** the unique pattern Alice created → ✅ *Content is intact.*
  - Is **broken, missing, or mismatched** → ❌ *Content has been tampered with.*

In cryptography, the **wax seal** is replaced by a **cryptographic fingerprint (hash)** or a **Message Authentication Code (MAC)** generated using a secret key. Any single bit change in the message produces a completely different fingerprint — this is the *avalanche effect*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU 2024 — Frequently Tested):**
> - **Content Integrity** → "Has the message been *changed*?"
> - **Authentication** → "Did the message really come from *the claimed sender*?"
> - **Confidentiality** → "Can *unauthorized* parties read the message?"
>
> These three goals are **independent** but often achieved together by a single cryptographic construction.

---

## 1.3 Integrity vs. Confidentiality — Why Both Are Needed

| Property | Goal | Tool (Typical) | Defeats Which Attack? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Confidentiality** | Hide content from eavesdroppers | Symmetric/Asymmetric Encryption | *Passive* eavesdropping (Eve) |
| **Content Integrity** | Detect any modification | Hash, MAC, Digital Signature | *Active* tampering (Mallory) |
| **Authentication** | Verify sender identity | MAC, Digital Signature | *Impersonation* / Spoofing |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often claim that *encryption alone* provides integrity. **This is false.** Encryption hides content but does NOT prevent an attacker from flipping encrypted bits, which would silently corrupt the plaintext on decryption. Integrity is a *separate* cryptographic service that must be explicitly added.

---

## 1.4 Threat Model for Content Integrity

The adversary **Mallory** is an *active* attacker who can:

1. **Intercept** a legitimate message on the channel.
2. **Modify** the message (bit-flipping, truncation, insertion).
3. **Replay** a previously valid message.
4. **Forge** a brand-new message and claim it came from Alice.

Bob must be able to detect **all** such manipulations with overwhelming probability.

---

## 1.5 Three Primary Mechanisms for Content Integrity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 syllabus groups content integrity mechanisms into **three categories** — students must know the differences:

1. **Hash Functions** (e.g., MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, SHA-3) → Produce a fixed-length *fingerprint* (digest) of variable-length data. Provides **unkeyed integrity**.
2. **Message Authentication Codes (MAC)** (e.g., HMAC, CMAC) → A keyed hash that simultaneously provides **integrity + authentication**.
3. **Digital Signatures** (e.g., RSA-PSS, ECDSA, EdDSA) → A *public-key* mechanism providing **integrity + authentication + non-repudiation**.

The remainder of these notes develops each of these mechanisms in detail.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cryptographic Hash Functions — The Backbone of Integrity

A **cryptographic hash function** $H$ maps an input $M$ of *arbitrary length* to a fixed-length output $h$ called the **message digest** (or *hash value*):

$$H: \{0,1\}^{*} \longrightarrow \{0,1\}^{n}$$

where $n$ is the **output bit-length** of the hash (e.g., $n = 256$ for SHA-256).

### 2.1.1 The Three Mandatory Security Properties (KTU High-Yield)

For a hash function to be considered *cryptographically secure*, it **must** satisfy:

> [!NOTE]
> **Property 1 — Pre-image Resistance (One-Wayness):**
> Given a hash value $h = H(M)$, it must be **computationally infeasible** to find *any* message $M'$ such that $H(M') = h$.
>
> *Intuition:* You cannot "reverse-engineer" the input from the output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Property 2 — Second Pre-image Resistance (Weak Collision Resistance):**
> Given a specific message $M_1$, it must be **computationally infeasible** to find a *different* message $M_2 \neq M_1$ such that $H(M_2) = H(M_1)$.
>
> *Intuition:* Mallory cannot forge an alternate message that hashes to the same fingerprint as Alice's original.

> [!NOTE]
> **Property 3 — Collision Resistance (Strong Collision Resistance):**
> It must be **computationally infeasible** to find *any pair* $(M_1, M_2)$ with $M_1 \neq M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.
>
> *Intuition:* Mallory cannot find *any* two distinct inputs with the same fingerprint.

### 2.1.2 Additional Practical Properties

- **Determinism:** The same input $M$ *always* produces the same output $h$.
- **Fixed Output Length:** Output is always $n$ bits regardless of input size.
- **Avalanche Effect:** Changing *one bit* in $M$ changes approximately **50% of the bits** in the output $h$ (on average).
- **Efficient Computation:** $H(M)$ must be computable in polynomial time.

### 2.1.3 The Birthday Paradox & Collision Probability (Crucial for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Birthday Attack Bound:**
> To find a collision in an $n$-bit hash function, an attacker needs approximately:
> $$\boxed{\,\sqrt{2^{n}} = 2^{n/2}\,}$$
> trials (hash evaluations).

| Hash Function | Output $n$ | Pre-image Security | Collision Security |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **MD5** | 128 bits | $2^{128}$ | $2^{64}$ (broken) |
| **SHA-1** | 160 bits | $2^{160}$ | $2^{80}$ (theoretically broken) |
| **SHA-256** | 256 bits | $2^{256}$ | $2^{128}$ |
| **SHA-512** | 512 bits | $2^{512}$ | $2^{256}$ |
| **SHA-3-256** | 256 bits | $2^{256}$ | $2^{128}$ |
| **SHA-3-512** | 512 bits | $2^{512}$ | $2^{256}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write $2^{128}$ security for MD5. This is wrong! The *pre-image* security of MD5 is $2^{128}$, but its *collision* security is only $2^{64}$ — and **real attacks have broken it**. Always check whether the question asks for *pre-image* or *collision* resistance.

### 2.1.4 Merkle–Damgård Construction

Most legacy hashes (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2) use the **Merkle–Damgård** iterated construction:

1. **Padding:** Append a `1` bit, then enough `0` bits, then the 64-bit (or 128-bit) length of $M$ to make the total length a multiple of the block size $b$.
2. **Block Splitting:** Split the padded message into $L$ blocks: $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_L$, each of size $b$ bits.
3. **Iterated Compression:** Use a *compression function* $f$ and process each block:
   $$H_0 = IV \quad (\text{Initial Value, fixed constant})$$
   $$H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i), \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, L$$
4. **Output:** $H(M) = H_L$ (final chaining value).

> [!NOTE]
> The Merkle–Damgård theorem guarantees: *if* the compression function $f$ is collision-resistant, *then* the iterated hash $H$ is also collision-resistant. SHA-3 (Keccak) deliberately abandoned this design in favor of a *sponge construction*.

---

## 2.2 Message Authentication Codes (MAC)

A **Message Authentication Code** is a *keyed* hash function that provides both **integrity** and **authentication**:

$$\text{MAC}: \mathcal{K} \times \{0,1\}^{*} \longrightarrow \{0,1\}^{n}$$

It requires a **shared secret key** $K$ between Alice and Bob. The process is:

1. **Alice** computes the tag: $T = \text{MAC}_K(M)$.
2. Alice transmits $(M, T)$ to Bob.
3. **Bob** recomputes $\text{MAC}_K(M)$ using the *shared* key $K$.
4. If the recomputed tag equals $T$, Bob accepts the message.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A MAC guarantees both **integrity** (message not changed) AND **authentication** (sender knows the secret key). It does **NOT** provide **non-repudiation** because both parties share the same key — either could have produced the tag.

### 2.2.1 HMAC — The KTU-Mandated Construction

> [!NOTE]
> **HMAC (Hash-based MAC)** is the standard construction (RFC 2104) that turns *any* cryptographic hash function (e.g., SHA-256) into a secure MAC. The formal definition:
>
> $$\boxed{\,\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\!\left(\,(K \oplus opad) \,\Vert\, H\!\left(\,(K \oplus ipad) \,\Vert\, M\right)\,\right)\,}$$
>
> where:
> - $K$ = shared secret key (padded/hashed to block length $b$)
> - $ipad$ = inner pad = `0x36` repeated $b$ times
> - $opad$ = outer pad = `0x5C` repeated $b$ times
> - $\Vert$ = concatenation
> - $H$ = underlying hash (e.g., SHA-256)

**Why two pads?** The inner key ($K \oplus ipad$) and outer key ($K \oplus opad$) are distinct, preventing extension-style attacks on the underlying hash.

### 2.2.2 CMAC — For Block Ciphers

CMAC uses a block cipher (e.g., AES) instead of a hash, producing a tag of the block-cipher's block size (e.g., **128 bits for AES**). Useful in resource-constrained environments.

---

## 2.3 Digital Signatures — Public-Key Integrity

A **digital signature** is the public-key analog of a MAC. It uses **asymmetric cryptography**:

1. **Alice** signs $M$ with her *private key*: $\sigma = \text{Sign}_{SK_A}(H(M))$.
2. Alice transmits $(M, \sigma)$ to Bob.
3. **Bob** verifies using Alice's *public key*: $\text{Verify}_{PK_A}(H(M), \sigma) \in \{\text{valid, invalid}\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Digital Signatures provide THREE services simultaneously:**
> 1. **Content Integrity** (message not modified)
> 2. **Authentication** (sender is who they claim)
> 3. **Non-Repudiation** (sender cannot later deny signing — only they hold the private key)

### 2.3.1 Signature Schemes (KTU Syllabus)

| Scheme | Underlying Problem | Signature Size | KTU Status |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **RSA-PSS** | Integer Factorization | $\geq 2048$ bits | Standard |
| **DSA** | Discrete Logarithm (mod $p$) | 320 bits | FIPS Standard |
| **ECDSA** | Elliptic Curve DLog | $\sim 2n$ bits ($n$ = curve bits) | Modern default |
| **EdDSA (Ed25519)** | Edwards Curve DLog | 512 bits | Fastest, modern |
| **ElGamal** | Discrete Logarithm | $\sim 2 \times \text{modulus}$ | Classical |

### 2.3.2 The "Hash-then-Sign" Paradigm

> [!NOTE]
> For efficiency, **never** sign $M$ directly. First hash it, then sign the digest:
> $$\sigma = \text{Sign}_{SK}(H(M))$$
> This reduces the signature size from $|M|$ to $|H(M)|$ + overhead, and leverages the collision resistance of $H$.

---

## 2.4 Comparison Matrix: MAC vs. Digital Signature

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparison appears almost every KTU semester exam — memorize it.**

| Property | MAC (e.g., HMAC) | Digital Signature (e.g., RSA) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Key type** | Symmetric (shared secret) | Asymmetric (public + private) |
| **Provides Integrity?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Provides Authentication?** | ✅ Yes (among key-sharers) | ✅ Yes (universal) |
| **Provides Non-Repudiation?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Verifier needs secret?** | Yes (shared key) | No (public key only) |
| **Speed** | Very fast | Slower (modular exponentiation) |
| **Typical use** | Internal APIs, TLS records, IPsec | Software signing, certificates, legal docs |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (One-Page Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it consolidates every formula/number you need for KTU 2024 board exams on Content Integrity.**

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Hash function signature | $H: \{0,1\}^{*} \to \{0,1\}^{n}$ | $n$ = digest size (bits) |
| Birthday attack trials | $2^{n/2}$ | e.g., $2^{128}$ for SHA-256 |
| Brute-force pre-image | $2^{n}$ | e.g., $2^{256}$ for SHA-256 |
| HMAC definition | $H((K \oplus opad) \Vert H((K \oplus ipad) \Vert M))$ | RFC 2104 |
| HMAC-SHA-256 output | $256$ bits | 32 bytes |
| HMAC-SHA-512 output | $512$ bits | 64 bytes |
| RSA signature size | $\geq 2048$ bits (key size) | FIPS 186-4 minimum |
| ECDSA-P256 signature | $2 \times 256 = 512$ bits | 64 bytes |
| SHA-256 block size | $512$ bits | Padding: $1 + 0\text{s} + 64$-bit length |
| MD5 output (legacy) | $128$ bits | **Deprecated — do not use** |
| SHA-1 output (legacy) | $160$ bits | **Deprecated since 2011** |
| Avalanche effect target | $\approx 50\%$ bits change | Per single input bit flip |
| Padding (Merkle–Damgård) | $M \Vert 1 \Vert 0^{k} \Vert \vert M \vert$ | $k$ = padding zeros |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Where is content integrity actually deployed in industry?**
>
> - **TLS 1.3 (HTTPS):** Uses HMAC-SHA-256 in the record layer to authenticate every transmitted record.
> - **Software Updates (e.g., Windows Update, apt, app stores):** Hash of the downloaded file is verified against a **digital signature** issued by the vendor.
> - **Git Version Control:** Each commit is identified by its **SHA-1/SHA-256 hash**, so any modification to source code is immediately detectable.
> - **Blockchain (Bitcoin, Ethereum):** Each block's hash chains to the previous — altering one block invalidates *all* subsequent blocks.
> - **Password Storage:** Systems store $\text{SHA-256}(\text{salt} \Vert \text{password})$ rather than the plaintext.
> - **Digital Certificates (X.509 / PKI):** The CA's digital signature on a certificate binds the public key to the identity.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **exhaustive derivations** the KTU 2024 board examiners love to ask. Every algebraic step is explicitly shown.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: The Birthday Paradox Bound for Hash Collisions

**Problem:** Estimate the number of hash evaluations required to find a collision in an $n$-bit hash function with probability $\geq 0.5$.

**Setup:**
- Let the hash output space have size $N = 2^{n}$.
- Suppose an attacker computes $k$ hash values: $h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_k$.
- The probability that **no collision** occurs is what we want to bound.

### Step 1: Probability of No Collision on the First Hash

The first hash value $h_1$ is unconstrained. Any value is fine.

$$P(\text{no collision after 1}) = 1$$

### Step 2: Probability of No Collision After Two Hashes

The second hash $h_2$ must differ from $h_1$. There are $N - 1$ "safe" choices out of $N$.

$$P(\text{no collision after 2}) = \frac{N - 1}{N}$$

### Step 3: Generalize to $k$ Hashes

For the $i$-th hash ($i \geq 2$), there are $N - (i - 1)$ safe values.

$$P(\text{no collision after } k) = \prod_{i=1}^{k-1} \frac{N - i}{N} = \prod_{i=1}^{k-1} \left(1 - \frac{i}{N}\right)$$

### Step 4: Apply the Taylor / Exponential Approximation

For small $x$, use $1 - x \approx e^{-x}$. Since $i \ll N$ in the regime we care about:

$$P(\text{no collision}) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{k-1} e^{-i/N} = e^{-\sum_{i=1}^{k-1} i/N}$$

### Step 5: Evaluate the Geometric Sum

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k-1} i = \frac{(k-1)k}{2} \approx \frac{k^2}{2} \quad \text{(for large } k\text{)}$$

Therefore:

$$P(\text{no collision}) \approx e^{-k^2 / (2N)}$$

### Step 6: Solve for 50% Collision Probability

We want $P(\text{collision}) \geq 0.5$, i.e., $P(\text{no collision}) \leq 0.5$:

$$e^{-k^2 / (2N)} = 0.5$$

Taking natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-\frac{k^2}{2N} = \ln(0.5) = -0.693$$

$$k^2 = 2N \cdot \ln(2) = 2N \cdot 0.693 \approx 1.386\, N$$

$$k \approx \sqrt{1.386\, N} \approx 1.177 \sqrt{N}$$

### Step 7: Final Result

$$\boxed{\,k \approx \sqrt{N} = \sqrt{2^{n}} = 2^{n/2}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** For a 128-bit hash (MD5), a collision can be found in roughly $2^{64}$ evaluations. For a 256-bit hash (SHA-256), the bound is $2^{128}$ — which is **computationally infeasible** today and for the foreseeable future.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: HMAC Security Reduction

**Problem:** Show that HMAC is a *secure PRF* (pseudorandom function) if the underlying compression function is a PRF.

**Setup:** Let $H$ be built from a Merkle–Damgård compression function $f$. The HMAC construction is:

$$\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H((K \oplus opad) \Vert H((K \oplus ipad) \Vert M))$$

### Step 1: Rewrite Using the Compression Function

The inner hash processes $M$ in blocks $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_L$, starting from the IV:

$$H_{inner} = f(f(\ldots f(IV, K \oplus ipad), M_1)\ldots, M_L)$$

Let $S = H_{inner}$ (the inner hash output, length $n$).

### Step 2: Outer Hash

The outer hash starts from IV and processes the two blocks $(K \oplus opad)$ and $S$:

$$\text{HMAC}_K(M) = f(f(IV, K \oplus opad), S)$$

### Step 3: Treat HMAC as a Nested PRF

Define the *inner keyed function*:

$$F_K(M) = f^{L}_{K \oplus ipad}(IV, M)$$

where $f^L$ is $L$ iterations of $f$. This is essentially $H(K \oplus ipad \Vert M)$.

### Step 4: Apply Bellare's Security Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bellare's Theorem (2006):** If the compression function $f$ is a *pseudorandom function*, and the key $K$ is uniformly random and independent of $M$, then:
> $$\text{HMAC}_K(\cdot) \text{ is a secure PRF.}$$
>
> The proof proceeds via the "indifferentiability" framework, showing that an attacker distinguishing HMAC outputs from random must break the PRF property of $f$.

### Step 5: Practical Implication

Because SHA-256's compression function is assumed to behave as a PRF, **HMAC-SHA-256** is provably secure (under standard assumptions) and is the recommended MAC in TLS 1.3, IPsec, and SSH.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** You don't need to reproduce the full proof. Just state: *"HMAC is secure as long as (i) the underlying hash is a PRF, (ii) the key has high entropy, and (iii) the key is longer than the IV size — otherwise pre-hash the key with $H$ first."*

---

## 3.3 Derivation: RSA Signature Verification Mathematics

**Problem:** Verify that Bob correctly recovers the message digest from Alice's RSA-PSS signature.

**Setup:** Alice has private key $(d, n)$ and public key $(e, n)$ where $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$.

### Step 1: Alice Signs the Hash

Alice computes:

$$\sigma = (H(M))^{d} \mod n$$

She transmits $(M, \sigma)$ to Bob.

### Step 2: Bob Verifies

Bob computes:

$$h' = \sigma^{e} \mod n = ((H(M))^{d})^{e} \mod n = (H(M))^{de} \mod n$$

### Step 3: Apply the RSA Identity

By Fermat's little theorem (for primes $p, q$ in $n = pq$):

$$(H(M))^{de} \equiv H(M)^{1 + k\phi(n)} \equiv H(M) \pmod{n}$$

for some integer $k$.

### Step 4: Final Verification

Bob accepts if and only if:

$$h' = H(M) \pmod{n}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Why hash before signing?** Signing $M$ directly would expose Alice's signing operation to a **forgery attack**: Mallory can choose any $\sigma$ and compute $M' = \sigma^{e} \mod n$ to forge a message Alice never signed. Hashing first binds the signature to a unique fingerprint of $M$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Merkle–Damgård Padding Length

**Problem:** For a message of length $\ell$ bits and a block size of $b = 512$ bits, compute the padding bits required.

### Step 1: Compute the Total After Adding the `1` Bit

After appending a single `1` bit, the length is $\ell + 1$ bits.

### Step 2: Append 64-Bit Length Field

The padded message must end with the **64-bit big-endian encoding** of $\ell$:

$$\text{Length} = 64 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Required Zero Padding

The total length must be a multiple of $b = 512$. So:

$$(\ell + 1 + 64 + k) \equiv 0 \pmod{512}$$

where $k$ is the number of zero padding bits.

$$k = (512 - ((\ell + 1 + 64) \mod 512)) \mod 512$$

### Step 4: Example

If $\ell = 1024$ bits:
- After `1` bit: 1025 bits
- After length field: 1089 bits
- $1089 \mod 512 = 65$
- $k = 512 - 65 = 447$ zero bits
- Total padded message: $1089 + 447 = 1536$ bits = $3 \times 512$ ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the length field?** It prevents **length-extension attacks** where an attacker, given $H(M)$ and $\vert M\vert$, can compute $H(M \Vert \text{extension})$ without knowing $M$. SHA-3's sponge construction eliminates this risk by design.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Hash Algorithm Comparison (Engineering Trade-off Table)

| Algorithm | Output (bits) | Block (bits) | Rounds | Speed (MB/s, x64) | Security Status |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **MD5** | 128 | 512 | 4 | ~700 | **Broken** (collisions trivial) |
| **SHA-1** | 160 | 512 | 80 | ~400 | **Deprecated** (SHAttered, 2017) |
| **SHA-256** | 256 | 512 | 64 | ~300 | **Secure** (industry standard) |
| **SHA-512** | 512 | 1024 | 80 | ~500 | **Secure** (faster on 64-bit) |
| **SHA-3-256** | 256 | 1088 (rate) | 24 (Keccak-f) | ~200 | **Secure** (post-quantum ready) |
| **BLAKE2** | 256/512 | 512/1024 | 10/12 | ~1000 | **Secure** (faster than MD5!) |
| **BLAKE3** | 256 | 64 | 7 (tree) | ~5000+ | **Secure** (parallel, modern) |

## 4.1 Generic Block Diagram: The Integrity Verification Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Alice: Original Message M] --> B[Hash Function H]
    B --> C[Message Digest h = HM]
    C --> D[Transmit Channel]
    A --> D
    D --> E[Bob: Receives M and h]
    E --> F[Bob Recomputes h prime = HM prime]
    F --> G{Compare h and h prime}
    G -->|Match| H[ACCEPT Message M is Intact]
    G -->|Mismatch| I[REJECT Message M is Tampered]
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style H fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    style I fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style G fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
```

## 4.2 HMAC Internal Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    K[Shared Secret Key K] --> P1[Pad or Hash K to block length b]
    P1 --> XOR1
    P1 --> XOR2
    ipad["ipad 0x36 repeated b times"] --> XOR1
    ipad2["ipad 0x36 repeated b times"] --> XOR2
    XOR1 --> K1[K XOR ipad]
    XOR2 --> K2[K XOR ipad]
    M[Message M] --> Inner
    K1 --> Inner
    Inner --> InnerH[Inner Hash H]
    InnerH --> Outer
    opad["opad 0x5C repeated b times"] --> XOR3
    K[Shared Secret Key K] --> XOR3
    XOR3 --> K3[K XOR opad]
    K3 --> Outer
    Outer --> OutH[Outer Hash H]
    OutH --> Tag[HMAC Tag T]
    style Tag fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    style InnerH fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style OutH fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
```

## 4.3 Digital Signature Flow (Sign-then-Verify)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Alice
    participant Channel
    participant Bob
    participant Mallory
    Alice->>Alice: Compute h = H(M)
    Alice->>Alice: Compute sigma = Sign SK of h
    Alice->>Channel: Send M, sigma
    Note over Channel,Mallory: Mallory can intercept
    Mallory--xChannel: Attempt bit flip on M
    Channel->>Bob: Deliver tampered M prime, sigma
    Bob->>Bob: Compute h prime = H of M prime
    Bob->>Bob: Compute v = Verify PK of h prime, sigma
    alt v equals h prime
        Bob->>Bob: ACCEPT M prime is authentic
    else v not equal to h prime
        Bob->>Bob: REJECT M prime TAMPERED
    end
```

## 4.4 Content Integrity Mechanism Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need Content Integrity] --> Q1{Is the channel trusted?}
    Q1 -->|No adversarial party| T1[Use simple checksum CRC32]
    Q1 -->|Adversary present| Q2{Need sender authentication?}
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Need non-repudiation?}
    Q3 -->|No| M1[Use HMAC SHA-256 symmetric key K]
    Q3 -->|Yes| M2[Use Digital Signature RSA or ECDSA]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q4{Shared secret key feasible?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| M3[Use HMAC with shared K]
    Q4 -->|No PKI available| M4[Use Digital Signature]
```
