```mermaid
    style M2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    style M3 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745

```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Differentiate between content integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation. State which cryptographic primitive provides all three simultaneously.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Service | Goal |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Content Integrity** | Assures the receiver that the message content has *not been altered* in transit. |
| **Authentication** | Assures the receiver that the message originated from the *claimed sender*. |
| **Non-Repudiation** | Prevents the sender from *denying* later that they sent the message. |

The cryptographic primitive that provides **all three simultaneously** is the **Digital Signature** (e.g., RSA-PSS, ECDSA). HMAC provides integrity + authentication but **not** non-repudiation, because both parties share the symmetric key.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List the three security properties of a cryptographic hash function. For a 256-bit hash, what is the approximate number of trials required to find a collision via the birthday attack?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three security properties are:
1. **Pre-image Resistance** — given $h$, hard to find $M$ such that $H(M) = h$.
2. **Second Pre-image Resistance** — given $M_1$, hard to find $M_2 \neq M_1$ with $H(M_2) = H(M_1)$.
3. **Collision Resistance** — hard to find *any* $M_1 \neq M_2$ with $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.

For a 256-bit hash, the birthday attack requires approximately:
$$2^{256/2} = 2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38} \text{ hash evaluations}$$
This is **computationally infeasible** with current technology.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of **HMAC**. State the formal definition and explain why the *ipad* and *opad* constants are used.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare **MD5, SHA-1, and SHA-256** with respect to digest length, block size, security status, and collision resistance. Recommend which one to use in a 2024 production banking application.

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

> [!NOTE]
> **HMAC = Hash-based Message Authentication Code** (RFC 2104). It is the *de facto* standard for keyed integrity.

**Block Diagram** (3 marks):

```
              ┌──────────────────────────────┐
              │      Shared Secret Key K      │
              └──────────────┬───────────────┘
                             │
                  ┌──────────┴──────────┐
                  │                     │
              K ⊕ ipad             K ⊕ opad
                  │                     │
              ┌───┴────┐                │
              │        │                │
              M        │                │
              │        │                │
              ▼        │                │
        ┌──────────┐   │                │
        │ Inner H  │   │                │
        │  = H((K⊕ipad)∥M)              │
        └────┬─────┘   │                │
             │  hash   │                │
             ▼         │                │
         hash₁         │                │
             │         │                │
             └────►┌───┴────────┐◄───────┘
                  │ Outer H   │
                  │ = H((K⊕opad)∥hash₁)  │
                  └─────┬──────┘
                        │
                        ▼
                  HMAC Tag T
```

**Formal Definition (2 marks):**
$$\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\!\left((K \oplus opad) \,\Vert\, H\!\left((K \oplus ipad) \,\Vert\, M\right)\right)$$

- $ipad$ = `0x36` repeated to block length
- $opad$ = `0x5C` repeated to block length
- $H$ = underlying hash (e.g., SHA-256)

**Why two pads? (2 marks):** The use of two different constant pads ensures that the *inner key* ($K \oplus ipad$) and *outer key* ($K \oplus opad$) are cryptographically independent. This prevents **length-extension attacks** on the underlying Merkle–Damgård hash and allows the security proof to reduce HMAC's security directly to the PRF property of the compression function.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

| Property | MD5 | SHA-1 | SHA-256 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Digest Length** | 128 bits | 160 bits | 256 bits |
| **Block Size** | 512 bits | 512 bits | 512 bits |
| **Rounds** | 4 | 80 | 64 |
| **Pre-image Security** | $2^{128}$ | $2^{160}$ | $2^{256}$ |
| **Collision Security** | $2^{64}$ (broken) | $2^{80}$ (theoretical break, 2017) | $2^{128}$ (secure) |
| **Status (2024)** | **Deprecated** | **Deprecated** (NIST, 2011) | **Recommended** |
| **Speed** | Fastest | Fast | Moderate |

**Recommendation for Banking Application (2 marks):** Use **SHA-256** (or higher) — preferably combined with **HMAC-SHA-256** if a shared key is available, or **RSA-PSS with SHA-256** for non-repudiation. MD5 and SHA-1 are explicitly **forbidden** by PCI-DSS 4.0 and NIST SP 800-131A for financial applications due to known collision attacks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] — ALTERNATIVE

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **Merkle–Damgård** construction for iterative hash functions. Show how a message is padded and processed block-by-block, and explain the security implication of the length field.

**(b) [7 marks]** A company uses a **64-bit MAC** for message authentication in a financial transaction system. **(i)** Calculate the number of forged messages an attacker can produce before a collision is found (use the birthday bound). **(ii)** Explain why this is *insecure* by modern standards. **(iii)** Recommend a fix.

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Merkle–Damgård Construction (4 marks):**
1. **Pad the message** $M$ to a multiple of block size $b$:
   $$M_{padded} = M \,\Vert\, 1 \,\Vert\, 0^{k} \,\Vert\, \text{len}(M)_{64}$$
   where $k$ zeros fill the gap to the next block boundary.
2. **Split** into $L$ blocks: $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_L$.
3. **Iterate** the compression function $f$:
   $$H_0 = IV \quad (\text{a fixed public constant})$$
   $$H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i), \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, L$$
4. **Output** $H(M) = H_L$.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):**

```
   IV → [f] → H₁ → [f] → H₂ → ... → [f] → H_L = H(M)
         ↑         ↑                    ↑
        M₁        M₂                  M_L
```

**Security Implication of Length Field (2 marks):** Appending the 64-bit message length prevents **length-extension attacks** — a class of attacks on naive iterative hashes where, given $H(M)$ and $\vert M \vert$, an attacker can compute $H(M \Vert M')$ *without knowing $M$*. The length field binds the hash to a specific, known message length, breaking the attacker's ability to extend. SHA-3 avoids this risk entirely via the *sponge construction*.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Birthday Bound Calculation (3 marks):**
- For a MAC of size $n = 64$ bits, the birthday attack finds a collision in approximately $2^{n/2}$ trials:
  $$\sqrt{2^{64}} = 2^{32} \approx 4.3 \times 10^{9} \text{ trials}$$
- This is **about 4.3 billion attempts** — feasible on a single modern GPU in hours.

**(ii) Why Insecure (2 marks):**
- NIST and PCI-DSS require MACs of **at least 128 bits** (and prefer 256 bits).
- $2^{32}$ operations falls well below the $2^{128}$ security bound required for financial transactions.
- An attacker forging messages with matching MACs is *practical* and would compromise the integrity of the entire transaction stream.

**(iii) Recommended Fix (2 marks):**
- **Use HMAC-SHA-256** (output = 256 bits, collision security $2^{128}$) or **HMAC-SHA-512** (output = 512 bits, collision security $2^{256}$).
- For per-message freshness, append a **nonce** or **timestamp** before hashing to prevent replay attacks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students compute $2^{64}$ instead of $2^{32}$ for the birthday bound on a 64-bit MAC. **The birthday bound is $\sqrt{N}$, not $N$.**
> - Students often forget the **length-extension** concern in Part (a) — it is a *direct* application of the length field and worth 1–2 marks.
> - In Part (b), do *not* recommend AES-ECB as a "fix" — ECB provides confidentiality only, not keyed integrity. Always recommend a *MAC*.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for Content Integrity (Module 4) — KTU 2024 Scheme**

- ☐ **Content Integrity** = assurance that data has not been altered. One pillar of the **CIA Triad**.
- ☐ Active adversary is **Mallory**; eavesdropper is **Eve**.
- ☐ **Hash function** $H$ maps arbitrary-length input to fixed $n$-bit output.
- ☐ Three security properties: **Pre-image, Second Pre-image, Collision Resistance** (memorize exact definitions).
- ☐ **Birthday attack** finds collisions in $2^{n/2}$ trials (not $2^n$).
- ☐ **MD5** is broken ($2^{64}$ collisions trivial); **SHA-1** is deprecated; use **SHA-256 or SHA-3**.
- ☐ **Merkle–Damgård**: pad with $1 \Vert 0^k \Vert \text{len}$; iterate $H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i)$ from a fixed $IV$.
- ☐ **Length-extension attacks** are prevented by the length field; SHA-3 sponge avoids them by design.
- ☐ **MAC** = keyed hash; provides integrity + authentication. **HMAC** is the standard construction.
- ☐ **HMAC** uses two pads: $ipad = 0x36$, $opad = 0x5C$, each repeated to block size.
- ☐ **Digital Signatures** = MAC's public-key analog; add **non-repudiation**; use **hash-then-sign** paradigm.
- ☐ **HMAC** does NOT provide non-repudiation; **Digital Signatures** DO.
- ☐ RSA signature verification: $\sigma^e \mod n = H(M)$ confirms integrity.
- ☐ Real-world deployments: **TLS 1.3, Git, Bitcoin, software updates, X.509 certificates, password storage**.
- ☐ For KTU exam: always specify *which* hash is broken/insecure; quantify security in bits; mention avalanche effect.
- ☐ Minimum recommended digest/MAC length in 2024: **128 bits collision, 256 bits pre-image** (NIST SP 800-131A).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strong Authentication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST744 - INFORMATION SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Strong Authentication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strong Authentication** (also called **Multi-Factor Authentication, MFA** or **Two-Factor Authentication, 2FA**) is a security mechanism that requires the user to provide **two or more independent verification factors** drawn from at least **two distinct categories** of credentials (Knowledge, Possession, and Inherence) in order to gain access to a digital resource, system, or application.

In the formal language of the KTU 2024 syllabus (Information Security, PECST744), *Strong Authentication* is defined as a layered authentication paradigm in which the security of the identity-verification process is derived not from a single secret (such as a password), but from the **cryptographic combination of multiple independent proofs of identity**, such that the compromise of any single factor does not result in unauthorized access.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module 4):**
> Strong authentication encompasses Kerberos authentication, One-Time Password (OTP) systems, challenge-response mechanisms, biometric verification, smart-card based authentication, and the broader multi-factor authentication framework. The aim is to defeat password-guessing, replay, credential-theft, and man-in-the-middle attacks that defeat *weak* (single-factor) authentication.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a **high-security bank vault**. The guard does not let you in just because you say a secret code (password). Instead, the system demands:

1. **Something you KNOW** → You recite a secret PIN (knowledge factor).
2. **Something you HAVE** → You insert your physical ATM card into a card reader (possession factor).
3. **Something you ARE** → A camera scans your face / fingerprint to verify your biometrics (inherence factor).

Only when **all three independent proofs** align does the vault door open. This is the essence of *strong authentication*: even if a thief steals your PIN, they still cannot enter without your card and your fingerprint. The layers reinforce each other.

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU viva question: *"What makes authentication 'strong'?"* — The answer is the **independence and diversity** of factors, not merely the *length* of a password. A 20-character password is still *single-factor* (knowledge) and is therefore *weak* compared to a 6-character PIN plus a hardware token.

## 1.3 The Three Authentication Factor Classes

The KTU syllabus emphasizes the **canonical trichotomy** of authentication factors introduced by the *Identity Assurance* framework:

| Factor Class | Definition | Standard Examples | Security Metric |
|--------------|------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Knowledge Factor (Type 1)** | Something the user *knows* | Password, PIN, passphrase, security question | **Weakest** — vulnerable to phishing, keylogging, social engineering |
| **Possession Factor (Type 2)** | Something the user *has* | Smart card, hardware token, OTP-generating device, mobile phone (SMS) | **Medium** — vulnerable to theft but requires physical access |
| **Inherence Factor (Type 3)** | Something the user *is* | Fingerprint, iris scan, face geometry, voiceprint, keystroke dynamics | **Strongest** — extremely difficult to forge; raises privacy concerns |

> [!WARNING]
> A pitfall KTU examiners often test: **using two factors from the same class (e.g., password + PIN) is NOT strong authentication**. Both are *Type 1* (Knowledge) — strong authentication requires factors from **at least two different classes**.

## 1.4 Why Single-Factor (Password-Only) Authentication Fails

Password-only authentication is classified as **weak authentication** in the KTU syllabus because it suffers from well-documented vulnerabilities:

- **Dictionary attacks** — automated tools try common passwords at high speed.
- **Brute-force attacks** — exhaustive key-space search.
- **Phishing** — fake login pages harvest credentials.
- **Keylogging malware** — captures keystrokes at the OS level.
- **Shoulder surfing** — visual observation of password entry.
- **Credential reuse** — one breach exposes many accounts.
- **Replay attacks** — an eavesdropped password is re-transmitted later.

Strong authentication neutralizes these attacks by requiring **additional independent factors** that cannot be captured by the same attack vector.

## 1.5 Scope of This Module

Within Module 4 of PECST744, Strong Authentication is decomposed into the following sub-topics (all examinable):

1. **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) Framework**
2. **Authentication Tokens** (Memory cards, Smart cards, Software/Hardware tokens)
3. **Biometric Authentication** (Verification 1:1 vs. Identification 1:N)
4. **One-Time Passwords (OTP)** — HOTP and TOTP algorithms
5. **Challenge-Response Authentication**
6. **Kerberos Authentication Protocol** (Realm, KDC, AS, TGS, Ticket-granting)

Each of these is treated in the sections that follow.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Strong Authentication

Strong authentication operates on a layered *Enrol → Challenge → Verify → Grant* pipeline. The KTU syllabus expects students to articulate each stage precisely.

### Step 1 — Enrolment Phase (Out-of-Band)
The user registers with the authentication server by submitting samples of each factor. The server stores:
- A salted hash of the password (never plaintext).
- The public key or serial number of the possession device.
- An enrolment template of the biometric (often a *minutiae map*, not the raw image).

### Step 2 — Claim of Identity
The user presents an *identifier* (username, account number, email) to declare *who they claim to be*.

### Step 3 — Challenge
The server issues a non-replayable *challenge* (a nonce, a counter value, or a server-generated random number) to the user over a secure channel. The challenge is fresh for every authentication attempt.

### Step 4 — Response Computation
The user's client device computes a cryptographic response to the challenge using the registered secret. This may involve:
- HMAC-SHA1 of a counter value (for HOTP).
- HMAC-SHA1 of a time-stamp truncated value (for TOTP).
- A signature with a private key stored on a smart card.
- A biometric match-score against the enrolled template.

### Step 5 — Server Verification
The server, which holds the same secret or template, independently computes the expected response. If the user's response **matches** within a defined tolerance (exact match for cryptographic factors, thresholded similarity for biometrics), access is granted; otherwise, the attempt is logged and rejected.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a 'Challenge' is essential:** A static password is a fixed secret and is therefore *replayable*. A challenge makes each authentication unique, defeating replay attacks even if the channel is eavesdropped.

## 2.2 Authentication Tokens — Deep Analysis

The KTU syllabus subdivides authentication tokens into three architectural classes:

### 2.2.1 Memory Cards
- Store data but **cannot process** it.
- Example: magnetic stripe on a bank card.
- Static; the same data is read each time.
- Considered **weak** because cloning is trivial (skimmers).

### 2.2.2 Smart Cards
- A plastic card with an **embedded microprocessor and memory**.
- Performs cryptographic operations *internally*; the secret key **never leaves the card**.
- Standards: **ISO 7816** (contact), **ISO 14443** (contactless).
- Two variants:
  - *Contact smart cards* — physical contact with a reader.
  - *Contactless (RFID/NFC) smart cards* — proximity-based.
- Often used in **PKI** to store X.509 certificates and private keys.

### 2.2.3 Software / Hardware Tokens
- Generate a **one-time password (OTP)** periodically or on demand.
- **Hardware tokens** — dedicated battery-powered devices with their own clock (e.g., RSA SecurID).
- **Software tokens** — apps on smartphones (Google Authenticator, Microsoft Authenticator).
- The token secret is seeded at manufacturing time and shared with the authentication server.

## 2.3 Biometric Authentication — Deep Analysis

Biometrics operate on a fundamentally different authentication model than cryptographic factors.

### 2.3.1 Verification (1:1) vs. Identification (1:N)
- **Verification (1:1)**: User claims an identity (e.g., employee ID); the system compares the live biometric against the *single* enrolled template. This is a 1:1 match.
- **Identification (1:N)**: User does *not* claim an identity; the system searches the *entire database* to find a match. This is computationally heavier and used in forensic / surveillance contexts.

### 2.3.2 Operating Characteristics
- **False Acceptance Rate (FAR)** — probability that an *impostor* is accepted.
- **False Rejection Rate (FRR)** — probability that a *genuine* user is rejected.
- **Crossover Error Rate (CER / EER)** — the operating point where FAR = FRR; a single-number indicator of biometric accuracy. *Lower CER = better biometric.*

### 2.3.3 Categories of Biometrics
- **Physiological**: fingerprint, iris, retina, face, hand geometry.
- **Behavioral**: voice, signature dynamics, gait, keystroke rhythm.

## 2.4 One-Time Password (OTP) Systems

OTP systems are the workhorse of strong authentication in modern enterprise and consumer systems.

### 2.4.1 HMAC-based OTP (HOTP) — RFC 4226
HOTP is an *event-based* OTP. Its algorithm:

$$
HOTP(K, C) = \text{Truncate}\big(\text{HMAC-SHA1}(K, C)\big) \bmod 10^{d}
$$

where:
- $K$ = shared secret key (typically **160-bit**).
- $C$ = 8-byte **moving counter** that increments with each OTP generated.
- $d$ = number of digits (typically **6**).
- $\text{Truncate}(\cdot)$ = dynamic truncation per RFC 4226 (selects 31 bits from HMAC output).
- $\bmod 10^{d}$ = reduces the result to $d$ decimal digits.

### 2.4.2 Time-based OTP (TOTP) — RFC 6238
TOTP is *time-based*: the counter $C$ is replaced by a time-slot value.

$$
T = \left\lfloor \frac{T_{\text{current}} - T_{0}}{X} \right\rfloor
$$

$$
TOTP(K, T) = HOTP(K, T)
$$

where:
- $T_{\text{current}}$ = current Unix timestamp.
- $T_{0}$ = epoch origin (default **0** = 1970-01-01 00:00 UTC).
- $X$ = time-step duration (default **30 seconds**).

A typical TOTP sequence: 30-second window → 6-digit code → auto-refresh.

## 2.5 Challenge-Response Authentication

The general protocol exchange:

1. User sends identity $A$ to server.
2. Server generates random nonce $N$ (the *challenge*) and sends it to user.
3. User computes $R = f(N, S)$ where $S$ is the shared secret; sends $R$ back.
4. Server independently computes $R' = f(N, S)$; accepts if $R = R'$.

Common function choices: $f = \text{HMAC-SHA1}$, $f = \text{AES}_S(N)$, or a public-key signature $f = \text{Sign}_{\text{Priv}_A}(N)$.

> [!NOTE]
> Kerberos is essentially a **multi-step challenge-response protocol** mediated by a trusted third party (the KDC), augmented with symmetric-key tickets and time-bound session keys.

## 2.6 Kerberos Authentication — KTU High-Yield Architecture

Kerberos (developed at MIT, RFC 4120) is the canonical KTU strong-authentication protocol. It uses **symmetric-key cryptography** and a **trusted Key Distribution Center (KDC)**.

### 2.6.1 Core Entities
- **Principal (C)** — the client (user/service).
- **Authentication Server (AS)** — authenticates the principal using its long-term key.
- **Ticket-Granting Server (TGS)** — issues service tickets.
- **Service Server (V)** — the resource the client wants to access.
- **Realm** — administrative domain; KDC serves one realm.

### 2.6.2 The Four-Step Kerberos Exchange (Simplified)

**Step 1 — AS Request/Reply (Authentication Service Exchange)**

Client → AS : $ID_C \parallel ID_{TGS} \parallel TS_1$

AS → Client : $E_{K_C}\big[K_{C,TGS} \parallel ID_{TGS} \parallel TS_2 \parallel \text{Lifetime}_2 \parallel \text{Ticket}_{TGS}\big]$

where $\text{Ticket}_{TGS} = E_{K_{TGS}}\big(K_{C,TGS} \parallel ID_C \parallel AD_C \parallel TS_2 \parallel \text{Lifetime}_2\big)$

**Step 2 — TGS Request/Reply (Ticket-Granting Exchange)**

Client → TGS : $ID_V \parallel \text{Ticket}_{TGS} \parallel \text{Authenticator}_C$

$\text{Authenticator}_C = E_{K_{C,TGS}}\big(ID_C \parallel AD_C \parallel TS_3\big)$

TGS → Client : $E_{K_{C,TGS}}\big(K_{C,V} \parallel ID_V \parallel TS_4 \parallel \text{Ticket}_V\big)$

**Step 3 — Client/Server Authentication Exchange (C/S Exchange)**

Client → V : $\text{Ticket}_V \parallel \text{Authenticator}_C'$

V → Client : $E_{K_{C,V}}\big(TS_5 + 1\big)$ *(optional mutual auth)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Kerberos is "strong":**
> (a) Passwords never traverse the network — the AS_REQ is only an *identity claim*; the AS_REPLY is encrypted with the user's key derived from the password.
> (b) Replay is prevented by timestamps inside authenticators and a replay cache.
> (c) Tickets are *time-limited* — short lifetime limits damage from credential theft.
> (d) Mutual authentication is provided — server proves identity to client in step 3.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Domain / Constants | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **HOTP** | $HOTP(K,C) = \text{Trunc}(\text{HMAC-SHA1}(K,C)) \bmod 10^d$ | $K$ = 160-bit secret, $C$ = 8-byte counter, $d=6$ | Event-based OTP |
| 2 | **TOTP time-slot** | $T = \lfloor (T_{cur} - T_0) / X \rfloor$ | $X = 30\text{ s}$, $T_0=0$ | Time-based OTP slot |
| 3 | **TOTP = HOTP** | $TOTP(K,T) = HOTP(K,T)$ | — | Reduces TOTP to HOTP |
| 4 | **Shannon Entropy (password)** | $H = L \cdot \log_2(N)$ | $L$ = length, $N$ = pool size | Password strength |
| 5 | **Biometric CER** | $\text{CER} = \text{FAR} = \text{FRR}$ | Operating point | Single-number accuracy |
| 6 | **Kerberos TGS ticket** | $E_{K_{TGS}}(K_{C,TGS} \parallel ID_C \parallel AD_C \parallel TS_2 \parallel \text{Lifetime}_2)$ | Symmetric encryption | Proof of identity to TGS |
| 7 | **Kerberos authenticator** | $E_{K_{C,TGS}}(ID_C \parallel AD_C \parallel TS_3)$ | Session-key encrypted | Proves ticket possession |
| 8 | **Crossover error rate relation** | $\text{CER} \downarrow \implies \text{Accuracy} \uparrow$ | — | Compare biometrics |
| 9 | **Mutual authentication** | $E_{K_{C,V}}(TS_5+1)$ | — | Server proves identity to client |
| 10 | **OTP length (digits)** | $d$ | Typically **6** | Display width on tokens |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Strong authentication is the cornerstone of:
- **Online banking** — PIN + OTP via SMS or hardware token.
- **Enterprise SSO** — Kerberos in Windows Active Directory (default domain auth).
- **Cloud services** — TOTP / FIDO2 / WebAuthn as second factor.
- **Government ID** — biometric (Aadhaar, e-passport) + smart card.
- **Healthcare HIPAA compliance** — multi-factor access to patient records.
- **PCI-DSS Requirement 8.3** — mandates MFA for remote network access (a frequent KTU viva question).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive, line-by-line analytical derivations of the high-yield KTU 2024 numerical and protocol-analysis questions on Strong Authentication.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 — Compute a HOTP Value

> **Problem (KTU-style):** Given a shared secret key $K = 0x3132...343536...$ (hex, 20 bytes), an 8-byte counter $C = 0x0000000000000001$, and a digit length $d=6$, compute the HOTP value. (We walk through the RFC 4226 algorithm symbolically; the symbolic Truncate step is shown in full.)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Concatenate the counter as an 8-byte big-endian integer.**

$$
C_{\text{bytes}} = \text{big-endian}(C) = \text{0x00 00 00 00 00 00 00 01}
$$

This byte string is the message input to HMAC-SHA1.

**Step 2 — Compute the HMAC-SHA1 of the counter using the shared key.**

$$
\text{HMAC} = \text{HMAC-SHA1}(K, C_{\text{bytes}})
$$

The HMAC-SHA1 algorithm (per RFC 2104) computes:

$$
\text{HMAC}(K, m) = H\Big(\big(K_{\text{opad}} \oplus \big) \Vert H\big((K_{\text{ipad}} \oplus) \Vert m\big)\Big)
$$

where:
- $H(\cdot)$ = SHA-1 hash (160-bit output).
- $K_{\text{ipad}}$ = 64-byte inner pad = 0x36 repeated.
- $K_{\text{opad}}$ = 64-byte outer pad = 0x5C repeated.
- $K$ is zero-padded to 64 bytes if shorter.

**Step 3 — Take the low-order 4 bits of the last byte as an offset $O$.**

$$
O = \text{HMAC}[19] \;\&\; 0\text{x}0F
$$

This gives an offset $O \in [0, 15]$, since the lower 4 bits encode values 0–15. This is the *dynamic truncation* that produces 4 bytes of pseudorandom data from a 20-byte digest.

**Step 4 — Extract 4 bytes starting at offset $O$, and apply the 0x7F mask to clear the sign bit.**

$$
P = \text{(HMAC}[O] \;\&\; 0\text{x}7F) \Vert \text{HMAC}[O+1] \Vert \text{HMAC}[O+2] \Vert \text{HMAC}[O+3]
$$

The mask **0x7F** is critical: the high bit of the first byte is cleared to prevent the resulting integer from being negative, which would break the modulo operation that follows.

**Step 5 — Convert the 4-byte big-endian word to a 31-bit unsigned integer.**

$$
P_{\text{int}} = \sum_{i=0}^{3} \text{HMAC}[O+i] \cdot 256^{(3-i)}
$$

Equivalently, treating the 4 bytes as a 32-bit unsigned integer and stripping the sign bit yields a 31-bit value: $0 \le P_{\text{int}} < 2^{31}$.

**Step 6 — Reduce modulo $10^d$.**

$$
HOTP = P_{\text{int}} \bmod 10^{6}
$$

This produces a 6-digit decimal OTP in the range $[0, 999999]$.

**Step 7 — Result interpretation.**

The HOTP value is a 6-digit code; the counter $C$ must be incremented by 1 (and resynchronized if drift occurs) before the next OTP is generated.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 — Convert Time to a TOTP Time-Slot

> **Problem:** Compute the TOTP time-slot $T$ for a current Unix timestamp $T_{cur} = 1700000000$, with $T_0 = 0$ and $X = 30$ seconds.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write down the TOTP slot formula.**

$$
T = \left\lfloor \frac{T_{cur} - T_0}{X} \right\rfloor
$$

This converts a continuous timestamp into a discrete integer index that increments every $X$ seconds.

**Step 2 — Substitute the given numerical values.**

$$
T = \left\lfloor \frac{1700000000 - 0}{30} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator.**

$$
1700000000 - 0 = 1700000000
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by the time-step.**

$$
\frac{1700000000}{30} = 56666666.6667
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the floor function.**

$$
T = \left\lfloor 56666666.6667 \right\rfloor = 56666666
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the resulting 6-digit TOTP.**

$$
TOTP = HOTP(K, 56666666)
$$

This is then passed through the Truncate / $\bmod 10^{6}$ pipeline described in §3.1.

**Step 7 — Validity window.**

The server typically accepts $T-1$, $T$, and $T+1$ to allow for clock drift, network latency, and user-typing time. The 90-second *look-back window* (3 × 30 s) is the default in RFC 6238.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation 3 — Kerberos TGS-Request Decryption

> **Problem:** A client receives the following encrypted blob from the AS (all values symbolic). Show, step by step, how the client derives the session key $K_{C,TGS}$ and constructs the authenticator for the TGS.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Client receives the AS_REPLY.**

$$
AS\_REPLY = E_{K_C}\Big(K_{C,TGS} \Vert ID_{TGS} \Vert TS_2 \Vert \text{Lifetime}_2 \Vert \text{Ticket}_{TGS}\Big)
$$

The entire blob is encrypted with $K_C$, the client's long-term key derived from the user's password.

**Step 2 — Client computes $K_C$ from the entered password.**

The password is passed through a one-way string-to-key function (e.g., PBKDF2 / bcrypt / AES key-wrap material). The output is the 256-bit symmetric key $K_C$.

**Step 3 — Client decrypts the AS_REPLY.**

$$
\text{Plaintext} = D_{K_C}\big(AS\_REPLY\big)
$$

After decryption the client extracts the five fields: $K_{C,TGS}$ (the *session key* for the client–TGS conversation), $ID_{TGS}$, $TS_2$, $\text{Lifetime}_2$, and $\text{Ticket}_{TGS}$.

**Step 4 — Client checks the timestamp $TS_2$ and lifetime.**

If $|T_{cur} - TS_2| > \text{Lifetime}_2$, the ticket is rejected as expired or replayed.

**Step 5 — Client constructs the TGS request.**

The client composes a request to the TGS that includes the *un-decryptable* $\text{Ticket}_{TGS}$ (which the TGS will decrypt with its own key $K_{TGS}$) plus a fresh *authenticator*:

$$
\text{Authenticator}_C = E_{K_{C,TGS}}\big(ID_C \Vert AD_C \Vert TS_3\big)
$$

The authenticator is encrypted with the *newly obtained* session key $K_{C,TGS}$, proving the client knows this key without revealing it.

**Step 6 — Client sends TGS request to TGS.**

$$
\text{TGS\_REQ} = ID_V \Vert \text{Ticket}_{TGS} \Vert \text{Authenticator}_C
$$

The TGS decrypts the authenticator, verifies $TS_3$ is fresh, and decrypts the ticket. Because both contain $ID_C$ and match, the TGS is assured the requester is the legitimate client.

**Step 7 — TGS issues a service ticket.**

$$
\text{TGS\_REPLY} = E_{K_{C,TGS}}\big(K_{C,V} \Vert ID_V \Vert TS_4 \Vert \text{Ticket}_V\big)
$$

The new session key $K_{C,V}$ is for the client-to-service-server conversation, and $\text{Ticket}_V$ is encrypted with $K_V$, the service's long-term key.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Authentication Mechanism Selection

The following analytical matrix guides selection of an appropriate strong-authentication mechanism under varying KTU-style constraints:

| Use-Case Context | Primary Threat | Recommended Factor Combination | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Online banking** | Phishing + credential theft | Password + SMS-OTP + biometric (mobile) | Combats phishing and SIM-swap with biometric |
| **Enterprise VPN (remote)** | Password spray | Smart card (PKI) + PIN | Hardware-bound private key, cannot be exfiltrated |
| **Cloud admin console** | Insider credential leak | Hardware token (FIDO2) + biometric | Phishing-resistant, hardware root of trust |
| **ATM withdrawal** | Card skimming + PIN capture | Card + PIN + (in some regions) biometric | Physical possession + knowledge + inherence |
| **Government e-services** | Large-scale identity fraud | Aadhaar/PKI + OTP + biometric | Federated identity with multi-modal verification |
| **Kerberos in AD domain** | Replay + on-path attacks | Ticket + authenticator (timestamped) | Time-limited tickets prevent replay; AS handles initial password-derived auth |

---

## 3.5 Mathematical Insight — Why a Single Factor Is Insufficient

The KTU syllabus expects students to articulate the *information-theoretic* weakness of single-factor authentication.

> [!NOTE]
> **Shannon's Argument for Multi-Factor Auth:** If a password has entropy $H(P)$ bits, the adversary's work to brute-force it is $2^{H(P)}$ on average. If we add a second independent factor of entropy $H(Q)$, the joint work becomes $2^{H(P)+H(Q)} = 2^{H(P)} \cdot 2^{H(Q)}$. Therefore, *independent factors multiply security multiplicatively, not additively*.

For a 6-digit OTP, the entropy is at most $\log_2(10^6) \approx 19.93$ bits. Combined with a password of entropy $\sim 30$ bits, the joint work is $2^{49.93} \approx 8.6 \times 10^{14}$ attempts — well beyond practical brute-force.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Strong Authentication — High-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the *Block-Level Functional Architecture* of a generic strong-authentication system, mapping the user, client, factors, and the authentication server.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    user1["USER"] --> client1["CLIENT DEVICE"]
    client1 --> kf["KNOWLEDGE FACTOR MODULE"]
    client1 --> pf["POSSESSION FACTOR MODULE"]
    client1 --> inf["INHERENCE FACTOR MODULE"]
    kf --> coll["FACTOR COLLECTOR"]
    pf --> coll
    inf --> coll
    coll --> crypt["CRYPTOGRAPHIC RESPONSE MODULE"]
    crypt --> ch["SECURE CHANNEL TLS 1.3"]
    ch --> as1["AUTHENTICATION SERVER"]
    as1 --> db1["USER TEMPLATE DATABASE"]
    as1 --> log1["AUDIT LOG"]
    as1 --> policy["RISK / POLICY ENGINE"]
    policy --> decision{"ALL FACTORS VALID?"}
    decision -- YES --> grant["GRANT SESSION TOKEN"]
    decision -- NO --> deny["DENY AND LOG"]
    grant --> resource["PROTECTED RESOURCE"]
    deny --> log1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **USER** initiates the flow via a **CLIENT DEVICE** (laptop, mobile, smart card reader).
- The client invokes one module per factor class: **Knowledge**, **Possession**, **Inherence**.
- The **FACTOR COLLECTOR** aggregates and normalises the inputs.
- The **CRYPTOGRAPHIC RESPONSE MODULE** computes a response (HMAC, signature, or template match-score).
- Communication with the **AUTHENTICATION SERVER** occurs over a **SECURE CHANNEL** (TLS 1.3).
- The server consults the **USER TEMPLATE DATABASE** and the **AUDIT LOG**, then delegates the accept/reject decision to a **RISK / POLICY ENGINE**.

---

## 4.2 Kerberos 4-Step Authentication Flow (Mermaid)

The canonical Kerberos V5 authentication flow, broken down into the AS, TGS, and C/S exchanges.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    c1["CLIENT C"] -->|1. AS_REQ: ID_C, ID_TGS| as2["AUTH SERVER AS"]
    as2 -->|2. AS_REPLY: E_KC KCTGS, Ticket_TGS| c1
    c1 -->|3. TGS_REQ: ID_V, Ticket_TGS, Authenticator| tgs3["TGS"]
    tgs3 -->|4. TGS_REPLY: E_KCTGS KCV, Ticket_V| c1
    c1 -->|5. AP_REQ: Ticket_V, Authenticator| v5["SERVICE V"]
    v5 -->|6. AP_REPLY: E_KCV TS5+1 optional| c1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Step 1:** Client sends AS_REQ with its identity and the TGS it wants to talk to.
- **Step 2:** AS returns a session key $K_{C,TGS}$ encrypted with the client's long-term key, plus a ticket that the TGS can decrypt.
- **Step 3:** Client forwards the ticket to the TGS, plus a fresh authenticator proving possession of $K_{C,TGS}$.
- **Step 4:** TGS returns a new session key $K_{C,V}$ for the client–service conversation and a service ticket.
- **Step 5:** Client presents the service ticket to the actual service $V$.
- **Step 6:** (Mutual auth) Service returns an encrypted $TS_5+1$ to prove identity to client.

---

## 4.3 HOTP / TOTP Generation Pipeline (Mermaid)

The internal generation of a one-time password, as used in HOTP and TOTP.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1["SHARED SECRET K"] --> hmac1["HMAC-SHA1 ENGINE"]
    counter1["COUNTER C OR TIME T"] --> hmac1
    hmac1 --> digest["20-BYTE DIGEST"]
    digest --> off["OFFSET O LOW 4 BITS OF LAST BYTE"]
    off --> slice["4 BYTES FROM DIGEST STARTING AT O"]
    slice --> mask["MASK HIGH BIT 0x7F"]
    mask --> int31["31-BIT UNSIGNED INTEGER"]
    int31 --> mod["MOD 10 TO THE POWER 6"]
    mod --> otp1["6-DIGIT OTP OUTPUT"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **SHARED SECRET K** and the **COUNTER C** (or **TIME T**) feed the **HMAC-SHA1 ENGINE**.
- The 20-byte digest is dynamically truncated: the offset $O$ is the low 4 bits of the last byte.
- 4 bytes are sliced out, the high bit is masked, and the result is reduced to 6 decimal digits.
- The output is the **6-DIGIT OTP** displayed to the user.

---

## 4.4 Strong Authentication Decision Matrix (Mermaid Flowchart)

A *decision tree* that a KTU student can use to choose an authentication factor given a threat model.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{"Is credential phishing\nthe primary concern?"}
    q1 -- YES --> a1["Use FIDO2 / smart card\nhardware-bound keys"]
    q1 -- NO --> q2{"Is the device\nphysically shared?"}
    q2 -- YES --> a2["Add biometric factor\nface or fingerprint"]
    q2 -- NO --> q3{"Is network access\nremote VPN?"}
    q3 -- YES --> a3["Kerberos SSO or\nTOTP via mobile token"]
    q3 -- NO --> a4["Password + smart card\nor single-sign-on"]
```

This decision matrix is highly useful for KTU 14-mark design questions where students are asked to "design a strong-authentication scheme for a given scenario".

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q1, 3 Marks]**

**Q1. Define strong authentication. Differentiate between weak and strong authentication with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Strong authentication** is an identity-verification mechanism that requires the user to present credentials from **at least two independent factor classes** (knowledge, possession, inherence) to gain access.

| Property | Weak Authentication | Strong Authentication |
|---|---|---|
| **Factors used** | Single factor (typically password) | $\geq 2$ independent factors |
| **Compromise impact** | One leak = full breach | Multi-stage defence |
| **Phishing resistance** | Low | High (with hardware token) |
| **Example** | Username + password | Password + TOTP + fingerprint |

A password-only login is a textbook example of **weak** authentication; an ATM requiring card + PIN + biometric is **strong** authentication.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q2, 3 Marks]**

**Q2. What is a one-time password (OTP)? Explain the difference between HOTP and TOTP.**

**Model Answer:**

A **One-Time Password (OTP)** is a single-use authentication code that is valid for only one login session or a short time window.

- **HOTP (HMAC-based OTP, RFC 4226):** The OTP is generated by applying **HMAC-SHA1** to a *moving counter* $C$ and a shared secret $K$. Each new OTP increments $C$. It is *event-driven*.
- **TOTP (Time-based OTP, RFC 6238):** TOTP replaces the counter with a *time-slot* value $T = \lfloor (T_{cur} - T_0)/X \rfloor$ where $X$ is typically 30 s. It is *time-driven* and self-synchronises to the clock.

| Property | HOTP | TOTP |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Event (button press) | Time (every 30 s) |
| **Drift handling** | Resync window | Look-back window of $\pm 1$ slot |
| **User experience** | Press button on token | Code refreshes automatically |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> The KTU 2024 scheme mandates that the student answer **one** 14-mark question from Module 4, chosen from two alternatives. Each alternative has two sub-parts, typically of **7 marks each**. Below are two complete alternatives.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 14 Marks]**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Kerberos authentication protocol** in detail. List the entities involved and explain all four message exchanges of the simplified version. Highlight how Kerberos provides **mutual authentication** and defeats **replay attacks**.

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction.** Kerberos is a trusted-third-party authentication protocol designed for client–server applications over insecure networks. It uses **symmetric-key cryptography** and a **Key Distribution Center (KDC)** that hosts the Authentication Server (AS) and the Ticket-Granting Server (TGS).

**Entities involved:**
- **Client (C)** — the user / principal.
- **Authentication Server (AS)** — verifies the user's identity using their long-term secret.
- **Ticket-Granting Server (TGS)** — issues service-specific tickets.
- **Service Server (V)** — the protected resource.
- **Realm** — administrative domain governed by one KDC.

**The four message exchanges:**

1. **C → AS :** $ID_C \Vert ID_{TGS}$
   *The client declares identity and the TGS it wants to use.*
2. **AS → C :** $E_{K_C}\big(K_{C,TGS} \Vert ID_{TGS} \Vert TS_2 \Vert \text{Ticket}_{TGS}\big)$
   *The AS returns a session key $K_{C,TGS}$ encrypted with the user's password-derived key, plus a ticket for the TGS. The user's password itself never traverses the network.*
3. **C → TGS :** $ID_V \Vert \text{Ticket}_{TGS} \Vert \text{Authenticator}_C$
   *The client requests a service ticket; the authenticator is encrypted with $K_{C,TGS}$ and contains a fresh timestamp.*
4. **TGS → C :** $E_{K_{C,TGS}}\big(K_{C,V} \Vert ID_V \Vert TS_4 \Vert \text{Ticket}_V\big)$
   *The TGS issues a service-specific session key and a ticket for the actual service V.*
5. **C → V :** $\text{Ticket}_V \Vert \text{Authenticator}_C'$
   *The client presents the service ticket; the service decrypts it with its long-term key $K_V$ and verifies the authenticator.*
6. **V → C :** $E_{K_{C,V}}(TS_5 + 1)$
   *The service returns the timestamp + 1, encrypted with the new session key, proving its identity to the client.*

**Mutual authentication:** Step 6 — the service's ability to encrypt $TS_5+1$ with $K_{C,V}$ proves it possesses the correct key, hence authenticates the server to the client.

**Replay defence mechanisms:**
- **Timestamps** in tickets and authenticators.
- **Replay cache** at each server to detect re-submitted authenticators.
- **Short ticket lifetimes** (typically 5 minutes to 8 hours).
- **Session keys** unique per client–server pair.

**Conclusion.** Kerberos achieves strong authentication through third-party trust, symmetric-key cryptography, time-bounded tickets, and explicit mutual authentication.

---

**Q. (b) [7 Marks]** Explain **biometric authentication** in detail. Differentiate between **verification (1:1)** and **identification (1:N)** modes. Define **FAR, FRR, and CER** and explain how CER is used as a single-number indicator of biometric accuracy.

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction.** Biometric authentication verifies a user based on **physiological** (fingerprint, iris, face) or **behavioural** (voice, signature, gait) characteristics. Unlike passwords, biometrics are *non-revocable* and *unique* per individual, making them a powerful inherence factor.

**Verification (1:1) vs. Identification (1:N):**

| Property | Verification (1:1) | Identification (1:N) |
|---|---|---|
| **User claim of identity** | Yes (e.g., employee ID) | No (search the whole database) |
| **Comparison** | Live sample vs. *one* template | Live sample vs. *all* templates |
| **Computational cost** | Low | High (scales with N) |
| **Use case** | Login, door access | Forensics, surveillance |
| **Decision** | Match / no-match | Top-K candidates |

**Performance metrics:**

- **False Acceptance Rate (FAR):** $FAR = \dfrac{FA}{N_{impostor}} \times 100\%$
  Probability that an *impostor* is incorrectly accepted. $FA$ = number of false acceptances.

- **False Rejection Rate (FRR):** $FRR = \dfrac{FR}{N_{genuine}} \times 100\%$
  Probability that a *genuine* user is rejected. $FR$ = number of false rejections.

- **Crossover Error Rate (CER / EER):** The operating point on the ROC curve where $FAR = FRR$. A *lower CER* means the biometric system is more accurate. Vendors typically quote CER as the headline accuracy metric.

**ROC relationship.** As the decision threshold tightens, both FAR and FRR move in opposite directions. The CER is the single operating threshold that balances both error types.

**Engineering utility.** A fingerprint scanner with CER = 0.001% is suitable for high-security access control; voice authentication with CER = 1% is acceptable for telephone banking. CER therefore guides system design selection.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Alternative, 14 Marks]**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, explain the **architecture of a multi-factor authentication (MFA)** system. Discuss the **three factor classes** and explain why *using two factors from the same class is not strong authentication*. Provide two real-world examples of MFA.

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture of an MFA system.** (Refer to §4.1 Mermaid diagram.) The user presents credentials from at least two factor classes. The client device collects and cryptographically combines the factors into a single authentication request transmitted to the server over TLS 1.3. The server consults a template database and a risk/policy engine to decide.

**Three factor classes:**

1. **Knowledge (Type 1)** — Password, PIN, secret question. *Vulnerable to phishing and keylogging.*
2. **Possession (Type 2)** — Smart card, hardware token, mobile phone. *Vulnerable to physical theft but requires proximity.*
3. **Inherence (Type 3)** — Fingerprint, iris, face. *Difficult to forge; raises privacy concerns.*

**Why same-class factors fail:**

> [!WARNING]
> Two passwords are NOT strong authentication. They are both *Type 1* (Knowledge). A single phishing attack captures both. Strong authentication requires **independent factor classes** so that compromising one does not yield the others.

**Real-world examples:**

1. **Online banking login:** Password (knowledge) + SMS OTP (possession — phone) + optional fingerprint (inherence on mobile). Three independent classes.
2. **Enterprise laptop login:** Smart card (possession) + PIN (knowledge) + Windows Hello fingerprint (inherence). Three independent classes.

**Engineering design rule (PCI-DSS 8.3, NIST SP 800-63B):** Choose one factor from at least two distinct classes; for high-assurance (Level 3 / 4), use all three.

---

**Q. (b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **HOTP and TOTP algorithms** with the relevant equations. Compute a 6-digit TOTP for the current timestamp $T_{cur} = 1700000300$ given $T_0 = 0$ and $X = 30$ s. Discuss the **look-back window** used by the server.

**Model Answer:**

**HOTP algorithm (RFC 4226):** An event-based one-time password.

$$
HOTP(K, C) = \text{Truncate}\big(\text{HMAC-SHA1}(K, C)\big) \bmod 10^{d}
$$

- $K$ = 160-bit shared secret.
- $C$ = 8-byte event counter.
- $d$ = digit length (typically 6).
- $\text{Truncate}(\cdot)$ selects 4 bytes from the HMAC output using dynamic offset.

**TOTP algorithm (RFC 6238):** A time-based one-time password.

$$
T = \left\lfloor \frac{T_{cur} - T_0}{X} \right\rfloor
$$

$$
TOTP(K, T_{cur}) = HOTP(K, T)
$$

- $T_0$ = Unix epoch origin (default 0).
- $X$ = time step (default 30 s).

**Numerical computation:**

$$
T = \left\lfloor \frac{1700000300 - 0}{30} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 56666676.6667 \right\rfloor = 56666676
$$

The TOTP code is then:

$$
TOTP = HOTP(K, 56666676) \bmod 10^{6}
$$

**Look-back window:** The server accepts the current slot $T$, plus $T-1$ and $T+1$, giving a $\pm 30$ second window. This compensates for **clock drift**, **network latency**, and **user typing time**. The default RFC 6238 window is 3 slots (90 s total).

**Synchronisation issues:** If the client's and server's counters drift beyond the window, the user must perform a re-synchronisation by submitting several consecutive codes; the server walks forward through the counter space to find the current value.

**Conclusion:** TOTP combines the cryptographic strength of HMAC-SHA1 with the freshness of time-slot counters, making it a strong, scalable authentication mechanism widely deployed in modern enterprise and consumer systems (e.g., Google Authenticator, Authy).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks on Strong Authentication questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing HOTP counter with TOTP counter.** HOTP uses an event counter (incremented on each press); TOTP uses a time-slot. Examiners specifically test this.
> 2. **Stating "two passwords = MFA".** This is *NOT* strong authentication. Both factors must come from *different classes* (Knowledge + Possession, or Knowledge + Inherence).
> 3. **Forgetting the replay cache and timestamp mechanism** in Kerberos answers. These are the key replay-defence elements; omitting them loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Mishandling the truncation step in HOTP.** The dynamic offset $O$ is the *low 4 bits of the LAST byte* of the HMAC-SHA1 digest, not the first.
> 5. **Confusing FAR and FRR.** FAR is for *impostors accepted*; FRR is for *genuine users rejected*. A common error is to swap them.
> 6. **Skipping the mutual-authentication step in Kerberos** (Step 6, $E_{K_{C,V}}(TS+1)$). This step is what makes Kerberos *mutual* and is worth explicit marks.
> 7. **Forgetting the modulus $10^d$** in HOTP/TOTP derivations. Without it, the value is not a 6-digit code.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for KTU Module 4 — Strong Authentication:**

- **Definition:** Strong authentication = credentials from **$\geq 2$ independent factor classes**.
- **Three factor classes:** Knowledge (Type 1), Possession (Type 2), Inherence (Type 3).
- **Rule:** Two factors from the same class = NOT strong authentication.
- **Authentication tokens:** Memory card (static, weak), Smart card (cryptographic, strong), Hardware/Software token (OTP-generating).
- **Biometrics:** Verification = 1:1, Identification = 1:N. **CER** is the key single-number accuracy metric.
- **Performance metrics:** **FAR** = impostor accepted, **FRR** = genuine rejected, **CER = FAR = FRR** (lower is better).
- **HOTP equation:** $HOTP(K, C) = \text{Truncate}(\text{HMAC-SHA1}(K, C)) \bmod 10^d$.
- **HOTP Truncate step:** Offset $O$ = low 4 bits of the **last** HMAC byte; extract 4 bytes; mask high bit 0x7F; reduce mod $10^6$.
- **TOTP time-slot:** $T = \lfloor (T_{cur} - T_0)/X \rfloor$ with $X = 30$ s.
- **TOTP = HOTP(K, T):** TOTP is just HOTP with a time-derived counter.
- **TOTP look-back window:** Default $\pm 1$ slot (90 seconds total) to tolerate clock drift.
- **Kerberos entities:** Client, AS, TGS, Service V, KDC, Realm.
- **Kerberos 6-message exchange:** AS_REQ → AS_REPLY → TGS_REQ → TGS_REPLY → AP_REQ → AP_REPLY.
- **Kerberos session keys:** $K_{C,TGS}$ (client–TGS), $K_{C,V}$ (client–service).
- **Kerberos ticket format:** $E_{K_{TGS}}(K_{C,TGS} \Vert ID_C \Vert AD_C \Vert TS \Vert \text{Lifetime})$.
- **Kerberos authenticator format:** $E_{K_{C,TGS}}(ID_C \Vert AD_C \Vert TS_3)$ — proves ticket possession.
- **Kerberos security features:** Passwords never on wire; replay cache; time-bounded tickets; mutual authentication via $E_{K_{C,V}}(TS+1)$.
- **Use-cases:** Banking (PIN + OTP + biometric), Enterprise VPN (smart card + PIN), Cloud admin (FIDO2 + biometric), Government ID (PKI + OTP + biometric).
- **Standards to remember:** RFC 4226 (HOTP), RFC 6238 (TOTP), RFC 4120 (Kerberos V5), ISO 7816 (smart card), NIST SP 800-63B (digital identity guidelines).
- **Entropy argument:** Independent factors multiply security multiplicatively: $2^{H(P)+H(Q)} = 2^{H(P)} \cdot 2^{H(Q)}$.
- **Exam tip:** Always state the *threat model* first, then justify the factor choice. Examiners reward design reasoning, not just factor lists.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Access Controls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST744 - INFORMATION SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Access Controls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔐 Access Controls — Module 4

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Access Control** is the security discipline that enforces policy decisions governing which authenticated entities (**subjects**) are permitted to perform what actions (read, write, execute, delete, etc.) on which protected resources (**objects**) within a computing system. It is the operational backbone of the *AAA framework* — **Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting (Auditing)**.

In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, access control is positioned as the **central enforcement mechanism** between three core entities:

- **Subject (S):** An active entity (user, process, daemon) requesting access.
- **Object (O):** A passive resource (file, database row, network socket, device) being protected.
- **Access Right (A):** The authorized operation tuple $\langle s, o, t \rangle$ where $t$ is the permitted access type.

$$\text{AccessControl} = f(S, O, A, P)$$

where $P$ denotes the prevailing security policy.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hotel Keycard System"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a luxury hotel.
> - **Authentication** = Scanning your ID at the front desk to prove *who you are*.
> - **Authorization / Access Control** = The hotel keycard that unlocks *only* your room, the gym, and the pool — but **not** the presidential suite or the server room.
>
> The hotel's central PMS (Property Management System) is the *Reference Monitor*; your keycard permissions are the *Access Rights*; and the policy is the hotel's management rules.

This analogy maps perfectly onto enterprise systems: an employee can access HR's self-service portal (authorized) but not the payroll database (denied) — even after being *authenticated* on the corporate network.

### 1.3 Why Access Control Is Foundational

| # | Pillar | Explanation |
|---|--------|-------------|
| 1 | **Confidentiality** | Prevents unauthorized *disclosure* of data. |
| 2 | **Integrity** | Prevents unauthorized *modification* of data. |
| 3 | **Availability** | Ensures authorized users are *not* unjustly denied service. |
| 4 | **Non-Repudiation** | Auditing trail binds actions to identities. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Authentication answers *"Who are you?"* — Access Control answers *"What are you allowed to do?"* Confusing these two is a recurring 2-mark deduction in board papers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Principles of Access Control

The **Saltzer-Schroeder** design principles (1975) — still heavily referenced in KTU board questions — are:

- **Least Privilege** — Grant the *minimum* rights required to perform a task.
- **Separation of Duties (SoD)** — No single individual should control an entire critical transaction.
- **Need-to-Know** — Access is granted only on a per-requirement basis.
- **Default Deny (Fail-Safe Defaults)** — When in doubt, deny access.
- **Open Design (Kerckhoffs' principle)** — Security must not depend on secrecy of mechanism.
- **Complete Mediation** — Every access is checked against the access control matrix.
- **Psychological Acceptability** — Security mechanism must be user-friendly.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Access Control Models

### A. Discretionary Access Control (DAC)

- The **resource owner** decides who can access the resource.
- Implemented using **Access Control Lists (ACLs)** or **Capability Lists**.
- Vulnerable to **Trojan Horse** attacks (owner may unwittingly grant malicious access).

### B. Mandatory Access Control (MAC)

- A **central authority** (Security Policy / Lattice) enforces access.
- Users **cannot** alter policies or transfer ownership.
- Subjects and objects are assigned **Sensitivity Labels** (e.g., Top Secret, Secret, Confidential, Unclassified).
- Implemented via the **Bell-LaPadula** and **Biba** models.
- Used in military / government-grade systems (e.g., SELinux).

### C. Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)

- Access rights are assigned to **roles** (e.g., Manager, Clerk, Auditor), not to individuals.
- Users acquire rights by being *assigned* to roles.
- Defined by the **NIST RBAC Standard (INCITS 359-2012)** with four components: Core RBAC, Hierarchical RBAC, Constrained RBAC (SoD), and Symmetric RBAC.

### D. Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)

- Access decisions are evaluated dynamically using **attributes** of subject, object, and environment.
- Policy expressed as boolean rules, e.g.,
  $$\text{Allow} \iff (\text{role} = \text{Doctor}) \land (\text{dept} = \text{Cardiology}) \land (\text{time} \in [09{:}00, 17{:}00])$$
- Provides **fine-grained, context-aware** control.

## 2.3 Access Control Matrix (ACM)

The ACM is a fundamental theoretical construct — a 2D table where rows are **subjects**, columns are **objects**, and each cell contains the set of permitted access types.

| Element | Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Subjects | $S = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_m\}$ | Active entities |
| Objects | $O = \{o_1, o_2, \dots, o_n\}$ | Protected resources |
| Rights | $R = \{r_1, r_2, \dots, r_k\}$ | Operations (read, write, execute, own) |
| ACM | $M : S \times O \to 2^R$ | Mapping from $(s, o)$ to a set of rights |

$$M[s_i, o_j] = \{r \in R \mid \text{subject } s_i \text{ is granted right } r \text{ on object } o_j\}$$

For a *sparse* matrix (large $m, n$, few non-empty cells), two compact representations are used:

1. **Access Control List (ACL)** — column-wise storage; one list per object.
2. **Capability List (C-List)** — row-wise storage; one list per subject (held as unforgeable tokens).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Notation | Boundary / Unit | Real-World Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Access Control Function | $f: S \times O \times A \to \{\text{allow}, \text{deny}\}$ | Boolean | Single decision-point for every access. |
| 2 | ACM Cell | $M[s, o] \subseteq R$ | $R = \{\text{read}, \text{write}, \text{execute}, \text{own}\}$ | Theoretical model for DAC. |
| 3 | Bell-LaPadula *No-Read-Up* | $\lambda(s) \ge \lambda(o)$ | Lattice of labels | Confidentiality enforcement. |
| 4 | Bell-LaPadula *No-Write-Down* | $\lambda(s) \le \lambda(o)$ | Lattice of labels | Prevents information leakage. |
| 5 | Biba *No-Read-Down* (integrity) | $I(s) \le I(o)$ | Integrity lattice | Integrity enforcement. |
| 6 | Biba *No-Write-Up* | $I(s) \ge I(o)$ | Integrity lattice | Prevents corruption by low-integrity. |
| 7 | RBAC Role Hierarchy | $r_1 \ge r_2 \Rightarrow \text{auth}(r_1) \supseteq \text{auth}(r_2)$ | Partial order | Senior role inherits junior's rights. |
| 8 | Clark-Wilson CDIs / TPs | $\text{TP}_i : \text{CDI}_a \to \text{CDI}_b$ | Well-formed transactions | Commercial integrity. |
| 9 | Separation of Duties (Static) | $\vert \text{roles}(u) \cap \text{conflicting} \vert \le 1$ | Cardinality constraint | Anti-fraud. |
| 10 | Reference Monitor | $\forall (s, o, r): \text{checked} = \text{True}$ | Universal quantifier | Tamper-proof, complete mediation. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When asked for differences between DAC and MAC, students often write *"MAC is mandatory"* — a tautology. Always mention that MAC's key distinction is the **centralized security policy / lattice label** that *cannot be overridden by the owner*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Bell-LaPadula Model (BLP) — Confidentiality

BLP is a state-machine model over subjects $S$, objects $O$, and access modes $A = \{\text{read}, \text{append}, \text{write}, \text{execute}\}$. Each subject and object carries a **security level** $\lambda(\cdot)$ from a partially ordered lattice.

### 3.1.1 The Three Mandatory Properties

Let $s \in S$, $o \in O$, and $x \in A$.

**Property 1 — Simple Security (No Read-Up / "ss-property"):**

$$s \text{ can read } o \iff \lambda(s) \ge \lambda(o)$$

**Property 2 — Star Property (No Write-Down / "*-property"):**

$$s \text{ can append to } o \iff \lambda(s) \le \lambda(o)$$

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{Read rule:} \quad & \lambda_{\text{subj}} \ge \lambda_{\text{obj}} \\
\text{Write rule:} \quad & \lambda_{\text{subj}} \le \lambda_{\text{obj}}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

**Property 3 — Discretionary Security:** Access must also satisfy the DAC matrix.

### 3.1.2 Tranquility and the BLP Controversy

- **Strong Tranquility:** Security levels are *static* — no change once assigned.
- **Weak Tranquility:** Levels *may* change, but only such that the system never enters a *less secure* state.

> [!NOTE]
> The **System Z** counter-example (McLean, 1990) shows BLP's *tranquility principle* is required to prevent trivial downgrading attacks. KTU 2024 board questions often ask *"Why is tranquility needed?"* — answer: it prevents silent de-classification that would silently break confidentiality.

## 3.2 Biba Model — Integrity

Biba is the *dual* of BLP, replacing the confidentiality lattice with an integrity lattice $I(\cdot)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{No Read-Down (Low-Water-Mark):} \quad & s \text{ reads } o \iff I(s) \le I(o) \\
\text{No Write-Up (Ring Property):} \quad & s \text{ writes } o \iff I(s) \ge I(o)
\end{aligned}
$$

This ensures that **high-integrity** subjects are not contaminated by **low-integrity** objects, and vice versa.

## 3.3 Clark-Wilson Model — Commercial Integrity

Clark-Wilson (1987) targets *well-formed transactions* on **Constrained Data Items (CDIs)**.

The certification rules $\text{CR}_i$ and enforcement rules $\text{ER}_i$ form a five-relation framework:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CR1:} \quad & \text{CDI}_a \xrightarrow{\text{TP}} \text{CDI}_b, \quad \text{TP} \in \mathcal{T} \\
\text{CR2:} \quad & \text{TP}_i \in \mathcal{T} \text{ is associated with user } u_i \\
\text{CR3:} \quad & \text{User-Identity authentication} \\
\text{CR4:} \quad & \text{Append-only log of TPs (UDI trail)} \\
\text{CR5:} \quad & \text{TP transforms UDI} \to \text{CDI on commit}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{T}$ = set of certified Transformation Procedures.
- $\text{UDI}$ = Unconstrained Data Item (input, not integrity-protected).
- $\text{CDI}$ = integrity-protected business data.

## 3.4 Access Control Matrix Worked Example

Suppose a system has:
- Subjects: $S = \{\text{Alice}, \text{Bob}, \text{Process}_P\}$.
- Objects: $O = \{\text{File}_1, \text{File}_2, \text{Printer}\}$.
- Rights: $R = \{r, w, x, o\}$ (read, write, execute, own).

The matrix is:

| Subject \ Object | $\text{File}_1$ | $\text{File}_2$ | $\text{Printer}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Alice | $\{r, w, o\}$ | $\{r\}$ | $\{w\}$ |
| Bob | $\{r\}$ | $\{r, w, o\}$ | $\{w\}$ |
| $\text{Process}_P$ | $\{r, x\}$ | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ |

### Equivalent Representations

- **ACL (column):**
  - $\text{File}_1$ → (Alice: r,w,o), (Bob: r), (Process$_P$: r,x)
  - $\text{File}_2$ → (Alice: r), (Bob: r,w,o)
  - $\text{Printer}$ → (Alice: w), (Bob: w)

- **Capability (row):**
  - Alice → (File$_1$: r,w,o), (File$_2$: r), (Printer: w)
  - Bob → (File$_1$: r), (File$_2$: r,w,o), (Printer: w)
  - Process$_P$ → (File$_1$: r,x)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ACLs are **object-centric** (easy to revoke, hard to enumerate all users). Capabilities are **subject-centric** (easy to delegate, hard to revoke). KTU 2024 questions frequently ask: *"Which is better for revocation?"* — Answer: **ACL**, because deletion is local to one column.

## 3.5 RBAC Role Hierarchy Derivation

Let roles be partially ordered by *seniority*: $r_1 \succeq r_2$ iff $r_1$ inherits all permissions of $r_2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{auth}(r_1) &= \text{direct\_perms}(r_1) \cup \bigcup_{r_2 \prec r_1} \text{auth}(r_2) \\
\text{users}(r) &= \{u \in U \mid u \text{ is authorized for } r\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Static SoD (SSD):** A user $u$ may not be a member of *both* mutually-exclusive roles $r_a$ and $r_b$ simultaneously. **Dynamic SoD (DSD):** Allows simultaneous membership but constrains *concurrent activation*.

## 3.6 Reference Monitor Formalism

The Anderson Report (1972) defines the **Reference Monitor** as a triple $(RM, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{E})$ that must satisfy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tamperproof:} \quad & \text{RM cannot be modified by subjects.} \\
\text{Complete Mediation:} \quad & \forall (s, o, a) : \text{RM is invoked.} \\
\text{Verifiable:} \quad & \text{RM is small enough to be formally verified.}
\end{aligned}
$$

The Reference Monitor Concept (RMC) is the *theoretical embodiment* of complete mediation. The **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)** is the *practical implementation* containing the reference monitor + OS kernel + hardware roots-of-trust.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reference-Monitor Mediated Access Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Subject s] -->|1. Request read on File F| B[Reference Monitor RM]
    B -->|2. Authenticate s| C[Authentication Subsystem]
    C -->|3. Identity token| B
    B -->|4. Lookup ACL of F| D[Access Control Matrix / Policy DB]
    D -->|5. Decision: allow or deny| B
    B -->|6a. If allow: forward to OS| E[Object F]
    B -->|6b. If deny: log + notify| F[Audit Log]
    E -->|7. Return result| A
    F -->|8. Append audit entry| G[SIEM / Audit Database]
```

## 4.2 RBAC Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    CFO[Chief Financial Officer] --> FinDir[Finance Director]
    FinDir --> AcctMgr[Accounting Manager]
    FinDir --> AudMgr[Audit Manager]
    AcctMgr --> AcctClk1[Accountant Clerk 1]
    AcctClk1 --> Intern[Intern]
    AudMgr --> Auditor1[Senior Auditor]
    Auditor1 --> Auditor2[Junior Auditor]
    classDef root fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mid fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef leaf fill:#118ab2,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px
    class CFO root
    class FinDir,AcctMgr,AudMgr,Auditor1 mid
    class AcctClk1,Intern,Auditor2 leaf
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of ABAC

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUB[Subject Attributes]
      S1[User ID]
      S2[Role]
      S3[Department]
      S4[Clearance Level]
    end
    subgraph OBJ[Object Attributes]
      O1[Resource Type]
      O2[Owner]
      O3[Sensitivity Tag]
      O4[Classification]
    end
    subgraph ENV[Environment Attributes]
      E1[Current Time]
      E2[IP Address]
      E3[Device Posture]
      E4[Geolocation]
    end
    subgraph PEP[Policy Enforcement Point]
      PEPR[Request Interceptor]
    end
    subgraph PDP[Policy Decision Point]
      PDPC[Rule Engine]
      PDPL[Policy Library]
    end
    subgraph PIP[Policy Information Point]
      PIPA[Attribute Database]
    end
    REQ[Access Request] --> PEPR
    SUB --> PDPC
    OBJ --> PDPC
    ENV --> PDPC
    PDPL --> PDPC
    PIPA --> PDPC
    PDPC -->|Decision Token| PEPR
    PEPR -->|Allow or Deny| RES[Protected Resource]
    PEPR --> AUD[Audit Log]
```

## 4.4 Bell-LaPadula Lattice Example (4-level classification)

```mermaid
graph TB
    TS[Top Secret]
    S[Secret]
    C[Confidential]
    U[Unclassified]
    TS --> S
    S --> C
    C --> U
    classDef top fill:#d62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef mid fill:#f77f00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef bot fill:#003566,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    class TS top
    class S,C mid
    class U bot
```

**Interpretation:** A subject with clearance $\text{Secret}$ may read objects labeled $\text{Unclassified}$, $\text{Confidential}$, or $\text{Secret}$ (No-Read-Up), but **not** $\text{Top Secret}$. By the *-property, it may write to $\text{Secret}$ objects only (No-Write-Down).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Authentication and Access Control. Why are they considered complementary?**

**Model Answer:**

Authentication is the process of *verifying the identity* of a subject (e.g., password, biometric, OTP). It answers *"Who are you?"* and produces an authenticated principal.

Access Control is the process of *enforcing policy* that determines what an *already-authenticated* subject is permitted to do. It answers *"What may you do?"*

They are **complementary** because:
1. Authentication *precedes* access control — no authorization decision can be made for an unverified subject.
2. Together they form the **AAA security triad** along with Accounting (logging).
3. Strong authentication without proper access control leads to authenticated-but-unauthorized abuse; strong access control without authentication is meaningless because anyone (even attackers) could be granted roles.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write *"Authentication is verifying the password and access control is the firewall."* That is **incorrect** — a firewall is a *network* access control device, not the definition. Stick to identity vs. authorization framing.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain the three mandatory properties of the Bell-LaPadula Model.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Simple Security Property (ss-property / No Read-Up):** A subject at security level $\lambda(s)$ may read an object at level $\lambda(o)$ only if $\lambda(s) \ge \lambda(o)$. Prevents unauthorized disclosure.

2. **Star Property (*-property / No Write-Down):** A subject may write (append) to an object only if $\lambda(s) \le \lambda(o)$. Prevents high-level information from leaking to lower-level containers.

3. **Discretionary Security Property:** In addition to the two mandatory checks, the access must also be permitted by the **Discretionary Access Matrix** (ACL / capability). This integrates BLP with DAC.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU typically follows this question with *"Why is tranquility needed?"* — answer: to prevent downgrading (silent label changes) that would otherwise allow write-down after the fact.

---

## 📗 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is mandatory.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Access Control Matrix** model in detail. Discuss its two practical implementations — *Access Control Lists* and *Capability Lists* — with a clear comparison of their advantages and disadvantages.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to the **Biba Model** of integrity, derive and explain the *No-Read-Down* and *No-Write-Up* properties. Compare Biba with the **Bell-LaPadula Model** in a tabular form.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A(a)

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The Access Control Matrix is a formal state-based model proposed by Lampson (1971) that maps every subject–object pair to the set of access rights granted. Formally:

$$
M : S \times O \to 2^R
$$

where $S$ = set of subjects, $O$ = set of objects, and $R = \{r, w, x, o\}$ (read, write, execute, own).

**Access Control List (ACL) — column-wise (2 marks):**

- Each object stores a list of $(subject, rights)$ tuples.
- Example: $\text{File}_F$ → (Alice: r,w), (Bob: r).
- *Advantages:* Efficient **revocation** (delete entry from the column), easy to see "who can access this resource".
- *Disadvantages:* Hard to enumerate "all resources Alice can touch", subject-side review is expensive.

**Capability List — row-wise (2 marks):**

- Each subject holds an unforgeable **capability token** listing $(object, rights)$ tuples.
- Example: Alice → (File$_1$: r,w,o), (Printer: w).
- *Advantages:* Efficient **delegation** (pass the token), distributed systems can carry capabilities across hosts.
- *Disadvantages:* Hard to **revoke** (must locate and invalidate tokens globally), requires protected kernel storage.

**Comparison Table (1.5 marks):**

| Criterion | ACL | Capability |
|---|---|---|
| Storage direction | Per object | Per subject |
| Revocation | Easy (column edit) | Hard (token revocation) |
| Delegation | Hard (need owner) | Easy (transfer token) |
| Review of subject's rights | Expensive | Cheap |
| Implementation in OS | UNIX file permissions | Hydra / Cambridge CAP |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A(b)

**Biba Model — Integrity Lattice (3.5 marks):**

Let $I(\cdot)$ be the integrity level of subjects and objects, drawn from a lattice $(L, \le)$. Biba's two mandatory properties are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{No-Read-Down (Low-Water-Mark):} \quad & s \text{ may read } o \iff I(s) \le I(o) \\
\text{No-Write-Up (Ring Property):} \quad & s \text{ may modify } o \iff I(s) \ge I(o)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Rationale (1.5 marks):**

- *No-Read-Down* prevents a high-integrity subject from being *fooled* by low-integrity (potentially malicious) data.
- *No-Write-Up* prevents a low-integrity subject from *corrupting* a high-integrity object.

**Comparison with Bell-LaPadula (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Bell-LaPadula | Biba |
|---|---|---|
| Concern | **Confidentiality** | **Integrity** |
| Direction of protection | Prevents *disclosure* | Prevents *modification* |
| Read rule | No Read-Up ($\lambda_s \ge \lambda_o$) | No Read-Down ($I_s \le I_o$) |
| Write rule | No Write-Down ($\lambda_s \le \lambda_o$) | No Write-Up ($I_s \ge I_o$) |
| Application domain | Military / classified | Commercial / financial |
| Origin | 1973 (DoD) | 1975 (Biba) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse the *directions* of the read/write rules. A quick memory aid: **BLP protects secrets from leaking down**, **Biba protects integrity from contamination coming up**. Drawing the lattice arrows correctly in the answer earns the full 7 marks.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** model as per the NIST standard. Explain the concept of **role hierarchy** and **separation of duties** with examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is a **Reference Monitor**? State and explain its three mandatory properties. How is it related to the **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)**?

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B(a)

**NIST RBAC Standard — Four Components (2.5 marks):**

1. **Core RBAC** — users $U$, roles $R$, permissions $P$, sessions $S$. $\text{UA} \subseteq U \times R$, $\text{PA} \subseteq P \times R$.
2. **Hierarchical RBAC** — partial order $\succeq$ on $R$: $r_1 \succeq r_2 \Rightarrow \text{auth}(r_1) \supseteq \text{auth}(r_2)$.
3. **Constrained RBAC** — enforces **Separation of Duties (SoD)**:
   - *Static SoD (SSD):* $\vert \text{UA}(u) \cap \text{SSD\_roles} \vert \le 1$.
   - *Dynamic SoD (DSD):* A user cannot activate two mutually-exclusive roles *in the same session*.
4. **Symmetric RBAC** — permission–role review operations are supported.

**Role Hierarchy Example (2 marks):**

```
        Director
       /        \
   Manager    Auditor
      |
   Clerk
```

- *Director* inherits all permissions of *Manager* and *Auditor*.
- *Manager* inherits all permissions of *Clerk*.
- Inheritance is **transitive**: Director → Clerk automatically.

**Separation of Duties Example (2.5 marks):**

- *Static:* A user cannot be assigned to both **Cashier** and **Approver** roles — prevents one person from issuing and approving their own cheques.
- *Dynamic:* A user may hold both roles simultaneously, but cannot **activate** both in a single session — useful in just-in-time access models (e.g., AWS IAM with temporary elevation).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** RBAC ≠ "groups in Windows". The examiner expects the **four NIST components** explicitly. Writing only *"RBAC is about roles"* loses 2–3 marks.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B(b)

**Reference Monitor — Definition (2 marks):**

The Reference Monitor is an *abstract machine* (Anderson Report, 1972) that mediates *every* access of any subject to any object, enforcing the system's access control policy.

**Three Mandatory Properties (3 marks):**

1. **Tamper-Proof:** The RM itself cannot be modified or circumvented by malicious subjects. Implemented via hardware protection, kernel-mode isolation, or TEE (Trusted Execution Environment).
2. **Complete Mediation:** $\forall (s, o, a) \in S \times O \times A$, the RM is *unconditionally invoked*. No "bypass path" may exist.
3. **Verifiable (Small enough to be tested):** The RM must be small, modular, and amenable to formal verification — e.g., seL4 microkernel.

**Relation to TCB (2 marks):**

- The **TCB (Trusted Computing Base)** is the *set of all hardware, firmware, and software components* essential for enforcing security.
- The **Reference Monitor is a *concept***; the **TCB is its *implementation***.
- For example, in SELinux: TCB = {kernel, LSM hooks, policy compiler}; the Reference Monitor is the *abstract policy-enforcement function* embodied in those hooks.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus sometimes asks: *"Is an anti-virus part of the TCB?"* — Answer: **No**, unless it is the only mechanism enforcing mandatory policy. TCB inclusion is a *necessity* test, not a *desirability* test.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🎯 KTU Examiner's Final Warning:** Across all Part-B questions on Access Control, the three most common causes of mark loss are:
> 1. **Confusing the read/write directions** in BLP and Biba (≈ 2 marks lost).
> 2. **Omitting an example** for Separation of Duties or Role Hierarchy (≈ 1 mark lost).
> 3. **Defining TCB and Reference Monitor as synonyms** (≈ 1.5 marks lost) — they are *concept vs. implementation*.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Access Controls**

- 🔑 **Authentication ≠ Authorization.** Authentication is *who you are*; Access Control is *what you may do*.
- 📐 **Access Control Matrix** is a theoretical model; **ACLs** and **Capability Lists** are its two practical implementations — choose ACL for revocation, Capability for delegation.
- 🏛️ **DAC** = owner controls; **MAC** = central lattice controls; **RBAC** = role-based; **ABAC** = attribute-based (most flexible).
- 🔒 **Bell-LaPadula (BLP)** → *Confidentiality.* Two properties: **No-Read-Up (ss)** and **No-Write-Down (★)**. Tranquility principle is required.
- 🛡️ **Biba** → *Integrity.* Two properties: **No-Read-Down** and **No-Write-Up**. Biba is the *dual* of BLP.
- 💼 **Clark-Wilson** → *Commercial Integrity.* Uses **CDIs (constrained data items)** transformed only by certified **TPs (transformation procedures)**.
- 🧩 **RBAC** (NIST standard) has 4 components: **Core, Hierarchical, Constrained (SoD), Symmetric**. SSD vs. DSD distinction is a common exam point.
- 🧱 **Reference Monitor** has 3 properties: **Tamper-Proof, Complete Mediation, Verifiable**. **TCB** is its physical implementation.
