- ⚖️ **Saltzer-Schroeder Principles** to memorize: Least Privilege, Separation of Duties, Need-to-Know, Fail-Safe Defaults, Complete Mediation, Open Design, Psychological Acceptability.
- 🚫 **Common Mistake:** Do NOT confuse "Mandatory" (MAC) with "Discretionary" (DAC) in the wrong direction. MAC = **government / military**, DAC = **personal / commercial** (e.g., UNIX file permissions).
- 🎯 **Exam Tip:** When asked to compare two models, **always draw a table** — the examiner's tick-mark allocation rewards tabular answers.
- 📊 **Formula to Remember:** $M : S \times O \to 2^R$ is the single most-quoted formal definition; write it on top of every access-control answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST744 - INFORMATION SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wireless Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 4):** Wireless Security encompasses the protection of radio-frequency (RF) communication channels against eavesdropping, injection, replay, and unauthorized access. The KTU syllabus specifically demands coverage of **WEP, WPA, WPA2, WPA3, 802.11i, EAP variants, and wireless threat models**.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wireless Security** is the discipline of safeguarding over-the-air (OTA) data transmissions—governed by the IEEE **802.11** family (Wi-Fi), **802.15** (Bluetooth/ZigBee), and **802.16** (WiMAX)—against confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and availability (CIAA) violations using cryptographic primitives, key management protocols, and physical-layer countermeasures.

Formally, a wireless security scheme is the tuple:

$$ \mathcal{S}_{wireless} = \{\mathcal{K}, \mathcal{E}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{I}, \mathcal{P}\} $$

where:

- $\mathcal{K}$: Key establishment & distribution protocol (e.g., 4-way handshake)
- $\mathcal{E}$: Encryption algorithm (e.g., **RC4**, **AES-CCMP**, **GCMP**)
- $\mathcal{A}$: Authentication framework (e.g., **PSK**, **EAP-TLS**, **SAE**)
- $\mathcal{I}$: Integrity protection (e.g., **MIC**, **CCM**, **BIP**)
- $\mathcal{P}$: Privacy / anti-tracking mechanism (e.g., randomized MAC)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Invisible Conversation

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Shouting in a crowded room vs. whispering through a wire":**
> A wired network is like passing sealed letters through a private pneumatic tube—only the sender and receiver can physically touch the message. A wireless network is like two people having a conversation using a megaphone in a public park—**anyone within radio range can hear it, jam it, or even impersonate one of the speakers** unless the words are encoded in a language only the two intended parties understand. **Wireless security** is the *secret language and the rulebook* that ensures only the right participants can listen, speak, and trust what is being said.

### 1.3 The CIAA Threat Model in Wireless

| Dimension | Wireless Threat | Real-World Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Eavesdropping via promiscuous-mode sniffer (e.g., Wireshark with monitor-mode adapter) | Credential theft, session hijacking |
| **Integrity** | Bit-flipping / frame injection | DNS poisoning, ARP spoofing over-the-air |
| **Authentication** | Rogue AP, Evil Twin, MAC spoofing | Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) |
| **Availability** | De-authentication flood, jamming, CTS/RTS abuse | Denial-of-Service (DoS) |

### 1.4 Historical Evolution Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU syllabus mandates chronological understanding: **WEP (1999) → WPA (2003) → WPA2 (2004) → 802.11i (2004) → WPA3 (2018)**. Each generation is a **cryptographic patch** over its predecessor's fatal flaws, not a clean redesign.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 WEP — Wired Equivalent Privacy (IEEE 802.11, 1997/1999)

### 2.1.1 Operational Pipeline

WEP uses the **RC4 stream cipher** with a **40-bit** or **104-bit** static secret key concatenated with a **24-bit IV**. The IV is transmitted in the clear in every frame.

Plaintext confidentiality and integrity are achieved in **three steps**:

1. **Integrity Check Value (ICV)** is computed as a **CRC-32** over the plaintext:

$$ ICV = \text{CRC}\text{-}32(M) $$

2. The combined payload $P = M \, || \, ICV$ is XORed with the RC4 keystream:

$$ C = P \oplus \text{RC4}(IV \, || \, K) $$

3. The transmitted frame becomes:

$$ \text{Frame} = IV \, || \, C $$

### 2.1.2 The Fatal Weakness — IV Reuse (Birthday Paradox)

Because the IV is only **24 bits**, the IV space is exactly $2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216$. By the **Birthday Paradox**, the probability of an IV collision reaches **50%** after roughly:

$$ N_{collision} \approx 1.177 \cdot \sqrt{2^{24}} \approx 4823 \text{ frames} $$

If two ciphertexts share the same IV, an attacker can recover the XOR of the two plaintexts:

$$ C_1 \oplus C_2 = (M_1 \oplus M_2) $$

Combined with the fact that **CRC-32 is linear**, an attacker can further inject arbitrary frames with correctly recomputed ICVs (the **"chopping" attack** by Arbaugh et al., 2001).

### 2.1.3 Additional WEP Vulnerabilities

- **Weak IV keys** (Klein's attack, 2005): ~9000 of the 16.7M IVs leak key information in the first bytes of RC4 keystream.
- **No replay protection** (no packet counters).
- **Static keys** — manual distribution only; no key management.
- **Authentication = Open System or Shared Key** — the latter is broken because the challenge plaintext is XORed with a keystream that the attacker can recover.

## 2.2 WPA — Wi-Fi Protected Access (2003, TKIP)

WPA was an **interim patch** on legacy hardware: same RC4 cipher but wrapped with two critical upgrades:

- **TKIP (Temporal Key Integrity Protocol)** — generates a **per-packet key** by mixing the base secret, the transmitter MAC, and a **48-bit TSC (TKIP Sequence Counter)**:

$$ \text{Per-Packet Key} = \text{Phase1}(TA, TK) \, || \, \text{Phase2}(TSC, TA) $$

- **MIC (Michael)** — a non-linear 8-byte integrity tag replacing CRC-32:

$$ MIC = \text{Michael}(M, K_{MIC}) $$

TSC exhaustion triggers **key rekeying**, defeating IV-replay attacks. WPA-Enterprise added **802.1X/EAP** for authentication.

> [!WARNING]
> Despite TKIP, the underlying RC4 weakness was never fully eliminated. WPA2 deprecated TKIP.

## 2.3 WPA2 — IEEE 802.11i (2004, CCMP/AES)

WPA2 introduced a **cryptographically sound** algorithm:

- **AES-CCMP** = **AES-CCM** mode = **AES-CTR** (confidentiality) + **AES-CBC-MAC** (integrity).
- **48-bit Packet Number (PN)** — never reused, providing replay protection.
- **CCM** produces a ciphertext + 8-byte MIC:

$$ (C, MIC) = \text{AES-CCM}_{TK}(PN, AAD, M) $$

The **PTK (Pairwise Transient Key)** is derived through the **4-way handshake**:

$$ PTK = \text{PRF-384}(PMK, \text{"Pairwise key expansion"} \vert\vert AA \vert\vert SA \vert\vert ANonce \vert\vert SNonce) $$

PTK decomposes into **5 sub-keys**:

$$ PTK = KCK \vert\vert KEK \vert\vert TK \vert\vert TK_{TX} \vert\vert TK_{RX} $$

- **KCK (128 bits)** — confirms handshake messages (MIC computation).
- **KEK (128 bits)** — encrypts the GTK delivery.
- **TK (128 bits)** — temporal key for data encryption.
- **TK_TX / TK_RX** — used by TKIP in mixed-mode.

## 2.4 WPA3 — SAE / Dragonfly (2018, NIST SP 800-119)

WPA3 introduced **SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals)**, replacing the vulnerable 4-way handshake's PSK-based dictionary attack surface.

**SAE operates in two phases:**

### Phase 1 — Password Element (PWE) derivation

$$ PWE = H_{MAC}(\text{Max}(MAC_A, MAC_B) \, || \, \text{Min}(MAC_A, MAC_B) \, || \, P \, || \, \text{Token} \, || \, \text{Identifier}) $$

where $P$ is the password and $H_{MAC}$ is a password-hashing MAC (e.g., **H2C** for the 19-byte ECC group).

### Phase 2 — Dragonfly Key Exchange (Diffie-Hellman analog over ECC)

Each peer commits to a random scalar, exchanges commitments, then reveals scalars:

$$ \sigma_A = \text{rand}_A + \text{mask}_A \pmod{q} $$
$$ \sigma_B = \text{rand}_B + \text{mask}_B \pmod{q} $$

Shared secret:

$$ SS_A = \sigma_A \cdot (B - \text{mask}_B \cdot PWE) = \sigma_A \cdot \sigma_B \cdot PWE = SS_B $$

**Forward secrecy** is now guaranteed — passive sniffing no longer enables offline dictionary attack.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU ESE — Memorize Before Exam**

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| WEP Ciphertext | $C = (M \, || \, ICV) \oplus \text{RC4}(IV \, || \, K)$ | $M$=plaintext, $K$=40/104-bit key |
| WEP ICV | $ICV = \text{CRC-32}(M)$ | Linear 32-bit checksum |
| IV Collision (50%) | $N \approx 1.177 \cdot \sqrt{2^{24}} \approx 4823$ | Frames before 50% collision |
| TKIP Per-Packet Key | $K_{pkt} = \text{Mix}(TA, TK, TSC)$ | $TA$=MAC, $TSC$=48-bit counter |
| WPA2 PTK | $PTK = \text{PRF-384}(PMK, \text{"Pairwise key expansion"} \vert\mid AA \vert\mid SA \vert\mid ANonce \vert\mid SNonce)$ | $AA$=Authenticator MAC, $SA$=Supplicant MAC |
| PMK (PSK) | $PMK = \text{PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA1}(P, SSID, 4096, 256)$ | $P$=passphrase, $SSID$=network name |
| PMK (EAP) | $PMK = \text{MSK} \, [0{:}32]$ | Derived from EAP-TLS master session |
| WPA3 PWE | $PWE = H_{MAC}(...)$ | Iterated H2C for ECC groups |
| SAE Shared Secret | $SS = \sigma_A \cdot \sigma_B \cdot PWE$ | Dragonfly exchange |
| CCMP Encryption | $(C, MIC) = \text{AES-CCM}_{TK}(PN, AAD, M)$ | 48-bit PN, 8-byte MIC |
| Rekey Interval | Recommended $T_{rekey} \le 2^{48}$ frames | Bounds PN exhaustion |
| 802.1X EAPOL | $EAPOL-Key = \text{Type} \vert\mid \text{Key\ Info} \vert\mid \text{Nonce} \vert\mid \text{MIC}$ | 4-way handshake frame |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Wireless security protocols are deployed in **enterprise mobility (BYOD), IoT mesh networks, vehicular ad-hoc networks (VANETs), industrial SCADA wireless links, and 5G NR sidelink** — each imposing different latency, energy, and crypto-agility constraints.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: PMK and PTK Generation in WPA2-PSK

The following derivation takes you from a user-typed passphrase all the way to the per-session temporal key used to encrypt Wi-Fi traffic.

### Step 1 — Passphrase to PMK (PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA1)

A user types a passphrase $P$ (8–63 ASCII characters) and the AP's SSID is known.

$$ PMK = \text{PBKDF2}(P, SSID, \text{iter}=4096, \text{dkLen}=256) $$

Expanded:

$$ PMK = T_1 \vert\mid T_2 \vert\mid \ldots \vert\mid T_{4096/20} $$

where each block $T_i$ is computed as:

$$ U_1 = \text{HMAC-SHA1}(P, SSID \vert\mid \text{INT}(i)) $$
$$ T_i = U_1 \oplus U_2 \oplus \ldots \oplus U_{iter} $$
$$ U_{j} = \text{HMAC-SHA1}(P, U_{j-1}) $$

**Significance:** The **4096 iterations** deliberately slow down brute-force dictionary attacks (≈300 ms per guess on commodity hardware).

### Step 2 — 4-Way Handshake (PTK Derivation)

Both AP and client already share $PMK$. They exchange nonces:

- **AP → Client**: ANonce (Message 1)
- **Client → AP**: SNonce + computed MIC using KCK (Message 2)
- **AP → Client**: GTK (encrypted with KEK) + MIC (Message 3)
- **Client → AP**: ACK + MIC (Message 4)

**Step 2.1 — Nonce Concatenation Order (Critical for KTU Marks)**

$$ \text{label} = \text{"Pairwise key expansion"} $$
$$ \text{nonce\ pair} = \text{Min}(ANonce, SNonce) \vert\mid \text{Max}(ANonce, SNonce) $$
$$ \text{mac\ pair} = \text{Min}(AA, SA) \vert\mid \text{Max}(AA, SA) $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Forgetting the **Min/Max ordering** of nonces and MACs is the #1 reason students lose marks in 14-mark derivations. Examiners explicitly test the lexicographic ordering.

**Step 2.2 — PRF-384 Application**

$$ PTK = \text{PRF-384}(PMK, \text{label} \vert\mid \text{nonce\ pair} \vert\mid \text{mac\ pair}) $$

The PRF-384 expands to 48 bytes using HMAC-SHA1 iteratively:

$$ \text{PRF-384}(K, A, B) = Y_0 \vert\mid Y_1 \vert\mid Y_2 $$

where:

$$ Y_0 = \text{HMAC-SHA1}(K, A \vert\mid 0 \vert\mid B \vert\mid 0) $$
$$ Y_1 = \text{HMAC-SHA1}(K, A \vert\mid 1 \vert\mid B \vert\mid Y_0) $$
$$ Y_2 = \text{HMAC-SHA1}(K, A \vert\mid 2 \vert\mid B \vert\mid Y_1) $$

Each $Y_i$ is 20 bytes; concatenated $3 \times 20 = 60$ bytes, truncated to **48 bytes (384 bits)**.

**Step 2.3 — Key Separation**

$$ PTK[0{:}16] = KCK \quad (\text{used for MIC of EAPOL-Key frames}) $$
$$ PTK[16{:}32] = KEK \quad (\text{used to encrypt GTK}) $$
$$ PTK[32{:}48] = TK \quad (\text{temporal key for CCMP/AES encryption}) $$

### Step 3 — Application to a Single CCMP Frame

For frame $i$ with packet number $PN_i$:

$$ \text{Nonce} = PN_i \vert\mid A2_{MAC} $$
$$ AAD = \text{Frame\ Control} \vert\mid A1 \vert\mid A2 \vert\mid A3 \vert\mid \ldots $$
$$ (C_i, MIC_i) = \text{AES-CCM}_{TK}(\text{Nonce}, AAD, M_i) $$

**Why AES-CCM and not AES-CBC?** CCM combines **CTR mode** (confidentiality, parallelizable, no padding) with **CBC-MAC** (integrity) in a single authenticated-encryption-with-associated-data (AEAD) construction — both provably secure and patent-free.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | WEP | WPA (TKIP) | WPA2 (CCMP) | WPA3 (SAE) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cipher** | RC4 | RC4 + TKIP | AES-128 CCM | AES-128/GCMP-256 |
| **Key Size** | 40/104-bit | 128-bit | 128-bit | 128/192/256-bit |
| **Integrity** | CRC-32 (broken) | MIC (Michael) | CCM-MIC | BIP-GMAC |
| **IV/PN Size** | **24-bit** ❌ | 48-bit TSC | 48-bit PN | 48-bit PN |
| **Key Mgmt** | Manual | 4-way + 802.1X | 4-way + 802.1X | SAE + 802.1X |
| **Forward Secrecy** | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| **Dictionary Attack** | Trivial | Hard | Hard | **Impossible offline** |
| **KRACK Resistant** | N/A | No | Vulnerable (CVE-2017-13082) | Immune |
| **Status (2024)** | Deprecated | Deprecated | Mandatory minimum | Recommended |

## 3.3 Wireless Threat Catalog (KTU 14-Mark Favorite)

1. **Passive Eavesdropping** — Sniffing with monitor-mode NIC.
2. **War Driving** — Locating networks via GPS + signal strength mapping.
3. **Rogue AP** — Unauthorised AP offering free connectivity to lure clients.
4. **Evil Twin** — Rogue AP cloning the SSID/BSSID of a legitimate AP.
5. **Deauthentication Attack** — Forged $\text{Deauth}(reason="Disassociated")$ broadcast.
6. **WPS PIN Brute Force** — 8-digit PIN split into 4+3 halves, only $10^4 + 10^3 = 11{,}000$ guesses (Reaver attack).
7. **KRACK (Key Reinstallation Attack)** — Resets PN by replaying handshake Msg 3.
8. **Jamming** — Continuous RF noise at 2.4 / 5 GHz bands.
9. **MAC Spoofing** — Impersonating an authorised station.
10. **Bluetooth Impersonation Attacks (BIAS)** — Master/slave role confusion.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 WEP Frame Encryption Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plaintext M] --> B[CRC-32 ICV]
    B --> C[M concatenated ICV]
    C --> D[XOR with RC4 keystream]
    K1[24-bit IV] --> E[Concatenate with Secret Key]
    K2[40 or 104-bit Secret Key] --> E
    E --> F[RC4 PRGA]
    F --> D
    D --> G[Ciphertext C]
    H[IV] --> I[Transmitted Frame: IV + C]
    G --> I
```

## 4.2 WPA2 4-Way Handshake — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant AP as Authenticator (AP)
    participant STA as Supplicant (Client)
    Note over AP,STA: Both share PMK
    AP->>STA: Msg1 EAPOL-Key (ANonce)
    STA->>STA: PTK = PRF-384(PMK, ANonce, SNonce, AA, SA)
    STA->>AP: Msg2 EAPOL-Key (SNonce + MIC_KCK)
    AP->>AP: PTK = PRF-384(PMK, ANonce, SNonce, AA, SA)
    AP->>STA: Msg3 EAPOL-Key (GTK encrypted with KEK + MIC)
    STA->>STA: Install TK and GTK
    STA->>AP: Msg4 EAPOL-Key (ACK + MIC)
    Note over AP,STA: Secure data channel established
```

## 4.3 Wireless Threat Model — Attack Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Wireless Attacks]
    R --> P[Passive]
    R --> A[Active]
    P --> P1[Eavesdropping]
    P --> P2[War Driving]
    P --> P3[Traffic Analysis]
    A --> A1[Injection]
    A --> A2[Impersonation]
    A --> A3[Availability DoS]
    A1 --> A1a[Frame Injection]
```

## 4.4 802.1X / EAP Authentication Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Supplicant - Wireless Client] -->|EAPOL| AP[Authenticator - Access Point]
    AP -->|RADIUS over UDP 1812| AS[Authentication Server - RADIUS]
    AS -->|EAP-TLS / PEAP / EAP-TTLS| IDP[Identity Provider / PKI]
    IDP -->|Certificate or Token| AS
    AS -->|Access Accept + PMK| AP
    AP -->|4-Way Handshake| S
```

## 4.5 SAE / Dragonfly Exchange (WPA3)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Station A
    participant B as Station B
    Note over A,B: Both know Password P and SSID
    A->>A: Compute PWE = H2C(P, MAC_A, MAC_B)
    B->>B: Compute PWE = H2C(P, MAC_A, MAC_B)
    A->>B: Commit(scalar_A, element_A)
    B->>B: Verify scalar_A, element_A in valid range
    A->>B: Confirm(scalar_A)
    B->>A: Commit(scalar_B, element_B)
    A->>A: Verify scalar_B, element_B
    B->>A: Confirm(scalar_B)
    A->>A: SS = scal_B * (element_A - mask_A * PWE)
    B->>B: SS = scal_A * (element_B - mask_B * PWE)
    Note over A,B: Shared Secret SS = scal_A * scal_B * PWE
    A->>B: Auth(MIC_A)
    B->>A: Auth(MIC_B)
```
